blocks:
Ayahs

الٓر ۚ كِتَـٰبٌ أُحْكِمَتْ ءَايَـٰتُهُۥ ثُمَّ فُصِّلَتْ مِن لَّدُنْ حَكِيمٍ خَبِيرٍ (١)
Alif, Lam Ra. [This is] a Book, whose signs have been made definitive and then elaborated, from One [who is] all-wise, all-aware, (1)
أَلَّا تَعْبُدُوٓا۟ إِلَّا ٱللَّهَ ۚ إِنَّنِى لَكُم مِّنْهُ نَذِيرٌ وَبَشِيرٌ (٢)
declaring: ‘Worship no one but Allah. I am indeed a warner to you from Him and a bearer of good news. (2)
وَأَنِ ٱسْتَغْفِرُوا۟ رَبَّكُمْ ثُمَّ تُوبُوٓا۟ إِلَيْهِ يُمَتِّعْكُم مَّتَـٰعًا حَسَنًا إِلَىٰٓ أَجَلٍ مُّسَمًّى وَيُؤْتِ كُلَّ ذِى فَضْلٍ فَضْلَهُۥ ۖ وَإِن تَوَلَّوْا۟ فَإِنِّىٓ أَخَافُ عَلَيْكُمْ عَذَابَ يَوْمٍ كَبِيرٍ (٣)
Plead with your Lord for forgiveness, then turn to Him penitently. He will provide you with a good provision for a specified term and grant His grace to every meritorious person. But if you turn your backs [on Him], indeed I fear for you the punishment of a terrible day. (3)
إِلَى ٱللَّهِ مَرْجِعُكُمْ ۖ وَهُوَ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ قَدِيرٌ (٤)
To Allah will be your return, and He has power over all things.’ (4)

Commentary

Four Important Principles in the Mission of the Prophets

This Surah, like the beginning of the previous Surah and many other Surahs of the Quran, begins by stating the importance of this great Heavenly Book, so that people may pay more attention to its contents and look upon it with a more discerning eye.

The mention of the disjointed letters (Huruful-Muqatta'ah) "Alif, Lam, Ra" is itself a proof of the importance of this great Heavenly Book, which, despite all its miracles and grandeur, is composed of simple disjointed letters available to everyone, such as "Alif, Lam, Ra" (الر). [^1] Following these disjointed letters, it describes one of the characteristics of the Holy Quran in two phrases: first, that it is "a Book whose verses are perfected and firmly established" (كِتابٌ أُحْكِمَتْ آياتُهُ).

And "then explained in detail, providing an exposition of all human needs in the realms of individual and social, material and spiritual life" (ثُمَّ فُصِّلَتْ).

From whom has this great Book with such characteristics been revealed? "From One who is both All-Wise and All-Aware" (مِنْ لَدُنْ حَكِيمٍ خَبِيرٍ).

By virtue of His being All-Wise (Hakim), He has made the verses of the Quran firm and robust, and by virtue of His being All-Aware (Khabir), He has expressed the verses of the Quran in various sections according to human needs. This is because unless one is aware of all the details of human spiritual and physical needs, He cannot issue instructions worthy of fostering perfection.

In fact, each of the attributes of the Quran mentioned in this verse stems from one of the attributes of Allah: the firmness of the Quran from His Wisdom, and its detailed explanation from His being All-Aware.

Regarding the difference between "Uhkimat" (perfected/firmly established) and "Fussilat" (explained in detail), commentators have discussed extensively and offered many possibilities. However, what seems closest to the meaning of the above verse is that the first phrase expresses the reality that the Quran is a single, interconnected whole, standing like a firm and stable structure, showing it was revealed by the One and Only God; for this reason, no contradiction or inconsistency is seen among its verses.

The second phrase, however, refers to the fact that this Book, while being a unity, has so many branches and divisions that it covers all the spiritual and physical needs of human beings. Therefore, it is many within unity, and one within multiplicity.

From the next verse, the most important and fundamental content of the Quran—namely Monotheism (Tawhid) and the struggle against polytheism—is stated as follows: "My first call is that you worship none but the One and Only Allah" (أَلَّا تَعْبُدُوا إِلَّا اللَّهَ) [^1] and this is the first detailed instruction of this great Book.

And the second program of my mission is that "I am for you from Him a warner and a bearer of glad tidings": I warn you against disobedience, injustice, corruption, polytheism, and disbelief, and I caution you against the consequences of your actions and Divine punishment; and in the face of obedience, purity, and piety, I give you glad tidings of a felicitous destiny (إِنَّنِي لَكُمْ مِنْهُ نَذِيرٌ وَ بَشِيرٌ).

My third call is that you seek forgiveness for your sins and wash yourselves of impurities (وَ أَنِ اسْتَغْفِرُوا رَبَّكُمْ), and my fourth call is that "you turn to Him in repentance." After washing away sins and becoming pure under the shadow of seeking forgiveness, adorn yourselves with Divine attributes, for returning to Him is nothing but emulating His attributes (ثُمَّ تُوبُوا إِلَيْهِ).

In fact, four important stages of the mission toward the Truth are stated within four phrases, two of which have an "ideological" and foundational aspect, and two have a "practical" and structural aspect: accepting the principle of Monotheism and fighting polytheism, and accepting the messengership of the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family) are two ideological principles.

Washing away sins and adopting Divine attributes, which encompasses constructive action in every sense, are two practical instructions of the Quran. If we reflect deeply, the entire content of the Quran is summarized in these four parts; this is an index for the whole Quran and for the entire content of this Surah.

Then, it states the practical results of "compliance" or "opposition" to these four instructions as follows:

"If you put these programs into practice, Allah will grant you enjoyment of a goodly life in this world until an appointed term" (يُمَتِّعْكُمْ مَتاعاً حَسَناً إِلى‌ أَجَلٍ مُسَمًّى).

And higher than that, He gives to everyone according to their work, and in no way ignores the differences among people in how they practice these four principles; rather, "He bestows His grace upon every possessor of merit in proportion to their merit" (وَ يُؤْتِ كُلَّ ذِي فَضْلٍ فَضْلَهُ).

"But if you turn away and rebel against these four ideological and practical instructions, I fear for you the punishment of a Great Day," the day you shall appear in the great court of Divine Justice (وَ إِنْ تَوَلَّوْا فَإِنِّي أَخافُ عَلَيْكُمْ عَذابَ يَوْمٍ كَبِيرٍ).

In any case, know that whoever you are and whatever position or status you hold, ultimately "to Allah is your return" (إِلَى اللَّهِ مَرْجِعُكُمْ).

This phrase refers to a fifth principle among the detailed principles of the Quran, namely the issue of the Hereafter and Resurrection.

But never think that your power is of any significance against the power of Allah, or that you can escape His command and His court of justice; nor imagine that He cannot gather your decayed bones after death and clothe them in the garment of life and a new existence, "for He has power over all things" (وَ هُوَ عَلى‌ كُلِّ شَيْ‌ءٍ قَدِيرٌ).

The Link Between Religion and the World

There are still many who imagine that being religious is only for building the abode of the Hereafter and comfort after death, and that good deeds are provisions for the next world, being completely indifferent to the role of pure and authentic religion in the life of this world, or giving it little importance.

In reality, before religion builds the abode of the Hereafter, it builds the abode of this world; and fundamentally, unless religion has an effect in this life, it will have no effect for that life! The Quran explicitly mentions this subject in many verses and sometimes even points to the details of the issues, as we read in Surah Nuh from the tongue of this great Prophet addressing his people: "So I said, 'Ask forgiveness of your Lord. Indeed, He is ever a Perpetual Forgiver. He will send [rain from] the sky upon you in abundance, and give you increase in wealth and children and provide for you gardens and provide for you rivers'" (Nuh 71:10-12).

Some consider the link between these material blessings of the world and seeking forgiveness and washing away sins to be exclusively a spiritual and unknown link, whereas there is no reason to provide an "unknown" interpretation for all of these.

Who does not know that lying, cheating, theft, and corruption tear apart the fabric of social life?

Who does not know that injustice, oppression, discrimination, and extortion darken the sky of human life?

And who doubts the truth that by accepting the principle of Monotheism and building a monotheistic society based on accepting the leadership of the Prophets, and cleansing the environment of sin and adorning it with human values—namely the same four principles mentioned in the above verses—society moves toward an evolutionary goal, and a safe and secure environment, full of peace, purity, prosperity, and freedom, is created.

For this reason, in the above verses, after mentioning these four principles, we read:

يُمَتِّعْكُمْ مَتاعاً حَسَناً إِلى‌ أَجَلٍ مُسَمًّى: "If you apply these principles, you will enjoy life in a worthy and good manner until the end of your life."

[^1]: The explanation of this meaning and other interpretations given for the disjointed letters of the Quran were mentioned at the beginning of Surah Al-Baqarah, Al-Imran, and Al-A'raf.

[^1]: Regarding the phrase أَلَّا تَعْبُدُوا إِلَّا اللَّهَ, two possibilities have been given: first, that it is from the tongue of the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family), as we said above, and its implied meaning is that "my call and command is that you worship none but Allah." Second, that it is the word of Allah, and its implied meaning is "I command you that you worship none but Allah." However, the phrase "Indeed, I am to you from Him a warner and a bearer of glad tidings" is more consistent with the first meaning.

Ayah

أَلَآ إِنَّهُمْ يَثْنُونَ صُدُورَهُمْ لِيَسْتَخْفُوا۟ مِنْهُ ۚ أَلَا حِينَ يَسْتَغْشُونَ ثِيَابَهُمْ يَعْلَمُ مَا يُسِرُّونَ وَمَا يُعْلِنُونَ ۚ إِنَّهُۥ عَلِيمٌۢ بِذَاتِ ٱلصُّدُورِ (٥)
Look! They fold up their breasts to hide [their secret feelings] from Him. Look! When they draw their cloaks over their heads, He knows whatever they keep secret and whatever they disclose. Indeed, He knows best whatever is in the breasts. (5)

Commentary

Although some commentators have mentioned specific occasions of revelation for this verse—including that it was revealed regarding the hypocrite "Akhnas ibn Shariq," who was a smooth-talking and deceitful man who expressed friendship and affection in the presence of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله while harboring enmity in his heart.

It has also been narrated from Imam al-Baqir عليه السلام from "Jabir ibn Abdullah" that a group of polytheists, when passing by the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, would lower their heads and even cover them with their garments so that the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله would not see them; this verse was revealed concerning them.

In any case, the verse generally refers to one of the foolish actions of the enemies of Islam and the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, who, by employing hypocritical methods and turning away from the truth, sought to hide their true nature from view so as not to hear the word of truth.

Therefore, it says: "Behold, they hide their enmity for the Prophet in their hearts and lower their heads, bending their chests forward to hide themselves from him" (أَلا إِنَّهُمْ يَثْنُونَ صُدُورَهُمْ لِيَسْتَخْفُوا مِنْهُ).

To precisely understand the meaning of the verse, the concept of the word "yathnuna" (يثنون) must be properly clarified.

"Yathnuna" is derived from the root "thana" (ثنى), which originally means bringing different parts of something close to one another. For example, regarding the folding of clothes or fabric, it is said: "thana thawbahu" (he folded his garment). The reason the number two is called "ithnan" (اثنان) is that we place one beside the other. And if praising someone is called "thana-khwani," it is because the prominent qualities of the person are recounted one after another.

This root also carries the meaning of "bending," because by doing so, a person brings parts of their body closer together.

Furthermore, it means harboring malice and enmity in the heart, because by doing this, a person brings the enmity of someone or something close to their heart. This expression is seen in Arabic literature: "athnuni sadrahu 'ala al-baghda'" (اثنونى صدره على البغضاء): "He harbored malice against me in his heart." [1]

Considering what has been mentioned, it appears that the above expression may refer to any kind of outward or inward "concealment" by the enemies of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله. On one hand, while harboring malice and enmity in their hearts, they outwardly expressed friendship with sweet tongues. On the other hand, when conversing with one another, they would bring their heads close together, pull their chests back, and even draw their garments over their heads to convey their slanders and conspiracies to one another in a mysterious way, so that no one would become aware of their secrets.

Therefore, the Quran immediately adds: "Behold, even when they cover themselves with their garments, their Lord knows what they conceal and what they reveal" (أَلا حِينَ يَسْتَغْشُونَ ثِيابَهُمْ يَعْلَمُ ما يُسِرُّونَ وَ ما يُعْلِنُونَ).

"For He is All-Knowing of what is within the breasts" (إِنَّهُ عَلِيمٌ بِذاتِ الصُّدُورِ).

[1]: Refer to "Taj al-Arus," "Majma' al-Bayan," "Al-Manar," and "Mufradat al-Raghib."

Ayah

۞ وَمَا مِن دَآبَّةٍ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ إِلَّا عَلَى ٱللَّهِ رِزْقُهَا وَيَعْلَمُ مُسْتَقَرَّهَا وَمُسْتَوْدَعَهَا ۚ كُلٌّ فِى كِتَـٰبٍ مُّبِينٍ (٦)
There is no animal on the earth, but that its sustenance lies with Allah, and He knows its [enduring] abode and its temporary place of lodging. Everything is in a manifest Book. (6)

Commentary

All Are His Guests!

In the final verse previously discussed, there was an allusion to the vastness of the Lord's knowledge and His encompassment of hidden and manifest secrets. The verse under discussion is, in fact, considered a proof for that, as it speaks of Allah providing sustenance to all creatures—a task that is impossible without complete scientific encompassment of the entire universe! First, it says: "There is no moving creature on earth but its sustenance is dependent upon Allah, and He knows its dwelling place and its temporary place of deposit" and wherever it may be, He delivers its sustenance to it:

(وَمَا مِن دَابَّةٍ فِي الْأَرْضِ إِلَّا عَلَى اللَّهِ رِزْقُهَا وَيَعْلَمُ مُسْتَقَرَّهَا وَمُسْتَوْدَعَهَا).

"All these truths, with all their boundaries and limits, are recorded in a Clear Book and the Preserved Tablet of Allah's knowledge" (كُلٌّ فِي كِتابٍ مُبِينٍ‌).

To understand the meaning of the verse, several points must be noted:

1- Although "dabbah" is derived from the root "dabib" meaning to walk slowly or take short steps, linguistically it encompasses every kind of moving creature. However, it is sometimes specifically applied to horses or any riding animal. It is clear that in the verse under discussion, it includes all living beings and all moving creatures.

2- "Rizq" (sustenance) means a continuous gift and bestowal. Since Divine sustenance is His continuous gift to creatures, it is called rizq. It is also necessary to mention that its concept is not limited solely to material needs; rather, it includes every kind of material and spiritual gift. Therefore, we say: "Allahumma-rzuqni 'ilman tamman" ("O Allah, grant me complete knowledge as sustenance") or we say: "Allahumma-rzuqni al-shahadata fi sabilik" ("O Allah, grant me martyrdom in Your path"). Of course, in the verse under discussion, the focus may be more on material provisions, although intending the general meaning is not far-fetched.

3- "Mustaqarr" originally means "dwelling place" or "settlement," as the root of this word is from "qarr" (rhyming with harr) meaning intense "cold" which keeps humans and living creatures indoors; for this reason, it has also come to mean stillness and stopping. "Mustawda'" and "wadi'ah" (deposit) are from the same root, and originally mean leaving something. Since unstable matters are left and return to their original state, every unstable matter is called "mustawda'." A deposit (wadi'ah) is also called so because it must eventually leave its place and return to its original owner.

In truth, the above verse says: let it not be imagined that Allah only grants sustenance to creatures that are in their primary dwelling place—bringing their "quota" to their homes, so to speak. Rather, wherever they may be and in whatever state or condition they find themselves, He grants them their share of sustenance, for He knows both their primary dwelling and all the points to which they move and relocate. From giant sea animals to tiny moving creatures that can hardly be seen by the eye, He has ordained for each what they need according to their condition.

This sustenance is so calculated and suited to the state of the creatures that in terms of "quantity" and "quality," it perfectly aligns with their desires and needs. Even the food of an infant in the mother's womb differs every month, or rather every day, from another month and day, even though it is apparently nothing more than one type of blood. Similarly, an infant during the nursing period, despite apparently having the same food for consecutive months, has the composition of this milk vary from one day to the next!

4- "Kitabin Mubin" means a "Clear Book" and refers to a stage of the Lord's vast knowledge which is sometimes expressed as the "Preserved Tablet" (al-Lawh al-Mahfuz). This expression may be an indication that no one should allow the slightest worry to enter their heart regarding obtaining their sustenance, nor imagine that they might be "overlooked," so to speak, in receiving their share, for the names of all are recorded in a Clear Book—a book that enumerates everyone and states it clearly. If in an institution the names of all employees and workers are clearly recorded in a ledger, is there any possibility of someone being overlooked?

The Distribution of Sustenance and the Struggle for Life

Regarding "rizq," there are important discussions, a part of which we review here:

1- "Rizq"—as we have said—linguistically means a continuous and ongoing gift, whether it be material or spiritual. Therefore, every kind of benefit that Allah grants His servants—from food, housing, and clothing, to knowledge, intellect, understanding, faith, and sincerity—all of these are called rizq. Those who limit the concept of this word to material aspects are not paying close attention to its usage. Regarding the martyrs in the way of Truth, the Quran says: بَلْ أَحْيَاءٌ عِندَ رَبِّهِمْ يُرْزَقُونَ "Nay, they are alive, finding their sustenance in the presence of their Lord" (Al-Imran: 169). It is clear that the sustenance of martyrs, especially in the world of Barzakh, is not material blessings, but rather those spiritual bounties that are difficult for us even to imagine in this material life.

2- The issue of providing for the needs of living creatures—or in other words, their sustenance—is one of the most fascinating subjects. With the passage of time and the progress of science, the veil is lifted from its secrets, and new, amazing scenes are revealed by science. In the past, all scientists wondered how food is provided for living creatures if they exist in the depths of the oceans, because the primary root of food returns to plants and vegetation, which in turn require sunlight, and at depths of more than 700 meters, there is absolutely no light, and an eternal dark night casts its shadow there. But it soon became clear that sunlight nurtures microscopic plants on the surface of the water and in the bed of the waves, and when they complete their developmental stage, like a ripe fruit, they sink into the depths of the sea, providing a banquet of blessings for the living creatures of the deep waters.

On the other hand, there are many birds that feed on sea fish; there are even types of bats that, in the darkness of night, dive into the depths of the water like a skilled diver and pull out their prey, which they have identified and targeted using special radar-like waves emitted from their noses. The sustenance of some types of birds is hidden between the teeth of certain giant sea animals! These animals, which after feeding on sea creatures need a "natural toothpick" for their teeth, come to the shore and keep their mouths open—which are not unlike small caves. That group of birds whose sustenance is assigned here enters this "cave" without any fear or anxiety, searching for their sustenance between the teeth of the giant animal. They both feast on this ready food and relieve the animal of these bothersome materials. When the program for both ends and the birds fly out, the animal, feeling relief, closes its mouth and dives back into the depths of the water!

The way Allah delivers sustenance to various creatures is truly astonishing—from the fetus in the mother's womb, where no one has access to it in that mysterious darkness, to the various insects living in the dark depths of the earth, in winding nests, among trees, atop mountains, and at the bottom of valleys. They are never hidden or concealed from His knowledge. As the Quran says, Allah knows both their "permanent address" and their mobile locations, and wherever they may be, He assigns their sustenance to them.

Interestingly, in the above verses, when discussing those who consume sustenance, the term "dabbah" (moving creature) is used. This is a subtle allusion to the relationship between "energy" and "movement." We know that wherever there is movement, there is a need for energy-producing matter—that is, matter that becomes the source of movement. The Quran also says in the verses under discussion that Allah grants sustenance to all moving creatures! If we interpret movement in its broad sense, it also includes plants, for they too have a very precise and delicate movement in the path of "growth." For this reason, in our philosophy, "growth" is considered one of the types of movement.

3- Is the "sustenance" of every person determined from the beginning to the end of their life, and will it reach them regardless? Or must one go after it, as the poet says, "It is the requirement of wisdom to seek from the doors"? Some lethargic and inactive individuals, relying on expressions like the above verse or narrations that describe sustenance as predestined and fixed, think there is no need for a person to strive much for their livelihood, because sustenance is predestined and will reach the person regardless, and no open mouth remains without food! Such ignorant individuals, whose understanding of religion and faith is extremely weak, provide an excuse for enemies to claim that religion is a factor for sedation, economic stagnation, extinguishing the positive activities of life, and submitting to all kinds of deprivation—under the pretext that "if such-and-forth blessing did not reach me, it certainly wasn't my sustenance; if it were my sustenance, it would have reached me without question." This gives a good opportunity to exploiters to milk the deprived masses even more and deprive them of the most basic means of life.

While a brief acquaintance with the Quran and Islamic hadiths is sufficient to realize the truth that Islam considers the basis of every kind of material and spiritual benefit for man to be effort, striving, and struggle—to the extent that in the slogan-like Quranic sentence لَيْسَ لِلْإِنْسَانِ إِلَّا مَا سَعَىٰ, it places man's benefit exclusively in his effort and work. The Islamic leaders, to set an example for others, worked on many occasions—hard and exhausting work. The previous prophets were no exception to this law, from shepherding to tailoring, armor-weaving, and farming. If the concept of Allah guaranteeing sustenance meant sitting at home and waiting for sustenance to arrive, the prophets and Imams, who are the most familiar with religious concepts, should not have strived so much for sustenance.

Therefore, we also say that the sustenance of every person is predestined and fixed, but at the same time, it is conditional upon effort and striving. Whenever this condition is not met, the conditioned result will also vanish. This is exactly like saying "everyone has an appointed term (ajal) and a fixed amount of life." Certainly, the meaning of this statement is not that if a person even commits suicide or consumes harmful substances, they will remain alive until that appointed term. Its meaning is that this body has the potential to survive for a considerable period, but on the condition that it observes health principles, avoids dangerous situations, and keeps away what causes premature death.

The important point is that the verses and narrations regarding the fixed nature of sustenance are actually a brake on the thoughts of greedy and worldly people who knock on every door to secure their lives and commit every oppression and crime, imagining that if they do not do so, their lives will not be secured. The verses of the Quran and Islamic hadiths warn such individuals not to struggle pointlessly and not to strive for sustenance through irrational and illegitimate ways. As long as they step onto the legitimate path and put in effort and striving, they should be certain that Allah will provide all their needs through this way.

The God who did not forget them in the darkness of the womb. The God who, during infancy when they were unable to feed on the food of this world, assigned their sustenance before birth to the breast of a kind mother. The God who, at the end of the nursing period when they were weak, placed their sustenance in the hands of a loving father who would toil from morning to night and be happy that "I am working to provide food for my children." Yes, how is it possible for this God to forget a person when they grow up and gain the ability and power for every kind of work and activity? Does intellect and faith allow a person in such a state, thinking that their sustenance might not be provided, to step into the valley of sin, oppression, and violation of the rights of others, and greedily seize the rights of the oppressed?

Of course, it cannot be denied that there are some types of sustenance that come to a person whether they go after them or not. Can it be denied that sunlight shines into our homes without our effort, or that rain and air rush to us without struggle and activity? Can it be denied that the intellect, intelligence, and talent stored in our being from the first day were not due to our effort!? But these so-called "windfall" bounties—or more correctly, bounties that reached us by Allah's grace without effort—if we do not maintain them correctly through our own effort and striving, they too will be lost to us or remain ineffective.

The famous hadith narrated from Ali عليه السلام where he said: "And know, my son! Indeed, sustenance is of two kinds: a sustenance that you seek, and a sustenance that seeks you" [^1] also refers to this same truth. It also cannot be denied that in some cases, a person does not go after something, but due to a series of coincidences, a blessing is granted to them. Although these events seem like coincidences to us, in reality and from the perspective of the system of creation, there is a calculation in them. Without doubt, the account of this kind of sustenance is separate from the sustenance obtained in the light of effort and striving, and the above hadith may refer to these as well.

But in any case, the fundamental point is that all Islamic teachings tell us that to secure a better life, both material and spiritual, one must strive more, and fleeing from work under the assumption that sustenance is pre-apportioned is wrong.

4- In the verses under discussion, only the issue of "rizq and sustenance" is mentioned, while a few verses later, where it speaks of repentant and faithful servants, it mentions "mata'un hasan" (goodly enjoyment/provision). The contrast between these two teaches us that for all moving creatures—humans, insects, predators, the good, and the wicked—there is a share of sustenance. However, "mata'un hasan" and "worthy and valuable bounties" are exclusively for faithful humans who have washed themselves with the water of repentance from every sin and pollution, and who utilize Allah's blessings in the path of obeying His command, not in the path of rebellious whims and desires!

[^1]: Nahj al-Balagha, the Imam's (عليه السلام) testament to his son Imam Hasan (عليه السلام).

Ayah

وَهُوَ ٱلَّذِى خَلَقَ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضَ فِى سِتَّةِ أَيَّامٍ وَكَانَ عَرْشُهُۥ عَلَى ٱلْمَآءِ لِيَبْلُوَكُمْ أَيُّكُمْ أَحْسَنُ عَمَلًا ۗ وَلَئِن قُلْتَ إِنَّكُم مَّبْعُوثُونَ مِنۢ بَعْدِ ٱلْمَوْتِ لَيَقُولَنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوٓا۟ إِنْ هَـٰذَآ إِلَّا سِحْرٌ مُّبِينٌ (٧)
It is He who created the heavens and the earth in six days—and His Throne was [then] upon the waters—that He may test you [to see] which of you is best in conduct. Yet if you say, ‘You will indeed be raised up after death,’ the faithless will surely say, ‘This is nothing but plain magic.’ (7)

Commentary

The Purpose of Creation

In this verse, three fundamental points are discussed: First, the creation of the universe, and specifically the beginning of creation, which is a sign of the Lord's power and evidence of His greatness: "He is the One who created the heavens and the earth in six days" (وَ هُوَ الَّذِي خَلَقَ السَّماواتِ وَ الْأَرْضَ فِي سِتَّةِ أَيَّامٍ).

It requires no explanation that the meaning of "day" here is not an ordinary twenty-four-hour day, because at the time when the heavens and the earth did not exist, there was neither the globe of the earth nor its twenty-four-hour rotation around itself. Rather, it means—as we have stated previously—an "aeon" or "era," whether this era was short or very long, even lasting billions of years. Since a sufficient and comprehensive explanation was provided regarding this matter under verse 54 of Surah Al-A'raf, there is no need for repetition (refer to Tafsir Nemouneh, Vol. 6, p. 200 onwards).

We also reminded there that the creation of the world in six consecutive eras—despite God having the power to create the entire universe in a single moment—is because this gradual creation, which at every moment takes on a new face, a new color, and a novel form, better and more extensively introduces the power and greatness of Allah.

He willed to manifest His power in thousands of faces, not just one; to show His wisdom in thousands of garments, not just one, so that the recognition of His Pure Essence, as well as the recognition of His power and wisdom, might be easier and clearer, and so that evidence numbering the days, years, centuries, and ages that have passed over the world would be at our disposal for His recognition.

Then it adds: and "His Throne was upon the water" (وَ كانَ عَرْشُهُ عَلَى الْماءِ).

To understand the commentary of this sentence, we must become acquainted with the concept of two words: "Arsh" (Throne) and "Ma’" (Water).

"Arsh" originally means "ceiling" or a "roofed structure," and high thrones like those of past monarchs are also called Arsh, as are the trellises upon which grapevines and the like are placed.

However, this word was later used to mean "power" as well, just as the word "throne" (takht) is used in Persian with the same meaning. In Arabic, it is said: so-and-so sat upon his Arsh or his Arsh was toppled, implying that he came to power or his power was destroyed. In Persian, we also say someone was "seated on the throne" or "pulled down from the throne." [^1]

[^1]: Of course, sometimes "Arsh" is used in contrast to "Kursi" and has another meaning, which we mentioned in the commentary of Ayat al-Kursi in Surah Al-Baqarah, Vol. 2, p. 200 onwards.

It should also be noted that sometimes the word "Arsh" refers to the entire collection of the universe, because the Throne of Allah's power encompasses this whole world. As for the word "Ma’," its ordinary meaning is "water," but sometimes any liquid substance is called "Ma’," such as liquid metals and the like.

Based on what we have said in the commentary of these two words, it is inferred that at the beginning of creation, the universe was in the form of molten materials (or extremely compressed gases that had the form of molten and liquid materials).

Then, intense movements and massive explosions occurred in this water-like mass, and parts of its surface were repeatedly hurled outward. This connection and cohesion turned into separation and detachment, and stars, planets, and solar systems were formed one after another.

Therefore, the universe and the foundation of the Throne of Allah's power were first situated upon this massive water-like matter.

This is the same thing referred to in verse 30 of Surah Al-Anbiya: "Have those who disbelieve not seen that the heavens and the earth were a joined entity, and We clove them asunder and made from water every living thing?" (أَ وَ لَمْ يَرَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا أَنَّ السَّماواتِ وَ الْأَرْضَ كانَتا رَتْقاً فَفَتَقْناهُما وَ جَعَلْنا مِنَ الْماءِ كُلَّ شَيْ‌ءٍ حَيٍ).

In the first sermon of Nahj al-Balagha, there are also clear indications of this same meaning.

The second point mentioned in the above verse is the purpose of the creation of the universe—the same purpose whose major part returns to the "pinnacle of this world," namely "Man." Man, who must be placed on the path of education and training, traverse the path of perfection, and draw closer to Allah at every moment. It says: "He created this magnificent creation for this reason: to test you as to which of you is best in conduct" (لِيَبْلُوَكُمْ أَيُّكُمْ أَحْسَنُ عَمَلًا).

"Liyabluwakum" is from the root "bala’" and "ibtila’," meaning "test." As we have previously indicated, divine tests are not for discovering a state or finding out the internal, spiritual, or intellectual condition of individuals; rather, they mean nurturing and training (the explanation of this subject was stated under verse 155 of Surah Al-Baqarah, Vol. 1, p. 384 onwards). It is interesting that this verse links the value of every human being to the "goodness" (husn) of their action, not to the "abundance" or "quantity" of their action. This shows that Islam everywhere emphasizes the "quality" of the action, not its quantity or volume.

In a hadith regarding this matter, it is narrated from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام: "It does not mean the one who does the most actions, but the one who is most correct in action. And correctness is the fear of Allah and a sincere intention." Then he said: "Maintaining the action until it is sincere is harder than the action itself; and the sincere action is that for which you do not want anyone to praise you except Allah, the Almighty and Sublime." [^1]

The third point mentioned in this verse is the issue of "Resurrection" (Ma'ad), which has an inseparable link with the issue of the creation of the world and the statement of the purpose of creation. For the creation of the world aims for the perfection of human beings, and the perfection of human beings is for the sake of preparing for life in a wider and more perfect world. Therefore, it says: "If you say to them, 'You shall indeed be raised after death,' the disbelievers will say out of wonder: 'This is unbelievable, this has no truth or reality; rather, this is nothing but obvious magic'" (وَ لَئِنْ قُلْتَ إِنَّكُمْ مَبْعُوثُونَ مِنْ بَعْدِ الْمَوْتِ لَيَقُولَنَّ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا إِنْ هذا إِلَّا سِحْرٌ مُبِينٌ).

The word "this" (hadha) refers to the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله statement regarding the Resurrection; meaning, this claim you have about the Resurrection is obvious magic. Therefore, the word "magic" (sihr) here means speech devoid of truth, baseless talk, or in simple terms, a trick or a scam. This is because magicians often show things to observers through their work that have no reality or truth; thus, the word "magic" can be used to mean anything devoid of truth.

However, the suggestion by some that "this" refers to the Holy Quran—because the Quran had a magical influence and attraction on listeners—does not seem correct, because the verse is speaking about the Resurrection, not the Quran, even though the extraordinary attraction of the Quran is undeniable.

[^1]: Tafsir al-Burhan, Vol. 2, p. 207.

Ayahs

وَلَئِنْ أَخَّرْنَا عَنْهُمُ ٱلْعَذَابَ إِلَىٰٓ أُمَّةٍ مَّعْدُودَةٍ لَّيَقُولُنَّ مَا يَحْبِسُهُۥٓ ۗ أَلَا يَوْمَ يَأْتِيهِمْ لَيْسَ مَصْرُوفًا عَنْهُمْ وَحَاقَ بِهِم مَّا كَانُوا۟ بِهِۦ يَسْتَهْزِءُونَ (٨)
And if We defer their punishment until a certain time, they will surely say, ‘What holds it back?’ Look! On the day it overtakes them it shall not be turned away from them, and they will be besieged by what they used to deride. (8)
وَلَئِنْ أَذَقْنَا ٱلْإِنسَـٰنَ مِنَّا رَحْمَةً ثُمَّ نَزَعْنَـٰهَا مِنْهُ إِنَّهُۥ لَيَـُٔوسٌ كَفُورٌ (٩)
If We let man taste a mercy from Us, and then withdraw it from him, he becomes despondent, ungrateful. (9)
وَلَئِنْ أَذَقْنَـٰهُ نَعْمَآءَ بَعْدَ ضَرَّآءَ مَسَّتْهُ لَيَقُولَنَّ ذَهَبَ ٱلسَّيِّـَٔاتُ عَنِّىٓ ۚ إِنَّهُۥ لَفَرِحٌ فَخُورٌ (١٠)
And if We let him have a taste of Our blessings after adversities have befallen him, he will surely say, ‘All ills have left me.’ Indeed, he becomes a vain braggart, (10)
إِلَّا ٱلَّذِينَ صَبَرُوا۟ وَعَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ لَهُم مَّغْفِرَةٌ وَأَجْرٌ كَبِيرٌ (١١)
excepting those who are patient and do righteous deeds. For such there will be forgiveness and a great reward. (11)

Commentary

Believers Possess Great Capacity, While the Faithless Lack It

In these verses—following the discussion regarding the faithless—certain aspects of the psychological states and moral weaknesses of such individuals are described; the very weaknesses that lead a person toward dark paths and corruption.

The first characteristic mentioned for them is joking with truths and mocking destiny-shaping matters. Due to ignorance and pride, when they hear the threats of the heavenly men regarding the punishment and retribution of the wicked, and "a few days pass while Allah, by His grace, delays the punishment, they say with a particular shamelessness: 'What is withholding this Divine punishment?'" What happened to this punishment? Where did this retribution go? (وَلَئِنْ أَخَّرْنَا عَنْهُمُ الْعَذَابَ إِلَىٰ أُمَّةٍ مَعْدُودَةٍ لَيَقُولُنَّ مَا يَحْبِسُهُ).

"Ummah" (أُمَّة) is derived from the root "umm" (meaning mother) and originally signifies the joining of things together. For this reason, any group gathered for a single goal, or in a single time or place, is called an ummah. However, this word is also used to mean "time" or "period," either because the components of time are interconnected or because every community lives within a specific era. In Surah Yusuf, verse 45, we read: وَادَّكَرَ بَعْدَ أُمَّةٍ ("The released prisoner remembered [Yusuf] after a period of time"). In the verse under discussion, "ummah" carries this same meaning, which is why it is described by the word ma'dudah (numbered/limited). That is, if We delay the punishment for a short, limited time, they say: "What has prevented it?"

In any case, this is the way of all proud and ignorant people: whatever does not align with their desires appears ridiculous to them. Thus, they take the soul-shaking and awakening threats of the men of truth as a joke and constantly play with fire.

However, the Quran explicitly answers them: "Behold! The day it comes to them, it will not be averted from them" (أَلَا يَوْمَ يَأْتِيهِمْ لَيْسَ مَصْرُوفًا عَنْهُمْ).

"And that which they used to mock will envelop them" and crush them (وَحَاقَ بِهِمْ مَا كَانُوا بِهِ يَسْتَهْزِئُونَ).

It is true that at that moment, their wails and cries will rise to the heavens, and they will regret their shameful words, but neither will that wailing reach anywhere, nor will this regret be of any benefit.

Another of their weaknesses is their lack of capacity when facing difficulties and hardships or the withdrawal of Divine blessings. As the next verse states: "And if We give man a taste of mercy from Us and then We withdraw it from him, indeed, he is despairing and ungrateful" (وَلَئِنْ أَذَقْنَا الْإِنْسَانَ مِنَّا رَحْمَةً ثُمَّ نَزَعْنَاهَا مِنْهُ إِنَّهُ لَيَئُوسٌ كَفُورٌ).

Although this verse speaks of "man" in general, as previously noted, the word "man" (al-insan) in such verses refers to unrefined, impulsive, and worthless individuals. Therefore, it aligns with the discussion in the previous verse regarding the faithless.

Their third weakness is that when they are immersed in luxury and blessings, such self-loss, pride, and arrogance overcome them that they forget everything. As the Quran says: "And if We give him a taste of favor after hardship has touched him, he is so proud of himself that he says, 'All my hardships and ills have gone away,' and for this reason, boundless joy, boasting, and misplaced pride envelop him from head to toe, such that he becomes heedless of thanking the blessings of his Lord" (وَلَئِنْ أَذَقْنَاهُ نَعْمَاءَ بَعْدَ ضَرَّاءَ مَسَّتْهُ لَيَقُولَنَّ ذَهَبَ السَّيِّئَاتُ عَنِّي ۚ إِنَّهُ لَفَرِحٌ فَخُورٌ).

There is another possibility in interpreting the phrase "ذَهَبَ السَّيِّئَاتُ عَنِّي" (the ills have gone from me). It is that when such people are caught in hardships and then, by the grace of the Lord, those hardships are replaced by blessings, they say: "The past hardships were the expiation for our sins, and all our transgressions have been washed away by them; we have become pure and clean, and among those near to Allah! Therefore, there is no need for repentance or returning to His threshold!"

Then it adds: "Only those who are patient in the face of hardships and the difficult events of life, and who do not neglect righteous deeds in any state, are free from narrow-mindedness, ingratitude, pride, and arrogance" (إِلَّا الَّذِينَ صَبَرُوا وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحَاتِ).

They are neither made arrogant by an abundance of blessings to forget Allah, nor do they despair and become ungrateful during times of hardship and calamity. Rather, their great souls and high intellects "digest" both blessing and trial, and in every state, they remain mindful of Allah and their duties.

For this reason, "for those, there will be forgiveness and a great reward" (أُولَٰئِكَ لَهُمْ مَغْفِرَةٌ وَأَجْرٌ كَبِيرٌ).

Key Points

The "Numbered Community" and the Companions of al-Mahdi (عليه السلام)

  1. In several narrations transmitted to us through the Ahl al-Bayt (عليه السلام), "ummah ma'dudah" (numbered community) has been interpreted as a small number of people, referring to the companions of al-Mahdi (عليه السلام). According to this, the meaning of the first verse would be: "If We defer the punishment of these oppressors and evildoers until the uprising of al-Mahdi and his companions, they will say: 'What has withheld the punishment of Allah?'"

However, as we mentioned, the apparent meaning of the verse is that "ummah ma'dudah" refers to a numbered and specified time. Coincidentally, in a narration from Amir al-Mu'minin Ali (عليه السلام) regarding the interpretation of this verse, "ummah ma'dudah" is explained in this very way. Therefore, the aforementioned narrations might refer to the secondary meaning of the verse—the so-called "inner meaning" (batn)—which in that case would be the statement of a general law regarding oppressors, not just an issue related to the polytheists and criminals of the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله) time. We know that the verses of the Quran have various meanings; the primary and apparent meaning may concern a specific issue or group, while the secondary meaning is a general one, independent of a specific time or group.

Four Phenomena of Short-Sightedness

  1. In the above verses, three different psychological states of the polytheists and sinners are depicted, within which four descriptions are given for them:

First, they are ya'us (extremely despairing) when blessings are cut off, and second, they are kafur (extremely ungrateful).

Conversely, when they are drowned in blessings, or even when a slight blessing reaches them, they "lose themselves" out of joy; that is, they become so excited and immersed in pleasure and delight that they become heedless of everything. This intoxication from the wine of pleasure and pride leads them toward corruption and transgressing Divine limits.

Furthermore, they are fakhur (boastful); meaning that at the time of blessing, they become very arrogant, self-important, and boastful.

In any case, these four attributes are all phenomena resulting from short-sightedness and a lack of capacity. These are not exclusive to a specific group of faithless and tainted individuals but are a series of general characteristics for all of them.

However, people of faith—who possess a great soul, high intellect, an open heart, and ample capacity—are neither shaken by the fluctuations of time, nor does the withdrawal of blessings lead them to ingratitude and despair, nor does the arrival of blessings drown them in pride and heedlessness.

Thus, in the final verse, which mentions this as an exception, instead of the word "faith" (iman), it uses "patience" (sabr) and steadfastness (take note).

The Measure of Low Capacity

  1. Another point to consider is that in both cases (both the withdrawal of blessing after it was given, and the giving of blessing after it was withdrawn), the expression "adhaqna" (from the root idhaqah, meaning "to give a taste") is used. This indicates that they lack capacity to such an extent that even if a slight blessing is given to them and then taken away, their cries and ingratitude rise; and if after hardships a slight blessing reaches them, they become so excited they lose all composure!

Blessings are All Bounties

  1. Interestingly, in the first verse, the blessing is described with the word "mercy" (rahmah), and in the second verse with the word "favor" (na'ma'). This may indicate that Divine blessings all reach man through Allah's favor and mercy, not through merit. If blessings were based on merit, only a few would be entitled to them, or perhaps no one at all.

Good Deeds Have Two Effects

  1. In the final verse, those with faith and steadfastness who perform righteous deeds are promised both "forgiveness" (maghfirah) for sins and a "great reward" (ajrun kabir). This points to the fact that good deeds have two effects: one is the washing away of sins, and the other is the attracting of great rewards.

Ayahs

فَلَعَلَّكَ تَارِكٌۢ بَعْضَ مَا يُوحَىٰٓ إِلَيْكَ وَضَآئِقٌۢ بِهِۦ صَدْرُكَ أَن يَقُولُوا۟ لَوْلَآ أُنزِلَ عَلَيْهِ كَنزٌ أَوْ جَآءَ مَعَهُۥ مَلَكٌ ۚ إِنَّمَآ أَنتَ نَذِيرٌ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ وَكِيلٌ (١٢)
[Look out] lest you should disregard aught of what has been revealed to you, and be upset because they say, ‘Why has not a treasure been sent down to him, or [why does] not an angel accompany him?’ You are only a warner, and Allah watches over all things. (12)
أَمْ يَقُولُونَ ٱفْتَرَىٰهُ ۖ قُلْ فَأْتُوا۟ بِعَشْرِ سُوَرٍ مِّثْلِهِۦ مُفْتَرَيَـٰتٍ وَٱدْعُوا۟ مَنِ ٱسْتَطَعْتُم مِّن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ إِن كُنتُمْ صَـٰدِقِينَ (١٣)
Do they say, ‘He has fabricated it?’ Say, ‘Then bring ten surahs like it, fabricated, and invoke whomever you can, besides Allah, should you be truthful.’ (13)
فَإِلَّمْ يَسْتَجِيبُوا۟ لَكُمْ فَٱعْلَمُوٓا۟ أَنَّمَآ أُنزِلَ بِعِلْمِ ٱللَّهِ وَأَن لَّآ إِلَـٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ ۖ فَهَلْ أَنتُم مُّسْلِمُونَ (١٤)
But if they do not respond to you, know that it has been sent down by Allah’s knowledge, and that there is no god except Him. Will you, then, submit [to Allah]? (14)

Circumstances of Revelation

Two accounts for the circumstances of revelation (Sha'n al-Nuzul) have been narrated for the above verses, both of which may be correct:

First, a group of the leaders of the Meccan disbelievers came to the Prophet صلي الله عليه وآله and said: "If you are truly a Prophet of God, turn the mountains of Mecca into gold for us! Or bring forth angels who will testify to your prophethood!" The above verses were revealed and provided them with an answer.

Another account is narrated from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, stating that the Prophet صلي الله عليه وآله said to Ali عليه السلام: "I have asked Allah to establish brotherhood between me and you, and this request was granted; and I also asked Him to make you my successor (Wasi), and this request was also answered." When this reached the ears of some opponents, they said (out of enmity and malice): "By God, a single measure of dates in a dried-out skin is better than what Muhammad صلي الله عليه وآله has asked of his Lord! (If he speaks the truth) why did he not ask Allah to send an angel to help him against his enemies, or a treasure to save him from poverty?" Consequently, the above verses were revealed and answered them.

Commentary

The Quran, the Eternal Miracle

It appears from these verses that sometimes, due to the intensity of the opponents' defiance and stubbornness, the Prophet صلي الله عليه وآله would delay the proclamation of certain verses until the last possible opportunity. Therefore, in the first verse under discussion, Allah forbids His Prophet from this act with this expression: "Perhaps you would abandon some of what is revealed to you, and your breast is straitened and distressed by it" (فَلَعَلَّكَ تارِكٌ بَعْضَ ما يُوحى‌ إِلَيْكَ وَ ضائِقٌ بِهِ صَدْرُكَ‌).

You are distressed lest they demand arbitrary miracles from you and "say: 'Why has a treasure not been sent down to him? Or why has an angel not come with him?'" (أَنْ يَقُولُوا لَوْ لا أُنْزِلَ عَلَيْهِ كَنْزٌ أَوْ جاءَ مَعَهُ مَلَكٌ‌).

Of course, as is evident from other verses of the Quran, such as Surah Al-Isra, verses 90 to 93, they did not make these demands to seek the truth or to verify the sincerity of his call; rather, their goal was excuse-making, stubbornness, and malice.

Thus, He immediately adds: "You are only a warner" (إِنَّما أَنْتَ نَذِيرٌ). Meaning, whether they accept or not, or whether they mock and show stubbornness, your duty remains the same.

And at the end of the verse, He says: "And Allah is the Guardian and Disposer of all things" (وَ اللَّهُ عَلى‌ كُلِّ شَيْ‌ءٍ وَكِيلٌ‌). This means: do not be concerned about their faith or disbelief; this is not your responsibility. Your duty is to convey the message. Allah Himself knows how to deal with them, and He is the One who keeps account of their deeds.

Since these excuses and criticisms were because they fundamentally denied divine revelation and claimed these verses were not from Allah, but rather sentences that "Muhammad" had falsely attributed to God, the next verse addresses this claim with the utmost clarity, saying: "Or do they say: 'He has fabricated it'?" (أَمْ يَقُولُونَ افْتَراهُ‌).

"Say to them: 'Then bring ten surahs like it, fabricated, and call upon whomever you can—besides Allah—if you are truthful'" (قُلْ فَأْتُوا بِعَشْرِ سُوَرٍ مِثْلِهِ مُفْتَرَياتٍ وَ ادْعُوا مَنِ اسْتَطَعْتُمْ مِنْ دُونِ اللَّهِ إِنْ كُنْتُمْ صادِقِينَ‌).

"But if they do not respond to you [Muslims]" and are not prepared to bring at least ten surahs like these, "then know that this [weakness and inability] is a sign that these verses have been sent down with the knowledge of Allah"; otherwise, if they were the product of human thought, they too are humans (فَإِلَّمْ يَسْتَجِيبُوا لَكُمْ فَاعْلَمُوا أَنَّما أُنْزِلَ بِعِلْمِ اللَّهِ‌).

And also know that "there is no deity except Him" and the revelation of these miracle-bearing verses is proof of this reality (وَ أَنْ لا إِلهَ إِلَّا هُوَ).

"Will you then, [O opponents], submit to the command of Allah?" (فَهَلْ أَنْتُمْ مُسْلِمُونَ‌).

Now that we have challenged you and your inability to meet this challenge has been proven, does any doubt remain that these verses are from Allah? Despite this clear miracle, will you still pursue the path of denial, or will you submit?

Here, several important points should be noted

1- The Meaning of "La'alla" (Perhaps)

As we know, "la'alla" is usually mentioned to express hope for something to happen, but here it carries the meaning of "prohibition." It is exactly like a father who wants to forbid his child from doing something and says: "Perhaps you are befriending so-and-so, which is why you are not serious in your work," meaning: do not befriend him, for his friendship makes you lax.

Therefore, although "la'alla" in such cases is used in its sense of "perhaps," its implied meaning is a prohibition against an action. In the verses under discussion, Allah emphasizes to His Prophet that he should not delay the proclamation of divine verses out of fear of the opponents' denial or their demand for "arbitrary miracles."

2- How Could the Prophet Delay the Proclamation?

A question arises here: How is it possible for the Prophet صلي الله عليه وآله to delay the proclamation of divine verses, or fundamentally refrain from proclaiming them, while the Prophet صلي الله عليه وآله is infallible (Ma'sum) and commits no sin or error?

The answer is that whenever the Prophet صلي الله عليه وآله was commanded to immediately proclaim a ruling, he would certainly do so without hesitation. However, sometimes the time for proclamation was flexible, and the Prophet, based on certain considerations—not personal ones, but those related to the public interest and the defense of the faith—would postpone the proclamation. This is certainly not a sin. We read a similar instance in Surah Al-Ma'idah, verse 67, where Allah emphasizes to the Prophet صلي الله عليه وآله to proclaim the divine verses and not to fear the people's threats, for Allah will protect him (يا أَيُّهَا الرَّسُولُ بَلِّغْ ما أُنْزِلَ إِلَيْكَ مِنْ رَبِّكَ وَ إِنْ لَمْ تَفْعَلْ فَما بَلَّغْتَ رِسالَتَهُ وَ اللَّهُ يَعْصِمُكَ مِنَ النَّاسِ‌).

In fact, although delaying the proclamation was not forbidden here, acting quickly with a display of decisiveness was considered "better" (Awla). In this way, Allah intends to psychologically strengthen His Prophet and increase his decisiveness against the opponents, so that he feels no fear of their clamor, baseless demands, and ridiculous excuses.

3- The Word "Am" in (أَمْ يَقُولُونَ افْتَراهُ‌)

Regarding the meaning of "am" at the beginning of the second verse (أَمْ يَقُولُونَ افْتَراهُ‌ ...), commentators have offered two possibilities: one is that it means "or" (aw), and the other is that it means "rather" (bal).

In the first case, the meaning of the verse would be: "Perhaps you did not recite Our verses to them for fear of the opponents' excuses, or you did recite the verses but they labeled them as fabrication and lies against Allah."

In the second case, the meaning would be: "Do not delay the proclamation of divine verses because of their excuses; rather, they fundamentally deny revelation and prophethood, and they say the Prophet has lied against Allah."

In truth, with this statement, Allah informs His Prophet that their requests for arbitrary miracles are not out of a search for truth, but because they fundamentally deny prophethood, and all these are merely excuses. In any case, a careful examination of the concept of the above verses, especially the literary nuances of the words, shows that the second meaning is closer to the intended sense (reflect on this).

4- Arbitrary Miracles

There is no doubt that the Prophet صلي الله عليه وآله must present miracles to truth-seekers as proof of his authenticity, and no Prophet can rely solely on a claim. However, without a doubt, the opponents mentioned in the above verses were not searching for the truth; the miracles they demanded were "arbitrary miracles" (meaning that each person suggests a miracle according to their own whims and desires and is not satisfied with any other miracle). Certainly, such individuals are excuse-seekers, not truth-seekers.

Is it absolutely necessary for a Prophet to possess vast treasures, as the Meccan polytheists suggested? Or must an angel necessarily accompany him to propagate the mission? Furthermore, was the Quran itself not superior to any other miracle? If they were not truly seeking excuses, why did they not listen to this very statement of the Quran which says: "If you believe the Prophet has said these verses of his own accord, go and bring the like of it and seek help from all the people of the world."

5- The Quran is a Miracle

The above verses once again emphasize the miraculous nature of the Quran, stating: this is not ordinary speech; it is not the product of a human brain, but rather a heavenly revelation originating from the infinite knowledge and power of Allah. For this reason, it issues a challenge (tahaddi) and calls the entire world to combat. Given that the contemporaries of the Prophet صلي الله عليه وآله and even the nations that have come since have been unable to produce such a thing—choosing to endure all sorts of hardships rather than successfully challenging the verses of the Quran—it becomes clear that such a task was and is fundamentally beyond human capability. Is a miracle anything other than this? This call of the Quran still rings in our ears, and this eternal miracle continues to call the world to itself and challenges all the scientific circles of the world—not only in terms of eloquence and rhetoric (the sweetness and attractiveness of the expressions and the clarity of the concepts) but also in terms of content: sciences that were hidden from humans at that time, laws and regulations that guarantee the happiness and salvation of humanity, statements free from any contradiction or inconsistency, and histories free from any superstitions or exaggerations (we discussed the miraculous nature of the Quran in detail in the commentary on verses 23 and 24 of Surah Al-Baqarah, Volume 1 of Tafsir Nemouneh, page 83 onwards).

6- The Whole Quran, Ten Surahs, or One Surah?

As we know, the Holy Quran in one place challenges the opponents to bring the like of the [entire] Quran (Surah Al-Isra, 88), in another place to bring ten surahs like the Quran (as in the verses under discussion), and in another place to bring one surah like the surahs of the Quran (Al-Baqarah, 23).

For this reason, a discussion has arisen among a group of commentators: what is the reason for this difference in the challenge? Why the whole Quran in one place, ten surahs in another, and one surah in yet another?

They have pursued various ways to answer this question:

A) Some believe this difference is a form of "descending" from a higher stage to a lower stage. It is exactly like someone saying to another: "If you are as skilled as I am in the art of writing and poetry, write a book and a collection of poems like mine." Then he descends and says: "Present one chapter like it, or even one page like it!" However, this answer is only correct if the Surahs Al-Isra, Hud, Yunus, and Al-Baqarah were revealed in that specific order. This is consistent with the order mentioned in the book Tarikh al-Quran quoted from Fihrist Ibn al-Nadim, which states that Surah Al-Isra is the 48th, Hud the 49th, Yunus the 51st, and Al-Baqarah the 90th surah revealed to the Prophet. Unfortunately, however, this does not align with the well-known order of the revelation of these surahs found in Islamic commentaries.

B) Some have said that although the order of revelation of the above surahs does not align with a descent from a higher to a lower stage, we know that all the verses of a surah were not revealed at once. Often, verses revealed later were placed in an earlier surah by the Prophet's command due to thematic relevance. In our context, this might be the case, and thus the chronology of the surahs does not contradict the idea of descending from a higher stage to a lower one.

C) Another possibility to solve this problem is that fundamentally, the word "Quran" is a term applied to both the "whole" and "part" of the Quran. For example, in the first verse of Surah Al-Jinn, we read: "Indeed, we have heard an amazing Quran" (إِنَّا سَمِعْنا قُرْآناً عَجَباً). It is clear that they had heard only a portion of the Quran. Fundamentally, "Quran" is derived from the root of "recitation" (qira'ah), and we know that recitation and reading apply to both the whole Quran and a part of it. Therefore, the challenge to bring the like of the "Quran" does not necessarily mean the entire Quran and is consistent with ten surahs or even one surah.

On the other hand, "Surah" also originally means a "collection" or "boundary," and it applies to a collection of verses even if it is not a complete surah in the conventional sense. In other words, "surah" is used in two senses: one meaning a collection of verses pursuing a specific goal, and the other a complete surah that begins with Bismillah and ends before the Bismillah of the next surah.

Evidence for this is verse 86 of Surah At-Tawbah, where it says: "And when a surah is revealed [saying], 'Believe in Allah and strive with His Messenger'..." (وَ إِذا أُنْزِلَتْ سُورَةٌ أَنْ آمِنُوا بِاللَّهِ وَ جاهِدُوا مَعَ رَسُولِهِ‌ ...). It is clear that "surah" here refers to those specific verses pursuing the aforementioned goal (faith in Allah and Jihad), even if they are part of a complete surah. Raghib also, in his book Al-Mufradat, in the commentary on the first verse of Surah An-Nur (سُورَةٌ أَنْزَلْناها ...), says: "Meaning a collection of rulings and wisdom..." As we observe, he interprets "surah" as a collection of rulings.

Therefore, not much difference remains between "Quran," "Surah," and "Ten Surahs" in terms of linguistic concept; they all refer to a collection of Quranic verses. The result is that the Quran's challenge is not regarding a single word or a single sentence—such that someone could claim "I can produce something like the verse 'By the morning brightness' (وَ الضُّحى‌) or the verse 'Dark green [in color]' (مُدْهامَّتانِ‌)" or simple sentences from the Quran—rather, the challenge everywhere is to a collection of verses that pursue a significant goal (reflect on this).

7- The Addressee in "But if they do not respond to you" (فَإِلَّمْ يَسْتَجِيبُوا لَكُمْ‌)

There is a discussion among commentators as to who is addressed in the phrase "But if they do not respond to you." Some have said the addressees are the Muslims, meaning: if the deniers do not respond to your call and do not bring ten surahs like the Quran, then know that this Quran is from Allah, and this itself is proof of the Quran's miraculous nature.

Others have said the addressees are the deniers, meaning: O deniers! If other humans and everything besides Allah do not respond to your call for help in bringing the like of the Quran and remain unable, then know that this Quran is from Allah. Of course, in terms of the final result, there is not much difference between the two interpretations, but the first possibility seems closer to the context.

Ayahs

مَن كَانَ يُرِيدُ ٱلْحَيَوٰةَ ٱلدُّنْيَا وَزِينَتَهَا نُوَفِّ إِلَيْهِمْ أَعْمَـٰلَهُمْ فِيهَا وَهُمْ فِيهَا لَا يُبْخَسُونَ (١٥)
As for those who desire the life of this world and its glitter, We will recompense them fully for their works therein, and they shall not be underpaid in it. (15)
أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ ٱلَّذِينَ لَيْسَ لَهُمْ فِى ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ إِلَّا ٱلنَّارُ ۖ وَحَبِطَ مَا صَنَعُوا۟ فِيهَا وَبَـٰطِلٌ مَّا كَانُوا۟ يَعْمَلُونَ (١٦)
They are the ones for whom there shall be nothing in the Hereafter but Fire: what they had accomplished in the world has failed, and their works have come to naught. (16)

Commentary

The preceding verses concluded the argument against the polytheists and deniers by mentioning the "evidences of the Quran's miraculous nature." Since even after the truth becomes clear, a group still refuses to submit solely to protect their material interests, the verses under discussion point to the fate of such world-worshipping individuals, stating: "Whoever desires only the life of this world and its glitz and adornment, We shall pay them in full for their deeds therein, and they will not be undersold" (مَنْ كانَ يُرِيدُ الْحَياةَ الدُّنْيا وَ زِينَتَها نُوَفِّ إِلَيْهِمْ أَعْمالَهُمْ فِيها وَ هُمْ فِيها لا يُبْخَسُونَ‌).

The word "bakhs" (بخس) lexically means the reduction of a right, and the phrase "وَ هُمْ فِيها لا يُبْخَسُونَ‌" indicates that they will receive the results of their actions without the slightest deficiency.

This verse expresses an eternal Divine law: that positive and effective actions do not lose their results. The difference is that if the primary goal is reaching the material life of this world, its fruit will be nothing else; however, if the goal is Allah and seeking His pleasure, it will be effective in this world and will also yield prolific results for the Next World.

In reality, the first category of these actions is like a non-durable, short-lived building constructed only for temporary use, which is later demolished. But the second category is like firm buildings with solid foundations that remain for consecutive centuries and are usable.

We clearly find examples of this today around us: the Western world, through its persistent and organized efforts, has unlocked the secrets of many sciences and disciplines, gained mastery over various forces of nature, and through continuous endeavor, perseverance against difficulties, and unity and solidarity, has provided abundant blessings.

Therefore, it goes without saying that they will receive the results of their actions and efforts and will achieve brilliant victories. However, since their goal is only life [in this world], the natural effect of these actions will be nothing but the provision of their material facilities.

Even their humanitarian and public works—such as establishing hospitals, clinics, cultural centers, and aid to some poor nations and the like—if they are not a trap for colonialism and exploitation, because they ultimately have a material goal and the preservation of material interests, they will only have a material effect.

The same applies to those who perform ostentatious acts—acts that have humanitarian or devotional aspects [but are done for show].

Hence, in the next verse, it explicitly states: "Those are the ones for whom there is nothing in the Hereafter but the Fire!" (أُولئِكَ الَّذِينَ لَيْسَ لَهُمْ فِي الْآخِرَةِ إِلَّا النَّارُ).

"And all that they performed in this world will be erased and destroyed in the other world, and they shall receive no reward for it" (وَ حَبِطَ ما صَنَعُوا فِيها).

And all the deeds they performed for other than Allah will become null and void (وَ باطِلٌ ما كانُوا يَعْمَلُونَ‌).

The word "habt" (حبط) originally means when an animal eats too much unsuitable grass until its belly swells and its digestive system becomes diseased and ruined. Due to this condition, the animal outwardly appears fat and plump, but inwardly it is sick and diseased.

This is a beautiful and eloquent expression regarding deeds that outwardly appear useful and humanitarian but inwardly were performed with a contaminated and lowly intention.

Here, several points must be noted

[Do these two verses conflict?]

  1. At first, it might seem that the two above verses have a kind of conflict, because the first verse says: "Those whose only goal is the life of this world, We give them the result of all their deeds without reduction," but the second verse says: "Their deeds become frustrated (habt), ineffective, and void!" However, by noting that one of these two verses refers to the environment of the life of this world and the other to the abode of the Hereafter, the answer to this objection becomes clear. They receive the results of their deeds in the world completely, but what benefit is there when these deeds—even if they were among the most important works—are ineffective for their Hereafter? This is because their goal was not pure and their intention was not sincere; they had no goal other than reaching a series of material benefits, which they attained.

[Adornment and the life of this world]

  1. The mention of the word "adornment" (zinat) after the life of this world is to condemn world-worship and the glitz of the world, not the moderate and calculated utilization of the blessings of this world.

The word "adornment," which is mentioned here in a general sense, is interpreted in other verses as beautiful women, vast treasures and wealth, expensive and flashy mounts, agricultural lands, and abundant livestock (زُيِّنَ لِلنَّاسِ حُبُّ الشَّهَواتِ مِنَ النِّساءِ وَ الْبَنِينَ وَ الْقَناطِيرِ الْمُقَنْطَرَةِ مِنَ الذَّهَبِ وَ الْفِضَّةِ وَ الْخَيْلِ الْمُسَوَّمَةِ وَ الْأَنْعامِ وَ الْحَرْثِ‌ - Aal-Imran: 14). [1]

[The word "void" after "frustrated"]

  1. The mention of the word "void" (batil) after "frustrated" (habt) might be an indication that their deeds have an appearance without substance, and for this reason, their result vanishes. Then it adds that their deeds were essentially void and ineffective from the beginning; however, since the realities of many things are not recognized in this world, and in the other abode—which is the place for the discovery of secrets—the reality reveals itself, it becomes clear that such deeds were nothing from the start.

  2. In the commentary of the above verses in the book Al-Durr al-Manthur, a hadith is narrated that clearly elucidates the meaning of the verse:

The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله said: "When the Day of Resurrection comes, my Ummah will be divided into three groups: a group who worshipped Allah sincerely, a group who worshipped Allah out of ostentation (riya), and a group who worshipped Allah to gain the world through it.

He will say to the one who used to worship Allah for the world: 'By My Might and Majesty, what did you intend by My worship?'

He will say: 'The world.' He will say: 'No doubt, what you gathered does not benefit you, and you shall not return to it. Take him to the Fire!'

And He will say to the one who worshipped Allah out of ostentation: 'By My Might and Majesty, what did you intend by My worship?' He will say: 'Ostentation.' He will say: 'Indeed, your worship through which you were showing off—nothing of it ascended to Me, and it does not benefit you today. Take him to the Fire!'

And He will say to the one who worshipped Allah sincerely: 'By My Might and Majesty, what did you intend by My worship?'

He will say: 'By Your Might and Majesty, You know better than I; I used to worship You for Your Face and for Your Abode.' He will say: 'My servant has spoken the truth. Take him to Paradise.'" [2]

[1]: For further explanation, refer to Volume 2 of Tafsir Nemouneh, page 38 onwards, under verse 14 of Surah Aal-Imran. [2]: As narrated in Tafsir al-Mizan, Vol. 10, page 186.

Ayah

أَفَمَن كَانَ عَلَىٰ بَيِّنَةٍ مِّن رَّبِّهِۦ وَيَتْلُوهُ شَاهِدٌ مِّنْهُ وَمِن قَبْلِهِۦ كِتَـٰبُ مُوسَىٰٓ إِمَامًا وَرَحْمَةً ۚ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ يُؤْمِنُونَ بِهِۦ ۚ وَمَن يَكْفُرْ بِهِۦ مِنَ ٱلْأَحْزَابِ فَٱلنَّارُ مَوْعِدُهُۥ ۚ فَلَا تَكُ فِى مِرْيَةٍ مِّنْهُ ۚ إِنَّهُ ٱلْحَقُّ مِن رَّبِّكَ وَلَـٰكِنَّ أَكْثَرَ ٱلنَّاسِ لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ (١٧)
Is he who stands on a manifest proof from his Lord, and whom a witness of his own [family] follows? And before him there was the Book of Moses, a guide and mercy. It is they who have faith in it, and whoever defies him from among the factions, the Fire is their tryst. So do not be in doubt about it; it is the truth from your Lord, but most people do not have faith. (17)

Commentary

There is much discussion among commentators regarding the interpretation of the above verse. Various opinions have been offered concerning the details of its words, pronouns, relative clauses, and demonstrative pronouns, the citation of all of which would be contrary to our method in this Tafsir. However, we present here two interpretations that appear the clearest, in order of importance:

  1. At the beginning of the verse, it says: "Is one who rests upon a clear proof from his Lord, followed by a witness from Him, and before it was the Book of Moses (the Torah) as a guide and a mercy [to manifest his greatness], like one who possesses none of these attributes, signs, and clear proofs?" (أَفَمَنْ كَانَ عَلَىٰ بَيِّنَةٍ مِنْ رَبِّهِ وَيَتْلُوهُ شَاهِدٌ مِنْهُ وَمِنْ قَبْلِهِ كِتَابُ مُوسَىٰ إِمَامًا وَرَحْمَةً).

This person is the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله), the "clear proof" (bayyinah) in his possession is the Majestic Quran, and the "witness" (shahid) to the truth of his prophethood are the true believers such as Ali (عليه السلام). Furthermore, his signs and attributes were mentioned in the Torah before him.

Thus, the truthfulness of his call is established through three clear paths: First, the Quran, which is a "clear proof" in his hand. Second, the previous Heavenly Books, which precisely described his characteristics, and the followers of those books during the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله) era knew them well and were awaiting him for that very reason. Third, the devoted followers and sincere believers who manifest the truth of his call and his words; for one of the signs of the truth of a school of thought is the sincerity, sacrifice, intellect, wisdom, and faith of its followers—since every school can be known by its adherents!

Can he, with these living proofs, be compared to other claimants, or can there be any doubt in the truth of his call? [^1] Following this, the verse refers to those who seek the truth and implicitly invites them to faith, saying: "Those [who seek the truth] believe in it [the Prophet who possesses all these clear proofs]" (أُولَٰئِكَ يُؤْمِنُونَ بِهِ). Although those referred to by the word ula'ika (those) are not explicitly mentioned in this verse,

[^1]: According to this interpretation, "who" (man) refers to the Prophet, "clear proof" (bayyinah) refers to the Quran, and "witness" (shahid)—which is used as a generic noun—refers to the true believers, at the head of whom stands the Commander of the Faithful, Ali (عليه السلام). The pronoun in "from Him" (minhu) refers to Allah, and the pronoun in "before it" (qablihi) refers to the Quran or the Prophet. The entire phrase is the subject (mubtada), and its predicate (khabar) is omitted, implied as "is he like one who is not so?" or "is he like one who desires the life of this world?"

their presence can be felt in the context of the preceding verses.

Subsequently, it describes the fate of the deniers: "And whoever of the [various] factions disbelieves in him—the Fire is his promised meeting place" (وَمَنْ يَكْفُرْ بِهِ مِنَ الْأَحْزَابِ فَالنَّارُ مَوْعِدُهُ).

At the end of the verse, as is the Quranic style in many instances, the speech is directed toward the Prophet, providing a general lesson for all people: "Now that this is the case, and there are so many witnesses to the truth of your call, never be in doubt about what has been revealed to you" (فَلَا تَكُ فِي مِرْيَةٍ مِنْهُ).

"For indeed, it is the truth from your Lord" (إِنَّهُ الْحَقُّ مِنْ رَبِّكَ).

"But most people, due to ignorance, prejudice, and selfishness, do not believe" (وَلَٰكِنَّ أَكْثَرَ النَّاسِ لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ).

  1. Another interpretation mentioned for the verse is that its primary goal is to describe the state of the true believers who, having clear proofs and witnesses to the truth of the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله) call and the testimonies in previous books, believe in the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) based on these evidences.

Therefore, the phrase "one who rests upon a clear proof from his Lord" refers to all those who, with open eyes and convincing evidence, hastened to follow the Quran and its bringer, rather than the person of the Prophet himself.

The advantage of this interpretation over the previous one is that the "predicate" of the subject is explicitly mentioned in the verse and nothing is omitted; the referent of "those" (ula'ika) is mentioned within the verse itself. Thus, the first part of the verse starting with "Is one who rests upon a clear proof..." until "those believe in it" forms a complete sentence without any omission or implication.

However, undoubtedly, other expressions in the verse do not align well with this interpretation, which is why we have placed it in the second rank (reflect upon this).

In any case, the verse points to the merits of Islam and the true Muslims and their reliance on firm proofs in choosing this path, while also describing the grim fate of the arrogant deniers.

Key Points

1- What is meant by "witness" (shahid) in the above verse?

Some commentators have said it refers to "Gabriel," the messenger of Allah's revelation. Some have considered it to be the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) himself, and some have interpreted it as the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله) tongue (taking yatlu from the root of tilawah meaning "recitation" rather than "following").

However, many great commentators have interpreted it as Ali (عليه السلام).

In numerous narrations reaching us from the Infallible Imams (عليهم السلام), and also appearing in some Sunni books of Tafsir, this interpretation is emphasized: that the "witness" is Imam Amir al-Mu'minin (عليه السلام), the first man to believe in the Prophet and the Quran, who was with him at every stage, never withheld his sacrifice for a moment, and strove in his support until his last breath. [^1] In a narration, we read that Ali (عليه السلام) said: "Regarding every famous man of the Quraysh, a verse or verses have been revealed." Someone asked, "O Commander of the Faithful, which verse was revealed regarding you?" The Imam replied: "Have you not read the verse in Surah Hud: 'Is one who rests upon a clear proof from his Lord, followed by a witness from Him...'? The Prophet had the 'divine proof' and I was the 'witness'." [^1]

[^1]: Refer to the commentaries of Al-Burhan, Nur al-Thaqalayn, Al-Qurtubi, Majma' al-Bayan, and other Tafsirs. [^1]: Tafsir al-Burhan, Vol. 2, p. 213.

In the final verse of Surah Ar-Ra'd, an expression is found that supports this meaning: "And those who disbelieve say, 'You are not a messenger.' Say, 'Sufficient is Allah as a witness between me and you, and whoever has knowledge of the Book'" (وَيَقُولُ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا لَسْتَ مُرْسَلًا قُلْ كَفَىٰ بِاللَّهِ شَهِيدًا بَيْنِي وَبَيْنَكُمْ وَمَنْ عِنْدَهُ عِلْمُ الْكِتَابِ).

In many narrations from both Shi'a and Sunni sources, we read that "whoever has knowledge of the Book" refers to Ali (عليه السلام).

It is also necessary to remind: as we previously mentioned, one of the best ways to recognize the truth of a school of thought is to study the condition of its followers, defenders, and supporters. According to the famous proverb, "The shrine is known by its pilgrims." When we observe a group of selfless, intelligent, faithful, sincere, and pious individuals gathered around a leader and a school, it can be easily understood that this school and that leader possess a high degree of honesty and integrity.

However, when we see a group of opportunists, deceivers, faithless, and impious people surrounding a school or a leader, it is hard to believe that the leader or the school is on the path of truth.

We also deem it necessary to mention that interpreting the word "witness" as Ali (عليه السلام) does not contradict the fact that it may include all true believers and individuals like Abu Dharr, Salman, and Ammar ibn Yasir; for such interpretations point to the most prominent and superior individual. That is, the primary intent is the group at whose head that prominent individual stands.

Evidence for this is a narration from Imam al-Baqir (عليه السلام) who said: "The witness is the Commander of the Faithful, then his successors, one after another."
