[^1] Although only the Infallibles are mentioned in this hadith, it shows that narrations interpreting "witness" exclusively as Ali (عليه السلام) do not mean only him, but rather the most prominent instance.

2- Why is only the Torah mentioned?

As we said, one of the proofs of the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله) truthfulness mentioned in the verse is the previous books, yet only the Book of Moses is mentioned, while we know that tidings of the Prophet of Islam's (صلى الله عليه وآله) appearance are also in the Gospel.

This might be because, in the environment where the Quran was revealed and Islam emerged—namely Mecca and Medina—the ideas of the Jews were more widespread among the People of the Book, while Christians lived in relatively more distant areas, such as Yemen, the Levant, and Najran (located in the northern mountains of Yemen).

Or it may be because the description of the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله) attributes was more comprehensive and extensive in the Torah.

In any case, the term "Imam" (guide) regarding the Torah may be because the laws of the Sharia of Moses (عليه السلام) were fully contained within it, and even Christians take many of their teachings from the Torah.

3- Who is the addressee in "So be not in doubt"?

Regarding who is addressed in this phrase (فَلَا تَكُ فِي مِرْيَةٍ), two possibilities have been given: First, the addressee is the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله), meaning: "Do not allow the slightest doubt or hesitation regarding the truth of the Quran or the religion of Islam."

Of course, the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله), by virtue of perceiving revelation through direct witnessing—where the descent of the Quran from Allah was perceptible to him, indeed higher than sensory perception—had no doubt in the truth of this call. But this is not the first time the Quran addresses the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) while intending the general public. According to the famous Arabic expression, such addresses are of the type: "I mean you [the daughter-in-law], but listen, O neighbor," which has a Persian equivalent: "I am telling the door so the wall hears." This is one of the techniques of eloquence where, in certain cases for emphasis, importance, or other purposes, someone else is addressed instead of the actual intended audience.

The other possibility is that the addressee is every rational, accountable person (mukallaf), meaning: "O rational and accountable human, with these clear proofs, have no doubt in the truth of this Quran." This possibility is based on the premise that "one who rests upon a clear proof from his Lord" does not refer to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) but to all true believers (reflect upon this).

However, in any case, the first interpretation is more consistent with the apparent wording of the verse.

[^1]: Tafsir al-Burhan, Vol. 2, p. 213.

Ayahs

وَمَنْ أَظْلَمُ مِمَّنِ ٱفْتَرَىٰ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ كَذِبًا ۚ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ يُعْرَضُونَ عَلَىٰ رَبِّهِمْ وَيَقُولُ ٱلْأَشْهَـٰدُ هَـٰٓؤُلَآءِ ٱلَّذِينَ كَذَبُوا۟ عَلَىٰ رَبِّهِمْ ۚ أَلَا لَعْنَةُ ٱللَّهِ عَلَى ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ (١٨)
Who is a greater wrongdoer than him who fabricates a lie against Allah? Such shall be presented before their Lord, and the witnesses will say, ‘It is these who lied against their Lord.’ Behold! The curse of Allah is upon the wrongdoers (18)
ٱلَّذِينَ يَصُدُّونَ عَن سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ وَيَبْغُونَهَا عِوَجًا وَهُم بِٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ هُمْ كَـٰفِرُونَ (١٩)
—those who bar [others] from the way of Allah, and seek to make it crooked, and disbelieve in the Hereafter. (19)
أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ لَمْ يَكُونُوا۟ مُعْجِزِينَ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ وَمَا كَانَ لَهُم مِّن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ مِنْ أَوْلِيَآءَ ۘ يُضَـٰعَفُ لَهُمُ ٱلْعَذَابُ ۚ مَا كَانُوا۟ يَسْتَطِيعُونَ ٱلسَّمْعَ وَمَا كَانُوا۟ يُبْصِرُونَ (٢٠)
They cannot frustrate [Allah] on the earth, nor do they have besides Allah any guardian. For them the punishment shall be doubled, for they could neither listen, nor did they use to see. (20)
أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ ٱلَّذِينَ خَسِرُوٓا۟ أَنفُسَهُمْ وَضَلَّ عَنْهُم مَّا كَانُوا۟ يَفْتَرُونَ (٢١)
They are the ones who have ruined their souls, and what they used to fabricate has forsaken them. (21)
لَا جَرَمَ أَنَّهُمْ فِى ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ هُمُ ٱلْأَخْسَرُونَ (٢٢)
Undoubtedly, they are the ones who will be the biggest losers in the Hereafter. (22)

Commentary

The Most Destitute of People

Following the previous verse which spoke of the Quran and the mission of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله), the verses under discussion describe the fate of the deniers, their characteristics, and their ultimate end.

In the first verse, He says: "And who is more unjust than he who invents a lie against Allah?" (وَ مَنْ أَظْلَمُ مِمَّنِ افْتَرى‌ عَلَى اللَّهِ كَذِباً).

This means that rejecting the call of a true Prophet is, in reality, a rejection of Allah's words and attributing them to falsehood. Fundamentally, to belie the Prophet is to belie Allah, and to fabricate lies against one who speaks only on behalf of Allah is considered fabricating lies against the Pure Essence of Allah Himself. [^1] As we have repeatedly mentioned, several verses in the Holy Quran identify a group as the "most unjust" of people using the term azlam (أظلم), while their actions seemingly differ. It is not possible for several groups with different actions to each be the "most unjust"; rather, one group should be "unjust," another "more unjust," and another "the most unjust."

[^1]: The possibility suggested by some commentators—that this sentence is a response to those who claimed the Prophet had fabricated lies against Allah—is very unlikely. This is because the preceding and succeeding verses do not align with this interpretation; rather, it is more appropriate that it refers to the disbelievers.

However, as we have frequently stated in response to this question, the root of all these actions traces back to one thing: polytheism and the denial of Divine signs, which is the greatest of slanders (for further explanation, refer to Volume 5, page 183).

Then, He describes their grim future on the Day of Resurrection: "Those will be presented before their Lord" with all their deeds and actions, appearing in His court of justice (أُولئِكَ يُعْرَضُونَ عَلى‌ رَبِّهِمْ‌).

"At this moment, the witnesses of deeds will testify and say: 'These are the ones who lied against their Lord,' the Great, the Merciful, and their Benefactor" (وَ يَقُولُ الْأَشْهادُ هؤُلاءِ الَّذِينَ كَذَبُوا عَلى‌ رَبِّهِمْ‌).

Then, they will proclaim with a loud voice: "Unquestionably, the curse of Allah is upon the wrongdoers" (أَلا لَعْنَةُ اللَّهِ عَلَى الظَّالِمِينَ‌).

Regarding who the "witnesses" (ashhad) are—whether they are the angels, the recorders of deeds, or the Prophets—commentators have offered various possibilities. However, considering that other verses of the Quran identify the Prophets of Allah as the witnesses of deeds, it appears that they are intended here as well, or perhaps a broader concept that encompasses them and all other witnesses of deeds.

In Surah An-Nisa, verse 41, we read: فَكَيْفَ إِذا جِئْنا مِنْ كُلِّ أُمَّةٍ بِشَهِيدٍ وَ جِئْنا بِكَ عَلى‌ هؤُلاءِ شَهِيداً: "So how [will it be] when We bring from every nation a witness and we bring you, [O Muhammad], against these [people] as a witness?"

And regarding Prophet Jesus (عليه السلام) in verse 117 of Surah Al-Ma'idah, it says: وَ كُنْتُ عَلَيْهِمْ شَهِيداً ما دُمْتُ فِيهِمْ‌: "And I was a witness over them as long as I was among them."

Furthermore, there is a discussion among commentators as to whether the speaker of the phrase "Unquestionably, the curse of Allah is upon the wrongdoers" is Allah or the witnesses of deeds. However, the apparent meaning of the verse is that this statement is a continuation of the witnesses' speech. The following verse describes the attributes of these wrongdoers in three phrases:

First, it says: "Those who avert [people] from the way of Allah" using various means (الَّذِينَ يَصُدُّونَ عَنْ سَبِيلِ اللَّهِ‌).

This is done either by casting doubts, or sometimes through threats, or at times through bribery and the like, all of which share a single goal: averting people from the path of Allah.

Secondly, they specifically strive to show the straight Divine path as crooked: "and seek to make it [seem] deviant" (وَ يَبْغُونَها عِوجاً).

That is, through various distortions, additions, subtractions, interpretation based on personal opinion (tafsir bi'l-ra'y), and concealing truths, they act in such a way that this straight path does not appear in its original form to the eyes, so that people cannot walk this path and truth-seekers cannot find the main road. [^1] Thirdly, "they are disbelievers in the Hereafter" (وَ هُمْ بِالْآخِرَةِ هُمْ كافِرُونَ‌).

Their lack of faith in the Resurrection becomes the source of their other deviations and crimes, for faith in that great court and the vast world after death disciplines the soul and spirit.

[^1]: 'Iwaj (عوج) means crookedness; an explanation of this was provided in Vol. 6, p. 180. Additionally, it should be noted that the pronoun in yabghunaha refers to sabil (way), which is a feminine noun by usage, or it refers to the meaning of "method" or "road." In Surah Yusuf, verse 108, we read: قُلْ هذِهِ سَبِيلِي أَدْعُوا إِلَى اللَّهِ‌: "Say, 'This is my way; I invite to Allah...'"

Interestingly, all these issues are gathered within the concept of "injustice" (zulm), as the broad meaning of this word includes any kind of deviation or displacement of "things," "actions," "attributes," and "beliefs" from their true position.

However, in the next verse, it says: despite all this, "Those were not causing failure [to Allah] on earth," nor can they escape His punishment or exit the realm of His power (أُولئِكَ لَمْ يَكُونُوا مُعْجِزِينَ فِي الْأَرْضِ‌).

"Nor did they have besides Allah any protectors" to help or support them (وَ ما كانَ لَهُمْ مِنْ دُونِ اللَّهِ مِنْ أَوْلِياءَ).

Finally, it refers to their heavy punishment, saying: "The punishment will be multiplied for them" (يُضاعَفُ لَهُمُ الْعَذابُ‌).

This is because they were both misguided and sinful themselves, and they led others toward these paths. For this reason, they carry both the burden of their own sins and the burden of the sins of others (without anything being diminished from the sins of the others).

The evidence for this is found in other verses of the Quran: وَ لَيَحْمِلُنَّ أَثْقالَهُمْ وَ أَثْقالًا مَعَ أَثْقالِهِمْ‌: "And they will surely carry their [own] burdens and [other] burdens along with their burdens" (Al-Ankabut: 13).

There are also numerous narrations stating that whoever establishes an evil tradition (sunnah), the "burden" and sin of all those who act upon that evil tradition will be recorded for him. Likewise, whoever establishes a good tradition will have a reward equivalent to those who act upon it recorded for him.

At the end of the verse, it explains the root cause of their misery: "They could not hear, nor did they see!" (ما كانُوا يَسْتَطِيعُونَ السَّمْعَ وَ ما كانُوا يُبْصِرُونَ‌).

In truth, the disabling of these two effective means for perceiving truths caused them to fall into misguidance themselves and to lead others into misguidance, for truth and reality cannot be perceived except with an open eye and a listening ear.

It is noteworthy that the phrase says they "were not able to hear" (the truth). This expression points to the reality that hearing the words of truth was so heavy and burdensome for them that it was as if they lacked the ability to hear the truth at all. This expression is exactly like saying a lover cannot bear to hear any mention of their beloved's faults.

Naturally, this inability to perceive truths—which is the result of intense stubbornness and enmity toward the truth—does not absolve them of responsibility. In technical terms, this is something for which they themselves provided the causes, while they could have removed this state from themselves, for power over the cause is power over the effect.

The next verse expresses the outcome of all their wrong efforts and strivings in a single sentence: "Those are the ones who have lost their souls" and have gone bankrupt (أُولئِكَ الَّذِينَ خَسِرُوا أَنْفُسَهُمْ‌).

This is the greatest loss that can afflict a human being—to lose one's very human existence. Then it adds that they had set their hearts on false deities, "and lost from them is what they used to invent" (وَ ضَلَّ عَنْهُمْ ما كانُوا يَفْتَرُونَ‌). All these fabricated idols eventually disappeared and vanished from their sight.

The final verse under discussion states the ultimate verdict on their fate in a decisive manner: "Without doubt, they are the greatest losers in the Hereafter" (لا جَرَمَ أَنَّهُمْ فِي الْآخِرَةِ هُمُ الْأَخْسَرُونَ‌).

This is because they were deprived of the blessing of an open eye and a listening ear, and they lost all the human capital of their existence; furthermore, they carry both the burden of their own responsibility and the burden of others!

The original meaning of "La Jarama": Jaram (on the pattern of haram) originally means picking fruit from a tree (as Raghib mentions in Al-Mufradat). Later, it was applied to any kind of acquisition or attainment of a matter, and due to frequent usage in "acquiring unpleasant things," it took on this connotation. For this reason, sin is called jurm. However, when this word is preceded by la as la jarama at the beginning of a sentence, it conveys the meaning that nothing can cut off or prevent this matter. Therefore, la jarama is used to mean "inevitably," "necessarily," and "certainly." (Note carefully).

Ayahs

إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَعَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ وَأَخْبَتُوٓا۟ إِلَىٰ رَبِّهِمْ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ أَصْحَـٰبُ ٱلْجَنَّةِ ۖ هُمْ فِيهَا خَـٰلِدُونَ (٢٣)
Indeed those who have faith and do righteous deeds and are humble before their Lord—they shall be the inhabitants of paradise, and they shall remain in it [forever]. (23)
۞ مَثَلُ ٱلْفَرِيقَيْنِ كَٱلْأَعْمَىٰ وَٱلْأَصَمِّ وَٱلْبَصِيرِ وَٱلسَّمِيعِ ۚ هَلْ يَسْتَوِيَانِ مَثَلًا ۚ أَفَلَا تَذَكَّرُونَ (٢٤)
The parable of the two parties is that of one who is blind and deaf and one who sees and hears. Are they equal in comparison? Will you not then take admonition? (24)

Commentary

Following the previous verses which described the state of a group of deniers of Divine revelation, these two verses present their opposite—namely, the state of the true believers.

First, it says: "Indeed, those who believed and performed righteous deeds and were humble and submissive before Allah and confident in His promises, they are the companions of Paradise and shall abide therein forever" (إِنَّ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَ عَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ وَ أَخْبَتُوا إِلى‌ رَبِّهِمْ أُولئِكَ أَصْحابُ الْجَنَّةِ هُمْ فِيها خالِدُونَ‌).

Here, attention must be paid to two points

1- The expression of the three attributes: "Faith," "Righteousness," and "Submission"

The mention of these three attributes—namely "faith," "righteous deeds," and "submission and humility before the call of Truth"—is, in reality, the expression of three interconnected realities. This is because righteous action is the fruit of the tree of faith; faith that does not bear such fruit is a weak and worthless faith that cannot be taken into account. Likewise, the matter of submission, humility, and confidence in the promises of the Lord are the effects of faith and righteous deeds, for correct belief and pure action are the source of the emergence of these high qualities and noble traits within the human soul.

2- The meaning of "Akhbatu" (أخبتوا)

"Akhbatu" is derived from the word ikhbat, from the root khabt (rhyming with sabt), which means a flat and vast land where a person can walk easily and with confidence. For this reason, this root is used to mean "confidence," and it has also come to mean "humility" and "submission," because such a land is both reassuring for walking and is humble and submissive before the travelers.

Therefore, the phrase "أَخْبَتُوا إِلى‌ رَبِّهِمْ" (they were humble before their Lord) may carry one of the following three meanings, while at the same time, there is no contradiction in combining all three:

1- True believers are humble before Allah. 2- They are submissive to the command of their Lord. 3- They are confident in the promises of Allah.

In any case, it refers to one of the highest human attributes of the believers, the effect of which is reflected in their entire lives.

Interestingly, in a hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, we read that one of his companions said: "Among us is a man named 'Kulayb'; no hadith is issued from you except that he immediately says, 'I am submissive to it,' and therefore we have named him 'Kulayb of Submission' (Kulayb al-Taslim)."

The Imam said: "May Allah's mercy be upon him." Then he added: "Do you know what submission is?"

We remained silent. He said: "By Allah, it is the very ikhbat mentioned in the words of Allah عز و جل: 'Indeed, those who believed and performed righteous deeds and were humble (akhbatu) before their Lord...'" [1]

In the next verse, Allah describes the state of these two groups through a clear and vivid example, saying: "The example of the two groups is like the 'blind and deaf' and the 'seeing and hearing'" (مَثَلُ الْفَرِيقَيْنِ كَالْأَعْمى‌ وَ الْأَصَمِّ وَ الْبَصِيرِ وَ السَّمِيعِ‌).

"Are these two groups equal in comparison?" (هَلْ يَسْتَوِيانِ مَثَلًا).

"Will you not then take heed and reflect?" (أَ فَلا تَذَكَّرُونَ‌). As stated in the science of rhetoric (Ma'ani wa Bayan), in order to personify intellectual truths and explain them at a general level, "intellectual concepts" are often likened to "sensory perceptions." The Quran uses this method extensively, and in explaining many sensitive and highly important issues, it clarifies truths in the most sublime manner by using clear and beautiful parables.

The above statement is of this kind, because the most effective means for recognizing sensory realities in the natural world are the eyes and ears. For this reason, it cannot be believed that individuals who are entirely deprived of sight and hearing—for example, from birth—could perceive anything correctly from this natural world; they will certainly live in a world of complete ignorance.

Similarly, those who, due to stubbornness and enmity toward the Truth and being trapped in the clutches of prejudice, selfishness, and self-worship, have lost the truth-seeking eye and ear, can never perceive the truths related to the world of the Unseen, the effects of faith, the pleasure of worshipping the Lord, and the glory of submission to His command. Such individuals are like the blind and deaf who live in absolute darkness and deadly silence, while the true believers, with open eyes and hearing ears, see every movement and hear every sound, and by paying attention to them, they open their path toward a destiny that creates happiness.

[^1]: Tafsir al-Burhan, Vol. 2, p. 216.

Ayahs

وَلَقَدْ أَرْسَلْنَا نُوحًا إِلَىٰ قَوْمِهِۦٓ إِنِّى لَكُمْ نَذِيرٌ مُّبِينٌ (٢٥)
Certainly We sent Noah to his people [to declare]: ‘Indeed I am a manifest warner to you. (25)
أَن لَّا تَعْبُدُوٓا۟ إِلَّا ٱللَّهَ ۖ إِنِّىٓ أَخَافُ عَلَيْكُمْ عَذَابَ يَوْمٍ أَلِيمٍ (٢٦)
Worship none but Allah. Indeed I fear for you the punishment of a painful day.’ (26)
فَقَالَ ٱلْمَلَأُ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ مِن قَوْمِهِۦ مَا نَرَىٰكَ إِلَّا بَشَرًا مِّثْلَنَا وَمَا نَرَىٰكَ ٱتَّبَعَكَ إِلَّا ٱلَّذِينَ هُمْ أَرَاذِلُنَا بَادِىَ ٱلرَّأْىِ وَمَا نَرَىٰ لَكُمْ عَلَيْنَا مِن فَضْلٍۭ بَلْ نَظُنُّكُمْ كَـٰذِبِينَ (٢٧)
But the elite of the faithless from among his people said, ‘We do not see you to be anything but a human being like ourselves, and we do not see anyone following you except those who are simpleminded riffraff from our midst. Nor do we see that you have any merit over us. Indeed, we consider you to be liars.’ (27)
قَالَ يَـٰقَوْمِ أَرَءَيْتُمْ إِن كُنتُ عَلَىٰ بَيِّنَةٍ مِّن رَّبِّى وَءَاتَىٰنِى رَحْمَةً مِّنْ عِندِهِۦ فَعُمِّيَتْ عَلَيْكُمْ أَنُلْزِمُكُمُوهَا وَأَنتُمْ لَهَا كَـٰرِهُونَ (٢٨)
He said, ‘O my people! Tell me, should I stand on a manifest proof from my Lord, and He has granted me His own mercy—though it should be invisible to you—shall we force it upon you while you are averse to it? (28)

Commentary

The Stirring Account of Noah and His People

As we stated at the beginning of this Surah, in order to awaken intellects, draw attention to the realities of life and the grim fate of evildoers, and ultimately illustrate the path to victory and success, significant portions of the history of former Prophets have been recounted.

It begins first with the story of "Noah عليه السلام," an Ulu al-'Azm (Arch-Prophet), explaining the fundamental points of his history in a stirring manner across 26 verses.

Undoubtedly, the story of Noah's عليه السلام uprising, his persistent and fierce struggle against the arrogant elite (mustakbirin) of his time, and their disastrous end, is one of the most lesson-filled chapters of human history, where a profound moral lesson is hidden in every step.

The above verses describe the first stage of this great call, stating:

"We sent Noah to his people, and he declared to them: 'I am a clear warner to you'" (وَ لَقَدْ أَرْسَلْنا نُوحاً إِلى‌ قَوْمِهِ إِنِّي لَكُمْ نَذِيرٌ مُبِينٌ‌).

The emphasis on the issue of "warning" (indhar), despite the fact that Prophets were both warners and bringers of glad tidings, is because the initial strikes of a revolution must begin with a declaration of danger and warning. This is because the impact of warning in awakening the sleepers and the heedless is greater than that of glad tidings.

Fundamentally, a human being will not move from his place until he senses a significant danger. For this reason, the warnings and danger alerts of the Prophets descended like lashes upon the indifferent souls of the misguided, so that anyone with the capacity for movement would be stirred! It is also for this reason that in many verses of the Quran (such as 22:49, 26:115, 29:50, 35:42, 38:70, 46:9, 51:50, and others), the word "Nadhir" (Warner) is emphasized when describing the call of the Prophets.

In the next verse, after the initial strike, he summarizes the content of his mission in a single sentence:

"That you worship none but Allah" (أَنْ لا تَعْبُدُوا إِلَّا اللَّهَ‌).

Immediately following that, he repeats the issue of warning and the declaration of danger, saying: "Indeed, I fear for you the punishment of a painful day" (إِنِّي أَخافُ عَلَيْكُمْ عَذابَ يَوْمٍ أَلِيمٍ‌) [1]. In reality, Monotheism (Tawhid) and the worship of Allah (the One and Only God) is the root, foundation, and infrastructure of the entire call of the Prophets. For this reason, regarding other Prophets—as seen in the second verse of this Surah, verse 40 of Surah Yusuf, and verse 23 of Surah Al-Isra—the same expression is observed: they summarized their call in Monotheism.

If indeed all members of society worshipped none but "Allah" and did not bow down before various fabricated idols—whether external idols or internal ones like selfishness, whims and desires, lusts, money, status, pomp, or family—no disorder would exist in human societies.

If people did not turn a weak, autocratic human into an idol, did not prostrate before him, and did not carry out his commands, neither tyranny nor colonialism would emerge, nor their grim consequences; neither humiliation nor captivity would occur, nor dependency and parasitism. All these miseries that afflict individuals and societies are due to that very deviation from the worship of Allah and turning toward the worship of idols and tyrants (taghuts).

[1]: Although "painful" is an attribute of the punishment, in the above verse, it is made an attribute for the "day" (yawm). This is a type of subtle metaphorical attribution (isnad majazi) found in the literature of various languages. In Persian, we also say "such and such a day was a painful day," even though the day itself was not painful, but rather its events were painful.

Now let us see what the first reaction of the tyrants, autocrats, and the wealthy and powerful of that era was toward this great call and clear warning.

Certainly, it was nothing but a handful of hollow excuses, false pretexts, and baseless arguments that constitute the agenda of all tyrants in every age and time.

They gave three responses to Noah's call:

First, "The eminent ones (al-mala') among his people who disbelieved said: 'We see you as nothing but a human being like ourselves'" (فَقالَ الْمَلَأُ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا مِنْ قَوْمِهِ ما نَراكَ إِلَّا بَشَراً مِثْلَنا).

They argued that a divine mission should be carried by angels, not by humans like them—under the assumption that the status of a human is lower than an angel, or that an angel understands human pain better than a human! Here again, we encounter the word "al-mala'," which refers to the wealthy, the powerful, and the hollow, "eye-filling" individuals who constitute the primary source of corruption and ruin in every society, and who are the ones to raise the flag of opposition against the Prophets.

Their second argument was: "O Noah, we do not see following you except those who are the lowest among us, the immature and uninformed who have never investigated matters" (وَ ما نَراكَ اتَّبَعَكَ إِلَّا الَّذِينَ هُمْ أَراذِلُنا بادِيَ الرَّأْيِ‌). "Aradhil" is the plural of "ardhal", which itself is the plural of "radhl", meaning a base and insignificant being, whether human or otherwise.

Of course, there is no doubt that those who believed in Noah were neither base nor insignificant. Rather, because Prophets primarily supported the oppressed (mustad'afin) and fought against the arrogant (mustakbirin), the first group to answer the Prophets' call was that very deprived, poor, and low-income group. In the eyes of the arrogant, who measured personality only by wealth and power, these were considered base and insignificant individuals.

As for labeling them as "badi al-ra'y" (superficial, without study, or one who falls for something at first sight), it was actually because they did not possess the stubbornness and undue prejudices that others had. Instead, they were mostly pure-hearted youths who quickly felt the first radiance of truth in their hearts and, with the alertness born of seeking truth, soon perceived the signs of sincerity in the words and actions of the Prophets.

Finally, their third objection was: "Regardless of the fact that you are a human and not an angel, and besides the fact that those who believe in you show that your call has no correct content, fundamentally we see no superiority in you over us that would make us follow you" (وَ ما نَرى‌ لَكُمْ عَلَيْنا مِنْ فَضْلٍ‌). For this reason, "Indeed, we think you are liars" (بَلْ نَظُنُّكُمْ كاذِبِينَ‌).

In the following verses, the logical responses of Noah to these troublemakers are stated. First, he says: "O my people! If I possess a clear proof and miracle from my Lord, and He has favored me with His mercy in fulfilling this mission, and this matter has remained hidden from you due to your lack of attention," can you still deny my mission and stop following me?! (قالَ يا قَوْمِ أَ رَأَيْتُمْ إِنْ كُنْتُ عَلى‌ بَيِّنَةٍ مِنْ رَبِّي وَ آتانِي رَحْمَةً مِنْ عِنْدِهِ فَعُمِّيَتْ عَلَيْكُمْ‌).

Regarding which of the three objections of the arrogant elite this response pertains to, commentators have much discussion. However, upon careful reflection, it becomes clear that this comprehensive response can answer all three objections.

For their first objection was: "Why are you a human?" He replies: "It is true that I am a human like you, but I have been encompassed by divine mercy and given a clear proof (bayyinah). Therefore, my humanity cannot hinder this great mission, and there is no necessity for me to be an angel."

Their second objection was that his followers were thoughtless and superficial. He says: "It is you who are thoughtless for denying this clear truth, for there are proofs with me that are sufficient and convincing for any truth-seeker—except for individuals like you whose truth-perceiving eyes have been disabled under the cover of pride, selfishness, arrogance, and ambition."

Their third objection was: "We find no superiority in you over us." He responds: "What superiority is higher than that Allah has encompassed me with His mercy and placed clear evidence at my disposal?"

Therefore, there is no reason for you to imagine me a liar, as the signs of the truthfulness of my words are manifest.

At the end of the verse, he says: "Can I compel you to accept this clear proof while you yourselves are not ready and are averse to accepting it, or even to reflecting and thinking about it?" (أَنُلْزِمُكُمُوها وَ أَنْتُمْ لَها كارِهُونَ‌).

Ayahs

وَيَـٰقَوْمِ لَآ أَسْـَٔلُكُمْ عَلَيْهِ مَالًا ۖ إِنْ أَجْرِىَ إِلَّا عَلَى ٱللَّهِ ۚ وَمَآ أَنَا۠ بِطَارِدِ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوٓا۟ ۚ إِنَّهُم مُّلَـٰقُوا۟ رَبِّهِمْ وَلَـٰكِنِّىٓ أَرَىٰكُمْ قَوْمًا تَجْهَلُونَ (٢٩)
O my people! I do not ask you any material reward for it. My reward lies only with Allah. But I will not drive away those who have faith: they will indeed encounter their Lord. But I see that you are an ignorant lot. (29)
وَيَـٰقَوْمِ مَن يَنصُرُنِى مِنَ ٱللَّهِ إِن طَرَدتُّهُمْ ۚ أَفَلَا تَذَكَّرُونَ (٣٠)
O my people! Who would come to my help against Allah were I to drive them away? Will you not then take admonition? (30)
وَلَآ أَقُولُ لَكُمْ عِندِى خَزَآئِنُ ٱللَّهِ وَلَآ أَعْلَمُ ٱلْغَيْبَ وَلَآ أَقُولُ إِنِّى مَلَكٌ وَلَآ أَقُولُ لِلَّذِينَ تَزْدَرِىٓ أَعْيُنُكُمْ لَن يُؤْتِيَهُمُ ٱللَّهُ خَيْرًا ۖ ٱللَّهُ أَعْلَمُ بِمَا فِىٓ أَنفُسِهِمْ ۖ إِنِّىٓ إِذًا لَّمِنَ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ (٣١)
I do not say to you that I possess the treasuries of Allah, neither do I know the Unseen. I do not claim to be an angel, neither do I say of those who are despicable in your eyes that Allah will not grant them any good—Allah knows best what is in their hearts—for then I would indeed be a wrongdoer.’ (31)

Commentary

I Will Not Repel Any Believer

In the preceding verses, we saw that the self-centered and excuse-seeking people raised various objections against Nuh (peace be upon him), to which he responded with eloquent and clear reasoning.

The verses under discussion continue the response to their pretext-seeking.

The first verse recounts one of the proofs of prophethood that Nuh (peace be upon him) presented to enlighten his dark-hearted people: "O my people! I ask of you no wealth, no reward, or recompense for this call" (وَ يا قَوْمِ لا أَسْئَلُكُمْ عَلَيْهِ مالًا).

"My reward is only from Allah," the One who has commissioned me with prophethood and commanded me to invite the creation (إِنْ أَجرِيَ إِلَّا عَلَى اللَّهِ).

This clearly demonstrates that I have no material objective in this mission and think of nothing but the spiritual divine reward. A false claimant could never be like this, taking upon himself all this trouble and hardship for nothing.

This serves as a criterion and a model for distinguishing true leaders from false opportunists, who have a material goal—directly or indirectly—for every step they take.

Then, in response to those who insisted that Nuh (peace be upon him) should drive away the poor or young believers, he says with absolute decisiveness: "I will never repel those who have believed" (وَ ما أَنَا بِطارِدِ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا).

"For they shall meet their Lord, and in the next world, they will be my adversaries before Him" (إِنَّهُمْ مُلاقُوا رَبِّهِمْ). [^1] At the end of the verse, he declares to them: "But I see you as a people who are ignorant" (وَ لكِنِّي أَراكُمْ قَوْماً تَجْهَلُونَ).

[^1]: There is also a possibility in the interpretation of this sentence that Nuh (peace be upon him) means: if my followers are liars in their hearts, Allah will settle their account on the Day of Resurrection; however, the first interpretation seems more correct.

What ignorance could be greater than losing the yardstick for measuring virtue and seeking it in wealth, financial capability, outward status, and age? You imagine that pure-hearted and faithful individuals, whose hands are empty and feet are bare, are far from Allah's threshold. This is your great mistake and a sign of your ignorance and unawareness!

Furthermore, due to your ignorance, you imagine that a prophet must be an angel, whereas the leader of humans must be of their own kind so that he may know and feel their needs, problems, and pains.

In the next verse, for further clarification, he says to them: "O my people! Who would help me against Allah (in that great court of justice, and even in this world) if I were to drive them away?" (وَ يا قَوْمِ مَنْ يَنْصُرُنِي مِنَ اللَّهِ إِنْ طَرَدْتُهُمْ).

Repelling righteous and faithful people is no simple matter; they will be my enemies on the Day of Resurrection, and no one will be able to defend me there. Moreover, divine punishment might overtake me in this world. "Will you not then reflect?" so that you may know that what I say is the very truth (أَ فَلا تَذَكَّرُونَ).

[The Difference Between "Reflection" (Tafakkur) and "Remembrance" (Tadhakkur)]

The difference between "reflection" (tafakkur) and "remembrance" (tadhakkur) is that reflection is actually for gaining knowledge of something even if we had no prior information about it. However, remembrance is used in cases where a person was previously acquainted with the subject, even if through innate (fitrah) awareness. Incidentally, the matters Nuh (peace be upon him) discussed with his people are all of this nature; they are truths that a person understands by referring to his own nature and inner self, but pride, prejudice, selfishness, and negligence have cast a veil over them.

The final word Nuh (peace be upon him) says in response to the vain objections of his people is that if you imagine and expect me to have any distinction other than through revelation and miracles, you are mistaken. I must say explicitly: "I do not say to you that the treasures of Allah are with me, nor that I can do whatever I wish" (وَ لا أَقُولُ لَكُمْ عِنْدِي خَزائِنُ اللَّهِ).

"Nor do I say that I know the Unseen" (وَ لا أَعْلَمُ الْغَيْبَ).

"Nor do I say that I am an angel" (وَ لا أَقُولُ إِنِّي مَلَكٌ).

Such grand and false claims are specific to lying pretenders, and a true prophet will never make such claims, because the "treasures of Allah" and "knowledge of the Unseen" belong solely to the Pure Essence of Allah, and being an angel is inconsistent with these human feelings.

Therefore, anyone who makes one or all of these three claims provides evidence of his own falsehood.

A similar expression regarding the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله is seen in verse 50 of Surah Al-An'am, where it says: قُلْ لا أَقُولُ لَكُمْ عِنْدِي خَزائِنُ اللَّهِ وَ لا أَعْلَمُ الْغَيْبَ وَ لا أَقُولُ لَكُمْ إِنِّي مَلَكٌ إِنْ أَتَّبِعُ إِلَّا ما يُوحى‌ إِلَيَ ("Say: I do not tell you that with me are the treasures of Allah, nor do I know the Unseen, nor do I tell you that I am an angel. I only follow what is revealed to me"). Restricting the Prophet's distinction in this verse to the matter of "revelation" and negating the three aforementioned things is proof that in the verses related to Nuh (peace be upon him), such a distinction is also implied in the meaning, even if not explicitly stated.

At the end of the verse, he once again addresses the subject of the downtrodden believers and emphasizes: "I cannot say concerning those whom your eyes despise that Allah will never grant them any good or reward" (وَ لا أَقُولُ لِلَّذِينَ تَزْدَرِي أَعْيُنُكُمْ لَنْ يُؤْتِيَهُمُ اللَّهُ خَيْراً).

On the contrary, the good of this world and the next belongs to them, even if their hands are empty of wealth. It is you who, due to crude fantasies, have restricted "good" to wealth, status, or age, and are completely unaware of truth and meaning.

And even assuming your words are true and they are the "lowest of the low," Allah knows best what is in their souls and their intentions (اللَّهُ أَعْلَمُ بِما فِي أَنْفُسِهِمْ). I see nothing from them but faith and sincerity, and for this reason, I am duty-bound to accept them. I am commissioned to judge by the outward, and Allah is the Knower of His servants! "If I were to do otherwise, I would surely be among the wrongdoers" (إِنِّي إِذاً لَمِنَ الظَّالِمِينَ).

There is also a possibility in the interpretation of the last sentence that it relates to the entire content of the verse; meaning, if I were to claim knowledge of the Unseen, or being an angel, or possessing the treasures of Allah, or if I were to repel the believers, I would be among the wrongdoers before Allah and conscience.

Here, several points should be noted

1- Knowledge of the Unseen

As we have pointed out many times, knowledge of the Unseen in an absolute and unconditional sense belongs to Allah. However, He grants whatever amount of this knowledge He deems appropriate to His prophets and deputies (awliya), as we read in verses 26 and 27 of Surah Al-Jinn: عالِمُ الْغَيْبِ فَلا يُظْهِرُ عَلى‌ غَيْبِهِ أَحَداً إِلَّا مَنِ ارْتَضى‌ مِنْ رَسُولٍ ("The Knower of the Unseen, and He does not disclose His Unseen to anyone, except to a messenger with whom He is well-pleased").

Therefore, there is no contradiction between the verses under discussion—which negate knowledge of the Unseen from the prophets—and the verses or narrations that attribute knowledge of some unseen matters to the prophets or Imams علیهم السلام.

Knowledge of the secrets of the Unseen essentially belongs to Allah, and whatever others possess is accidental and through divine teaching; for this reason, it is limited to the boundaries He wills. [^2]

2- The Yardstick for Measuring Virtue

Once again in these verses, we encounter the reality that the possessors of gold and power, and the materialist world-worshippers—who see everything through the lens of their own thoughts in that same material color—imagine all respect and personality to lie in possessing wealth, status, and positions of equal weight. Therefore, it is no surprise that true believers whose hands were empty of wealth were introduced in their lexicon as "the lowest" (aradhal) and looked upon with contempt and inferiority.

This was not unique to the people of Nuh (peace be upon him) who considered the downtrodden believers—especially the revolutionary youth who surrounded him—to be empty-headed, shallow-minded, and riffraff. History shows that this logic existed against other prophets, especially in the case of the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله and the early believers.

Even now, we see similar logic in our own time, where Pharaoh-like arrogant powers, relying on their satanic strength, accuse true believers of all these things and, as a repetition of history, attribute the same titles to their opponents. However, when a corrupt environment is purified by a divine revolution, these types of yardsticks for measuring personality are thrown into the dustbin of history along with other illusory titles, and authentic human yardsticks take their place—yardsticks that are situated in the essence of human life, upon which objective realities are built, and from which a pure, prosperous, and free society draws its strength, such as faith, knowledge, awareness, sacrifice, forgiveness, piety, chastity, courage, bravery, experience, alertness, management, and order.

3- A Word from the Author of Tafsir al-Manar

Some commentators, such as the author of Al-Manar, when reaching this verse, refer in a short sentence to those who attribute knowledge of the Unseen to other than Allah or seek the resolution of problems from them, saying: "These two matters (knowledge of the Unseen and the treasures of Allah) are the very things the Quran negated from the prophets, yet the innovators among Muslims and the People of the Book affirm them for the saints and holy figures." [^3] If his intention is to negate any kind of awareness of the Unseen even through divine teaching, this contradicts the explicit texts of the Holy Quran. And if his intention is to negate seeking intercession (tawassul) from the prophets and divine deputies—in the sense that we ask them to request Allah to solve our problems—this statement also contradicts Quranic verses and established hadiths narrated through both Shi'a and Sunni chains. (For more explanation on this, refer to Tafsir Nemouneh, Volume 4, pages 363-369).

[^2]: For further explanation, refer to Tafsir Nemouneh, Volume 5, page 245, and Volume 7, page 46. [^3]: Al-Manar, Volume 12, page 67.

Ayahs

قَالُوا۟ يَـٰنُوحُ قَدْ جَـٰدَلْتَنَا فَأَكْثَرْتَ جِدَٰلَنَا فَأْتِنَا بِمَا تَعِدُنَآ إِن كُنتَ مِنَ ٱلصَّـٰدِقِينَ (٣٢)
They said, ‘O Noah, you have disputed with us already, and you have disputed with us exceedingly. Now bring us what you threaten us with, should you be truthful. (32)
قَالَ إِنَّمَا يَأْتِيكُم بِهِ ٱللَّهُ إِن شَآءَ وَمَآ أَنتُم بِمُعْجِزِينَ (٣٣)
He said, ‘Allah will indeed bring it on you if He wishes, and you cannot frustrate [Him]. (33)
وَلَا يَنفَعُكُمْ نُصْحِىٓ إِنْ أَرَدتُّ أَنْ أَنصَحَ لَكُمْ إِن كَانَ ٱللَّهُ يُرِيدُ أَن يُغْوِيَكُمْ ۚ هُوَ رَبُّكُمْ وَإِلَيْهِ تُرْجَعُونَ (٣٤)
My exhorting will not benefit you, much as I may seek to exhort you, if Allah desires to consign you to perversity. He is your Lord, and to Him you shall be brought back.’ (34)
أَمْ يَقُولُونَ ٱفْتَرَىٰهُ ۖ قُلْ إِنِ ٱفْتَرَيْتُهُۥ فَعَلَىَّ إِجْرَامِى وَأَنَا۠ بَرِىٓءٌ مِّمَّا تُجْرِمُونَ (٣٥)
Do they say, ‘He has fabricated it?’ Say, ‘Should I have fabricated it, then my guilt will be upon me, and I am absolved of your guilty conduct.’ (35)

Commentary

Enough Talk, Where is the Punishment?

These verses refer to the continuation of the dialogue between Nuh and his people. The first verse quotes the people of Nuh as saying: "They said: O Nuh, you have disputed and argued with us enough; you have spoken much to us, and there is no longer any room for further discussion" (قَالُوا يَا نُوحُ قَدْ جَادَلْتَنَا فَأَكْثَرْتَ جِدَالَنَا).

"If you are truthful, then bring upon us those painful promises of divine punishment that you keep threatening us with" (فَأْتِنَا بِمَا تَعِدُنَا إِنْ كُنْتَ مِنَ الصَّادِقِينَ).

This is exactly like a person or persons speaking to us about an issue while including threats, and we respond by saying, "Enough talk! Go and do whatever you are capable of and do not delay for a moment." This indicates: we neither pay heed to your reasons, nor do we fear your threats, nor are we willing to listen to your words any longer! Choosing such a method in the face of all that love and kindness from the Divine Prophets—whose words sit upon the heart like pure, refreshing water—demonstrates the ultimate level of stubbornness, prejudice, and ignorance.

Furthermore, it is clearly understood from this statement that Nuh (peace be upon him) had strived for a long time to guide them, utilizing every opportunity to achieve this goal—namely, their guidance—to the extent that those misguided people expressed exhaustion from his words and instructions.

This reality is also clearly evident from other verses in the Quran regarding Nuh. In Surah Nuh, verses 5 to 13, this meaning is explained in detail: "He said: My Lord! Indeed, I have called my people night and day, but my call has only increased them in flight. And indeed, every time I called them so that You might forgive them, they put their fingers in their ears, covered themselves with their garments, persisted, and became arrogant with [great] arrogance. Then indeed, I called them openly. Then I announced to them and confided to them secretly." He pleaded: "O Lord! I called my people toward You day and night, but my invitation only added to their flight! Every time I invited them so that You might forgive them, they placed their fingers in their ears and wrapped their clothes around themselves, persisting in their opposition and showing arrogance and wilfulness. Yet, I did not give up calling them; I invited them toward You openly and then secretly, persisting continuously, but they would not listen to my words at all!"

In the verse under discussion, the phrase "Jadaltana" (جَادَلْتَنَا) is used, which is derived from the root "Mujadalah" (مجادله). Originally, it comes from "Jadal" (جدل), meaning the tight twisting and braiding of a rope. For this reason, a predatory hawk is called "Ajdal" (اجدل) because it is the most aggressive and "twisting" of all birds. Subsequently, it came to be used for "entangling" an opponent in discussion and debate.

Although "Jadal" (جدal), "Mira'" (مراء), and "Hijjaj" (حجاج) are similar in meaning, as some researchers have noted, "Mira'" carries a sense of blame and reproach, as it is used in cases where a person insists on and argues for a falsehood. However, this concept is not necessarily present in the meaning of "Jadal" or "Mujadalah." The difference between "Jadal" and "Hijjaj" is that "Jadal" is used to turn an opponent away from their belief, while "Hijjaj" is used to invite them toward a belief and provide evidence for it.

In response to this indifference, stubbornness, and defiance, Nuh replied with a short sentence: "Only Allah will bring it [the punishment] upon you if He wills" (قَالَ إِنَّمَا يَأْتِيكُمْ بِهِ اللَّهُ إِنْ شَاءَ). In any case, this is out of my hands and not within my power; I am His messenger and I follow His command. Therefore, do not demand the punishment and torment from me.

But know that when the command for punishment arrives, "you will not be able to escape [the grasp of His power, nor find a sanctuary or refuge]" (وَمَا أَنْتُمْ بِمُعْجِزِينَ).

"Mu'jizin" (معجزین) is from the root "I'jaz" (اعجاز), meaning to make another person powerless. This word is sometimes used when a person prevents another's work and stops them, rendering them helpless; sometimes when one escapes from someone's grasp and goes out of reach, making them powerless; and sometimes by taking the initiative to bring the opponent to their knees or to place oneself in immunity.

All of these are different facets of the meaning of "I'jaz" and making the opponent powerless, and in the above verse, all these meanings are possible, as there is no contradiction between them. That is, in no way can you remain safe from His punishment.

Then he adds: "And my advice will not benefit you, even if I wish to advise you, if Allah intends to leave you in your straying [due to your sins and physical and intellectual pollutions]" (وَلَا يَنْفَعُكُمْ نُصْحِي إِنْ أَرَدْتُ أَنْ أَنْصَحَ لَكُمْ إِنْ كَانَ اللَّهُ يُرِيدُ أَنْ يُغْوِيَكُمْ).

Because "He is your Lord, and to Him you will be returned," and your entire existence is within the grasp of His power (هُوَ رَبُّكُمْ وَإِلَيْهِ تُرْجَعُونَ).

Question

Upon studying this verse, a question immediately comes to mind—and many commentators have pointed it out—is it possible for Allah to intend to lead someone astray? Would this not be evidence of predestination (Jabr)? And is such a thing acceptable while accepting the principle of free will and choice?

Answer

As clarified throughout the preceding discussions—and as we have mentioned many times—sometimes a series of actions are committed by a human being whose result is perpetual misguidance, deviation, and the inability to return to the Truth. Continuous stubbornness, insistence on sins, and constant enmity toward seekers of truth and righteous leaders cast such a thick veil over a person's intellect that they lose the ability to see even the smallest ray of the sun of Truth and reality!

Since this state is an effect of the actions the person themselves performed, it is by no means evidence of predestination; rather, it is the very essence of choice. What pertains to Allah is that He has placed such an effect within those specific actions.

In the Holy Quran, numerous verses refer to this reality, which we have noted under verse 7 of Surah Al-Baqarah and elsewhere.

In the final verse under discussion, a statement is made as a parenthetical sentence to emphasize the discussions raised in the story of Nuh in the preceding and succeeding verses:

"Or do they say, 'He [Muhammad صلى الله عليه وآله] has fabricated it'?" (أَمْ يَقُولُونَ افْتَرَاهُ).

In response to them, say: "If I have fabricated it [and falsely attributed it to Allah], then upon me is my sin" (قُلْ إِنِ افْتَرَيْتُهُ فَعَلَيَّ إِجْرَامِي).

"But I am innocent of the sins you commit" (وَأَنَا بَرِيءٌ مِمَّا تُجْرِمُونَ).

Here, several points must be noted

[The Meaning of "Ijram"]

  1. "Ijram" (إجرام) is from the root "Jurm" (جرم). As previously mentioned, it originally referred to picking unripe fruit and was later applied to any unpleasant act, as well as to inciting someone to sin. Since a human being, in their essence and fitrah (innate nature), has a connection with spirituality and purity, committing sins severs them from this divine connection.

[Does this verse refer to the Prophet of Islam or Nuh?]

  1. Some have suggested that the final verse is not about the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله, but rather concerns Nuh himself, since all these verses relate to him and the upcoming verses also speak of him. Therefore, it would be more appropriate for this verse to also relate to Nuh, and a parenthetical sentence would be contrary to the apparent flow.

However, considering that: firstly, a similar expression with almost the same wording appears in Surah Al-Ahqaf, verse 8, regarding the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله; secondly, what is mentioned about Nuh عليه السلام in these verses is all in the third person (absent) form, whereas the verse under discussion is in the second person (address) form (and the issue of "Iltifat"—transitioning from third to second person—is contrary to the apparent style); and if we were to consider the verse to be about Nuh, the phrase "Yaqulun" (they say), which is a present-tense verb, as well as "Qul" (say), which is an imperative verb, would all require implied context. Thirdly, in a Hadith narrated from Imam al-Baqir عليه السلام and Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام in Tafsir al-Burhan regarding this verse, it is stated that the above verse was revealed concerning the disbelievers of Mecca. From the sum of these reasons, it appears that the verse relates to the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله, the unjust accusations of the disbelievers, and the Prophet's response to them.

It is also necessary to mention that the meaning of a "parenthetical sentence" is not a statement unrelated to the main discourse. Rather, parenthetical sentences often contain content that emphasizes and supports the meaning of the speech. Since it temporarily breaks the flow of the speech, it relieves the listener from monotony and gives the discourse elegance, spirit, and freshness. Certainly, a parenthetical sentence can never be entirely foreign to the discourse; otherwise, it would contradict the principles of eloquence (Fasahah) and rhetoric (Balaghah), whereas parenthetical sentences are always seen in eloquent and rhetorical speech.

[Can one take responsibility for another's sin?]

  1. When studying the final verse, an objection might arise: what kind of logic is it for the Prophet or Nuh to say to the disbelievers, "If this speech is a fabrication, the sin is on us"? Does accepting responsibility for the sin of "fabrication" make their speech true and consistent with reality, such that people are obligated to obey and follow it?!

However, by looking closely at the preceding verses, we find the answer to this objection: they actually wanted to say that with these words of ours, which contain various types of rational arguments, even if—for the sake of argument—we were not from Allah, the sin would be ours, but the rational arguments themselves remain valid, and by opposing them, you will always be in sin—a continuous and lasting sin (note that "Tujrimun" is in the present/continuous tense, which usually denotes persistence).

Ayahs

وَأُوحِىَ إِلَىٰ نُوحٍ أَنَّهُۥ لَن يُؤْمِنَ مِن قَوْمِكَ إِلَّا مَن قَدْ ءَامَنَ فَلَا تَبْتَئِسْ بِمَا كَانُوا۟ يَفْعَلُونَ (٣٦)
It was revealed to Noah: ‘None of your people will believe except those who already have faith; so do not sorrow for what they used to do. (36)
وَٱصْنَعِ ٱلْفُلْكَ بِأَعْيُنِنَا وَوَحْيِنَا وَلَا تُخَـٰطِبْنِى فِى ٱلَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوٓا۟ ۚ إِنَّهُم مُّغْرَقُونَ (٣٧)
Build the ark before Our eyes and by Our revelation, and do not plead with Me for those who are wrongdoers: they shall indeed be drowned.’ (37)
وَيَصْنَعُ ٱلْفُلْكَ وَكُلَّمَا مَرَّ عَلَيْهِ مَلَأٌ مِّن قَوْمِهِۦ سَخِرُوا۟ مِنْهُ ۚ قَالَ إِن تَسْخَرُوا۟ مِنَّا فَإِنَّا نَسْخَرُ مِنكُمْ كَمَا تَسْخَرُونَ (٣٨)
As he was building the ark, whenever the elders of his people passed by him, they would ridicule him. He said, ‘If you ridicule us [today], we shall ridicule you [tomorrow] just as you ridicule us [now]. (38)
فَسَوْفَ تَعْلَمُونَ مَن يَأْتِيهِ عَذَابٌ يُخْزِيهِ وَيَحِلُّ عَلَيْهِ عَذَابٌ مُّقِيمٌ (٣٩)
Soon you will know whom a disgraceful punishment will overtake and on whom a lasting punishment will descend.’ (39)

Commentary

The Purification Begins

The story of Nuh (Noah), as presented in the verses of this Surah, is actually recounted in several segments, each corresponding to a period of Nuh’s عليه السلام struggles against the arrogant elite:

What preceded in the previous verses was the stage of continuous and persistent invitation and preaching by Nuh عليه السلام with utmost seriousness, utilizing all available means. During this stage, which lasted for many long years, only a small group believed in him—small in number, but great in quality and steadfastness.

The verses under discussion refer to the second stage of this struggle: the stage where the period of preaching ends and preparation for Divine purification begins! In the first verse, we read: "And it was revealed to Nuh that none of your people will believe except those who have already believed." (وَ أُوحِيَ إِلى‌ نُوحٍ أَنَّهُ لَنْ يُؤْمِنَ مِنْ قَوْمِكَ إِلَّا مَنْ قَدْ آمَنَ‌).

This indicates that the ranks have been completely separated, and further invitation toward faith and reform is no longer beneficial; he must prepare for the final purification and revolution.

At the end of the verse, Allah comforts Nuh, saying: "Now that this is the case, do not be distressed by what they used to do." (فَلا تَبْتَئِسْ بِما كانُوا يَفْعَلُونَ‌).

Furthermore, it is understood from this verse that Allah places portions of the knowledge of the Unseen (Ghayb) at the disposal of His Prophet whenever necessary, just as He informs Nuh here that no one else among them will believe in the future.

In any case, this rebellious and stubborn group must be punished—a punishment that cleanses the world from the filth of their existence and liberates the believers from their clutches forever. The command for their drowning has been issued, but everything requires means. Nuh must build a suitable Ark for the salvation of the true believers, so that the believers become more disciplined in their path during the time of its construction, and so that the argument is sufficiently completed against the disbelievers.

"We commanded Nuh to build the Ark, before Our eyes and according to Our command." (وَ اصْنَعِ الْفُلْكَ بِأَعْيُنِنا وَ وَحْيِنا). The phrase "before Our eyes" (بأعيننا) refers to the fact that all your efforts and endeavors in this regard are in Our presence; therefore, continue your work with a tranquil mind. Naturally, the feeling that Allah is present, watching, protecting, and observing grants a person both strength and power, as well as a greater sense of responsibility! From the word "Our revelation" (وحينا), it appears that Nuh also learned the method of building the Ark from Allah’s command. And it had to be so, for Nuh himself did not know the magnitude of the dimensions of the coming flood to build his Ark accordingly; it was Divine revelation that aided him in choosing the best specifications.

At the end of the verse, He warns Nuh that from now on, "do not plead with Me concerning those who have done wrong, for they are surely to be drowned." (وَ لا تُخاطِبْنِي فِي الَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا إِنَّهُمْ مُغْرَقُونَ‌). This sentence clearly shows that intercession (shafa'ah) is not possible for everyone; rather, it has conditions. If those conditions are not present in someone, even the Prophet of Allah does not have the right to intercede or request pardon for them (refer to Volume 1 of Tafsir Nemouneh).

But let us hear a few sentences about the people of Nuh: instead of taking Nuh’s invitation seriously for even a moment and considering at least the possibility that all this persistence and repeated invitations might stem from Divine revelation, and that the matter of the flood and certain punishment might be real, they continued their mockery and ridicule, as is the habit of all arrogant and conceited individuals: "And whenever a group of the chiefs of his people passed by him and saw him and his companions busy with the effort of preparing wood, nails, and shipbuilding tools, they mocked him, laughed, and passed by." (وَ يَصْنَعُ الْفُلْكَ وَ كُلَّما مَرَّ عَلَيْهِ مَلَأٌ مِنْ قَوْمِهِ سَخِرُوا مِنْهُ‌).

The "Mala'" (the self-satisfied elite) everywhere mock the oppressed and consider them lowly and contemptible beings because they lack gold and power. Not only do they mock them, but no matter how lofty their thoughts, how deep-rooted their school of thought, or how calculated their actions, in their view, they are worthy of disdain. For this reason, advice, warnings, and alarms do not affect them; only the lashes of painful Divine punishment must be struck upon their backs.

It is said that these groups of the elite among Nuh’s people each chose a type of mockery that was a source of laughter and amusement for them.

One would say: "O Nuh, it seems the claim of prophethood didn't work out, so in the end, you became a carpenter!..."

Another would say: "You are building a ship? Very well, build the sea for it too! Have you ever seen a sane person build a ship in the middle of dry land?!..."

Others perhaps said: "Oh, why do you want such a large ship? At least build a smaller one so that if you want to pull it toward the sea, it would be possible for you!" They would say this, laugh loudly, and pass by. This subject was the so-called "topic of discussion" in their homes and workplaces, and they would speak to one another about Nuh and the lack of intellect of his followers: "Look at the old man, what a state he has fallen into at the end of his life! Now we understand that if we did not believe in his words, we were right. He has no sound mind at all!!"

However, Nuh, with an extraordinary steadfastness born of faith, continued his work with great diligence. Indifferent to the baseless words of these self-satisfied, blind-hearted people, he progressed rapidly, and day by day the skeleton of the Ark became more complete and ready. Occasionally, he would only raise his head and say this short and meaningful sentence to them:

"If you mock us today, we shall surely mock you in the near future just as you mock!" (قالَ إِنْ تَسْخَرُوا مِنَّا فَإِنَّا نَسْخَرُ مِنْكُمْ كَما تَسْخَرُونَ‌):

On that day when you will be wandering in the midst of the storm, running frantically in every direction with no shelter, crying out from among the waves and pleading for us to save you—yes, on that day, the believers will laugh at your thoughts, your negligence, your ignorance, and your lack of awareness.

"On that day you will know who it is to whom a humiliating punishment will come, and upon whom an everlasting torment will descend." (فَسَوْفَ تَعْلَمُونَ مَنْ يَأْتِيهِ عَذابٌ يُخْزِيهِ وَ يَحِلُّ عَلَيْهِ عَذابٌ مُقِيمٌ‌). This indicates that although your harassment of us is a painful torment, firstly, we are proud and honored in enduring these hardships, and secondly, these are fleeting, whereas Divine punishment is both humiliating and endless, and the two are not comparable.

Key Points

1- Purification, Not Revenge

It is clearly understood from the above verses that Divine punishments do not have a vengeful aspect; rather, they occur for the sake of purifying the human race, the removal of those who are not worthy of life, and the survival of the righteous.

This means that an arrogant, corrupt, and mischief-making people, for whose faith there is no hope, have no right to life according to the system of creation and must be eliminated. The people of Nuh were such, for the above verses say: "Now that there is no longer hope for the faith of the rest, prepare to build the Ark, and do not make any intercession or request for pardon regarding the wrongdoers."

This same theme is seen in the supplication of this great Prophet mentioned in Surah Nuh: "My Lord, leave not upon the earth a single inhabitant from among the disbelievers. For if You leave them, they will mislead Your servants and will beget none but wicked disbelievers!" (رَبِّ لا تَذَرْ عَلَى الْأَرْضِ مِنَ الْكافِرِينَ دَيَّاراً إِنَّكَ إِنْ تَذَرْهُمْ يُضِلُّوا عِبادَكَ وَ لا يَلِدُوا إِلَّا فاجِراً كَفَّاراً). Fundamentally, in the organization of creation, every being is created for a purpose. When it completely deviates from its purpose and closes all paths of reform upon itself, its remaining is without reason and it must inevitably be removed.

As the poet says: Neither freshness, nor leaf, nor flower, nor fruit do I have; I wonder why the Farmer left me to remain!

2- Signs of the Arrogant

Self-centered arrogant people always treat serious matters that are not in line with their desires, whims, and interests as a game or a joke. For this reason, mocking truths—especially those related to the lives of the oppressed—forms a part of their lives. We have often seen that to add "color and flavor" to their sin-filled gatherings, they look for a poor person of faith to make him a so-called plaything and object of ridicule.

And if they do not have access to such individuals in their gatherings, they make one or more of them the subject of their talk in their absence, speaking, mocking, and laughing.

They consider themselves the "total intellect" and, imagining that their vast and forbidden wealth is a sign of their merit, personality, and value, they consider others meritless, worthless, and lacking in personality! But the Holy Quran directs its harshest attacks toward such conceited and arrogant individuals and specifically condemns their mockery.

For example, we read in Islamic history that when "Abu Aqil al-Ansari," that faithful and poor worker, stayed awake at night drawing water from the wells of Medina for houses and brought the few dates he received as wages as a contribution to the Muslim army for the Battle of Tabuk to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, a group of arrogant hypocrites laughed at him. The verses of the Quran were revealed and fell upon them like a thunderbolt: (الَّذِينَ يَلْمِزُونَ الْمُطَّوِّعِينَ مِنَ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ فِي الصَّدَقاتِ وَ الَّذِينَ لا يَجِدُونَ إِلَّا جُهْدَهُمْ فَيَسْخَرُونَ مِنْهُمْ سَخِرَ اللَّهُ مِنْهُمْ وَ لَهُمْ عَذابٌ أَلِيمٌ‌):

"Those who criticize the contributors among the believers concerning their charities and [criticize] those who find nothing except their effort, so they mock them—Allah will mock them, and for them is a painful punishment."

3- The Ark of Nuh

Undoubtedly, the Ark of Nuh was not a simple boat, and it was not completed easily or simply with the tools of that time. It was a massive vessel that, in addition to the true believers, accommodated a pair of every species of animal and carried abundant provisions for the long-term life of the humans and animals housed within it. Such a ship with such capacity was certainly unprecedented in those days, especially since this ship had to pass safely through a sea as vast as this world with mountain-like waves without being destroyed. Therefore, we read in some narrations of the commentators that this ship was one thousand two hundred cubits long and six hundred cubits wide! (Each cubit is about half a meter).

In some Islamic narrations, it is mentioned that forty years before the appearance of the flood, a type of illness afflicted the women of Nuh’s people such that no more children were born to them, and this was in fact a prelude to their punishment and torment.

Ayahs

حَتَّىٰٓ إِذَا جَآءَ أَمْرُنَا وَفَارَ ٱلتَّنُّورُ قُلْنَا ٱحْمِلْ فِيهَا مِن كُلٍّ زَوْجَيْنِ ٱثْنَيْنِ وَأَهْلَكَ إِلَّا مَن سَبَقَ عَلَيْهِ ٱلْقَوْلُ وَمَنْ ءَامَنَ ۚ وَمَآ ءَامَنَ مَعَهُۥٓ إِلَّا قَلِيلٌ (٤٠)
When Our edict came and the oven gushed [a stream of water], We said, ‘Carry in it a pair of every kind [of animal], along with your family—except those [of them] against whom the edict has already been given—and those who have faith.’ And none believed with him except a few. (40)
۞ وَقَالَ ٱرْكَبُوا۟ فِيهَا بِسْمِ ٱللَّهِ مَجْر۪ىٰهَا وَمُرْسَىٰهَآ ۚ إِنَّ رَبِّى لَغَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ (٤١)
He said, ‘Board it: In the Name of Allah it shall set sail and cast anchor. Indeed my Lord is all-forgiving, all-merciful.’ (41)
وَهِىَ تَجْرِى بِهِمْ فِى مَوْجٍ كَٱلْجِبَالِ وَنَادَىٰ نُوحٌ ٱبْنَهُۥ وَكَانَ فِى مَعْزِلٍ يَـٰبُنَىَّ ٱرْكَب مَّعَنَا وَلَا تَكُن مَّعَ ٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ (٤٢)
And it sailed along with them amid waves [rising] like mountains. Noah called out to his son, who stood aloof, ‘O my son! ‘Board with us, and do not be with the faithless!’ (42)
قَالَ سَـَٔاوِىٓ إِلَىٰ جَبَلٍ يَعْصِمُنِى مِنَ ٱلْمَآءِ ۚ قَالَ لَا عَاصِمَ ٱلْيَوْمَ مِنْ أَمْرِ ٱللَّهِ إِلَّا مَن رَّحِمَ ۚ وَحَالَ بَيْنَهُمَا ٱلْمَوْجُ فَكَانَ مِنَ ٱلْمُغْرَقِينَ (٤٣)
He said, ‘I shall take refuge on a mountain; it will protect me from the flood.’ He said, ‘There is none today who can protect from Allah’s edict, except someone upon whom He has mercy.’ Then the waves came between them, and he was among those who were drowned. (43)

Commentary

The Storm Begins

In the preceding verses, we saw how Noah and the true believers set about building the Ark of Salvation, enduring all the hardships and the ridicule of the arrogant, faithless majority, and prepared themselves for the Storm—the very storm that was to cleanse the surface of the earth from the filth of the faithless oppressors.

The verses under discussion vividly describe the third stage of this narrative: how the punishment descended upon this tyrannical people.

First, it states: "This situation continued until Our command was issued and the signs of punishment appeared, and water gushed forth from the oven (Tannur)!" (حَتَّى إِذا جاءَ أَمْرُنا وَ فارَ التَّنُّورُ).

"Tannur" (with a shaddah on the nun) carries the same meaning as "Tannur" in modern Persian: a place where bread is baked.

Commentators have discussed at length the significance of water gushing from an oven in relation to the approaching storm.

Some have said that the gushing of water from inside the oven was a divine sign for Noah, so that he would become aware of the unfolding event and board the ship with his companions and the necessary provisions.

Others have suggested that "Tannur" is used here metaphorically or idiomatically, referring to the "oven" of God's wrath and anger erupting and flaming up, signifying the approach of the crushing divine punishment. This expression exists in both Persian and Arabic, where intense anger is likened to the eruption of fire.

However, it seems more likely that "Tannur" is used in its literal and well-known sense, and it does not refer to a specific oven, but rather to highlight this point: when water began to gush from inside an oven—which is typically the center of fire—Noah and his companions realized that conditions were about to change and a revolution and transformation were imminent. "Where there is water, there is no fire!" In other words, when they saw the groundwater level rise so high that it gushed from inside an oven—usually built in a dry and protected spot—they understood that a momentous event was ahead and a novel phenomenon was about to take shape. This very event was a warning and a signal for Noah and his companions to rise and get ready! Perhaps the heedless and unaware people also saw water gushing from the ovens in their homes, but as always, they passed by such meaningful divine warnings with closed eyes and ears, not allowing themselves even a moment of reflection that perhaps an event was unfolding, or that Noah's warnings might be reality.

At this moment, We commanded Noah: "Load onto the ship a pair (male and female) of every species of animal," so that their lineage would not be severed in the deluge (قُلْنَا احْمِلْ فِيها مِنْ كُلٍّ زَوْجَيْنِ اثْنَيْنِ).

"And also load your family—except those against whom the word [of destruction] has already gone forth—and the believers" (وَ أَهْلَكَ إِلَّا مَنْ سَبَقَ عَلَيْهِ الْقَوْلُ وَ مَنْ آمَنَ).

"But none believed with him except a few" (وَ ما آمَنَ مَعَهُ إِلَّا قَلِيلٌ).

This verse, on one hand, refers to Noah's faithless wife and his son "Canaan"—whose story appears in the following verses—who, due to deviating from the path of faith and cooperating with the sinners, severed their bond with Noah and had no right to board that Ark of Salvation, because the primary condition for boarding was "faith."

On the other hand, it points out that the product of Noah's (عليه السلام) many long years of persistent effort in preaching his religion was nothing but a small group of believers. According to some narrations, only eighty people believed in him during this long period, and some have recorded an even smaller number. This itself demonstrates the extent of this great Prophet's steadfastness and perseverance, as he labored an average of ten years to guide each one of them toward God! A labor that ordinary people would not endure even for the guidance and salvation of their own children.

Noah quickly gathered his relatives and faithful companions, and as the moment of the storm and the arrival of the crushing divine punishments drew near, "he commanded them: 'Embark in it in the name of Allah; mention the name of Allah at the time of the ship's movement and its anchorage, and be mindful of Him'" (بِسْمِ اللَّهِ مَجْراها وَ مُرْساها) 1. Why does he say: be mindful of Him in all states and seek help from His name and remembrance?

"Because my Lord is indeed Forgiving and Merciful" (إِنَّ رَبِّي لَغَفُورٌ رَحِيمٌ). By His mercy, He has placed this means of salvation at the disposal of you, the faithful servants, and by His forgiveness, He overlooks your lapses.

Finally, the ultimate moment arrived and the command for the punishment of this rebellious people was issued. Dark and gloomy clouds, like pieces of a pitch-black night, covered the entire sky, becoming so densely packed that the likes of them had never been seen. The roar of thunder and the blinding flash of lightning repeatedly filled the atmosphere, heralding a truly massive and terrifying event.

The rain began; it grew faster and faster, the drops larger and larger, and as the Quran says in verse 11 of Surah Al-Qamar, it was as if all the gates of heaven were opened and an ocean of water was pouring down from between the clouds. On the other hand, the groundwater level rose so high that gushing springs erupted from every corner.

In this way, the waters of the earth and the sky joined together, joining hands and flowing over the lands, mountains, plains, and valleys, and soon the surface of the earth became like an ocean. The blowing winds drew mountain-like waves upon this ocean, and these waves climbed over each other's shoulders and rolled upon one another.

"And Noah's Ark, with its passengers, cleaved through the mountain-like waves and continued forward" (وَ هِيَ تَجْرِي بِهِمْ فِي مَوْجٍ كَالْجِبالِ).

"Noah addressed his son, who stood apart from his father, and cried out: 'O my son! Embark with us and be not with the disbelievers,' for annihilation and destruction will overtake you" (وَ نادى نُوحٌ ابْنَهُ وَ كانَ فِي مَعْزِلٍ يا بُنَيَّ ارْكَبْ مَعَنا وَ لا تَكُنْ مَعَ الْكافِرِينَ). Noah, this great Prophet, not only as a father but as a tireless and hopeful educator, did not abandon his duty even at the last moment, hoping that his words might affect his son's hardened heart.

But unfortunately, the influence of bad company was greater than the desired effect of this compassionate father's words.

Therefore, this stubborn and short-sighted son, thinking that one could struggle against God's wrath, "cried out: 'Do not worry about me, father; I will soon take refuge on a mountain that will protect me from the water'" (قالَ سَآوِي إِلى جَبَلٍ يَعْصِمُنِي مِنَ الْماءِ).

Noah did not despair yet; he once again turned to advice and counsel, hoping the short-sighted son would dismount from the steed of pride and obstinacy and take the path of truth. He said to him:

"My son, today there is no protector from the decree of Allah" (قالَ لا عاصِمَ الْيَوْمَ مِنْ أَمْرِ اللَّهِ).

"Salvation is only for the one who is covered by God's mercy, and that is all" (إِلَّا مَنْ رَحِمَ).

Let alone a mountain, let alone the planet Earth—the Sun and the entire solar system with all their staggering grandeur are nothing more than an insignificant atom before His eternal power.

Are not the highest mountains compared to the Earth like the tiny bumps on the surface of an orange? The same Earth that must be enlarged one million two hundred thousand times to equal the size of the Sun—the same Sun which is but a medium, ordinary star among millions of millions of stars in the expanse of creation.

So what a vain fantasy and what a short-sighted thought that a "mountain" could accomplish anything! At that very moment, a wave rose and came forward, and further forward, and plucked Noah's son like a piece of straw, crushing him within itself, "and it came between the father and the son, placing him among the drowned!" (وَ حالَ بَيْنَهُمَا الْمَوْجُ فَكانَ مِنَ الْمُغْرَقِينَ)

Key Points

1- Was Noah's (عليه السلام) Storm Universal?

From the literal appearance of many Quranic verses, it seems that Noah's (عليه السلام) storm was not regional, but rather an event for the entire surface of the earth. This is because the word Ard (earth) is mentioned absolutely, such as: (رَبِّ لا تَذَرْ عَلَى الْأَرْضِ مِنَ الْكافِرِينَ دَيَّاراً): "O Lord, do not leave upon the earth a single inhabitant from among the disbelievers [who have no hope of reform]" 1. Also, verse 44 of this same Surah Hud (the upcoming verse): (وَ قِيلَ يا أَرْضُ ابْلَعِي ماءَكِ): "O earth, swallow your waters..." From many histories as well, the universality of Noah's storm is inferred; for this reason, all current races are traced back to one of the three sons of Noah (Ham, Shem, and Japheth) who remained after Noah.

In natural history, too, a period called the "Pluvial Period" (era of torrential rains) is observed, which, if not necessarily attributed to the time before the birth of living beings, can be applied to Noah's storm.

There is also a theory in the natural history of the Earth that the Earth's axis gradually changes—meaning the North and South Poles turn into the Equator, and the Equator takes the place of the North and South Poles. It is clear that during the melting of the extremely dense polar ice, the sea levels rise to such an extent that they cover much of the land, and by penetrating the Earth's layers, they emerge as gushing springs from other points. This expansion of waters causes an increase in clouds and heavier rainfall.

The fact that Noah took specimens of the animals of the earth with him also supports the universality of the storm. If we consider Noah's place of residence to be Kufa—as mentioned in some narrations—and if, according to other narrations, the scope of the storm extended to Mecca and the House of the Kaaba, this would be another piece of evidence for the universality of this storm.

Nevertheless, the possibility of it being regional is not entirely ruled out, as the application of the word "Ard" to a vast region of the world occurs frequently in the Quran. For instance, in the story of the Children of Israel, we read: (وَ أَوْرَثْنَا الْقَوْمَ الَّذِينَ كانُوا يُسْتَضْعَفُونَ مَشارِقَ الْأَرْضِ وَ مَغارِبَهَا): "And We made the people who were deemed weak inherit the eastern and western parts of the land" [2]. Carrying animals in the Ark might also have been to ensure that the animal species in that part of the earth would not become extinct, especially since transporting animals from distant points was not an easy task in those days (reflect on this).

Furthermore, the other clues mentioned above can also be applied to the storm being regional.

It is also noteworthy that Noah's storm was a punishment for that rebellious people, and we have no evidence that Noah's call reached the entire earth. In principle, with the means of that time, it seems very unlikely for a Prophet's call (in his own era) to reach all points.

In any case, the Quran's goal in recounting this exemplary story is to convey the important educational points hidden within it, whether it was universal or regional.

2- Is Repentance Possible After the Descent of Punishment?

From the preceding verses, it is understood that Noah continued to preach to his son even after the storm began. This indicates that if he had believed, his faith would have been accepted. Here, the question arises: considering other Quranic verses—examples of which we have seen before—the doors of repentance are closed after the descent of punishment, because at this time, most rebellious sinners who see the punishment with their own eyes repent involuntarily and out of necessity—a worthless and hollow repentance! However, by looking closely at the above verses, the answer to this question can be found: the start of the storm and its beginning were not a clear sign of [divine] punishment, but rather appeared to be a heavy and unprecedented rain. For this reason, Noah's son told his father, "I will take refuge on a mountain to be saved from the deluge," thinking that the rain and storm were natural. At such a time, the doors of repentance being open is not a strange matter!

Another question that may arise regarding Noah's son is why he addressed only his son at this critical moment, and not all the people?

This might be because he had fulfilled his duty of public invitation even toward his son, but regarding his son, he had a heavier duty—the duty of fatherhood in addition to "prophethood." For this reason, he emphasized his son more at the last moment to fulfill this duty.

Another possibility, according to some commentators, is that Noah's son at that time was neither in the ranks of the disbelievers nor in the ranks of the believers. They consider the phrase (وَ كانَ فِي مَعْزِلٍ) ("he was in a place of isolation") as evidence for this. Although he deserved punishment for not being in the ranks of the believers, his isolation from the ranks of the disbelievers necessitated that he be treated with more affection and kindness through preaching. Additionally, this separation from the ranks of the disbelievers had given Noah the thought that perhaps he had regretted his actions.

There is also the possibility, considering future verses, that Noah's son did not explicitly oppose his father but acted as a "hypocrite," sometimes expressing agreement in his presence. For this reason, Noah prayed to God for his salvation.

In any case, the above verse does not contradict other Quranic verses which state that the doors of repentance are closed at the time of the descent of punishment.

3- Educational Lessons in Noah's Storm
