As we know, the primary goal of the Quran in recounting the stories of the predecessors is to present lessons of warning and instructive, educational points. In this very section of Noah's story that we have read so far, very important points are hidden, some of which are mentioned below:

A: Cleansing the Earth

It is true that God is "Merciful" and kind, but it must not be forgotten that He is also "Wise." By the requirement of His wisdom, whenever a people and nation become corrupt and the call of divine advisors and educators has no effect on them, they have no right to life. Eventually, through social revolutions or natural revolutions, the structure of their lives is crushed and destroyed.

This was not limited to the people of Noah, nor to a specific time or period; it is a divine sunnah (law) in all ages and centuries, for all peoples and nations, and even in our own era! Perhaps the First and Second World Wars were forms of this cleansing.

B: Why Punishment by Storm?

It is true that a corrupt people and nation must be destroyed, and the means of their destruction does not matter. However, a close look at the Quranic verses shows that there was and is a correspondence between the nature of the punishments and the sins of the peoples (reflect on this).

Pharaoh based his power on the "Great Nile" and its blessed waters, and interestingly, his destruction was also by the same! Nimrod relied on his massive army, and as we know, a small army of insects defeated him and his companions! The people of Noah were an agricultural and pastoral population, and such a population considers everything they have to come from the life-giving grains of rain; yet, in the end, this very rain destroyed them.

From this, it becomes clearly evident how calculated the divine programs are. If we see the rebellious humans of our era crushed in the First and Second World Wars by their most modern weapons, it should not surprise us, for it was these very advanced industries that were their mainstay in the colonization and exploitation of the oppressed peoples of the world!

C: The Name of God in Every State and Everywhere

In the verses above, we read that Noah commanded his companions not to forget the name of God at the time of the ship's movement and its anchorage. Everything must be in His name, in His remembrance, and by seeking help from His Pure Essence. Every movement, every stop, in times of calm and in times of storm—all must begin with His name, for any work started without His name will be "abtar" (severed/incomplete).

As narrated in a famous Hadith from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله): "Every important matter that is not mentioned with 'Bismillah' (In the name of Allah) will be unfinished" 1. Mentioning the name of God is not a mere formality, but rather a motive and a goal. That is, any work that does not have a divine motive and whose goal is not God is "abtar," because material motives come to an end, but divine motives are inexhaustible. Material goals, once they reach their peak, are extinguished, but divine goals, like His Pure Essence, will be eternal.

D: Flimsy Refuges

Usually, everyone takes refuge in something during life's difficulties: some in their wealth, some in their position and rank, some in their physical strength, and some in their intellectual power. But as the above verses tell us, and as history has shown, none of these have the slightest resistance against the command of the Lord, and like spider webs placed in the path of a storm, they quickly fall apart.

The ignorant and stubborn son of the Prophet Noah (عليه السلام) was also in this error; he thought a mountain could provide him refuge against the storm of God's wrath. But what a great mistake! The movement of a single wave finished him and sent him to the land of non-existence.

For this reason, we read in some supplications: "I flee from Your wrath toward You" (هارب منك اليك) [2]. That is, if there is a refuge against the storm of Your wrath, it is again Your Pure Essence and returning to You, not anything else.

E: The Ark of Salvation

Escape from any storm is impossible without an Ark of Salvation. It is not necessary for this Ark to be made of wood and iron; rather, this Ark of Salvation is often a constructive, life-giving, and positive school of thought that resists the waves of storms of deviant ideas and brings its followers to the shore of salvation.

On this basis, in narrations from the Holy Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) found in both Shiite and Sunni books, his family—namely the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt and the carriers of the Islamic school—have been introduced as the "Ark of Salvation."

"Hanash ibn Mughirah" says: I came with Abu Dharr to the side of the Kaaba. He held the ring of the door of the House and called out: "It is I, Abu Dharr al-Ghifari; whoever does not know me, let him know me. I am that same Jundub (Abu Dharr's original name was Jundub). I am a companion of the Prophet; I heard with my own ears that he said: 'The likeness of my Ahl al-Bayt is like the Ark of Noah; whoever boards it is saved'" 1. In some other chains of the Hadith, the phrase: "And whoever stays behind from it is drowned" [2]—or—"Whoever stays behind from it perishes" [3] has been added.

This Hadith of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) explicitly states that when intellectual, ideological, and social storms occur in Islamic society, the only way to salvation is to take refuge in this school. We have well tested this matter in the glorious revolution of the Iranian nation, where followers of non-Islamic schools were defeated by the Taghut (tyrant), except for that group who took refuge in the school of Islam, the Ahl al-Bayt, and their revolutionary programs.

1: Surah Noah, verse 26. [2]: Al-A'raf, 137. 1: Safinat al-Bihar, Vol. 1, p. 663. [2]: Supplication of Abu Hamza al-Thumali. 1: Ibn Qutaybah al-Dinawari, a famous Sunni scholar, recorded this Hadith in Uyun al-Akhbar, Vol. 1, p. 211. [2]: Mu'jam al-Kabir by Hafiz al-Tabarani, p. 130 (manuscript). [3]: Ibid. A group of other Sunni luminaries such as Hakim al-Nishapuri in al-Mustadrak, Ibn al-Maghazili in Manaqib Amir al-Mu'minin, Allamah al-Khwarizmi in Maqtal al-Husayn, and Hamuwayni in Fara'id al-Simtayn, as well as many others, have recorded it in their books (for more detail, see Ihqaq al-Haq, Vol. 9, p. 280, new edition).

Ayah

وَقِيلَ يَـٰٓأَرْضُ ٱبْلَعِى مَآءَكِ وَيَـٰسَمَآءُ أَقْلِعِى وَغِيضَ ٱلْمَآءُ وَقُضِىَ ٱلْأَمْرُ وَٱسْتَوَتْ عَلَى ٱلْجُودِىِّ ۖ وَقِيلَ بُعْدًا لِّلْقَوْمِ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ (٤٤)
Then it was said, ‘O earth, swallow your water! O sky, leave off!’ The waters receded; the edict was carried out, and it settled on [Mount] Judi. Then it was said, ‘Away with the wrongdoing lot!’ (44)

Commentary

The End of an Event

As we read in the previous verses in a brief and concise manner, eventually the roaring waves of water encompassed everywhere. The water rose higher and higher; the heedless sinners, imagining it to be an ordinary storm, sought refuge in high points, elevations, and the mountains of the earth. However, the water surpassed even those, and everywhere was hidden beneath the flood. The lifeless bodies of the transgressors and the remains of their houses and life's belongings could be seen amidst the foam upon the water! Noah had entrusted the helm of the Ark to the hand of God; the waves carried the Ark in every direction. It is mentioned in narrations that for six full months (from the beginning of the month of Rajab until the end of the month of Dhu al-Hijjah, and according to one narration, from the tenth of Rajab until the day of Ashura), this Ark was wandering [1] and traversed various locations, and even according to some narrations, it traveled through the land of Makkah and around the House of the Kaaba.

Finally, the command for the end of the punishment and the return of the earth to its normal state was issued.

The above verse describes the nature of this command, its details, and its result in very short and concise phrases, yet exceptionally eloquent and beautiful, within six sentences. It says: "The command was given to the earth: O earth! Swallow your water!" (وَ قِيلَ يا أَرْضُ ابْلَعِي ماءَكِ).

"And the command was given to the sky: O sky! Withhold [your rain]" (وَ يا سَماءُ أَقْلِعِي). "And the water subsided" (وَ غِيضَ الْماءُ).

"And the matter was concluded" (وَ قُضِيَ الْأَمْرُ).

"And the Ark came to rest on the side of Mount Judi" (وَ اسْتَوَتْ عَلَى الْجُودِيِ). "At this moment it was said: Away with the wrongdoing people!" (وَ قِيلَ بُعْداً لِلْقَوْمِ الظَّالِمِينَ).

The expressions in the above verse are so eloquent and pleasing, and despite their brevity, so expressive and vivid, and with all their beauty, so shaking and crushing that, according to a group of Arabic scholars, this verse is considered the "most eloquent and most articulate" of the Quranic verses, even though all verses of the Quran are at the peak of miracle in terms of eloquence and articulation.

A vivid witness to this statement is what we read in Islamic narrations and histories: a group of the pagans of Quraysh rose to challenge the Quran and decided to innovate verses like those of the Quran. Their supporters provided them with the best food and drinks they desired for forty days—pure wheat marrow, lamb meat, and aged wine!—so they could comfortably compose sentences similar to the Quran.

However, when they reached the above verse, it shook them so much that they looked at one another and said: "This is a speech that no words resemble, and fundamentally it bears no similarity to the speech of creatures." They said this, abandoned their decision, and dispersed in despair [1].

Where is "Judi"?

Many commentators have said that Judi, which is introduced in the above verse as the place where Noah's Ark rested, is a famous mountain near Mosul [2]. Other commentators have considered it a mountain in the regions of Sham (Levant), or near "Amid," or in northern Iraq.

In the book Mufradat by Raghib, it is described as a mountain between Mosul and Al-Jazira (the name of a region in northern Iraq, which is different from modern-day Algeria or the well-known Al-Jazeera). It is not unlikely that all of these refer to the same meaning, as "Mosul," "Amid," and "Jazira" are all part of the northern regions of Iraq and near Sham.

Some other commentators have suggested the possibility that "Judi" refers to any mountain or firm ground; meaning Noah's Ark rested on a firm ground that was ready for its passengers to disembark. However, the first meaning is the most famous and well-known.

In the book A'lam al-Quran, research has been conducted regarding Mount Judi, which we present below:

"Judi is the name of the mountain upon which Noah's Ark settled, and its name is mentioned in Surah Hud, verse 44, which is close in content to the contents of the Torah...

Regarding the location of Mount Judi, three opinions have been expressed:

  1. According to the opinion of Isfahani, Mount Judi is in Arabia and is one of the two mountains located in the territory of the 'Tayy' tribe.

  2. Mount Judi is the Cardin range located northeast of Jazira ibn Umar, east of the Tigris, near Mosul. The Kurds call it 'Kardu' in their dialect, the Greeks call it 'Gordy,' and the Arabs call it 'Judi.'

In the Targum, which is the Chaldean translation of the Torah, and also in the Syriac translation of the Torah, the place where Noah's Ark settled is identified as the mountain fortress of the Kurds, 'Cardin.'

Arab geographers have also identified the Judi mentioned in the Quran with this mountain and have said that the planks of Noah's Ark remained on the peak of this mountain until the time of the Abbasids, and the polytheists used to visit it.

In Babylonian stories, there is a story similar to the story of Noah's flood; additionally, it can be assumed that the Tigris flooded and the people of those regions were caught in a storm.

On Mount Judi, there are Assyrian inscriptions known as the 'Misir' inscriptions, and in these inscriptions, the name 'Urartu' is seen.

  1. In the current translation of the Torah, the place where Noah's Ark settled is designated as the mountains of Ararat, which is Mount Masis located in Armenia.

The author of the Bible Dictionary recorded the initial meaning as 'cursed' and said:

According to narrations, Noah's Ark settled atop this mountain, and the Arabs call it 'Judi,' the Persians call it 'Kuh-e Nuh' (Mountain of Noah), and the Turks call it 'Kurdagh,' meaning 'steep mountain,' and it is located near the Aras River.

Until the fifth century, the Armenians in Armenia did not know a mountain by the name of Judi, and from that century, perhaps due to the mistake of the translators of the Torah who translated the 'Mountain of the Kurds' as Mount Ararat, such a notion arose for Armenian scholars.

Perhaps the justification for this notion was that the Assyrians gave the name Ararat or Urartu to the mountains north and south of Lake Van.

It is said that Prophet Noah built a mosque atop Mount Judi after the flood subsided, and the Armenians also say that at the foot of Mount Judi, the village of 'Thamanin' or 'Thaman' (Eighty) was the first place where Noah's companions descended [1]."

[1]: Tafsir al-Qurtubi, Vol. 5, p. 3269; Abu al-Futuh al-Razi, Vol. 6, p. 278; Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 5, p. 164; and Tabari, Vol. 12, p. 29. [1]: Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 5, p. 165; Ruh al-Ma'ani, Vol. 12, p. 57. [2]: Majma' al-Bayan, Ruh al-Ma'ani, and Qurtubi under the aforementioned verse. [1]: A'lam al-Quran, Khaza'eli, p. 281.

Ayahs

وَنَادَىٰ نُوحٌ رَّبَّهُۥ فَقَالَ رَبِّ إِنَّ ٱبْنِى مِنْ أَهْلِى وَإِنَّ وَعْدَكَ ٱلْحَقُّ وَأَنتَ أَحْكَمُ ٱلْحَـٰكِمِينَ (٤٥)
Noah called out to his Lord, and said, ‘My Lord! My son is indeed from my family. Your promise is indeed true, and You are the fairest of all judges.’ (45)
قَالَ يَـٰنُوحُ إِنَّهُۥ لَيْسَ مِنْ أَهْلِكَ ۖ إِنَّهُۥ عَمَلٌ غَيْرُ صَـٰلِحٍ ۖ فَلَا تَسْـَٔلْنِ مَا لَيْسَ لَكَ بِهِۦ عِلْمٌ ۖ إِنِّىٓ أَعِظُكَ أَن تَكُونَ مِنَ ٱلْجَـٰهِلِينَ (٤٦)
Said He, ‘O Noah! Indeed, He is not of your family. Indeed, he is [personification of] unrighteous conduct. So do not ask Me [something] of which you have no knowledge. I advise you lest you should be among the ignorant.’ (46)
قَالَ رَبِّ إِنِّىٓ أَعُوذُ بِكَ أَنْ أَسْـَٔلَكَ مَا لَيْسَ لِى بِهِۦ عِلْمٌ ۖ وَإِلَّا تَغْفِرْ لِى وَتَرْحَمْنِىٓ أَكُن مِّنَ ٱلْخَـٰسِرِينَ (٤٧)
He said, ‘My Lord! I seek Your protection lest I should ask You something of which I have no knowledge. If You do not forgive me and have mercy upon me I shall be among the losers.’ (47)

Commentary

The Painful Fate of Noah’s Son

In the previous verses, we read that Noah’s son did not heed his father’s advice and counsel, and until his final breath, he did not cease his stubbornness and defiance. Ultimately, he was caught amidst the waves of the storm and drowned.

The verses under discussion describe another part of this event: when Noah saw his son among the waves, paternal affection surged within him. Remembering God’s promise regarding the salvation of his family, he turned to the threshold of Allah and said:

"O my Lord! Verily, my son is of my family! And Your promise is true, and You are the Most Just of all judges, and the most steadfast in fulfilling a covenant" (وَنَادَىٰ نُوحٌ رَّبَّهُ فَقَالَ رَبِّ إِنَّ ابْنِي مِنْ أَهْلِي وَإِنَّ وَعْدَكَ الْحَقُّ وَأَنتَ أَحْكَمُ الْحَاكِمِينَ).

This promise refers to what was mentioned in verse 40 of this same Surah, where He says: قُلْنَا احْمِلْ فِيهَا مِن كُلٍّ زَوْجَيْنِ اثْنَيْنِ وَأَهْلَكَ إِلَّا مَن سَبَقَ عَلَيْهِ الْقَوْلُ—"We commanded Noah: 'Load therein two of every kind, and your family, except him against whom the Word has already gone forth.'"

Noah thought that the phrase "except him against whom the Word has already gone forth" referred only to his faithless and polytheistic wife, and that his son Canaan was not among them; therefore, he presented such a plea before Allah.

However, he immediately received a response—a jolting and enlightening answer regarding a great reality; a reality that places ideological bonds above lineage and kinship.

"O Noah! He is not of your family!" (قَالَ يَا نُوحُ إِنَّهُ لَيْسَ مِنْ أَهْلِكَ).

"Rather, he is [an embodiment of] unrighteous conduct" (إِنَّهُ عَمَلٌ غَيْرُ صَالِحٍ).

He is an unworthy individual whose family bond is considered naught due to the severance of his ideological bond with you.

"Since it is so, do not ask of Me that of which you have no knowledge" (فَلَا تَسْأَلْنِ مَا لَيْسَ لَكَ بِهِ عِلْمٌ).

"I admonish you, lest you be among the ignorant" (إِنِّي أَعِظُكَ أَن تَكُونَ مِنَ الْجَاهِلِينَ).

Noah realized that this request from the presence of the Lord was not correct, and he should never have considered the salvation of such a son to be included in the divine promise for the salvation of his family. Thus, he turned to the threshold of the Lord and said: "O my Lord! I seek refuge in You from asking You that of which I have no knowledge" (قَالَ رَبِّ إِنِّي أَعُوذُ بِكَ أَنْ أَسْأَلَكَ مَا لَيْسَ لِي بِهِ عِلْمٌ).

"And unless You forgive me and have mercy on me, I shall indeed be among the losers" (وَإِلَّا تَغْفِرْ لِي وَتَرْحَمْنِي أَكُن مِّنَ الْخَاسِرِينَ).

Key Points

1. Why was Noah’s son "unrighteous conduct"?

Some commentators believe that a word is implied in this verse, and its original meaning is: Innahu dhu 'amalin ghayri salih—"Your son is a possessor of unrighteous conduct."

However, considering that sometimes a human being progresses so far in performing an action that they seemingly become the very essence of that action, this expression is frequently seen in the literature of various languages for the sake of emphasis. For example, it is said: "So-and-so is justice and generosity from head to toe," or "So-and-so is theft and corruption personified," as if they have become so immersed in that act that their essence has become identical to it.

This son of a Prophet also sat with the wicked for so long and became so immersed in their evil deeds and incorrect thoughts that it was as if his very existence transformed into an "unrighteous conduct."

Therefore, while the above expression is very brief and concise, it speaks of an important reality regarding Noah’s son. That is: O Noah, if the wrongness, injustice, and corruption in this son’s existence were superficial, there would be a possibility of intercession for him. But now that he is drowned from head to toe in corruption and ruin, there is no place for intercession; do not speak of it at all!

The possibility suggested by some commentators that this son was not truly his biological son (either an illegitimate child or his wife’s legitimate child from another husband) does not seem correct. This is because the phrase "إِنَّهُ عَمَلٌ غَيْرُ صَالِحٍ" (He is unrighteous conduct) actually serves as the reason for "إِنَّهُ لَيْسَ مِنْ أَهْلِكَ" (He is not of your family). That is: "The reason I say he is not of your family is that he has distanced himself from you in terms of action and conduct, even though his lineage is linked to you."

2. How was Noah unaware that his son was not included in the divine promise?

Given Noah’s words in the above verses and the response Allah gave him, the question arises: how was Noah not mindful of the fact that his son Canaan was not included in the divine promise?

The answer to this question can be given as follows: this son—as previously mentioned—did not have a clearly defined status; sometimes he was with the believers and sometimes with the disbelievers, and his hypocritical demeanor apparently misled everyone.

Furthermore, the intense sense of responsibility Noah felt toward his son, and the natural love and affection every father has for his child—from which Prophets are not exempt—caused him to make such a request of Allah.

However, as soon as he became aware of the reality of the matter, he immediately sought to apologize to Allah and ask for forgiveness. Although no sin had been committed by him, the status and position of a Prophet require him to be even more watchful of his words and behavior. This "leaving the better option" (tark al-awla) was significant for a personality like him, and for this reason, he requested forgiveness from the presence of Allah.

From this, the answer to another question becomes clear: do Prophets commit sins that they should ask for forgiveness? [The answer is that their "repentance" is often for tark al-awla or from a sense of humility before the Divine].

3. Where bonds are severed

The above verses reflect another of the most sublime human and educational lessons within the narrative of Noah—a lesson that has absolutely no meaning in materialistic schools of thought but is a fundamental principle in a divine and spiritual school.

Material bonds (lineage, kinship, friendship, and companionship) in heavenly schools are always overshadowed by spiritual bonds.

In this school, being a "favorite" or having the privilege of kinship has no meaning in the face of ideological and spiritual bonds.

Where an ideological connection exists, Salman al-Farsi—who was far-removed, belonging neither to the Prophet’s (صلى الله عليه وآله) family, nor the Quraysh, nor even the people of Mecca, and was not even of the Arab race—is considered part of the Prophet’s family according to the famous Hadith: "Salman is from us, the Ahl al-Bayt" (Salman minna Ahl al-Bayt). Conversely, the actual and immediate son of a Prophet like Noah is so rejected due to the severance of his ideological bond with his father that he is met with: "He is not of your family" (إِنَّهُ لَيْسَ مِنْ أَهْلِكَ).

Such an important matter may be difficult to accept for those who think materialistically, but it is a reality found in all heavenly religions.

For this reason, in the Hadiths of the Ahl al-Bayt (عليه السلام) regarding those "Shias" who only carry the name of Shiism but no significant trace of the teachings and practical programs of the Ahl al-Bayt (عليه السلام) is seen in their lives, we read explicit and jolting sentences that express the same approach taken by the Quran in the verses above.

It is narrated from Imam Ali ibn Musa al-Rida (عليه السلام) that one day he asked his companions: "How do the people interpret this verse: 'إِنَّهُ عَمَلٌ غَيْرُ صَالِحٍ'?" One of those present said: "Some believe it means that Noah’s son (Canaan) was not his biological son." The Imam (عليه السلام) said:

"No, it is not so; he was truly his son, but when he committed sin and stepped out of the path of obedience to Allah’s command, Allah negated his sonship. Likewise, those who are from us but do not obey Allah are not from us." [1]

4. The Rejected Muslims!

It is not inappropriate, inspired by the above verse, to refer to a portion of Islamic Hadiths that also consider many groups—who are apparently among the Muslims or followers of the school of the Ahl al-Bayt—as rejected, removing them from the ranks of the believers and the Shias:

  1. The Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله) says: "Whoever cheats a Muslim is not of us." [2]
  2. Imam al-Sadiq (عليه السلام) says: "He is not a friend of mine who consumes the property of a believer unlawfully." [3]
  3. The Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) says: "Behold! Whoever is honored by people out of fear of his evil is not of me."
  4. The Imam (عليه السلام) said: "He is not of our Shias who oppresses people."
  5. Imam al-Kazim (عليه السلام) said: "He is not of us who does not hold himself to account every day." [4]
  6. The Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) said: "Whoever hears a man calling out, 'O Muslims, come to my aid!' and does not respond to him, is not a Muslim." [5]
  7. Imam al-Baqir (عليه السلام) said to one of his companions named Jabir: "Know, O Jabir, that you will not be a friend to us until even if all the people of your city gathered and said, 'You are a bad man,' it would not grieve you; and if they said, 'You are a righteous man,' it would not please you. Rather, present yourself before the Book of Allah [the Quran]..." [6]

These Hadiths strike a line of cancellation through the delusions of those who content themselves with only a name, while there is no sign of action or ideological connection among them. They clearly prove that in the school of divine leaders, the fundamental and foundational principle is faith in the ideology and acting upon its programs, and everything must be measured by this standard.

[1] Tafsir al-Safi, under the mentioned verses. [2] Safinat al-Bihar, Vol. 2, p. 318. [3] Wasa'il al-Shia, Vol. 12, p. 53. [4] Bihar al-Anwar, Vol. 15 (old edition), Ethics section. [5] Usul al-Kafi, Vol. 2, p. 164. [6] Safinat al-Bihar, Vol. 2, p. 691.

Ayahs

قِيلَ يَـٰنُوحُ ٱهْبِطْ بِسَلَـٰمٍ مِّنَّا وَبَرَكَـٰتٍ عَلَيْكَ وَعَلَىٰٓ أُمَمٍ مِّمَّن مَّعَكَ ۚ وَأُمَمٌ سَنُمَتِّعُهُمْ ثُمَّ يَمَسُّهُم مِّنَّا عَذَابٌ أَلِيمٌ (٤٨)
It was said, ‘O Noah! Disembark in peace from Us and with [Our] blessings upon you and upon nations [to descend] from those who are with you, and nations whom We shall provide for, then a painful punishment from Us shall befall them.’ (48)
تِلْكَ مِنْ أَنۢبَآءِ ٱلْغَيْبِ نُوحِيهَآ إِلَيْكَ ۖ مَا كُنتَ تَعْلَمُهَآ أَنتَ وَلَا قَوْمُكَ مِن قَبْلِ هَـٰذَا ۖ فَٱصْبِرْ ۖ إِنَّ ٱلْعَـٰقِبَةَ لِلْمُتَّقِينَ (٤٩)
These are accounts of the Unseen, which We reveal to you. Neither you nor your people used to know them before this. So be patient. Indeed the outcome will be in favour of the Godwary. (49)

Commentary

Noah Disembarked in Safety

These verses are the final ones in this Surah regarding Noah and his cautionary life story, referring to Noah’s descent from the Ark and the resumption of life and normal existence on earth.

In the first verse, it says: "It was said, 'O Noah, disembark in peace from Us and blessings upon you and upon nations [descending] from those with you'" (قِيلَ يا نُوحُ اهْبِطْ بِسَلامٍ مِنَّا وَ بَرَكاتٍ عَلَيْكَ وَ عَلى‌ أُمَمٍ مِمَّنْ مَعَكَ‌). Undoubtedly, the "Flood" had crushed all signs of life; naturally, cultivated lands, green pastures, and lush gardens had all been destroyed. At this moment, there was a fear that Noah and his companions might face severe hardship regarding "livelihood" and "sustenance." However, Allah reassured this group of believers that the doors of Divine blessings would be opened to them, and they should harbor no anxiety regarding their living conditions.

Furthermore, another concern regarding health might have arisen for Noah and his followers—that living in the vicinity of these marshes and swamps left behind by the Flood might endanger their health. Therefore, Allah reassured them in this regard as well, that no danger would threaten them; for He who sent the Flood to destroy the transgressors is also capable of providing a "healthy" and "blessed" environment for the believers.

This short sentence shows us to what extent the Quran pays attention to the subtleties of matters and reflects them in very condensed and beautiful expressions.

The word "Umam" (أُمَمٍ) is the plural of "Ummah" (nation/community). This expression suggests that there were nations with Noah. This phrasing might be because each individual who was with Noah became the source of a tribe or a nation, or it could be that those with Noah were groups from different tribes and clans who collectively formed nations.

There is also the possibility that "Umam" includes the animal species that were with Noah, as the word "Ummah" is also applied to them in the Holy Quran. As we read in Surah Al-An'am, verse 38: وَ ما مِنْ دَابَّةٍ فِي الْأَرْضِ وَ لا طائِرٍ يَطِيرُ بِجَناحَيْهِ إِلَّا أُمَمٌ أَمْثالُكُمْ‌: "There is no creature on the earth, nor a bird that flies with its two wings, but they are communities like you." Therefore, just as Noah and his companions lived in safety and blessing by the infinite grace of the Lord despite all the difficulties of life after the Flood, the various types of living creatures that disembarked from the Ark with Noah and stepped onto the earth also possessed this safety and immunity by Divine grace.

Then it adds: "Yet [there will be] nations to whom We will grant enjoyment, then a painful punishment from Us will touch them" (وَ أُمَمٌ سَنُمَتِّعُهُمْ ثُمَّ يَمَسُّهُمْ مِنَّا عَذابٌ أَلِيمٌ‌). Thus, it is not the case that this selection of the fittest and the reformation of the human race through the Flood was the final selection or the final reformation. Rather, until the human race reaches the highest stage of growth and perfection, due to the misuse of free will, it sometimes steps onto the path of evil and corruption, and the same program of punishment in this world and the Hereafter will overtake it.

It is interesting that in the above sentence, He only says: "We will grant them enjoyment" (سَنُمَتِّعُهُمْ), and immediately speaks of their punishment and chastisement. This indicates that the enjoyment of abundant blessings in individuals with low capacity and weak faith, instead of awakening a sense of gratitude and obedience, often increases their rebellion and arrogance, subsequently severing the bonds of servitude to Allah.

The statement quoted by the late Tabarsi in Majma' al-Bayan from one of the commentators regarding this verse is interesting, where he says: "Those who enjoyed the world perished because ignorance and negligence overcame them; they thought of nothing but the world, its development, and its pleasures" (هلك المستمتعون فى الدنيا لان الجهل يغلب عليهم و الغفلة، فلا يتفكرون الا فى الدنيا و عمارتها و ملاذها). This reality is clearly seen in the lives of the affluent and wealthy countries of the world, who are often submerged in corruption. Not only do they not think of the oppressed of the world, but day by day they devise new schemes to suck their blood even more.

For this reason, it often happens that Allah brings down upon them wars and painful events that temporarily strip away their blessings, so that perhaps they may wake up.

In the final verse, which concludes the story of Noah in this Surah, a general reference is made to all that has passed, stating:

"These are from the news of the unseen which We reveal to you [O Prophet]" (تِلْكَ مِنْ أَنْباءِ الْغَيْبِ نُوحِيها إِلَيْكَ‌).

"Neither you nor your people knew them before this" (ما كُنْتَ تَعْلَمُها أَنْتَ وَ لا قَوْمُكَ مِنْ قَبْلِ هذا).

Considering what you have heard and all the difficulties Noah faced in his mission, yet he remained steadfast, you too be patient and steadfast, for indeed the [best] outcome is for the righteous (فَاصْبِرْ إِنَّ الْعاقِبَةَ لِلْمُتَّقِينَ‌).

1- The latter verse points to several key points:

  1. Recounting the stories of the Prophets in a factual manner, free from any superstition or distortion, is only possible through Divine revelation. Otherwise, the history books of the ancients are so mixed with myths and legends that distinguishing truth from falsehood in them is impossible, and the further back we go, the more this entanglement increases.

Therefore, the narration of the lives of the Prophets and past nations, free from all superstitions, is itself one of the signs of the truthfulness of the Quran and the Prophet of Islam. [^1]

  1. It is understood from this verse that, contrary to what some imagine, the Prophets were aware of the knowledge of the unseen (Ghayb). However, this awareness was through Divine means and to the extent that Allah willed, not that they knew anything of their own accord. If we see the denial of the knowledge of the unseen in some verses, it refers to the fact that their knowledge is not inherent but is only from Allah.

  2. This verse also clarifies another reality: that the narration of the lives of the Prophets and past nations in the Quran is not only a lesson for the Islamic Ummah, but also a kind of comfort, consolation, and strengthening of the will and morale for the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله. For he too is a human being and must learn in the Divine school through this path to become more prepared to fight the tyrants of his time and not fear the multitude of problems in his way. That is, just as Noah exercised patience and steadfastness despite all those agonizing hardships and was content with the faith of a very small number during his famously long life, you too must not lose patience and steadfastness under any circumstances.

At this point, we leave the story of Noah with all its wonders and lessons and move on to another great Prophet, namely Hud, after whom this Surah is named:

[^1]: A detailed explanation on this subject is provided in the book "The Quran and the Last Prophet."

Ayahs

وَإِلَىٰ عَادٍ أَخَاهُمْ هُودًا ۚ قَالَ يَـٰقَوْمِ ٱعْبُدُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ مَا لَكُم مِّنْ إِلَـٰهٍ غَيْرُهُۥٓ ۖ إِنْ أَنتُمْ إِلَّا مُفْتَرُونَ (٥٠)
And to ‘Ad [We sent] Hud, their brother. He said, ‘O my people! Worship Allah. You have no other god besides Him: you merely fabricate [the deities that you worship]. (50)
يَـٰقَوْمِ لَآ أَسْـَٔلُكُمْ عَلَيْهِ أَجْرًا ۖ إِنْ أَجْرِىَ إِلَّا عَلَى ٱلَّذِى فَطَرَنِىٓ ۚ أَفَلَا تَعْقِلُونَ (٥١)
‘O my people! I do not ask you any reward for it. My reward lies only with Him who originated me. Do you not exercise your reason? (51)
وَيَـٰقَوْمِ ٱسْتَغْفِرُوا۟ رَبَّكُمْ ثُمَّ تُوبُوٓا۟ إِلَيْهِ يُرْسِلِ ٱلسَّمَآءَ عَلَيْكُم مِّدْرَارًا وَيَزِدْكُمْ قُوَّةً إِلَىٰ قُوَّتِكُمْ وَلَا تَتَوَلَّوْا۟ مُجْرِمِينَ (٥٢)
‘O my people! Plead with your Lord for forgiveness, then turn to Him penitently: He will send copious rains for you from the sky, and add power to your [present] power. So do not turn your backs [on Him] as guilty ones.’ (52)

Commentary

The Brave Idol-Breaker

As we have mentioned, this Surah recounts the stories of the dawah (call) of five great Prophets, the hardships and difficulties of these missions, and their outcomes. In the preceding verses, the discussion was about "Noah," and now it is the turn of "Hud."

All these Prophets shared a single logic and a single goal; they rose to rescue humanity from various forms of bondage and to call toward Monotheism (Tawhid) in all its dimensions. The slogan of all of them was faith, sincerity, effort, struggle, and steadfastness in the path of God. The reaction of various nations toward all of them was also harsh, violent, and accompanied by various forms of coercion and pressure.

In the first verse of this account, He says: "And to ‘Ad [We sent] their brother Hud" (وَ إِلى‌ عادٍ أَخاهُمْ هُوداً).

Here, Hud is referred to as "brother." This expression is either because the Arabs refer to all members of a tribe as "brother" since they share a common ancestral root—for example, they call a person from the Bani Asad tribe "Akho Asadi" and from the Madhhij tribe "Akho Madhhij"—or it is an indication that Hud’s behavior toward his people, like that of other Prophets, was entirely brotherly. He did not act in the manner of a ruler or commander, or even a father toward his children, but rather like a brother toward other brothers, without any claim to privilege or superiority.

Hud’s first call was the same as the call of all Prophets: the call to Monotheism and the rejection of any form of polytheism: "Hud said to them: O my people! Worship Allah" (قالَ يا قَوْمِ اعْبُدُوا اللَّهَ‌).

"For there is no deity or worthy object of worship for you other than Him" (ما لَكُمْ مِنْ إِلهٍ غَيْرُهُ‌).

"You are mistaken in the beliefs you hold regarding idols, and you are inventing falsehoods against Allah" (إِنْ أَنْتُمْ إِلَّا مُفْتَرُونَ‌).

These idols are neither His partners nor the source of good or evil, and they are capable of nothing. What slander or fabrication could be greater than attributing such high status to such worthless beings?

Hud عليه السلام then added: "O my people! I ask no reward from you for my call; I seek no recompense from you," lest you imagine that my outcry and fervor are to attain wealth or status, or that you refuse to submit because of the weight of the reward you might think you owe me.

(يا قَوْمِ لا أَسْئَلُكُمْ عَلَيْهِ أَجْراً).

"My reward is only with Him who created me," who gave me soul and body, and to whom I am indebted for everything—my Creator and Provider (إِنْ أَجْرِيَ إِلَّا عَلَى الَّذِي فَطَرَنِي‌).

Fundamentally, if I take a step for your guidance and prosperity, it is in obedience to His command; therefore, I must seek the reward from Him, not from you. Furthermore, do you possess anything of your own to give me? Whatever you have is from Him. "Do you not then understand?" (أَ فَلا تَعْقِلُونَ‌).

Finally, to encourage them and to utilize every possible means to awaken the truth-seeking spirit of this misguided people, he resorts to mentioning the conditional material rewards that Allah places at the disposal of believers in this world, saying:

"O my people! Seek forgiveness from Allah for your sins" (وَ يا قَوْمِ اسْتَغْفِرُوا رَبَّكُمْ‌).

"Then turn to Him in repentance" (ثُمَّ تُوبُوا إِلَيْهِ‌).

"If you do so, He will command the sky to send down life-giving drops of rain upon you abundantly" (يُرْسِلِ السَّماءَ عَلَيْكُمْ مِدْراراً) [1]

[1]: "Midrar," as we have previously stated, is derived from the root "darr," meaning the flowing of milk from the udder; subsequently, it was also applied to the falling of rain. Interestingly, the verse above does not say He sends rain from the sky upon you, but rather says He sends "the sky" upon you—meaning it rains so much that it is as if the entire sky is pouring down. Given that "Midrar" is also an intensive form (sighah al-mubalaghah), the ultimate emphasis is derived from this sentence.

...so that your crops, plantings, and gardens are not threatened by water scarcity and remain ever green and lush. Furthermore, under the shadow of faith, piety, avoidance of sin, and returning to Allah, "He will add strength to your strength" (وَ يَزِدْكُمْ قُوَّةً إِلى‌ قُوَّتِكُمْ‌).

Never think that faith and piety diminish your strength; no, never. Rather, He increases your physical strength by utilizing spiritual power, and with this significant support, you will be able to have a prosperous society, a large population, a healthy economy, and a powerful, free, and independent nation.

Therefore, "do not turn away from the path of truth and do not step onto the road of sin" (وَ لا تَتَوَلَّوْا مُجْرِمِينَ‌).

Key Points

1- Monotheism: The Essence of the Call of All Prophets

The history of the Prophets shows that all of them began their call with Monotheism (Tawhid) and the rejection of polytheism and all forms of idol-worship. In reality, no reform in human societies is possible without this call, because social unity, cooperation, mutual aid, and self-sacrifice are all matters nourished by the root of the Monotheism of the Worshiped.

Conversely, polytheism (shirk) is the source of all kinds of fragmentation, contradiction, conflict, despotism, self-centeredness, and exclusivity. The connection between these concepts and polytheism or idol-worship in its broad sense is not hidden.

One who is self-centered or exclusive sees only himself, and for this reason, he is a polytheist. Monotheism dissolves the drop of an individual's existence into the vast ocean of society; the monotheist sees nothing but one great unit—the entirety of human society and the servants of Allah.

Those who seek superiority draw from another type of polytheism, as do those who are constantly at war and strife with their fellow humans, seeing their interests as separate from the interests of others. This duality and multiplicity are nothing but polytheism in different guises.

For this reason, the Prophets, for their extensive reforms, all started from here: Monotheism of the Worshiped (Allah), followed by unity of word, unity of action, and unity of society.

2- True Leaders Seek No Reward from Followers

A true leader can continue his path free from any accusation and in ultimate freedom, correcting any deviation or crookedness in his followers, only if he has no material dependence or need for them. Otherwise, that very need will become a chain on his hands and feet, and a lock and bolt on his tongue and mind! Deviants enter through this very channel to place him under pressure—either through the threat of cutting off material aid or by offering more assistance. No matter how pure and sincere a leader may be, he is still human, and his step might falter at this stage.

For this reason, in the verses above and other verses of the Quran, we read that the Prophets explicitly declared at the beginning of their call that they had no material need or expectation of reward from their followers.

This is a model for all leaders, especially spiritual and religious leaders. However, since those who serve Islam and Muslims full-time must have their needs met in a proper manner, the establishment of a relief fund and the Islamic Treasury (Bayt al-Mal) is intended to meet the needs of this group; this is one of the philosophies behind the formation of the Bayt al-Mal in Islam.

3- Sin and the Ruin of Societies

Again, in the verses above, we see that the Quran establishes a clear link between spiritual and material issues, introducing seeking forgiveness for sins and returning to Allah as the cause of prosperity, lushness, freshness, greenery, and the addition of strength to existing strengths.

This reality is observed in many other verses of the Quran, including in Surah Nuh, where we read from the tongue of this great Prophet: "I said: Seek forgiveness from your Lord, for He is ever-forgiving. He will send the sky upon you in abundance, and aid you with wealth and children, and provide for you gardens and provide for you rivers" [1].

It is noteworthy that we read in Islamic traditions that Rabi' ibn Sabih said: I was with Hasan [al-Basri] when a man entered and complained about the drought in his village. Hasan said to him: "Seek forgiveness." Another came and complained of poverty; he also said: "Seek forgiveness." A third came and said: "Pray that Allah grants me a son"; he also said: "Seek forgiveness." Rabi' says: (I was surprised) and said to him: "Everyone who comes to you with a problem or a request for a blessing, you give them this same instruction and tell them all to seek forgiveness and ask pardon from Allah."

He replied to me: "What I said was not from myself; I derived this matter from the word of Allah recounting His Prophet Noah," and then he recited the verses of Surah Nuh mentioned above [2]. Those who are accustomed to passing over these matters lightly immediately posit an unknown spiritual connection between these things and relieve themselves of any further analysis.

However, if we look closer, we find close links between these matters that, when considered, weave material and spiritual issues together in the fabric of society like warp and weft, or link them like the root and stem of a tree to its flower and fruit.

Which society is it that becomes tainted with sin, betrayal, hypocrisy, theft, oppression, and laziness, yet remains prosperous and full of blessing?

Which society is it that loses the spirit of cooperation and replaces it with war, conflict, and bloodshed, yet possesses lush green lands and a comfortable economic situation?

Which society is it whose people are tainted by various lusts, yet stand strong and steadfast against enemies?

It must be said explicitly: there is no moral issue unless it has a beneficial and constructive effect on the material life of the people, and no correct belief or faith is found unless it plays a significant role in building a prosperous, free, independent, and powerful society.

[1]: Surah Nuh, Verses 10-12. [2]: Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 10, p. 361.

Those who separate moral issues, religious faith, and Monotheism from material issues have understood neither spiritual matters nor material ones correctly.

If religion exists among people as a series of formalities and outward rituals devoid of content, it obviously will have no impact on the material system of society. But when spiritual and soulful beliefs penetrate the depths of the human soul such that their effects appear in the hands, feet, eyes, ears, tongue, and every atom of one's being, the constructive effects of these beliefs in society will be hidden from no one.

We may not be able to fully grasp some stages of the link between seeking forgiveness and the descent of material blessings, but undoubtedly, a large part of it is understandable to us.

In the Islamic Revolution of our country, Iran, in this day and age, we clearly observed how Islamic beliefs and the power of morality and spirituality were able to triumph over the most powerful weapons of the time and the strongest armies and colonial powers. This shows to what extent the application of religious beliefs and positive spiritual morality is significant in social and political issues.

4- What is meant by "He will add strength to your strength"?

The literal meaning of this sentence is: Allah, in the light of repentance and seeking forgiveness, adds a strength to your strength. Some have taken this sentence as a reference to an increase in human resources (as was also indicated in the verses of Surah Nuh), and others have considered it a reference to the addition of material powers to spiritual power. However, the expression in the verse is absolute and includes any kind of increase in material and spiritual strength, encompassing all these interpretations.

Ayahs

قَالُوا۟ يَـٰهُودُ مَا جِئْتَنَا بِبَيِّنَةٍ وَمَا نَحْنُ بِتَارِكِىٓ ءَالِهَتِنَا عَن قَوْلِكَ وَمَا نَحْنُ لَكَ بِمُؤْمِنِينَ (٥٣)
They said, ‘O Hud, you have not brought us any manifest proof. We are not going to abandon our gods for what you say, and we are not going to believe you. (53)
إِن نَّقُولُ إِلَّا ٱعْتَرَىٰكَ بَعْضُ ءَالِهَتِنَا بِسُوٓءٍ ۗ قَالَ إِنِّىٓ أُشْهِدُ ٱللَّهَ وَٱشْهَدُوٓا۟ أَنِّى بَرِىٓءٌ مِّمَّا تُشْرِكُونَ (٥٤)
All we say is that some of our gods have visited you with some evil.’ He said, ‘I call Allah to witness—and you too be [my] witnesses—that I repudiate what you take as [His] partners (54)
مِن دُونِهِۦ ۖ فَكِيدُونِى جَمِيعًا ثُمَّ لَا تُنظِرُونِ (٥٥)
besides Him. Now try out your stratagems against me, together, without granting me any respite. (55)
إِنِّى تَوَكَّلْتُ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ رَبِّى وَرَبِّكُم ۚ مَّا مِن دَآبَّةٍ إِلَّا هُوَ ءَاخِذٌۢ بِنَاصِيَتِهَآ ۚ إِنَّ رَبِّى عَلَىٰ صِرَٰطٍ مُّسْتَقِيمٍ (٥٦)
Indeed I have put my trust in Allah, my Lord and your Lord. There is no living being but He holds it by its forelock. Indeed my Lord is on a straight path. (56)
فَإِن تَوَلَّوْا۟ فَقَدْ أَبْلَغْتُكُم مَّآ أُرْسِلْتُ بِهِۦٓ إِلَيْكُمْ ۚ وَيَسْتَخْلِفُ رَبِّى قَوْمًا غَيْرَكُمْ وَلَا تَضُرُّونَهُۥ شَيْـًٔا ۚ إِنَّ رَبِّى عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ حَفِيظٌ (٥٧)
But if you turn your backs [on me], then [know that] I have communicated to you whatever I was sent to you with. My Lord will make another people succeed you, and you will not hurt Allah in the least. Indeed my Lord is watchful over all things.’ (57)

Commentary

The Powerful Logic of Hud

Now let us see how this rebellious and arrogant people—the people of ‘Ad—reacted to their brother Hud (peace be upon him) and his advice, exhortations, and guidance.

"They said: 'O Hud, you have not brought us any clear proof'" (قَالُوا يَا هُودُ مَا جِئْتَنَا بِبَيِّنَةٍ).

"And we are not going to abandon our gods [idols] because of your word" (وَمَا نَحْنُ بِتَارِكِي آلِهَتِنَا عَنْ قَوْلِكَ).

"And we are not believers in you!" (وَمَا نَحْنُ لَكَ بِمُؤْمِنِينَ).

After these three illogical sentences, they added: "We think you have gone mad, and the reason is that you have become an object of hatred to our gods, and they have damaged your intellect" (إِنْ نَقُولُ إِلَّا اعْتَرَاكَ بَعْضُ آلِهَتِنَا بِسُوءٍ). Undoubtedly, Hud—as is the program and duty of all Prophets—had presented a miracle or miracles to them to prove his truthfulness. However, due to their pride and arrogance, like other stubborn nations, they denied the miracles and considered them sorcery, or a series of coincidences and accidental events that could not serve as proof for anything.

Furthermore, the rejection of idol worship does not require a proof; anyone who possesses a modicum of intellect and consciousness and frees himself from prejudice perceives it well. Even if a proof were required, do proofs need a miracle in addition to being logical and rational?

In other words, what appeared in Hud's invitation in the previous verses was a call toward the One God, returning to Him, seeking forgiveness for sins, and the rejection of any form of polytheism and idol worship. All of these are matters whose proof is entirely possible through rational evidence.

Therefore, if by denying "clear proof" (bayyinah) they meant the denial of rational evidence, this statement is certainly incorrect. And if they meant the denial of a "miracle," this claim did not require a miracle. In any case, their statement that "we will never forget our idols because of your words" is the best evidence of their stubbornness, for a rational and truth-seeking person accepts the word of truth from whomever it may come.

Specifically, the sentence where they accused Hud of "madness"—a madness resulting from the wrath of the gods!—is itself the best evidence of their superstitious nature and worship of myths.

How can lifeless and senseless stones and wood, which require the protection of their own worshippers, take away the intellect and consciousness of a rational human being?

Moreover, what proof did they have for Hud's madness other than that he had broken tradition and risen to combat the superstitious customs and traditions of his environment? If this is proof of madness, then all the world's reformers and revolutionary men who rose against wrong methods must be mad.

And this is nothing new; past and contemporary history is full of attributions of madness to right-thinking and tradition-breaking men and women who rose against superstitions, colonialisms, and enslavements.

In any case, Hud had to give a crushing response to this misguided and stubborn people—a response that was both infused with logic and delivered from a position of strength. The Quran says:

In response to them, he stated these few sentences:

"I call Allah to witness, and you too bear witness, that I am innocent of these idols and gods of yours" (قَالَ إِنِّي أُشْهِدُ اللَّهَ وَاشْهَدُوا أَنِّي بَرِيءٌ مِمَّا تُشْرِكُونَ - مِنْ دُونِهِ).

This is an indication that if these idols have any power, ask them to remove me; I who have openly risen to war against them and publicly declare my innocence and detestation of them—why are they hesitating? What are they waiting for? And why do they not destroy me?! Then he adds: Not only are they capable of nothing, but you too, with your vast numbers, are not capable of anything: "If you are truthful, all of you join hands and plot whatever scheme you can against me, and do not give me a moment's respite" (فَكِيدُونِي جَمِيعاً ثُمَّ لَا تُنْظِرُونِ).

Why do I consider your vast numbers as nothing? And why do I not have the slightest regard for your strength and power? You who are thirsty for my blood and possess all kinds of power.

Because I have a supporter whose power is above all powers: "I have put my trust in Allah, who is my Lord and your Lord" (إِنِّي تَوَكَّلْتُ عَلَى اللَّهِ رَبِّي وَرَبِّكُمْ).

This itself is proof that I am not lying; it is a sign that I have set my heart elsewhere. If you think correctly, it is a kind of miracle in itself that a single man rises to combat the superstitious beliefs of a powerful and fanatical population, and even incites them to rise against him, yet neither allows fear into himself nor do his enemies have the power to make a decision against him.

And then he continued: Not only you, "there is no moving creature on earth but that it is within the grasp of Allah's power and command," and until He wills, they can do nothing (مَا مِنْ دَابَّةٍ إِلَّا هُوَ آخِذٌ بِنَاصِيَتِهَا).

But know this also: my God is not like those powerful ones whose power leads to autocracy and whimsy, using it for other than the truth; rather, "my Lord is always upon a straight path and the road of justice and equity," and He does nothing contrary to wisdom and correctness (إِنَّ رَبِّي عَلَى صِرَاطٍ مُسْتَقِيمٍ).

Here, two points must be noted

First, "nasīyah" (forelock) originally means the hair on the front of the head, and it comes from the root "nasā" meaning connection and continuity. "Taking by the forelock" (akhdh bi-l-nasīyah) is a metaphor for dominance, coercion, and victory over something. When Allah says in the above sentence that there is no moving creature except that We take its forelock, it refers to His overwhelming power over everything, such that no being has any power of resistance against His will; for usually, when the hair on the front of the head of a human or animal is held firmly, the power of resistance is stripped from them.

This expression is used so that arrogant oppressors, self-satisfied idolaters, and tyrannical power-seekers do not think that if they have been given a few days of leeway, it means they can offer the slightest resistance against the will of the Lord. May they pay attention to this reality and descend from the steed of arrogance.

Second, the sentence "Indeed, my Lord is on a straight path" (إِنَّ رَبِّي عَلَى صِرَاطٍ مُسْتَقِيمٍ) is one of the most beautiful expressions regarding power combined with the justice of the Lord. For the powerful are often bullies and oppressors, but Allah, with His infinite power, is always on the straight path of justice and the smooth road of wisdom, order, and accountability.

One should also not overlook the point that Hud's words before the polytheists express the reality that the more stubborn enemies increase their obstinacy, the more a decisive leader must increase his steadfastness. Hud's people tried to frighten him severely with their idols; in response, he frightened them even more severely with the overwhelming power of Allah.

Finally, in his last word to them, Hud says: "If you turn away from the path of truth, it does not harm me, for I have conveyed my message to you" (فَإِنْ تَوَلَّوْا فَقَدْ أَبْلَغْتُكُمْ مَا أُرْسِلْتُ بِهِ إِلَيْكُمْ).

This indicates: do not imagine that if my invitation is not accepted, it is a defeat for me. I have performed my duty; performing one's duty is victory, even if my invitation is not accepted. This is a lesson for all true leaders and guides of the path of truth, that they should never feel fatigue or anxiety in their work, even if the people do not accept their call.

Then, just as the idolaters had threatened him, he threatens them even more severely with divine punishment and says: "If you do not accept the call of truth, Allah will soon destroy you and replace you with another group, and you will not harm Him in the least" (وَيَسْتَخْلِفُ رَبِّي قَوْماً غَيْرَكُمْ وَلَا تَضُرُّونَهُ شَيْئاً).

This is the law of creation: whenever a people lack the merit to receive the blessing of guidance or other blessings of the Lord, He removes them and places a deserving group in their stead.

"Know also that my Lord is the Protector of all things and the Keeper of every account" (إِنَّ رَبِّي عَلَى كُلِّ شَيْءٍ حَفِيظٌ).

Neither does opportunity escape Him, nor does He forget the situation, nor does He consign His Prophets and friends to oblivion, nor is the account of anyone outside His knowledge; rather, He knows everything and is dominant over everything.

Ayahs

وَلَمَّا جَآءَ أَمْرُنَا نَجَّيْنَا هُودًا وَٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ مَعَهُۥ بِرَحْمَةٍ مِّنَّا وَنَجَّيْنَـٰهُم مِّنْ عَذَابٍ غَلِيظٍ (٥٨)
When Our edict came, We delivered Hud and the faithful who were with him, by a mercy from Us, and We delivered them from a harsh punishment. (58)
وَتِلْكَ عَادٌ ۖ جَحَدُوا۟ بِـَٔايَـٰتِ رَبِّهِمْ وَعَصَوْا۟ رُسُلَهُۥ وَٱتَّبَعُوٓا۟ أَمْرَ كُلِّ جَبَّارٍ عَنِيدٍ (٥٩)
Such were [the people of] Ad: they impugned the signs of their Lord and disobeyed His apostles, and followed the dictates of every obstinate tyrant. (59)
وَأُتْبِعُوا۟ فِى هَـٰذِهِ ٱلدُّنْيَا لَعْنَةً وَيَوْمَ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ ۗ أَلَآ إِنَّ عَادًا كَفَرُوا۟ رَبَّهُمْ ۗ أَلَا بُعْدًا لِّعَادٍ قَوْمِ هُودٍ (٦٠)
So they were pursued by a curse in this world and on the Day of Resurrection. Behold! ‘Ad indeed defied their Lord. Now, away with ‘Ad, the people of Hud! (60)

Commentary

Eternal Curse and Condemnation Upon This Oppressive People

In the final part of the verses concerning the story of the people of ‘Ad and their Prophet Hud, reference is made to the painful punishment of these rebels. First, it says:

"And when Our command [for their punishment] came, We saved Hud and those who believed with him, by a mercy from Us" (وَ لَمَّا جاءَ أَمْرُنا نَجَّيْنا هُوداً وَ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا مَعَهُ بِرَحْمَةٍ مِنَّا).

Tafsir Nemouneh; Vol. 9; p. 141

Again, for further emphasis, He says: "And We saved them from a severe and thick punishment" (وَ نَجَّيْناهُمْ مِنْ عَذابٍ غَلِيظٍ).

It is interesting to note that before mentioning the punishment of the disbelieving, rebellious, and oppressive individuals, He mentions the salvation and rescue of the believing people. This is so that the misconception does not arise that during divine punishment—according to the famous proverb—the "dry and the wet will burn together." For He is All-Wise and Just, and it is impossible for Him to punish even a single believing individual amidst a multitude of disbelievers and sinners.

Rather, divine mercy moves such persons to a place of safety and security before the punishment strikes. As we have seen, before the Flood arrived, Noah’s Ark of salvation was ready; and before the cities of Lot were crushed, Lot and his few believing companions departed at night by divine command.

Regarding why the phrase "We saved" (نَجَّيْنا) is repeated in this verse, various interpretations exist. Some believe that the first "We saved" refers to salvation from the punishment of this world, while the second refers to salvation from the punishment of the Hereafter, which is perfectly consistent with the description of being "thick" (ghaliz).

Others have pointed out a subtle point: since divine mercy was mentioned, if the word "punishment" had followed immediately, it would lack harmony—where is mercy and where is "thick punishment"? Therefore, "We saved" was repeated once more to create a distance between the two, so that nothing of the intensity of the punishment and the emphasis upon it would be diminished.

It should also be noted that in the verses of the Quran, "punishment" ('adhab) is described as "thick" (ghaliz) in four instances [1]. Upon careful examination of those verses, it appears that "thick punishment" pertains to the next world; specifically, the verses of Surah Ibrahim that mention "thick punishment" explicitly describe the state of the people of Hell. And it must be so, for no matter how severe the punishment of this world may be, it is light and insignificant compared to the punishment of the Hereafter.

This correspondence is also noteworthy: the people of ‘Ad—as will come, Allah willing, in Surah Al-Qamar and Surah Al-Haqqah—were harsh, coarse, and tall-statured people whose bodies were likened to the trunks of palm trees. Correspondingly, they had firm, large, and tall buildings, to the extent that we read in pre-Islamic history:

The Arabs attributed tall and firm structures to ‘Ad and called them "‘Adi." Therefore, their punishment was also "thick" and harsh like themselves. Not only in the other world, but in this world too, their punishment was very severe and harsh, as will be explained in the commentary of the aforementioned Surahs.

Subsequently, He summarizes the sins of the people of ‘Ad into three subjects: First, they denied the signs of their Lord, and with stubbornness, they rejected every clear proof and evidence of the truthfulness of their Prophet’s call: "And that was ‘Ad; they rejected the signs of their Lord" (وَ تِلْكَ عادٌ جَحَدُوا بِآياتِ رَبِّهِمْ).

Second, in terms of action, they "rose in disobedience and rebellion against His messengers" (وَ عَصَوْا رُسُلَهُ). The fact that "messengers" (rusul) is mentioned in the plural is either because the call of all Prophets is toward one reality (Monotheism and its branches); therefore, denying one Prophet is equivalent to denying all Prophets. Or, it is because Hud invited them to believe in the previous Prophets as well, and they rejected them.

Their third sin was that they abandoned the command of Allah "and followed the command of every obstinate tyrant" (وَ اتَّبَعُوا أَمْرُ كُلِّ جَبَّارٍ عَنِيدٍ). What sin could be greater than these: abandoning faith, opposing the Prophets, and submitting to the command of obstinate tyrants?

"Jabbar" (tyrant) refers to one who strikes, kills, and destroys out of anger and wrath, and does not follow the command of reason. In other words, a jabbar is one who forces others to follow him, or seeks to compensate for his own deficiency by claiming greatness and outward arrogance. "‘Anid" (obstinate) is one who is exceedingly opposed to the truth and reality and never submits to the truth.

These two attributes are the prominent characteristics of the Taghuts (false deities/tyrants) and the arrogant ones of every age and time, whose ears are never open to the word of truth, and whoever they oppose, they torture, crush, and eliminate with cruelty and ruthlessness.

A question arises here: if this is the meaning of jabbar, why is "Al-Jabbar" mentioned as one of the attributes of Allah in the Quran (Surah Al-Hashr, verse 23) and other Islamic sources?

The answer is: Jabbar, in its original linguistic root as mentioned above, is either from the root jabr meaning power, dominance, and might, or from the root jubran meaning to rectify or compensate for the deficiency of something.

However, whether jabbar carries the first or second meaning, it is used in two forms. Sometimes it is used in a derogatory sense, which is when a human seeks to compensate for his shortcomings and deficiencies through "self-superiority," arrogance, and false claims, or when he seeks to subdue and humiliate another against his own will and desire.

This meaning appears in many verses of the Quran and is occasionally accompanied by other blameworthy attributes, such as in the verse above where it is mentioned alongside "‘anid." In Surah Maryam, verse 32, we read from the tongue of Jesus عليه السلام, the Prophet of God: "And He has not made me a wretched tyrant" (وَ لَمْ يَجْعَلْنِي جَبَّاراً شَقِيًّا). Or regarding the state of the Children of Israel and the oppressive inhabitants of Jerusalem, we read that they said to Moses: "Indeed, within it is a people of tyrannical strength" (إِنَّ فِيها قَوْماً جَبَّارِينَ) (Al-Ma'idah, verse 22).

However, sometimes jabbar is used from these same two roots in a laudatory sense and is applied to one who compensates for the needs of the people and their deficiencies, and sets broken bones, or one who possesses such immense power that others are humble before Him without Him seeking to oppress anyone or abuse His power. For this reason, when jabbar carries this meaning, it is accompanied by other laudatory attributes, as we read in Surah Al-Hashr, verse 23: "The Sovereign, the Pure, the Perfection, the Giver of Faith, the Guardian, the Exalted in Might, the Compeller (Al-Jabbar), the Superior" (الْمَلِكُ الْقُدُّوسُ السَّلامُ الْمُؤْمِنُ الْمُهَيْمِنُ الْعَزِيزُ الْجَبَّارُ الْمُتَكَبِّرُ). It is clear that attributes such as "the Pure," "the Perfection," and "the Giver of Faith" are never compatible with jabbar in the sense of an oppressor or tyrant, or mutakabbir in the sense of one who views himself as superior. This phrasing clearly shows that jabbar here carries the second meaning.

But since some have only considered certain usages of jabbar and have not paid attention to the linguistic root and its multiple meanings, they have imagined that applying it to Allah (and likewise the term mutakabbir) does not seem correct. However, by considering the original roots of the word, the objection is resolved [1].

In the final verse under discussion, where the story of Hud and the people of ‘Ad concludes, the result of their ugly and incorrect actions is stated as follows: "They were followed by a curse in this world [due to their deeds], and after their death, nothing remained of them but a bad name and a disgraceful history" (وَ أُتْبِعُوا فِي هذِهِ الدُّنْيا لَعْنَةً).

And on the Day of Resurrection, it will be said: "Unquestionably, ‘Ad disbelieved in their Lord" (وَ يَوْمَ الْقِيامَةِ أَلا إِنَّ عاداً كَفَرُوا رَبَّهُمْ).

"Away with ‘Ad, the people of Hud [from the mercy of their Lord]" (أَلا بُعْداً لِعادٍ قَوْمِ هُودٍ). Although the word "‘Ad" is sufficient to identify this group, in the verse above, after mentioning ‘Ad, "the people of Hud" is also mentioned. This provides emphasis and also indicates that this group is the very same people who so troubled and accused their compassionate Prophet, Hud; for this reason, they are far from Allah's mercy.

Key Points

1. The People of ‘Ad from a Historical Perspective

Although some Western historians like "Sprenger" have sought to deny the existence of the people of ‘Ad historically—perhaps because they found no mention of them outside Islamic works and saw no trace of them in the books of the Old Testament (Torah)—there is evidence showing that the story of ‘Ad was generally famous during the Pre-Islamic era of the Arabs. Pre-Islamic poets also spoke of the people of Hud; even in the Age of Ignorance, tall and firm buildings were attributed to "‘Ad" and the term "‘Adi" was applied to them.

Some historians believe that "‘Ad" refers to two tribes. One was a tribe of prehistoric humans who lived in the Arabian Peninsula, then perished, and their traces vanished. Human history has preserved nothing of their lives except legends that are not reliable. They take the Quranic expression "the first ‘Ad" (‘Ad al-Ula) (Surah An-Najm, verse 50) as a reference to them.

However, during the era of human history, and likely around 700 BC or earlier, another people named ‘Ad existed who lived in the land of "Ahqaf" or "Yemen." They possessed tall statures and strong, powerful bodies, and for this reason, they were considered elite warriors.

Furthermore, they were significantly advanced in terms of civilization; they had prosperous cities, lush and green lands, and vibrant gardens, such that the Quran says in describing them: "The like of which had not been created in the lands" (الَّتِي لَمْ يُخْلَقْ مِثْلُها فِي الْبِلادِ) (Al-Fajr, verse 8). For this reason, some orientalists have said that the people of ‘Ad lived around Barahut (one of the regions of Hadramaut, Yemen) and many of them perished due to surrounding volcanoes, while their remnants scattered.

In any case, this people lived in luxury and blessings for a time, but as is the way of most who live in luxury, they became intoxicated with pride and negligence. They abused their power for oppression, tyranny, colonialism, and the exploitation of others, and they made obstinate tyrants their leaders. They established the practice of idol worship. When their Prophet Hud invited them—despite all his efforts in advising, enlightening their thoughts and minds, and completing the proof against them—they not only paid the slightest heed but rose to extinguish the call of this great man of truth.

Sometimes they attributed madness and folly to him, and at other times they frightened him with the wrath of their gods. But he stood like a mountain against the anger of this proud and powerful people. Ultimately, he was able to purify and call a group of about four thousand people to the true religion, but others remained in their stubbornness and enmity.

Finally, as will come in the verses of Surah Adh-Dhariyat, Al-Haqqah, and Al-Qamar, a severe and very crushing storm dominated them for seven nights and six days, which crushed their palaces and carried their corpses like autumn leaves upon the waves of the wind, scattering them around. He had previously taken the true believers out from among them and saved them, and their lives and fate became a great lesson for all tyrants and autocrats [1].

2. Eternal Curse and Condemnation Be Upon the People of ‘Ad

This expression and similar ones appear in numerous verses of the Quran regarding various peoples. After explaining part of their states, He says: "Away with Thamud" (أَلا بُعْداً لِثَمُودَ) (Surah Hud, verse 68); "Away with Madyan as Thamud was taken away" (أَلا بُعْداً لِمَدْيَنَ كَما بَعِدَتْ ثَمُودُ) (Surah Hud, verse 89); "So away with the wrongdoing people" (فَبُعْداً لِلْقَوْمِ الظَّالِمِينَ) (Surah Al-Mu'minun, verse 41); "So away with a people who do not believe" (فَبُعْداً لِقَوْمٍ لا يُؤْمِنُونَ) (Surah Al-Mu'minun, verse 44). Likewise, in the story of Noah, we previously read: "And it was said, 'Away with the wrongdoing people'" (وَ قِيلَ بُعْداً لِلْقَوْمِ الظَّالِمِينَ) (Surah Hud, verse 44).

In all these verses, there is a slogan-like condemnation regarding those who have committed a great sin, concerning their distance from Allah's mercy.

This is exactly like the slogans used today for rebellious, colonialist, and oppressive individuals and groups. However, this Quranic slogan is so beautiful and comprehensive that it does not look at only one aspect or dimension. For when we say "Away with such-and-such a group," it includes distance from Allah's mercy, distance from happiness, distance from every kind of good, blessing, and bounty, and distance from the servants of Allah. Of course, their distance from good and happiness is the reaction to their internal distance—in soul and thought and in the environment of action—from God and God's creation. For every idea and action has a reflection in the other abode and the world after death—a reflection perfectly similar to it. For this reason, these distances in this world will be the source of distance and remoteness in the Hereafter from divine mercy, pardon, forgiveness, and bounties [1].

[1]: Surah Ibrahim verse 17, Luqman verse 24, and Surah Fussilat verse 50.

[1]: Refer to the book Taj al-Arus by Zabidi, Mufradat by Raghib, Tafsir Majma' al-Bayan, and Tafsir al-Manar under the verse in question or the final verses of Surah Al-Hashr.

[1]: Tafsir al-Mizan, Tafsir Majma' al-Bayan, and the book A'lam al-Qur'an.

[1]: The word "bu'dan" in terms of grammatical structure in the above verse is an "absolute object" (maf'ul mutlaq) for the sentence "ab'adahum Allah" which is implied. Of course, normally it should be "ib'adan" instead of "bu'dan" because the infinitive of "ab'ada" is "ib'ad," but sometimes the triliteral root infinitive is used as the absolute object instead of the Af'al form infinitive, such as: "وَ اللَّهُ أَنْبَتَكُمْ مِنَ الْأَرْضِ نَباتاً" (Note carefully).

Ayah

۞ وَإِلَىٰ ثَمُودَ أَخَاهُمْ صَـٰلِحًا ۚ قَالَ يَـٰقَوْمِ ٱعْبُدُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ مَا لَكُم مِّنْ إِلَـٰهٍ غَيْرُهُۥ ۖ هُوَ أَنشَأَكُم مِّنَ ٱلْأَرْضِ وَٱسْتَعْمَرَكُمْ فِيهَا فَٱسْتَغْفِرُوهُ ثُمَّ تُوبُوٓا۟ إِلَيْهِ ۚ إِنَّ رَبِّى قَرِيبٌ مُّجِيبٌ (٦١)
And to Thamud [We sent] Salih, their brother. He said, ‘O my people! Worship Allah. You have no other god besides Him. He brought you forth from the earth and made it your habitation. So plead with Him for forgiveness, then turn to Him penitently. My Lord is indeed nearmost [and] responsive.’ (61)

Commentary

The Beginning of the Story of the People of Thamud

The story of the people of ‘Ad, with all its cautionary lessons, has concluded in a condensed manner, and now it is the turn of the people of Thamud. They were the population who, according to historical accounts, lived in the land of Wadi al-Qura between Medina and Sham (Greater Syria).

Once again, we see that when the Holy Quran speaks of their Prophet, Salih, it refers to him as their "brother." What expression could be more eloquent and beautiful than this? We previously alluded to part of its significance in the commentary of the preceding verses: a compassionate and kind brother who has no goal other than sincere well-wishing. "And to Thamud [We sent] their brother Salih" (وَ إِلى‌ ثَمُودَ أَخاهُمْ صالِحاً). Again, we see that Salih’s program is the same fundamental program of all Prophets—a program that begins with "Monotheism" (Tawhid) and the negation of every form of polytheism and idol worship, which is the root of all human suffering.

"He said, 'O my people! Worship Allah; you have no deity other than Him'" (قالَ يا قَوْمِ اعْبُدُوا اللَّهَ ما لَكُمْ مِنْ إِلهٍ غَيْرُهُ‌). Then, to stimulate their sense of gratitude, He points to a corner of the Lord's significant blessings that encompass their entire existence, saying:

"He is the One who produced you from the earth" (هُوَ أَنْشَأَكُمْ مِنَ الْأَرْضِ‌). Where is the earth and that worthless, insignificant soil, and where is this sublime existence and wondrous creation? Does any intellect allow a human to set aside such a Creator and Sustainer—Who possesses all this power and has bestowed all these blessings—and turn toward these ridiculous idols?

After referring to the blessing of creation, He reminds these rebellious humans of other blessings He has placed on the earth: "He is the One who entrusted the cultivation and development of the earth to you and placed the power and means for it at your disposal" (وَ اسْتَعْمَرَكُمْ فِيها).

The terms "Isti‘mar" and "I‘mar" in the Arabic language originally mean delegating the development of the earth to someone. Naturally, this necessitates placing the required means at their disposal. This is what linguists like Raghib in Al-Mufradat and many commentators have stated in the interpretation of the above verse.

There is also a possibility suggested in the meaning of the verse that it refers to Allah granting you a long life; however, the first meaning seems more accurate in light of linguistic texts.

In any case, both meanings were true regarding the people of Thamud, for they possessed developed, lush, and green lands and bountiful gardens. Fundamentally, they exhibited great initiative and power in agriculture. Furthermore, they possessed long lives and strong, powerful physiques, and were advanced in constructing sturdy buildings, as the Quran says: "And they used to carve houses out of the mountains, [feeling] secure" (Surah Al-Hijr, Verse 82). It is noteworthy that the Quran does not say Allah developed the earth and gave it to you; rather, it says He delegated the cultivation and development of the earth to you. This indicates that the means are ready from every perspective, but you must develop the earth through labor and effort and extract its resources; without labor and effort, you have no share.

Additionally, this reality implies that for development and prosperity, a nation must be given the opportunity, their affairs must be entrusted to them, and the necessary tools and means must be placed at their disposal.

"Now that it is so, repent for your sins and return to Allah, for my Lord is near to His servants and responds to their requests" (فَاسْتَغْفِرُوهُ ثُمَّ تُوبُوا إِلَيْهِ إِنَّ رَبِّي قريبٌ مُجِيبٌ‌).

Isti‘mar (Colonization/Development) in the Quran and Our Era

As we saw in the above verses, the Prophet of Allah, Salih, in order to fulfill his educational role among the misguided people of Thamud, speaks to them about the magnificent creation of man from dust and the delegation of the earth's development and its resources to him.

However, this word Isti‘mar, with its specific beauty and profound concept—which encompasses development, the delegation of authority, and the provision of means and tools—has become so distorted in our era that it stands exactly opposite to its Quranic meaning.

It is not only the word Isti‘mar that has suffered this grim fate; we find many words in Persian, Arabic, and other languages that have fallen victim to this same distortion, corruption, and inversion. Examples include "Hadarah" (civilization), "Thaqafah" (culture), and "Hurriyah" (liberty) in Arabic, and words like civilization, intellectualism, freedom, nobility, art, and artistry in Persian. Under the shadow of these distortions, every kind of alienation, materialism, enslavement of humans, denial of reality, spread of corruption, and the performance of hasty, unstudied actions is carried out.

In any case, the actual meaning of Isti‘mar (colonialism) in our era is the domination of great political and industrial powers over oppressed and weak nations, the product of which is looting, plundering, blood-sucking, and the theft of their vital resources.

This colonialism, which has various grim faces—sometimes manifesting in cultural, intellectual, economic, or political and military forms—is what has darkened and blackened the face of our world today. A minority in this world possesses everything, while a vast majority possesses nothing. This colonialism is the source of wars, destruction, crimes, and the back-breaking arms race.

The term the Quran uses for this concept is Istid‘af (oppression/weakening), which perfectly fits this meaning—that is, making someone weak in the broad sense of the word: weakening their intellect, politics, economy, and everything else.

The scope of colonialism in our era is so vast that the word Isti‘mar itself has become "colonized," because its literal meaning has been completely inverted.

In any case, colonialism has a long and tragic story that can be said to span the entirety of human history. Although it constantly changes its face, it is not precisely known when it will be uprooted from human societies, and when human life will finally be established on the basis of cooperation, mutual respect, and helping one another progress in all fields.

Ayahs

قَالُوا۟ يَـٰصَـٰلِحُ قَدْ كُنتَ فِينَا مَرْجُوًّا قَبْلَ هَـٰذَآ ۖ أَتَنْهَىٰنَآ أَن نَّعْبُدَ مَا يَعْبُدُ ءَابَآؤُنَا وَإِنَّنَا لَفِى شَكٍّ مِّمَّا تَدْعُونَآ إِلَيْهِ مُرِيبٍ (٦٢)
They said, ‘O Salih! You were a source of hope to us before this. Do you forbid us to worship what our fathers have been worshiping? Indeed we have grave doubts concerning that to which you invite us.’ (62)
قَالَ يَـٰقَوْمِ أَرَءَيْتُمْ إِن كُنتُ عَلَىٰ بَيِّنَةٍ مِّن رَّبِّى وَءَاتَىٰنِى مِنْهُ رَحْمَةً فَمَن يَنصُرُنِى مِنَ ٱللَّهِ إِنْ عَصَيْتُهُۥ ۖ فَمَا تَزِيدُونَنِى غَيْرَ تَخْسِيرٍ (٦٣)
He said, ‘O my people! Tell me, should I stand on a manifest proof from my Lord, and He has granted me His own mercy, who will protect me from Allah should I disobey Him? For then you will increase me in nothing but loss. (63)
وَيَـٰقَوْمِ هَـٰذِهِۦ نَاقَةُ ٱللَّهِ لَكُمْ ءَايَةً فَذَرُوهَا تَأْكُلْ فِىٓ أَرْضِ ٱللَّهِ وَلَا تَمَسُّوهَا بِسُوٓءٍ فَيَأْخُذَكُمْ عَذَابٌ قَرِيبٌ (٦٤)
O my people! This she-camel of Allah is a sign for you. Let her graze [freely] in Allah’s land, and do not cause her any harm, for then you shall be seized by a prompt punishment.’ (64)
فَعَقَرُوهَا فَقَالَ تَمَتَّعُوا۟ فِى دَارِكُمْ ثَلَـٰثَةَ أَيَّامٍ ۖ ذَٰلِكَ وَعْدٌ غَيْرُ مَكْذُوبٍ (٦٥)
But they hamstrung her, whereupon he said, ‘Enjoy yourselves in your homes for three days: that is a promise not to be belied!’ (65)

Commentary

Now let us see what response the opponents of Salih gave to his vibrant and truth-seeking logic?

To influence Salih, or at least to neutralize the impact of his words among the masses, they employed a psychological tactic. In colloquial terms, they tried to "flatter him" and said: "O Salih! Before this, you were the source of our hope."
