We used to seek refuge in you during difficulties, consult with you, and we believed in your intellect, wisdom, and sagacity; we never harbored any doubt regarding your benevolence and compassion (قَالُوا يَا صَالِحُ قَدْ كُنْتَ فِينَا مَرْجُوًّا قَبْلَ هَذَا).

But unfortunately, you have dashed our hopes. By opposing the practice of idol-worship and our gods—which is the way of our ancestors and considered among the honors of our people—you have shown that you neither hold respect for the elders, nor believe in our intellect and wisdom, nor are you a defender of our traditions.

"Do you really want to forbid us from worshipping what our fathers worshipped?" (أَتَنْهَانَا أَنْ نَعْبُدَ مَا يَعْبُدُ آبَاؤُنَا).

"The truth is that regarding the faith to which you invite us—namely, the creed of Monotheism—we are in doubt and hesitation; not only do we have doubt, but we are also suspicious of it" (وَإِنَّنَا لَفِي شَكٍّ مِمَّا تَدْعُونَا إِلَيْهِ مُرِيبٍ).

Here we see that the strayed people, to justify their wrongdoing and incorrect thoughts and actions, take refuge under the umbrella of their ancestors and the halo of sanctity that usually surrounds them. This is the same archaic logic that has existed among all deviant nations since ancient times to justify superstitions, and it remains in full force even now in the age of the atom and space.

However, this great Prophet of God, without becoming despondent of their guidance, and without their deceitful words having the slightest effect on his great soul, replied with his characteristic composure: "He said: O my people! Tell me, if I have a clear proof from my Lord, and mercy has come to me from Him, illuminating my heart and awakening my thought, and I have become acquainted with truths I did not know before, can I still choose silence and fail to convey the Divine message and not fight against deviations and ugliness?!" (قَالَ يَا قَوْمِ أَرَأَيْتُمْ إِنْ كُنْتُ عَلَى بَيِّنَةٍ مِنْ رَبِّي وَآتَانِي مِنْهُ رَحْمَةً...). In this state, "If I disobey the command of Allah, who can help me against His punishment?" (فَمَنْ يَنْصُرُنِي مِنَ اللَّهِ إِنْ عَصَيْتُهُ).

"But know that these words of yours and your arguments based on the ways of your ancestors and the like will have no effect on me except to increase my conviction in your being losers" (فَمَا تَزِيدُونَنِي غَيْرَ تَخْسِيرٍ).

Then, to demonstrate a miracle and a sign of the truthfulness of his call through deeds that are beyond human power and rely solely on the power of the Lord, he approached them and said: "O my people! This she-camel of the Lord is for you a sign and a miracle" (وَيَا قَوْمِ هَذِهِ نَاقَةُ اللَّهِ لَكُمْ آيَةً).

"Leave it to graze in Allah's earth from the pastures and grasses of the wilderness" (فَذَرُوهَا تَأْكُلْ فِي أَرْضِ اللَّهِ) "and never touch it with harm, for if you do so, a Divine punishment will soon overtake you" (وَلَا تَمَسُّوهَا بِسُوءٍ فَيَأْخُذَكُمْ عَذَابٌ قَرِيبٌ).

The She-Camel of Salih

"Naqah" (ناقة) linguistically means a female camel. In the above verse and some other verses of the Quran, it is attributed to "Allah" [^1], and this indicates that this she-camel possessed special characteristics. Given that it is mentioned in the above verse as a "Sign" (Ayah), a Divine miracle, and proof of truthfulness, it becomes clear that this was no ordinary she-camel and was extraordinary in one or more aspects.

However, the verses of the Quran do not detail what the specific characteristics of this she-camel were. We only know that it was not a normal, everyday camel.

The only thing mentioned in two places in the Quran is that Salih announced to his people regarding this she-camel that the water of that region must be rationed: one day for the she-camel and one day for the people (هَذِهِ نَاقَةٌ لَهَا شِرْبٌ وَلَكُمْ شِرْبُ يَوْمٍ مَعْلُومٍ—Surah Ash-Shu'ara, Verse 155; and in Surah Al-Qamar, Verse 28, we also read: وَنَبِّئْهُمْ أَنَّ الْمَاءَ قِسْمَةٌ بَيْنَهُمْ كُلُّ شِرْبٍ مُحْتَضَرٌ). In Surah Ash-Shams, a brief reference is also made to this matter where it says: فَقَالَ لَهُمْ رَسُولُ اللَّهِ نَاقَةَ اللَّهِ وَسُقْيَاهَا (Surah Ash-Shams, Verse 13).

But it is not entirely clear how this division of water was extraordinary. One possibility is that the animal consumed a vast amount of water such that it claimed the entire spring for itself. Another possibility is that when the animal entered the watering place, other animals did not dare to enter. As for how this animal could use all the water, there is a possibility that the water of that village was scarce, like villages that have nothing more than a small spring and are forced to dam the water in a pit throughout the day and night so that a certain amount collects and becomes usable.

On the other hand, it can be inferred from some verses of Surah Ash-Shu'ara that the people of Thamud did not live in a water-scarce region; rather, they possessed gardens, springs, crops, and palm trees: أَتُتْرَكُونَ فِي مَا هَاهُنَا آمِنِينَ فِي جَنَّاتٍ وَعُيُونٍ وَزُرُوعٍ وَنَخْلٍ طَلْعُهَا هَضِيمٌ (Surah Ash-Shu'ara, Verses 146 to 148).

In any case, as we mentioned, the Quran has expressed this matter regarding the she-camel of Salih in a general and brief manner.

However, in some narrations reported through both Shi'a and Sunni sources, we read that among the wonders of this she-camel's creation was that it emerged from the heart of a mountain, and other characteristics are also narrated for it which are not appropriate to detail here.

Regardless, despite all the emphasis that this great Prophet, Salih, placed regarding that she-camel, they finally decided to destroy it. This was because its existence, with its extraordinary nature, caused the people to wake up and lean toward Salih. Therefore, a group of the rebellious leaders of Thamud—who saw the influence of Salih's call as a hindrance to their interests and never wished for the people to awaken (since with the awakening of God's creation, the foundations of their colonialism and exploitation would crumble)—plotted to eliminate the she-camel. A group was commissioned for this task, and finally, one of them attacked the she-camel and, with a blow or blows dealt to it, brought it down (فَعَقَرُوهَا).

"Aqaruha" (عقروها) is derived from the root "Aqr" (عقر) (rhyming with zulm), meaning the origin, foundation, and root of something. "Aqartu al-ba'ir" (عقرت البعير) means I slaughtered the camel. Since killing the camel causes its existence to be uprooted from its foundation, this root is used in this sense. Sometimes it is interpreted as hamstringing the camel or cutting its legs instead of slaughtering, but in reality, they all return to one thing and the result is the same (take note).

[^1]: This type of attribution is called "Idafah Tashrifi" (Honorific Attribution) in literary terms; meaning an attribution that indicates the honor and importance of something. Two examples are seen in the above verse: "Naqat Allah" (The She-Camel of Allah) and "Ard Allah" (The Earth of Allah). In other cases, "Bayt Allah" (The House of Allah) and "Shahr Allah" (The Month of Allah) and the like are also used.

Ideological Bond

Interestingly, we read in Islamic narrations that the person who killed the she-camel was only one individual; yet, the Quran attributes this act to the entire population of Salih's opponents and uses the plural form: "فَعَقَرُوهَا" (they hamstrung her).

This is because Islam considers internal satisfaction with a matter and an ideological bond with it as equivalent to participating in it. In reality, the conspiracy for this act was not individualistic; even the person who carried out the deed did not rely solely on his own strength but was encouraged by the collective power and support of the group. Certainly, such an act cannot be considered an individual deed but rather a group and collective action.

The Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام, says: "Only one man hamstrung the she-camel of Thamud, but Allah encompassed them all with punishment because they all encompassed it with their approval." [^1]

Numerous other narrations with the same meaning or similar have been narrated from the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله and the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt عليهم السلام, which clarify the extraordinary importance Islam places on ideological bonds and coordinated intellectual programs. As an example, we read a few parts below:

The Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله said: "Whoever witnesses an affair and hates it is like one who was absent from it; and whoever is absent from an affair but is pleased with it is like one who witnessed it." [^2]

Imam Ali ibn Musa al-Rida عليه السلام says: "If a man is killed in the East and another man in the West is pleased with his killing, the one who is pleased is, in the sight of Allah, the Almighty and Majestic, a partner to the killer." [^3]

It is also narrated from Ali عليه السلام that he said: "One who is pleased with the action of a group is like one who entered into it with them. And for everyone who enters into falsehood, there are two sins: the sin of doing it and the sin of being pleased with it." (And the one who was only pleased has one sin). [^4]

To understand the depth and breadth of the intellectual and ideological bond in Islam—which knows no boundaries in terms of time and space—it is sufficient to consider this meaningful and stirring statement of Ali عليه السلام in the Nahj al-Balagha:

When he was victorious over the fire-starting rebels in the Battle of Jamal, and the companions of Ali عليه السلام were happy with this victory (which was the victory of Islam over polytheism and ignorance), one of them said: "How I wished my brother were present in this field to see your victory over the enemy with his own eyes." The Imam turned to him and asked:

"Was your brother's heart with us?" He replied: "Yes." The Imam said: "Then he was present with us (do not grieve; he too participated with us in this field)."

Then he added: "Indeed, there have participated with us in this army of ours people who are still in the loins of men and the wombs of women; time will soon bring them forth, and faith will be strengthened by them." [^5]

Undoubtedly, those who participate in a program and endure all its difficulties and hardships possess a special distinction. However, this does not mean that others have absolutely no share in it; rather, both at that time and in future centuries and ages, all those who have a bond with that program in terms of thought and ideology are, in a way, partners in it.

This issue—which perhaps has no parallel or equivalent in any of the world's ideologies—is based on an important social reality: those who are similar to another in their way of thinking, even if they did not participate in a specific program he performed, will certainly enter into similar programs in their own environment and time. This is because human actions are always a reflection of their thoughts, and it is impossible for a person to be committed to an ideology without it appearing in their actions.

From the first step, Islam implements reforms in the realm of the human soul and spirit, so that the stage of action is reformed automatically. According to this instruction we read above, whenever news reaches a Muslim that a certain good deed should be done, he immediately tries to take a correct stance toward it, harmonizing his heart and soul with the good and seeking hatred for the evil. This internal effort will undoubtedly affect his actions, and his intellectual bond will lead to a practical bond.

At the end of the verse, we read: "After the rebellion and disobedience of the people and the destruction of the she-camel, Salih warned them and said: 'Enjoy yourselves in your homes for three full days with whatever blessings you desire, and know that after these three days, the Divine punishment and retribution will arrive'" (فَقَالَ تَمَتَّعُوا فِي دَارِكُمْ ثَلَاثَةَ أَيَّامٍ).

Take this seriously; I am not lying; this is a true and certain promise (ذَلِكَ وَعْدٌ غَيْرُ مَكْذُوبٍ).

[^1]: Nahj al-Balagha, Sermon 201. [^2]: Wasa'il al-Shi'a, Vol. 11, p. 409. [^3]: Wasa'il al-Shi'a, Vol. 11, p. 410. [^4]: Wasa'il al-Shi'a, Vol. 11, p. 411. [^5]: Nahj al-Balagha, Sermon 12.

Ayahs

فَلَمَّا جَآءَ أَمْرُنَا نَجَّيْنَا صَـٰلِحًا وَٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ مَعَهُۥ بِرَحْمَةٍ مِّنَّا وَمِنْ خِزْىِ يَوْمِئِذٍ ۗ إِنَّ رَبَّكَ هُوَ ٱلْقَوِىُّ ٱلْعَزِيزُ (٦٦)
So when Our edict came, We delivered Salih and the faithful who were with him by a mercy from Us and from the [punishment and] disgrace of that day. Your Lord is indeed the All-strong, the All-mighty. (66)
وَأَخَذَ ٱلَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا۟ ٱلصَّيْحَةُ فَأَصْبَحُوا۟ فِى دِيَـٰرِهِمْ جَـٰثِمِينَ (٦٧)
The Cry seized those who were wrongdoers, and they lay lifeless prostrate in their homes, (67)
كَأَن لَّمْ يَغْنَوْا۟ فِيهَآ ۗ أَلَآ إِنَّ ثَمُودَا۟ كَفَرُوا۟ رَبَّهُمْ ۗ أَلَا بُعْدًا لِّثَمُودَ (٦٨)
as if they had never lived there. Behold! Thamud indeed defied their Lord. Now, away with Thamud! (68)

Commentary

The Fate of the People of Thamud

In these verses, the manner in which the punishment descended upon this rebellious people (the people of Thamud) after the conclusion of the three-day period is described: "When Our command for the punishment of this group arrived, We delivered Salih and those who believed with him by a mercy from Us" (فَلَمَّا جاءَ أَمْرُنا نَجَّيْنا صالِحاً وَ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا مَعَهُ بِرَحْمَةٍ مِنَّا).

Not only were they saved from physical and material punishment, but "We also saved them from the disgrace, humiliation, and ignominy that overtook this rebellious people on that day" (وَ مِنْ خِزْيِ يَوْمِئِذٍ) [1]. "For indeed, your Lord is the All-Strong, the All-Powerful over all things, and Dominant over every affair" (إِنَّ رَبَّكَ هُوَ الْقَوِيُّ الْعَزِيزُ). Nothing is impossible for Him, and no power has the ability to oppose His will. For this reason, saving a group of believers from the midst of a vast multitude drowning in divine punishment causes Him no difficulty or trouble. It is Divine Mercy that necessitates that the innocent do not burn in the fire of the sinners, and that the believers are not ensnared because of the faithless.

"But the [heavenly] blast overtook those who did wrong, and that blast was so severe, heavy, and terrifying that because of it, they all fell prostrate [dead] in their homes" (وَ أَخَذَ الَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا الصَّيْحَةُ فَأَصْبَحُوا فِي دِيارِهِمْ جاثِمِينَ‌). They died and were destroyed, and their traces were scattered to the wind in such a way "as if they had never dwelt there" (كَأَنْ لَمْ يَغْنَوْا فِيها).

"Know that the people of Thamud disbelieved in their Lord and cast His commands behind them" (أَلا إِنَّ ثَمُودَ كَفَرُوا رَبَّهُمْ‌).

"Away with the people of Thamud from the grace and mercy of the Lord, and a curse be upon them" (أَلا بُعْداً لِثَمُودَ).

Here, several points should be noted

1- Divine Mercy Toward the Believers

In these verses, we see once again that Divine Mercy toward the believers is so abundant that before the punishment descends, He moves all of them to a place of safety and security. He never burns the "dry and the wet" together (the innocent with the guilty) as a matter of divine retribution and punishment.

Of course, unpleasant events such as floods, general epidemics, and earthquakes may occur that encompass both the small and the great, but these events do not necessarily have the aspect of divine retribution and punishment. Otherwise, it would be impossible within the logic of the Lord's justice for even one innocent person to be seized for the crime of millions of sinners.

Certainly, it is entirely possible that individuals who remain silent and passive among a group of sinners and do not fulfill their responsibilities in combating corruption may meet the same fate. However, if they fulfill their responsibilities, it is impossible for an event sent as a punishment to overtake them. (We have explained this subject in discussions related to theology regarding the descent of calamities and events in books of theology [1]).

2- The Punishment of the Rebellious is Not Merely Material

From the above verses, it is clearly understood that the punishment of the rebellious and the transgressive does not only have a material aspect but also includes a spiritual dimension. This is because the end of their affair, their deadly fate, and their lives tainted with disgrace are recorded as scandalous chapters in history, while for the people of faith, golden lines of history are written.

3- What is Meant by the "Blast" (Sayhah)?

Sayhah (صيحة) literally means a great sound that usually comes from the mouth of a human or an animal, but it is not exclusive to that; rather, it includes any kind of massive sound.

In the verses of the Quran, we read that several sinful nations were punished by a heavenly blast: one was this very people of Thamud, another was the people of Lut (Surah Al-Hijr, Verse 73), and another was the people of Shu'ayb (Surah Hud, Verse 94).

From other verses of the Quran regarding the people of Thamud, it is understood that their punishment was by means of a thunderbolt: "But if they turn away, then say: I have warned you of a thunderbolt like the thunderbolt of 'Ad and Thamud" (فَإِنْ أَعْرَضُوا فَقُلْ أَنْذَرْتُكُمْ صاعِقَةً مِثْلَ صاعِقَةِ عادٍ وَ ثَمُودَ - Fussilat, Verse 13). This indicates that what is meant by the "blast" is the terrifying sound of a thunderbolt. Can the terrifying sound of a thunderbolt destroy a population? The answer to this question is certainly positive, for we know that when sound waves exceed a certain limit, they can break glass, even destroy some buildings, and disable the internal organism of the human body.

We have heard that when aircraft break the sound barrier (moving at a speed greater than the speed of sound waves), individuals fall unconscious to the ground, or women suffer miscarriages, or all the windows of buildings located in that area shatter.

Naturally, if the intensity of the sound waves is even greater than this, it can easily produce fatal disturbances in the nerves, cerebral vessels, and heart movements, causing the death of human beings.

Of course, according to the verses of the Quran, the end of this world will also be with a universal blast (ما يَنْظُرُونَ إِلَّا صَيْحَةً واحِدَةً تَأْخُذُهُمْ وَ هُمْ يَخِصِّمُونَ‌ - Ya-Sin, Verse 49), just as the Resurrection will begin with an awakening blast (إِنْ كانَتْ إِلَّا صَيْحَةً واحِدَةً فَإِذا هُمْ جَمِيعٌ لَدَيْنا مُحْضَرُونَ‌ - Ya-Sin, Verse 53).

4- The Literal Meaning of "Jathim"

Jathim (جاثم) is from the root jatham (جثم) meaning to sit on one's knees, and also meaning to fall on one's face. (For further explanation in this regard, refer to Volume 6 of Tafsir Nemouneh, page 240).

From the expression jathimin, it is inferred that the heavenly blast caused their death while their lifeless bodies fell to the ground. However, it appears from some narrations that the fire of the thunderbolt burned them. These two are not contradictory, as the terrifying effect of the thunderbolt's sound appears immediately, while the effects of its burning—especially for those who were inside buildings—appear afterward.

5- The Meaning of "Yaghnaw"

Yaghnaw (يغنوا) is from the root ghina (غنى) meaning to reside in a place. It is not unlikely that it is derived from the original concept of ghina meaning "self-sufficiency," because one who is self-sufficient has a prepared home and is not forced to move from one house to another at any time. The phrase "as if they had never dwelt there" (كَأَنْ لَمْ يَغْنَوْا فِيها), which appears regarding the people of Thamud and also the people of Shu'ayb, means that the scroll of their lives was folded in such a way that it was as if they had never been among the inhabitants of that land.

[^1]: Khizy (خزي) in the language means a break or defeat that comes upon a person, whether by himself or another, and it includes any kind of extraordinary scandal and humiliation. [^1]: In Volume 7 of Tafsir Nemouneh, page 130, there are also explanations that are effective for understanding the purpose of such verses—refer to the books "The Creator of the World" and "In Search of God."

Ayahs

وَلَقَدْ جَآءَتْ رُسُلُنَآ إِبْرَٰهِيمَ بِٱلْبُشْرَىٰ قَالُوا۟ سَلَـٰمًا ۖ قَالَ سَلَـٰمٌ ۖ فَمَا لَبِثَ أَن جَآءَ بِعِجْلٍ حَنِيذٍ (٦٩)
Certainly Our messengers came to Abraham with the good news, and said, ‘Peace!’ ‘Peace!’ He replied. Presently he brought [for them] a roasted calf. (69)
فَلَمَّا رَءَآ أَيْدِيَهُمْ لَا تَصِلُ إِلَيْهِ نَكِرَهُمْ وَأَوْجَسَ مِنْهُمْ خِيفَةً ۚ قَالُوا۟ لَا تَخَفْ إِنَّآ أُرْسِلْنَآ إِلَىٰ قَوْمِ لُوطٍ (٧٠)
But when he saw their hands not reaching for it, he took them amiss and felt a fear of them. They said, ‘Do not be afraid. We have been sent to the people of Lot.’ (70)
وَٱمْرَأَتُهُۥ قَآئِمَةٌ فَضَحِكَتْ فَبَشَّرْنَـٰهَا بِإِسْحَـٰقَ وَمِن وَرَآءِ إِسْحَـٰقَ يَعْقُوبَ (٧١)
His wife, standing by, laughed as We gave her the good news of [the birth of] Isaac, and of Jacob, after Isaac. (71)
قَالَتْ يَـٰوَيْلَتَىٰٓ ءَأَلِدُ وَأَنَا۠ عَجُوزٌ وَهَـٰذَا بَعْلِى شَيْخًا ۖ إِنَّ هَـٰذَا لَشَىْءٌ عَجِيبٌ (٧٢)
She said, ‘Oh, my! Shall I, an old woman, bear [children], and [while] this husband of mine is an old man?! That is indeed an odd thing!’ (72)
قَالُوٓا۟ أَتَعْجَبِينَ مِنْ أَمْرِ ٱللَّهِ ۖ رَحْمَتُ ٱللَّهِ وَبَرَكَـٰتُهُۥ عَلَيْكُمْ أَهْلَ ٱلْبَيْتِ ۚ إِنَّهُۥ حَمِيدٌ مَّجِيدٌ (٧٣)
They said, ‘Are you amazed at Allah’s dispensation? [That is] Allah’s mercy and His blessings upon you, members of the household. Indeed He is all-laudable, all-glorious.’ (73)

Commentary

A Glimpse into the Life of the Idol-Breaker

Now it is the turn for a segment from the life of Ibrahim, this hero and idol-breaker. Of course, the account of the eventful life of this great Prophet (عليه السلام) appears more extensively in other Surahs of the Quran (such as Al-Baqarah, Al-Imran, An-Nisa, Al-An'am, Al-Anbiya, and others). However, here, only one part of His life is mentioned, which pertains to the story of the people of Lut and the punishment of that defiled and rebellious group. First, it says:

"And certainly, Our messengers came to Ibrahim with glad tidings" (وَلَقَدْ جَاءَتْ رُسُلُنَا إِبْرَاهِيمَ بِالْبُشْرَىٰ).

As is understood from the subsequent verses, these divine messengers were the same angels commissioned to demolish the cities of the people of Lut, but they first came to Ibrahim (عليه السلام) to deliver a message.

Regarding what this "glad tiding" they carried was, two possibilities exist, and there is no obstacle to combining both: First, the glad tidings of the birth of Ishaq and Ya'qub, for a long life had passed for Ibrahim and He still had no child, while He longed to have a child or children who would carry the banner of prophethood. Therefore, the announcement of the birth of Ishaq and Ya'qub was considered a great glad tiding for Him.

The second is that Ibrahim was deeply distressed by the corruption and rebellion of the people of Lut; when He learned that they [the angels] had such a mission [to punish them], He became happy.

In any case: "When the messengers entered upon Him, they said, 'Peace!'" (قَالُوا سَلَامًا). "He also replied to them with 'Peace'" (قَالَ سَلَامٌ). "And it was not long before He brought a roasted calf" (فَمَا لَبِثَ أَنْ جَاءَ بِعِجْلٍ حَنِيذٍ). "‘Ijl" (عجل) means calf, and "Hanidh" (حنيذ) means roasted. Some have suggested that Hanidh does not refer to just any kind of roasting, but specifically to meat placed upon stones and set beside a fire so that it roasts and cooks tenderly without the fire directly touching it.

From this sentence, it is understood that one of the etiquettes of hospitality is to prepare food for the guest as quickly as possible, because when a guest arrives—especially if they are a traveler—they are often tired and hungry; they need both food and rest. Their food should be prepared early so they can rest.

Some critics might say that one roasted calf is too much for a few guests! However, considering that, firstly, there is debate regarding the number of these guests—which the Quran does not explicitly state (some have written three, some four, some nine, and some eleven, and even more is possible)—and secondly, Ibrahim had followers, friends, workers, and acquaintances. It is customary that sometimes when guests arrive, food is prepared several times the guests' needs, and everyone partakes in it.

But at this moment, a strange event occurred: Ibrahim observed that the newly arrived guests did not stretch their hands toward the food. This was unusual for Him, and for this reason, He felt a sense of alienation toward them, which caused Him fear (فَلَمَّا رَأَىٰ أَيْدِيَهُمْ لَا تَصِلُ إِلَيْهِ نَكِرَهُمْ وَأَوْجَسَ مِنْهُمْ خِيفَةً).

This matter stemmed from an ancient custom and habit that still exists today among peoples who adhere to the good traditions of the past: if someone partakes of another's food—eating their "bread and salt," so to speak—they will not harbor ill intentions toward them. For this reason, if someone truly intends harm toward another, they try not to eat their bread and salt. On this basis, Ibrahim became suspicious of these guests' actions and thought they might have ill intentions.

The messengers, having realized this, quickly relieved Ibrahim of this thought and "said to Him: 'Do not fear, for we have been sent to the people of Lut'" (قَالُوا لَا تَخَفْ إِنَّا أُرْسِلْنَا إِلَىٰ قَوْمِ لُوطٍ). Meaning, we are angels and commissioned to punish a tyrannical people, and angels do not eat food.

At this moment, Ibrahim's wife (Sarah), who was standing there, laughed (وَامْرَأَتُهُ قَائِمَةٌ فَضَحِكَتْ).

This laughter might have been because she too was deeply distressed and worried about the atrocities of the people of Lut, and learning of the proximity of their punishment became a source of joy and delight for her.

There is also the possibility that this laughter was out of wonder or even fear, for laughter is not exclusive to joyful events; rather, sometimes a person laughs out of intense fear or distress. There is a famous proverb among the Arabs: "The worst of hardships is that which makes one laugh" (شر الشدائد ما يضحك).

Or the laughter was because of why the newly arrived guests, despite the hospitality being prepared, did not reach for the food.

The possibility has also been raised that her laughter was due to joy over the glad tidings of a child, although the literal appearance of the verse seems to negate this interpretation, as the glad tidings of Ishaq were given to her after this laughter. Unless it is said that they first gave Ibrahim the glad tidings that He would have a child, and Sarah considered the possibility that she would bear such a child for Ibrahim, but she wondered how it was possible for an old woman of her age to bear a child for her elderly husband. Thus, she questioned them in wonder, and they explicitly told her that yes, this child would be from her. A careful look at the verses of Surah Adh-Dhariyat also supports this meaning.

It is noteworthy that some commentators insist that "Dahikat" (ضَحِكَتْ) here should be from the root "Dahk" (ضَحْك) meaning menstruation. They have said it was exactly at this moment that Sarah, at her advanced age and after reaching menopause, once again experienced the monthly cycle which was a sign of the possibility of childbirth; hence, when she was given the glad tidings of Ishaq's birth, she was fully able to believe it. They have argued this using the Arabic linguistic phrase "Dahikat al-aranib" (ضَحِكَتِ الأرانبُ), meaning "the rabbits menstruated." However, this possibility is unlikely for several reasons: First, it has not been heard that this root is used in this sense for humans in the Arabic language. Therefore, Raghib in the book Al-Mufradat, when mentioning this meaning, explicitly states that this is not the interpretation of the phrase "Fa-dahikat," as some commentators have imagined; rather, the meaning of the phrase is the same meaning of laughing, but concurrent with the state of laughter, the monthly cycle also came upon her, and these two have been confused.

Thirdly, if this phrase meant the appearance of that feminine state, Sarah should not have wondered at the glad tidings of Ishaq afterward, because with the presence of this state, bearing a child is not strange. Yet, from the subsequent sentences in this very verse, it is understood that she not only wondered but cried out: "Woe to me! Shall I bear a child while I am an old woman?!" In any case, this possibility in the interpretation of the verse seems very unlikely.

Then it adds: "And We gave her glad tidings of Ishaq, and after Ishaq, of Ya'qub" (فَبَشَّرْنَاهَا بِإِسْحَاقَ وَمِنْ وَرَاءِ إِسْحَاقَ يَعْقُوبَ).

In reality, they gave her glad tidings of both a son and a "grandson"—one Ishaq and the other Ya'qub—both of whom were Prophets of God.

Ibrahim's wife, Sarah, who was deeply hopeless and despondent about having a child given her and her husband's advanced age, "cried out in a very astonished tone: 'Woe to me! Shall I bear a child while I am an old woman, and this my husband is an old man? Indeed, this is a strange thing!'" (قَالَتْ يَا وَيْلَتَىٰ أَأَلِدُ وَأَنَا عَجُوزٌ وَهَٰذَا بَعْلِي شَيْخًا ۖ إِنَّ هَٰذَا لَشَيْءٌ عَجِيبٌ).

She had the right to wonder, for firstly, according to verse 29 of Surah Adh-Dhariyat, she was a barren woman even in her youth, and on the day they gave her this news, according to commentators and the Book of Genesis in the Torah, she was ninety years old or more, and her husband Ibrahim was about a hundred years old or more.

Here the question arises: why did Sarah argue both with her own old age and her husband's old age, while we know that women usually stop their monthly cycles—which is the sign of readiness for childbirth—after the age of fifty, after which the possibility of bearing children is weak for them, but medical tests have shown that men, in terms of sperm production, have the readiness to become fathers until an advanced age?

The answer to this question is clear: although this is possible in men, in any case, for them too at a very advanced age, this probability becomes weak. Therefore, according to verse 54 of Surah Al-Hijr, Ibrahim Himself also wondered at this glad tiding because of old age.

Additionally, from a psychological perspective, Sarah perhaps was not unwilling to avoid taking the "blame" alone!

In any case, the messengers of the Lord immediately brought her out of this wonder and reminded her of the history of God's extraordinary favors upon this family and their miraculous salvation from the clutches of events, and "said to her: 'Do you wonder at the decree of Allah?'" (قَالُوا أَتَعْجَبِينَ مِنْ أَمْرِ اللَّهِ).

While "the mercy of Allah and His blessings be upon you, O people of the house" (رَحْمَتُ اللَّهِ وَبَرَكَاتُهُ عَلَيْكُمْ أَهْلَ الْبَيْتِ).

The same God who rescued Ibrahim from the clutches of the tyrant Nimrod and kept Him safe in the heart of the fire; the same God who gave Ibrahim, the hero and idol-breaker who single-handedly attacked all tyrants, power, steadfastness, and insight. [^1] This divine mercy and blessing were not only for that day and that time, but have continued and still continue in this household. What blessing is greater than the existence of the Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله) and the Infallible Imams (علیهم السلام) who appeared in this household?

Some commentators have argued with this verse that a person's wife is also included in the title "Ahl al-Bayt," and this title is not exclusive to children and parents. Of course, this argument is correct, and even if this verse did not exist, this meaning is correct according to the content of the word "Ahl." However, there is no obstacle to some being part of the "Ahl al-Bayt" of a Prophet like the Prophet of Islam, yet being excluded from the "Ahl al-Bayt" spiritually due to separating their path (more explanation on this will come, God willing, under verse 33 of Surah Al-Ahzab).

And at the end of the verse, for further emphasis, the angels said: "Indeed, He is Praiseworthy and Glorious" (إِنَّهُ حَمِيدٌ مَجِيدٌ). In fact, mentioning these two attributes of the Lord is a reason for the preceding sentence, for "Hamid" means one whose actions are praised—this name of God refers to the abundant blessings He bestows upon His servants for which they praise Him—and "Majid" is said of one who grants blessings even before they are deserved. Is it strange for a God who possesses these attributes to grant such a blessing (meaning noble children) to the household of His Prophet?

[^1]: The sentence "رَحْمَتُ اللَّهِ وَبَرَكَاتُهُ عَلَيْكُمْ" may be a declarative sentence in the position of a state (hal), and it may also have the aspect of a supplication (du'a), but the first possibility seems closer.

Ayahs

فَلَمَّا ذَهَبَ عَنْ إِبْرَٰهِيمَ ٱلرَّوْعُ وَجَآءَتْهُ ٱلْبُشْرَىٰ يُجَـٰدِلُنَا فِى قَوْمِ لُوطٍ (٧٤)
So when the awe had left Abraham and the good news had reached him, he pleaded with Us concerning the people of Lot. (74)
إِنَّ إِبْرَٰهِيمَ لَحَلِيمٌ أَوَّٰهٌ مُّنِيبٌ (٧٥)
Abraham was indeed most forbearing, plaintive, [and] penitent. (75)
يَـٰٓإِبْرَٰهِيمُ أَعْرِضْ عَنْ هَـٰذَآ ۖ إِنَّهُۥ قَدْ جَآءَ أَمْرُ رَبِّكَ ۖ وَإِنَّهُمْ ءَاتِيهِمْ عَذَابٌ غَيْرُ مَرْدُودٍ (٧٦)
‘O Abraham, let this matter alone! Your Lord’s edict has certainly come, and an irrevocable punishment shall overtake them.’ (76)

Commentary

In the preceding verses, we saw that Ibrahim soon realized that the newly arrived guests were not dangerous or intrusive individuals, but rather the messengers of the Lord, who, according to their own words, were heading toward the people of Lut to carry out a mission.

When Ibrahim's fear of them subsided, and on the other hand, they gave him the glad tidings of a son and a worthy successor, he immediately thought of the people of Lut—whom those messengers were commissioned to destroy—and began to dispute and converse with them regarding this matter: (فَلَمَّا ذَهَبَ عَنْ إِبْراهِيمَ الرَّوْعُ وَ جاءَتْهُ الْبُشْرى‌ يُجادِلُنا فِي قَوْمِ لُوطٍ) [^1] "When the fear had left Ibrahim and the glad tidings had reached him, he began to dispute with Us concerning the people of Lut." Here, a question may arise: why did Ibrahim engage in a discussion regarding a defiled and sinful people and dispute with the messengers of the Lord whose mission was by Allah's command (and for this reason, the expression "يُجادِلُنا"—meaning "he disputed with Us"—is used), while such an act seems beneath the status of a Prophet, especially one as great as Ibrahim?

Therefore, the Quran immediately states in the following verse: "Indeed, Ibrahim was forbearing, most tender-hearted, and ever-turning to Allah" (إِنَّ إِبْراهِيمَ لَحَلِيمٌ أَوَّاهٌ مُنِيبٌ‌) [^2]. In fact, with these three phrases, a concise and implicit answer is given to this question.

To explain: mentioning these attributes for Ibrahim clearly shows that his dispute was a praiseworthy one. This is because it was not clear to Ibrahim whether the decree of punishment had been definitively issued by Allah; rather, he considered the possibility that a window of hope for the salvation of these people still remained, and there was a chance of them awakening. For this reason, there was still room for intercession; thus, he sought the delay of this punishment and retribution, because he was Halim (forbearing), and also very compassionate, and he turned to Allah in all matters.

Therefore, as for what some have said—that if Ibrahim's dispute was with Allah, it would be meaningless, and if it was with His messengers, they could not do anything of their own accord, so in either case, this dispute could not be correct—the answer is as follows:

The answer is that one cannot argue against a definitive decree, but non-definitive commands can be changed with the alteration of conditions and circumstances, as the path to return is not closed. In other words, these are conditional commands, not absolute ones.

However, some have suggested the possibility that the dispute was regarding the salvation of the believers, citing verses 31 and 32 of Surah Al-Ankabut as evidence, where it says:

وَ لَمَّا جاءَتْ رُسُلُنا إِبْراهِيمَ بِالْبُشْرى‌ قالُوا إِنَّا مُهْلِكُوا أَهْلِ هذِهِ الْقَرْيَةِ إِنَّ أَهْلَها كانُوا ظالِمِينَ قالَ إِنَّ فِيها لُوطاً قالُوا نَحْنُ أَعْلَمُ بِمَنْ فِيها لَنُنَجِّيَنَّهُ وَ أَهْلَهُ إِلَّا امْرَأَتَهُ كانَتْ مِنَ الْغابِرِينَ‌: "And when Our messengers came to Ibrahim with the glad tidings, they said, 'Indeed, we will destroy the people of this city; indeed, its people have been wrongdoers.' [Ibrahim] said, 'Indeed, within it is Lut.' They said, 'We are more knowing of who is within it. We will surely save him and his family, except his wife; she is to be of those who remain behind.'"

This interpretation is not correct because it is in no way consistent with the subsequent verse which we will now discuss.

In the next verse, it says: "The messengers soon said to Ibrahim: 'O Ibrahim, desist from this [proposal]'" and set aside intercession, for it is not the place for it (يا إِبْراهِيمُ أَعْرِضْ عَنْ هذا), "for indeed, the decree of your Lord has come" (إِنَّهُ قَدْ جاءَ أَمْرُ رَبِّكَ‌) and the punishment of Allah will come upon them without further discussion (وَ إِنَّهُمْ آتِيهِمْ عَذابٌ غَيْرُ مَرْدُودٍ).

The use of the term "ربك" (your Lord) indicates that this punishment was not merely an act of vengeance, but rather originated from the attribute of the Lordship (Rububiyyah) of God, which is a sign of the nurturing and cultivation of His servants and the reform of human society!

And as for what we read in some narrations: Ibrahim said to the messengers of the Lord: "If there are a hundred believers among these people, will you still destroy them?" They said, "No." He said, "If there are fifty?" They said, "No." He said, "If there are thirty?" They said, "No." He said, "If there are ten?" They said, "No." He said, "If there are five?" They said, "No." He said, "Even if there is one believer among them?" They said, "No." Ibrahim said, "Surely Lut is among them." They replied, "We are more knowing: we will save him and his family except his wife." [^3] This narration in no way proves that the "dispute" refers to this specific conversation; rather, this conversation was about the believers and is separate from the discussion he had regarding the disbelievers. From this, it becomes clear that the verses of Surah Al-Ankabut also do not contradict the interpretation provided above. (Reflect on this).

[^1]: "Raw'" (rhyming with naw') means fear and terror, and "ru'" (rhyming with nuh) means the soul or the part of the soul which is the center where fear and terror descend (refer to Qamus al-Lughah).

[^2]: "Halim" is from hilm, meaning forbearance in the path of reaching a sacred goal. "Awwah" originally means one who sighs frequently, whether due to fear of his responsibilities or the problems and calamities that have befallen the people. "Munib" is from the root inabah, meaning to return and turn back.

[^3]: Tafsir al-Burhan, vol. 2, p. 226.

Ayahs

وَلَمَّا جَآءَتْ رُسُلُنَا لُوطًا سِىٓءَ بِهِمْ وَضَاقَ بِهِمْ ذَرْعًا وَقَالَ هَـٰذَا يَوْمٌ عَصِيبٌ (٧٧)
When Our messengers came to Lot, he was distressed on their account and in a predicament for their sake, and he said, ‘This is a terrible day!’ (77)
وَجَآءَهُۥ قَوْمُهُۥ يُهْرَعُونَ إِلَيْهِ وَمِن قَبْلُ كَانُوا۟ يَعْمَلُونَ ٱلسَّيِّـَٔاتِ ۚ قَالَ يَـٰقَوْمِ هَـٰٓؤُلَآءِ بَنَاتِى هُنَّ أَطْهَرُ لَكُمْ ۖ فَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ وَلَا تُخْزُونِ فِى ضَيْفِىٓ ۖ أَلَيْسَ مِنكُمْ رَجُلٌ رَّشِيدٌ (٧٨)
Then his people came running toward him, and they had been committing vices aforetime. He said, ‘O my people, these are my daughters: they are purer for you. Be wary of Allah and do not humiliate me with regard to my guests. Is there not a right-minded man among you?’ (78)
قَالُوا۟ لَقَدْ عَلِمْتَ مَا لَنَا فِى بَنَاتِكَ مِنْ حَقٍّ وَإِنَّكَ لَتَعْلَمُ مَا نُرِيدُ (٧٩)
They said, ‘You certainly know that we have no interest in your daughters, and indeed you know what we want.’ (79)
قَالَ لَوْ أَنَّ لِى بِكُمْ قُوَّةً أَوْ ءَاوِىٓ إِلَىٰ رُكْنٍ شَدِيدٍ (٨٠)
He said, ‘If only I had the power to deter you, or could take refuge in a mighty support!’ (80)

Commentary

The Ignominious Life of the People of Lot

In the verses of Surah Al-A'raf, a portion of the fate of the people of Lot was mentioned, and we saw its interpretation there. However, here, in accordance with the explanation of the stories of the Prophets and their nations, and fitting the connection that the previous verses had with the life of Lot and his people, it pulls back the curtain on another part of the life of this deviant and misguided folk, in order to pursue the primary goal—which is the salvation and prosperity of the entire human society—from another angle.

First, it says: "When Our messengers came to Lot, he was distressed by them and felt straitened in spirit for them, and grief and sorrow overwhelmed his entire being" (وَلَمَّا جَاءَتْ رُسُلُنَا لُوطًا سِيءَ بِهِمْ وَضَاقَ بِهِمْ ذَرْعًا). It is mentioned in Islamic narrations and commentaries that Lot was working in his field at that time when he suddenly saw a group of handsome young men approaching him, wishing to be his guests. His desire to host guests on one hand, and his awareness of the fact that the presence of these beautiful youths in a city drowned in the filth of sexual deviation would cause all sorts of trouble and likely public disgrace, put him under severe pressure.

These matters passed through his mind like agonizing thoughts, and he whispered to himself: "This is a distressing and terrifying day" (وَقَالَ هَذَا يَوْمٌ عَصِيبٌ).

"Si'a" (سِيءَ) is from the root "sa'a" (ساء), meaning to become ill-disposed or distressed.

Some have interpreted "dhar'" (ذَرْع) as the heart and others as character; therefore, "daqa bihim dhar'an" (ضَاقَ بِهِمْ ذَرْعًا) means his heart became constricted and troubled because of these uninvited guests under such difficult circumstances.

However, as Fakhr al-Razi quoted from "al-Azhari" in his commentary, "dhar'" in such contexts means capacity or power, and originally refers to the distance between a camel's forelegs while walking.

Naturally, when a camel is loaded beyond its capacity, it is forced to place its legs closer together and shorten the distance between them while walking. For this reason, this expression gradually came to mean distress due to the weight of an event.

From some lexicons like "Al-Qamus," it is understood that this expression is used when the severity of an event is such that a person sees all paths of remedy closed to him.

"Asib" (عَصِيب) is from the root "asb" (عَصْب), meaning to bind things together. Since difficult and distressing events entwine a person and seemingly place them amidst hardship, the title "Asib" is applied to them; the Arabs also call scorching hot days "Yawm al-Asib."

In any case, Lot had no choice but to accept the newly arrived guests into his home and host them. However, so as not to mislead them, he warned them several times along the way that this city had wicked and deviant people, so that if the guests were unable to confront them, they could take account of their own situation.

In a narration, we read that Allah had commanded the angels not to punish them until this Prophet testified three times to the wickedness and deviation of this people (meaning that even in executing Allah's command regarding a sinful nation, the standards of a fair trial and judgment must be observed!). These messengers heard Lot's testimony three times during the journey. [1] In some narrations, it is stated that Lot delayed his guests until night fell, hoping that away from the eyes of that wicked and filthy people, he could host them while preserving his dignity and honor.

But what can be done when a person's enemy is inside his own house?

[1]: Majma' al-Bayan, under the verse.

Lot's wife, who was a faithless woman and aided this sinful people, became aware of the arrival of these young and handsome guests and went upon the roof. First by clapping her hands, and then by lighting a fire and raising smoke, she alerted a group of this deviant people that "fat prey" had fallen into the trap! [1] Here, the Quran says: "His people came rushing toward him with haste, greed, and gluttony to reach their goal" (وَجَاءَهُ قَوْمُهُ يُهْرَعُونَ إِلَيْهِ). [2] The same people and group whose pages of life were black and stained with ignominy, "and before they used to commit evil deeds" (وَمِنْ قَبْلُ كَانُوا يَعْمَلُونَ السَّيِّئَاتِ).

At this moment, Lot had every right to tremble and cry out from the intensity of his distress, and he said to them: "I am even ready to give my own daughters to you in marriage; these are purer for you" (قَالَ يَا قَوْمِ هَؤُلَاءِ بَنَاتِي هُنَّ أَطْهَرُ لَكُمْ).

Come and "fear Allah, do not disgrace me, and do not shame me regarding my guests with your evil intentions" (فَاتَّقُوا اللَّهَ وَلَا تُخْزُونِ فِي ضَيْفِي).

Alas! "Is there not among you a single right-minded, wise, and worthy man" who can restrain you from these shameful and brazen acts? (أَلَيْسَ مِنْكُمْ رَجُلٌ رَشِيدٌ).

But this criminal people shamelessly replied to all this nobility of Prophet Lot: "You well know that we have no right to your daughters" (قَالُوا لَقَدْ عَلِمْتَ مَا لَنَا فِي بَنَاتِكَ مِنْ حَقٍّ).

"And you certainly know what we want!" (وَإِنَّكَ لَتَعْلَمُ مَا نُرِيدُ).

[1]: Al-Mizan, Vol. 10, p. 362. [2]: "Yuhra'un" (يُهْرَعُونَ) is from the root "ihra'" (إهراع), meaning to drive intensely; as if the rebellious sexual instinct was driving this misguided people intensely toward the guests of Prophet Lot!

It was here that this noble Prophet found himself so besieged by the event and so distressed that he cried out: "Would that I had power and strength within myself" to defend my guests and crush you stubborn ones (قَالَ لَوْ أَنَّ لِي بِكُمْ قُوَّةً).

"Or that I had a strong support of kin, tribe, followers, and powerful allies at my disposal, so that with their help I could overcome you deviants" (أَوْ آوِي إِلَى رُكْنٍ شَدِيدٍ).

Several points should be noted here

[Who are meant by the daughters of Lot?]

1- This sentence that Lot said when the people attacked his house with the intention of assaulting his guests—that "these daughters of mine are pure and lawful for you, use them and do not approach sin"—has raised questions among commentators.

First, did he mean Lot's biological and actual daughters? While according to historical accounts they were no more than two or three, how could he propose them to this entire crowd? Or did he mean all the daughters of the tribe and the city, whom the leader of a tribe usually refers to as his own daughters?

The second possibility seems weak because it goes against the apparent meaning, and the first possibility is correct. This proposal by Lot was because the attackers were a group of the villagers, not all of them. Furthermore, he wanted to show his ultimate sacrifice here: that "I am even ready to give my own daughters in marriage to you to combat sin and preserve the dignity of my guests." Perhaps through this unparalleled sacrifice, their dormant consciences would awaken and they would return to the path of truth.

Another question: was the marriage of a faithful girl like Lot's daughters to faithless disbelievers permissible that he made such a proposal? Two answers have been given to this: One is that in the law of Lot, as in the beginning of Islam, the prohibition of such marriages did not exist; hence the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) gave his daughter "Zaynab" in marriage to "Abu al-As" before he accepted Islam, but this ruling was later abrogated. [1] The other is that Lot's proposal was a conditional marriage (conditioned upon faith), meaning: "These are my daughters; come and believe so that I may give them to you in marriage."

From this, it becomes clear that the criticism against Prophet Lot—asking how he could propose his pure daughters to a group of ruffians—is incorrect, because his proposal was conditional and intended to prove his ultimate desire for their guidance.

[What is the meaning of the word "Athar"?]

2- It should be noted that the word "Athar" (أَطْهَرُ - purer) does not mean that their shameful and ugly act was "pure" and marriage was "purer." Rather, this is an expression used in Arabic and other languages when making a comparison. For example, to someone driving at a breakneck speed, it is said: "Arriving late is better than never arriving." Or "Abstaining from suspicious food is better than risking one's life." In a narration, we also read that the Imam (عليه السلام), during a time of intense Taqiyya (precautionary dissimulation) and feeling danger from the Abbasid caliphs, said:

"By Allah, to break my fast for one day of the month of Ramadan is more beloved to me than having my neck struck."

Even though neither being killed is good nor is never arriving, such expressions are used. [1]

[A "Right-minded" Man]

3- Lot's expression at the end of his speech, "Is there not among you a single right-minded man?" conveys the truth that even the existence of one right-minded man among a people or tribe is sufficient to prevent their shameful acts. That is, if there were one wise person with intellectual maturity among you, you would never have come to my house with the intention of assaulting my guests.

This expression clearly highlights the role of a "right-minded man" (رجل رشيد) in leading human societies. This is the same reality of which we have seen many examples throughout human history.

[The Ultimate Deviation of This Group]

4- It is astonishing that those misguided people said to Lot, "We have no right to your daughters." This expression reflects the ultimate deviation of this group; that is, a polluted society reaches a point where it sees truth as falsehood and falsehood as truth. It does not consider marriage to pure and faithful girls to be within its "right" at all, but conversely, it considers sexual deviation to be its right.

Becoming accustomed and habituated to sin in its final and dangerous stages is such that one considers the most shameful and ugly acts as their right and deems the purest forms of sexual enjoyment as "not their right."

[1]: Wasa'il, Vol. 7, p. 95 (Book of Fasting, Chapter 57).

[A Hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام]

5- In a hadith from Imam al-Sadiq (عليه السلام), we read that in interpreting the above verses, he said: "The meaning of 'power' (قوة) is the 'Qa'im' (the Riser), and the 'strong support' (ركن شديد) is his 313 companions!" [1] This narration might seem strange—how can one believe that Lot in his own era wished for the appearance of such a person with such companions?

However, familiarity with the narrations regarding the interpretation of Quranic verses has taught us that they often express a general law through one of its clearest manifestations. In reality, Lot wished that he had at his disposal determined men with sufficient spiritual and physical power to establish a divine government—like the men who will form the global government in the era of the uprising of the Mahdi (عليه السلام)—so that he could rise up and, relying on power, combat corruption and deviation, and crush such stubborn and shameless individuals.

[1]: Tafsir al-Burhan, Vol. 2, p. 228.

Ayahs

قَالُوا۟ يَـٰلُوطُ إِنَّا رُسُلُ رَبِّكَ لَن يَصِلُوٓا۟ إِلَيْكَ ۖ فَأَسْرِ بِأَهْلِكَ بِقِطْعٍ مِّنَ ٱلَّيْلِ وَلَا يَلْتَفِتْ مِنكُمْ أَحَدٌ إِلَّا ٱمْرَأَتَكَ ۖ إِنَّهُۥ مُصِيبُهَا مَآ أَصَابَهُمْ ۚ إِنَّ مَوْعِدَهُمُ ٱلصُّبْحُ ۚ أَلَيْسَ ٱلصُّبْحُ بِقَرِيبٍ (٨١)
They said, ‘O Lot, we are messengers of your Lord. They will never get at you. Set out with your family in a watch of the night; and none of you shall turn round, except your wife; indeed she will be struck by what strikes them. Indeed their tryst is the dawn. Is not the dawn [already] near?’ (81)
فَلَمَّا جَآءَ أَمْرُنَا جَعَلْنَا عَـٰلِيَهَا سَافِلَهَا وَأَمْطَرْنَا عَلَيْهَا حِجَارَةً مِّن سِجِّيلٍ مَّنضُودٍ (٨٢)
So when Our edict came, We made its topmost part its nethermost, and We rained on it stones of laminar shale, (82)
مُّسَوَّمَةً عِندَ رَبِّكَ ۖ وَمَا هِىَ مِنَ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ بِبَعِيدٍ (٨٣)
sent in from your Lord [for the profligate], never far from the wrongdoers. (83)

Commentary

The End of This Oppressive Group

Finally, when the messengers of the Lord witnessed Lut’s intense anxiety and the spiritual torment he was enduring, they unveiled the secret of their mission and "said: 'O Lut, we are the messengers of your Lord; do not worry, and know that they shall never reach you!'" (قَالُوا يَا لُوطُ إِنَّا رُسُلُ رَبِّكَ لَنْ يَصِلُوا إِلَيْكَ).

It is interesting that the angels of God do not say "they cannot harm us," but rather "they shall not reach you, O Lut," to cause harm. This expression is either because they did not consider themselves separate from Lut—since they were his guests and any violation of their sanctity was a violation of Lut’s—or because they wanted to make him understand: "We are the messengers of God and it is certain we are beyond their reach; even you, who are a human of their kind, shall not be reached by them through the grace of the Lord."

In verse 37 of Surah Al-Qamar, we read: وَ لَقَدْ راوَدُوهُ عَنْ ضَيْفِهِ فَطَمَسْنا أَعْيُنَهُمْ: "And they indeed demanded of him his guests, so We blinded their eyes." This verse indicates that at this moment, the attacking mob lost their sight by the will of the Lord and were unable to attack. In some narrations, we also read that one of the angels threw a handful of dust at their faces, and they became blind.

In any case, Lut’s awareness of the status of his guests and their mission was like cold water poured upon the burning heart of this great Prophet. In an instant, he felt the heavy burden of grief and sorrow lifted from his heart; a spark of joy began to shine in his eyes, and like a critically ill patient who catches sight of a physician with a Christ-like breath, he felt a sense of peace. He realized that the era of sorrow and grief was about to end, and the time for joy and salvation from the clutches of this disgraceful, animalistic people had arrived!

The guests immediately gave this command to Lut: "Take your family and leave this land in the darkness of this very night" (فَأَسْرِ بِأَهْلِكَ بِقِطْعٍ مِنَ اللَّيْلِ) [1].

[1]: "Asra" is derived from "Isra," meaning to travel at night. Therefore, the mention of "Layl" (night) in the verse is for further emphasis, and "Qit'in" refers to the darkness of the night, indicating: leave from among them unnoticed at a time when the black veils of night have fallen everywhere and these heedless people are either asleep or intoxicated by wine and lust.

"But be careful that none of you looks back" (وَ لا يَلْتَفِتْ مِنْكُمْ أَحَدٌ).

"The only one who will violate this command and will be afflicted by the same calamity that strikes the sinful people is your sinful wife" (إِلَّا امْرَأَتَكَ إِنَّهُ مُصِيبُها ما أَصابَهُمْ).

In the commentary of the phrase "لا يَلْتَفِتْ مِنْكُمْ أَحَدٌ" (none of you shall look back), commentators have offered several possibilities: 1. That no one should physically look behind them. 2. That you should not think about your wealth and belongings in the city; only save yourselves from this peril. 3. That none of your family members should lag behind this small caravan. 4. That as you exit, the earthquake and the precursors of the punishment will begin; do not look back and move away quickly.

However, there is no obstacle to all these possibilities being included in the meaning of the verse [1]. Finally, they spoke the last word to Lut: "The moment of the descent of the punishment and their appointed time is the morning, and with the first rays of dawn, the life of this people will set" (إِنَّ مَوْعِدَهُمُ الصُّبْحُ).

"Now arise and leave the city as soon as possible; is not the morning near?" (أَ لَيْسَ الصُّبْحُ بِقَرِيبٍ).

[1]: Regarding which phrase "إِلَّا امْرَأَتَكَ" (except your wife) is an exception to, commentators have two views: First, it is an exception to the phrase "none of you shall look back," meaning Lut, his family, and even his wife all set out together, and none of them looked back according to the messengers' command except Lut's wife. Due to her attachment to the people and her concern for their fate, she stood for a moment and looked back, and according to a narration, at that moment a stone from those raining down on the city struck and killed her! The second possibility is that it is an exception to the phrase "take your family," meaning take all your family except your wife. In this case, Lut's wife remained in the city. However, the first possibility seems more appropriate.

In some narrations, we read that when the angels mentioned the morning as the time of punishment, Lut—due to the intense distress he felt from these corrupt people whose disgraceful acts had wounded his heart and filled his soul with grief—asked the angels: "Since they are to be destroyed, the sooner the better!"

But they comforted Lut and said: "Is not the morning near?"

Finally, the moment of punishment arrived and ended the wait of the Prophet Lut, just as the Quran says: "When Our command came, We turned it upside down, and rained upon them stones of layered baked clay" (فَلَمَّا جاءَ أَمْرُنا جَعَلْنا عالِيَها سافِلَها وَ أَمْطَرْنا عَلَيْها حِجارَةً مِنْ سِجِّيلٍ مَنْضُودٍ).

"Sijjil" is originally a Persian word derived from "Sang" (stone) and "Gil" (clay). Thus, it is something that is neither completely hard like stone nor soft like clay, but rather an intermediate state between the two.

"Mandud" is from the root "Nadad," meaning to be placed one upon another or coming in succession. This means the rain of stones was so rapid and continuous that it was as if the stones were riding upon one another.

But these stones were not ordinary stones; they were "marked stones, with your Lord" (مُسَوَّمَةً عِنْدَ رَبِّكَ).

However, do not imagine that these stones were exclusive to the people of Lut; "they are not far from any oppressive and tyrannical people" (وَ ما هِيَ مِنَ الظَّالِمِينَ بِبَعِيدٍ).

This deviant people oppressed both themselves and their society; they gambled with the fate of their nation as well as with faith and human morality. No matter how much their compassionate leader cried out, they did not listen and instead mocked him. They reached such a level of insolence and shamelessness that they even wanted to violate the sanctity of their leader's guests.

These people, who had turned everything upside down, deserved to have their city overturned! Not only should it be turned upside down, but a rain of stones should crush the last traces of life there and bury them, such that not even a trace of them remains in that land—only a terrifying, desolate wasteland and a silent graveyard buried under pebbles.

Is it only the people of Lut who must be punished so? Certainly not. Such a fate awaits every deviant group and oppressive nation—sometimes under a rain of pebbles, sometimes under the strikes of incendiary bombs, and sometimes under the pressures of lethal social conflicts; ultimately, each in some form or another.

Key Points

1- Why was the moment of the descent of punishment "the morning"? A careful look at the above verses raises this question: what role did the morning play? Why did the punishment not descend in the middle of the night?

Is it because the group attacking Lut's house, once they were blinded, returned to their people and recounted the event, causing them to reflect for a moment on what was happening, and Allah gave them this respite until morning so they might wake up and return to Him?!

Or is it that Allah did not wish to launch a night raid upon them, and for this reason, He commanded the agents of punishment to wait until the arrival of morning?

Commentaries have written almost nothing on this matter, but what we mentioned above are possibilities worth considering.

2- Why "upside down"? We have said that punishment must be proportionate to the nature of the sin. Since this people overturned everything in the path of sexual deviation, Allah also turned their cities upside down. And since—according to narrations—they constantly rained down foul language upon one another, Allah rained down stones upon their heads.

3- Why a rain of stones? Was the rain of pebbles before the overturning of their cities, during it, or after it? There is discussion among commentators, and the Quranic verses are not explicit in this regard, as the phrase is connected with the conjunction "wa" (and), which does not necessarily imply chronological order.

However, some commentators, such as the author of Al-Manar, believe that this rain of stones was either before the overturning or during it. Its philosophy was that those scattered individuals who were in corners and were not buried under the rubble should not escape; they too should receive the retribution for their foul deeds!

The narration stating that Lut's wife turned her head when she heard the sound and was struck by a stone at that moment indicates that these two (the overturning and the rain of stones) occurred together.

But setting these aside, there is no obstacle to pebbles being rained upon them even after the overturning to intensify the punishment and erase their traces, such that their land was hidden beneath them and its remains vanished.

4- Why "marked"? We said the phrase "مُسَوَّمَةً عِنْدَ رَبِّكَ" conveys that these stones were marked by God. However, there is debate among commentators as to how they were marked. Some have said there were signs on these stones showing they were not ordinary stones but were specifically sent for divine punishment, so they would not be mistaken for other falling rocks. For this reason, others have said the stones bore no resemblance to earthly stones; rather, observing their condition showed they were a type of celestial stone descending from outside the Earth's sphere!

Some have also said they had marks in the knowledge of the Lord, such that each was precisely aimed at a specific individual and a specific spot, indicating that divine punishments are so calculated that it is even known which person must be crushed by which stone; it is not random or without criteria.

5- The Prohibition of Homosexuality Homosexuality, whether among men or women, is among the very great sins in Islam, and both carry a legal penalty (hadd).

The penalty for homosexuality among men, whether for the active or passive partner, is execution. Various methods for this execution are described in jurisprudence. Of course, the proof of this sin must be established through the authoritative and definitive methods mentioned in Islamic jurisprudence and the narrations from the Infallibles (علیهم السلام), and even three confessions alone are not sufficient; one must confess to this act at least four times.

As for the penalty for homosexuality among women, after four confessions or proof by four just witnesses (under the conditions stated in jurisprudence), it is one hundred lashes. Some jurists have stated that if a married woman performs this act, her penalty is execution.

The implementation of these penalties has precise and calculated conditions mentioned in Islamic jurisprudence books.

The narrations cited from the leaders of Islam in condemnation of homosexuality are so numerous and shocking that upon studying them, anyone feels that the ugliness of this sin is such that few sins stand on its level.

Among them, we read in a narration from the Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله): "When the people of Lut did what they did, the earth wept to its Lord until its tears reached the sky, and the sky wept until its tears reached the Throne (Arsh). At this, Allah revealed to the sky: 'Stone them!' and revealed to the earth: 'Swallow them!'" [1] (It is obvious that weeping and tears are metaphorical and allegorical).

And in a hadith from Imam al-Sadiq (عليه السلام), we read that the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) said: "Whoever has intercourse with a youth will arrive at the gathering on the Day of Resurrection in a state of ritual impurity (janaba) such that all the waters of the world will not purify him. Allah's wrath is upon him, He has cursed him, and He has prepared Hell for him—and what an evil destination it is..." Then he said: "When a male mounts a male, the Throne (Arsh) trembles because of it." [2]

In another hadith from Imam al-Sadiq (عليه السلام), we read: "Those who submit to such an act are the remnants of Sodom (the people of Lut)." Then he added: "I do not say they are from their children, but they are from their nature (tinat)." He was asked: "The same city of Sodom that was overturned?" He replied: "Yes, there were four cities: Sodom, Sarim, Ladna, and Ghamira (Gomorrah)." [3]

In another narration from the Commander of the Faithful, Ali (عليه السلام), we read that he said: "I heard the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) saying: 'Allah curses the men who imitate women (by having intercourse with men) and the women who imitate men.'" [4]

[1]: Tafsir al-Burhan, Vol. 2, p. 231. [2]: Wasa'il al-Shi'a, Vol. 14, p. 249. [3]: Wasa'il al-Shi'a, Vol. 14, p. 253. [4]: Wasa'il al-Shi'a, Vol. 14, p. 255.

The Philosophy of Prohibiting Homosexuality

Although in the Western world, where sexual pollution is extremely high, such ugliness is not loathed—and it is even heard that in some countries like England, according to a law passed with utter insolence by Parliament, this subject has found legal permission!!—the prevalence of such ugliness never diminishes its vileness, and its moral, psychological, and social harms remain established in their place.

Sometimes, some followers of the materialist school who have such pollutions say to justify their actions: "We know of no medical prohibition for it!"

But they have forgotten that, fundamentally, any sexual deviation affects the entire psyche and structure of the human being and disrupts his balance.

To explain: A human being, in a natural and healthy state, has a sexual inclination toward the opposite sex, and this inclination is among the most deep-rooted human instincts and the guarantor of the survival of his species.

Any act that diverts this inclination from its natural path creates a type of illness and psychological deviation in the human being.

A man who has an inclination toward the same sex, or a man who submits to such an act, is not a complete man. In books on sexual matters, "homosexuality" is mentioned as one of the most important deviations.

Continuing this practice gradually kills sexual inclinations toward the opposite sex in a person. In the case of one who submits to this act, feminine feelings gradually emerge in him, and both suffer from extreme sexual weakness and so-called "frigidity," such that after a while, they will not be capable of natural intercourse (intercourse with the opposite sex).

Given that the sexual feelings of a man and a woman affect both their bodily organism and their specific psyche and character, it becomes clear to what extent losing natural feelings strikes a blow to the human body and soul. It is even possible that individuals afflicted with such deviation may suffer such sexual weakness that they no longer have the power to produce children.

Such persons are often not psychologically healthy and feel a kind of alienation from themselves and from the society to which they belong.

They gradually lose the power of will, which is the condition for any kind of victory, and a type of wandering and indifference nests in their souls.

If they do not soon decide to reform themselves—and even, if necessary, seek help from a physical or psychological physician—and this act becomes a habit for them, quitting it will become difficult. However, in any case, it is never too late to quit this ugly habit; it requires decision and action.

In any case, psychological wandering will gradually lead them toward narcotics, alcoholic beverages, and other moral deviations, and this is another great misfortune.

Interestingly, in Islamic narrations, these harms are pointed out in short and meaningful phrases. For instance, in a narration from Imam al-Sadiq (عليه السلام), we read that someone asked him: "Why has Allah forbidden sodomy?" He replied: "Because if intercourse with boys were permissible, men would have no need for women (and would lose desire for them), and this would lead to the cutting off of the human race and the neglect of natural intercourse, and in the permission of that is much corruption." [1]

It is also noteworthy that one of the punishments Islam has considered for such individuals is that marriage to the sister, mother, and daughter of the passive partner is forbidden for the active partner; that is, if such an act occurred before marriage, these women become eternally forbidden (haram) to him.

[1]: Wasa'il al-Shi'a, Vol. 14, p. 252.

The final point to be reminded of here is that the drawing of individuals toward this kind of sexual deviation has many different causes. Sometimes even the way parents behave with their children, or lack of supervision over children of the same sex, and the way they socialize and sleep together in the house may be factors in this pollution.

Sometimes another moral deviation may lead to this one.

It is noteworthy that in the accounts of the people of Lut, we read that the factor for their pollution with this sin was that they were a stingy people. Since their cities were located on the route of the caravans to Sham (Syria) and they did not want to host guests and travelers, they initially pretended to them that they intended sexual assault to make the guests and travelers flee from them. However, this act gradually became a habit for them, and deviant sexual inclinations gradually awakened in their being, and their situation reached a point where they were polluted from head to toe [1].

Even inappropriate jokes that sometimes occur among boys or girls toward their own sex can sometimes be the motivation for being drawn into these deviations. In any case, one must carefully watch these issues, rescue the polluted ones quickly, and seek success from God in this path.

The Morals of the People of Lut

In Islamic narrations and histories, other ugly and disgraceful acts are reported from them alongside sexual deviation. For instance, in Safinat al-Bihar, we read: "It is said their gatherings included various types of abominations such as insults, absurdity, slapping, gambling, playing with sticks, throwing stones at those who passed by them, playing musical instruments, and exposing their private parts!" [2]

It is clear that in such a polluted environment, deviation and ugliness take on new dimensions every day, and fundamentally the vileness of disgraceful acts is removed. They advance so far in this path that no act is considered ugly or abominable in their eyes!

And more wretched than them are the nations and peoples who, in the era of the progress of sciences and knowledge, walk in that same path, and sometimes their acts are so disgraceful and scandalous that they cause the acts of the people of Lut to be forgotten.

[1]: Bihar al-Anwar, Vol. 12, p. 147. [2]: Safinat al-Bihar, p. 517.

Ayahs

۞ وَإِلَىٰ مَدْيَنَ أَخَاهُمْ شُعَيْبًا ۚ قَالَ يَـٰقَوْمِ ٱعْبُدُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ مَا لَكُم مِّنْ إِلَـٰهٍ غَيْرُهُۥ ۖ وَلَا تَنقُصُوا۟ ٱلْمِكْيَالَ وَٱلْمِيزَانَ ۚ إِنِّىٓ أَرَىٰكُم بِخَيْرٍ وَإِنِّىٓ أَخَافُ عَلَيْكُمْ عَذَابَ يَوْمٍ مُّحِيطٍ (٨٤)
And to Midian [We sent] Shu‘ayb, their brother. He said, ‘O my people! Worship Allah. You have no other god besides Him. Do not diminish the measure or the balance. Indeed I see that you are faring well, but I fear for you the punishment of an all-embracing day.’ (84)
وَيَـٰقَوْمِ أَوْفُوا۟ ٱلْمِكْيَالَ وَٱلْمِيزَانَ بِٱلْقِسْطِ ۖ وَلَا تَبْخَسُوا۟ ٱلنَّاسَ أَشْيَآءَهُمْ وَلَا تَعْثَوْا۟ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ مُفْسِدِينَ (٨٥)
‘O my people! Observe fully the measure and the balance, with justice, and do not cheat the people of their goods, and do not act wickedly on the earth, causing corruption.’ (85)
بَقِيَّتُ ٱللَّهِ خَيْرٌ لَّكُمْ إِن كُنتُم مُّؤْمِنِينَ ۚ وَمَآ أَنَا۠ عَلَيْكُم بِحَفِيظٍ (٨٦)
‘What remains of Allah’s provision is better for you, should you be faithful, and I am not a keeper over you.’ (86)

Commentary

Madyan, the Land of Shu’ayb

With the conclusion of the cautionary story of the people of Lot, it is now the turn of the people of "Shu’ayb" and the inhabitants of "Madyan." These were a people who abandoned the path of Monotheism (Tawhid) and wandered into the stony wilderness of polytheism and idol-worship. They did not worship idols alone; rather, they worshipped their dirhams, dinars, property, and wealth. For the sake of these, they tainted their flourishing trade and commerce with fraud, short-changing (under-measuring), and other wrongdoings.

At the beginning, it says: "And to Madyan [We sent] their brother Shu’ayb":

(وَ إِلى‌ مَدْيَنَ أَخاهُمْ شُعَيْباً) The word "their brother" (أخاهم), as we have previously noted, is used to convey the utmost affection of the Prophets toward their people. This is not only because he was a member of their tribe and clan, but also because he was as well-wishing and compassionate toward them as a brother.

"Madyan" (rhyming with Maryam) is the name of the settlement of Shu’ayb and his tribe. This city was located east of the Gulf of Aqaba, and its people were descendants of Ishmael (Isma’il) عليه السلام. They engaged in trade with Egypt, Lebanon, and Palestine.

Today, the city of Madyan is called "Ma'an," though some geographers have applied the name Madyan to the people living between the Gulf of Aqaba and Mount Sinai.

The name "Midian" also appears in the Torah, but as the name of certain tribes (and of course, it is common to apply a single name to both a city and its inhabitants). [^1] This Prophet, this compassionate and kind brother, followed the method of all Prophets at the start of their mission: he first invited them to the most fundamental pillar of religion, namely "Monotheism," and "said: O my people! Worship Allah; you have no deity other than Him" (قالَ يا قَوْمِ اعْبُدُوا اللَّهَ ما لَكُمْ مِنْ إِلهٍ غَيْرُهُ‌).

This is because the call to Monotheism is a call to break all false deities (Taghuts) and all ignorant customs; no social or moral reform is possible without it.

Then, he pointed to one of the economic vices that stems from the spirit of polytheism and idol-worship, which was rampant among the people of Madyan at that time, and said:

"Do not decrease the measure and the weight when buying and selling" (وَ لا تَنْقُصُوا الْمِكْيالَ وَ الْمِيزانَ‌).

"Mikyal" and "Mizan" refer to the measure and the scale, and decreasing them means short-selling and failing to give people their due rights.

The prevalence of these two practices among them was a sign of the lack of order, accountability, and standards in their affairs, and an example of plunder, exploitation, and oppression within their wealthy society.

Immediately after this command, this great Prophet pointed to two reasons for it:

First, he says: "Accepting this advice will cause the doors of blessings to open for you, leading to the progress of trade, the lowering of prices, and social tranquility; in short, 'I see you in prosperity' (I am your well-wisher), and I am certain that this advice will be a source of goodness and blessing for your society." (إِنِّي أَراكُمْ بِخَيْرٍ).

There is also another possibility in interpreting this sentence: Shu’ayb is saying, "I see you possessing abundant blessings and much good," therefore there is no reason for you to stoop to meanness, violate people's rights, and show ingratitude instead of thankfulness for these blessings.

Secondly: "I fear for you the punishment of an encompassing day" (وَ إِنِّي أَخافُ عَلَيْكُمْ عَذابَ يَوْمٍ مُحِيطٍ).

"Muheet" (encompassing) here is an adjective for "Day," meaning an "all-encompassing" day. Naturally, the encompassing nature of the day implies the encompassing nature of the punishment of that day. This could refer to the punishment of the Hereafter as well as the all-encompassing punishments of this world.

Therefore, you neither need such [corrupt] practices, nor are you safe from Allah's punishment which lies in wait for you; thus, you must reform your situation as soon as possible.

The next verse again emphasizes their economic system. While Shu’ayb had previously forbidden his people from short-selling, here he invites them to fulfill people's rights, saying: "O my people! Give full measure and weight with justice" (وَ يا قَوْمِ أَوْفُوا الْمِكْيالَ وَ الْمِيزانَ بِالْقِسْطِ).

This principle—namely the establishment of equity and justice and giving everyone their due right—must govern your entire society.

Then, he goes a step further and says: "Do not depreciate the things and goods of the people, and do not decrease anything from them" (وَ لا تَبْخَسُوا النَّاسَ أَشْياءَهُمْ‌).

"Bakhs" originally means to decrease something by way of oppression and injustice.

The reason why lands farmed without irrigation are called "bakhs" is because their water is scarce (relying only on rain) or because their yield is less compared to irrigated lands.

If we look at the breadth of this sentence's meaning, it is a call to respect all individual and social rights for all peoples and nations. "Depreciating rights" (Bakhs al-haqq) manifests in different forms in every environment and era—sometimes even in the form of "unconditional aid," "cooperation," or "granting loans" (as is the method of exploiters in our own age).

At the end of the verse, he goes even further, saying: "And do not act wickedly on the earth, spreading corruption" (وَ لا تَعْثَوْا فِي الْأَرْضِ مُفْسِدِينَ‌).

Corruption through short-selling, corruption through usurping people's rights and encroaching upon the rights of others, corruption by disrupting social standards and measures, corruption by finding fault with property and persons, and finally, corruption by violating the sanctity of the dignity, honor, reputation, and lives of the people!

The phrase "la ta’thaw" means "do not commit corruption"; therefore, mentioning "mufsideen" (corruptors) after it is for the sake of emphasizing this issue as much as possible.

The two verses above beautifully reflect the reality that after the issue of belief in Monotheism and correct ideology, a healthy economy holds special importance. It also shows that the collapse of the economic system will be the source of widespread corruption in society.

Finally, he reminded them that increasing the quantity of wealth—wealth obtained through oppression, injustice, and the exploitation of others—will not make them self-sufficient. Rather, "What remains with Allah is better for you, if you are believers" (بَقِيَّتُ اللَّهِ خَيْرٌ لَكُمْ إِنْ كُنْتُمْ مُؤْمِنِينَ‌).

The expression "Baqiyyatullah" (What remains with Allah) is used either because a small amount of lawful profit is "Baqiyyatullah" since it is by Allah's command;

Or because seeking lawful gain causes the permanence of Divine blessing and the continuation of grace;
