Or it refers to the spiritual rewards and merits that remain forever, even if the world and all that is in it perish. Verse 46 of Surah Al-Kahf: "And the enduring good deeds are better with your Lord for reward and better for hope" also points to this.

The phrase "if you are believers" (إِنْ كُنْتُمْ مُؤْمِنِينَ‌) indicates that this reality is understood only by those who have faith in Allah, His wisdom, and the philosophy of His commands.

In numerous narrations, we read that "Baqiyyatullah" has been interpreted as the existence of "Mahdi" عليه السلام or some of the other Imams. For instance, in the book Ikmal al-Din, it is narrated from Imam al-Baqir عليه السلام:

"The first thing the Qa'im عليه السلام will speak when he emerges is this verse: 'Baqiyyatullahi khayrun lakum in kuntum mu'minin.' Then he will say: 'I am the Baqiyyatullah, His Proof (Hujjah), and His Caliph over you.' Then no Muslim shall greet him except by saying: 'Peace be upon you, O Baqiyyatullah on His earth.'" [^2]

We have said many times that although Quranic verses may have been revealed regarding a specific case, they possess universal concepts that can be applied to broader and more comprehensive instances in subsequent ages and centuries.

It is true that in the verse under discussion, the addressees are the people of Shu’ayb, and the meaning of "Baqiyyatullah" is lawful profit and capital or Divine reward. However, any beneficial being that remains from Allah for humanity and becomes a source of good and happiness is considered "Baqiyyatullah."

All the Prophets of Allah and the great leaders are "Baqiyyatullah."

All truthful leaders who remain for a people and nation after struggling against a fierce enemy are "Baqiyyatullah" in this sense.

Similarly, the struggling soldiers who return from the battlefield after victory are also "Baqiyyatullah."

And since the Promised Mahdi عليه السلام is the last leader and the greatest revolutionary leader after the uprising of the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله, he is one of the clearest instances of "Baqiyyatullah" and is most worthy of this title, especially since he is the sole remainder after the Prophets and Imams.

At the end of the verse under discussion, we read from the tongue of Shu’ayb: "My duty was only this delivery of the message, warning, and cautioning that I have given, 'and I am not a guardian over you' (responsible for your actions or tasked with forcing you to accept this path). Here you are, and here is the path, and here is the pit!" (وَ ما أَنَا عَلَيْكُمْ بِحَفِيظٍ).

[^1]: A'lam al-Qur'an, page 573. [^2]: As cited in Tafsir al-Safi under the aforementioned verse.

Ayahs

قَالُوا۟ يَـٰشُعَيْبُ أَصَلَوٰتُكَ تَأْمُرُكَ أَن نَّتْرُكَ مَا يَعْبُدُ ءَابَآؤُنَآ أَوْ أَن نَّفْعَلَ فِىٓ أَمْوَٰلِنَا مَا نَشَـٰٓؤُا۟ ۖ إِنَّكَ لَأَنتَ ٱلْحَلِيمُ ٱلرَّشِيدُ (٨٧)
They said, ‘O Shu‘ayb, does your worship require that we abandon what our fathers have been worshiping, or that we should not do with our wealth whatever we wish? You are indeed [a] gentle and sensible [person].’ (87)
قَالَ يَـٰقَوْمِ أَرَءَيْتُمْ إِن كُنتُ عَلَىٰ بَيِّنَةٍ مِّن رَّبِّى وَرَزَقَنِى مِنْهُ رِزْقًا حَسَنًا ۚ وَمَآ أُرِيدُ أَنْ أُخَالِفَكُمْ إِلَىٰ مَآ أَنْهَىٰكُمْ عَنْهُ ۚ إِنْ أُرِيدُ إِلَّا ٱلْإِصْلَـٰحَ مَا ٱسْتَطَعْتُ ۚ وَمَا تَوْفِيقِىٓ إِلَّا بِٱللَّهِ ۚ عَلَيْهِ تَوَكَّلْتُ وَإِلَيْهِ أُنِيبُ (٨٨)
He said, ‘O my people! Have you considered, should I stand on a manifest proof from my Lord, who has provided me a good provision from Himself? I do not wish to oppose you by what I forbid you. I only desire to put things in order, as far as I can, and my success lies only with Allah: in Him I have put my trust, and to Him I turn penitently. (88)
وَيَـٰقَوْمِ لَا يَجْرِمَنَّكُمْ شِقَاقِىٓ أَن يُصِيبَكُم مِّثْلُ مَآ أَصَابَ قَوْمَ نُوحٍ أَوْ قَوْمَ هُودٍ أَوْ قَوْمَ صَـٰلِحٍ ۚ وَمَا قَوْمُ لُوطٍ مِّنكُم بِبَعِيدٍ (٨٩)
O my people, do not let your defiance toward me lead you to be visited by the like of what was visited on the people of Noah, or the people of Hud, or the people of Salih, and the people of Lot are not distant from you. (89)
وَٱسْتَغْفِرُوا۟ رَبَّكُمْ ثُمَّ تُوبُوٓا۟ إِلَيْهِ ۚ إِنَّ رَبِّى رَحِيمٌ وَدُودٌ (٩٠)
Plead with your Lord for forgiveness, then turn to Him penitently. My Lord is indeed all-merciful, all-affectionate.’ (90)

Commentary

The Groundless Logic of the Stubborn.

Now let us see how these stubborn people reacted to the call of this heavenly reformer.

Those who considered idols to be the legacy of their ancestors and a sign of their cultural authenticity, and who reaped enormous profits from short-changing and fraud in transactions, spoke to Shu'ayb as follows:

"O Shu'ayb! Does your prayer command you that we should leave what our fathers worshipped?!" (قالُوا يا شُعَيْبُ أَ صَلاتُكَ تَأْمُرُكَ أَنْ نَتْرُكَ ما يَعْبُدُ آباؤُنا).

"Or that we should lose our freedom regarding our own wealth and not be able to dispose of our property as we wish?" (أَوْ أَنْ نَفْعَلَ فِي أَمْوالِنا ما نَشاءُ).

"You, who are a forbearing, patient, and understanding person," such words are far-fetched coming from you! (إِنَّكَ لَأَنْتَ الْحَلِيمُ الرَّشِيدُ).

Here, the question arises: why did they focus on Shu'ayb's "prayer" (salat)?

Some commentators have said this was because Shu'ayb prayed extensively and would tell the people: "Prayer restrains man from shameful and evil deeds." However, the ignorant masses, who did not understand the connection between prayer and the abandonment of evils, said to him mockingly: "Do these recitations and movements of yours command you that we should trample upon the traditions of our ancestors and our religious culture, or that we should be deprived of authority over our own wealth?!"

Others have suggested that "salat" refers to his creed and religion, as prayer is the most prominent symbol of faith.

In any case, had they reflected correctly, they would have realized that prayer awakens the sense of responsibility, piety, God-fearingness, and gratitude in man. It reminds him of Allah and His court of justice, washes the dust of selfishness and egoism from the tablet of his heart, and turns him from the limited and polluted world toward the metaphysical realm—the world of purity and goodness. For this very reason, it restrains him from polytheism, idol worship, blind imitation of ancestors, short-changing, and all types of fraud.

Another question that arises here is whether they said the phrase: "إِنَّكَ لَأَنْتَ الْحَلِيمُ الرَّشِيدُ" ("Indeed, you are the forbearing, the right-minded") out of sincerity and belief, or as a form of mockery and sarcasm?

Commentators have entertained both possibilities, but given the mocking tone in the preceding sentence (أَ صَلاتُكَ تَأْمُرُكَ), it appears they said this sarcastically. They were implying that a forbearing and patient person is one who does not express an opinion until they have studied it sufficiently and become certain of its correctness, and a right-minded and wise person is one who does not trample upon the traditions of a people nor strip owners of their freedom of action over their wealth. Thus, [they meant] it is clear you have neither sufficient study, nor a sound mind, nor deep thought, because a sound mind and deep thought require that a person does not abandon the way of his ancestors and does not deprive anyone of their freedom of action!

However, Shu'ayb, in response to those who had interpreted his words as folly and evidence of irrationality, said: "O my people! (O group who are of me and I of you, and I desire for you what I desire for myself) if Allah has given me clear evidence, revelation, and prophethood, and in addition to this, has granted me pure provision and wealth sufficient for my needs, would it then be right for me to oppose His command or to have some [ulterior] motive regarding you and not be your well-wisher?!" (قالَ يا قَوْمِ أَ رَأَيْتُمْ إِنْ كُنْتُ عَلى‌ بَيِّنَةٍ مِنْ رَبِّي وَ رَزَقَنِي مِنْهُ رِزْقاً حَسَناً) [1]. With this statement, Shu'ayb wants to say: "In this task, I have only spiritual, human, and educational motives; I know truths that you do not know, and man is always an enemy to what he does not know."

It is noteworthy that in these verses, the expression "يا قَوْمِ" (O my people) is repeated to mobilize their emotions for accepting the truth and to make them understand that "you are from me and I am from you" (whether "people" here means tribe, clan, and kin, or the group among whom he lived and of whose society he was considered a part).

Then this great Prophet adds: "Do not imagine that I wish to forbid you from something while I myself pursue it" (وَ ما أُرِيدُ أَنْ أُخالِفَكُمْ إِلى‌ ما أَنْهاكُمْ عَنْهُ‌).

"Should I tell you not to short-change and not to permit fraud and cheating in transactions, yet I myself amass wealth by performing these acts? Or should I forbid you from worshipping idols while I myself bow my head in reverence before them? No, it is never so."

From this sentence, it appears they were accusing Shu'ayb of seeking personal profit, and thus he explicitly denies this.

Finally, he tells them: "I have but one goal, and that is your reform and the reform of your society as much as I am able" (إِنْ أُرِيدُ إِلَّا الْإِصْلاحَ مَا اسْتَطَعْتُ‌).

This is the same goal that all prophets pursued: reform of belief, reform of ethics, reform of action, and reform of social relations and systems.

And to reach this goal, "my success is only through Allah" (وَ ما تَوْفِيقِي إِلَّا بِاللَّهِ‌).

For this reason, to carry out my mission and reach this great goal, "upon Him I rely, and to Him I turn" (عَلَيْهِ تَوَكَّلْتُ وَ إِلَيْهِ أُنِيبُ‌).

To solve problems, I strive by relying on His help, and to endure the hardships of this path, I return to Him.

Then he draws their attention to an ethical point: that it often happens that a person, due to hatred and enmity toward someone, or prejudice and stubbornness toward something, ignores all his own interests and forgets his own destiny. He says to them: "O my people! Let not your enmity and hostility toward me lead you to sin, disobedience, and rebellion" (وَ يا قَوْمِ لا يَجْرِمَنَّكُمْ شِقاقِي‌).

"Lest there befall you the same calamities, afflictions, sufferings, and punishments that befell the people of Nuh, or the people of Hud, or the people of Salih" (أَنْ يُصِيبَكُمْ مِثْلُ ما أَصابَ قَوْمَ نُوحٍ أَوْ قَوْمَ هُودٍ أَوْ قَوْمَ صالِحٍ‌).

"Even the people of Lut, with that great calamity—namely the overturning of their cities and being pelted with stones—are not far from you" (وَ ما قَوْمُ لُوطٍ مِنْكُمْ بِبَعِيدٍ).

Neither is their time far from yours, nor the location of their living, nor are your deeds and sins any less than theirs!

Of course, "Madyan," which was the center of Shu'ayb's people, was not far from the land of the people of Lut, as both were in the regions of Greater Syria (Sham). In terms of time, although there was a gap, it was not so great that their history had been forgotten. As for their deeds, although there was apparently a great difference between the sexual deviations of the people of Lut and the economic deviations of the people of Shu'ayb, both were similar in producing corruption in society, disrupting the social order, destroying moral virtues, and spreading depravity. For this reason, we sometimes see in narrations that one dirham of usury—which naturally relates to economic issues—is compared to adultery, which is a sexual impurity [1].

Finally, he gives two instructions to this misguided people, which are in fact the result of all his previous preaching:

First, "seek forgiveness from your Lord" so that you may be cleansed of sin and stay away from polytheism, idol worship, and betrayal in transactions (وَ اسْتَغْفِرُوا رَبَّكُمْ‌).

"And after being purified from sin, turn to Him in repentance," for He is Pure, and one must become pure to go toward Him (ثُمَّ تُوبُوا إِلَيْهِ‌).

In reality, seeking forgiveness (istighfar) is stopping on the path of sin and washing oneself, and repentance (tawbah) is returning toward Him, who is an Infinite Being.

And know that however great and heavy your sin may be, the way of return is open to you, "for my Lord is Merciful and Loving" (إِنَّ رَبِّي رَحِيمٌ وَدُودٌ).

"Wadud" is an intensive form of "wudd," meaning love. Mentioning this word after the word "Rahim" (Merciful) indicates that not only does Allah, by virtue of His mercy, attend to sinful servants who repent, but beyond this, He loves them dearly. Each of these two (mercy and love) is itself a motive for accepting the seeking of forgiveness and the repentance of the servants.

[^1]: It should be noted that the result (jaza) of the conditional clause in the above verse is omitted. The implied result was: "Should I then, despite that, turn away from what I am upon of His worship and the propagation of His religion?"

Ayahs

قَالُوا۟ يَـٰشُعَيْبُ مَا نَفْقَهُ كَثِيرًا مِّمَّا تَقُولُ وَإِنَّا لَنَرَىٰكَ فِينَا ضَعِيفًا ۖ وَلَوْلَا رَهْطُكَ لَرَجَمْنَـٰكَ ۖ وَمَآ أَنتَ عَلَيْنَا بِعَزِيزٍ (٩١)
They said, ‘O Shu‘ayb, we do not understand much of what you say. Indeed we see that you are weak amongst us, and were it not for your tribe, we would have stoned you, and you are not a formidable [challenge] for us.’ (91)
قَالَ يَـٰقَوْمِ أَرَهْطِىٓ أَعَزُّ عَلَيْكُم مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ وَٱتَّخَذْتُمُوهُ وَرَآءَكُمْ ظِهْرِيًّا ۖ إِنَّ رَبِّى بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ مُحِيطٌ (٩٢)
He said, ‘O my people! Is my tribe more formidable in your sight than Allah, to whom you pay no regard? Indeed my Lord comprehends whatever you do. (92)
وَيَـٰقَوْمِ ٱعْمَلُوا۟ عَلَىٰ مَكَانَتِكُمْ إِنِّى عَـٰمِلٌ ۖ سَوْفَ تَعْلَمُونَ مَن يَأْتِيهِ عَذَابٌ يُخْزِيهِ وَمَنْ هُوَ كَـٰذِبٌ ۖ وَٱرْتَقِبُوٓا۟ إِنِّى مَعَكُمْ رَقِيبٌ (٩٣)
O my people! Act according to your ability; I too am acting. Soon you will know who will be overtaken by a punishment that will disgrace him, and who is a liar. So be on the watch; I too will be watching along with you.’ (93)

Commentary

The Mutual Threats of Shu'ayb and His People

Shu'ayb, this great Prophet who was titled Khatib al-Anbiya (the Orator of the Prophets) [^1] due to his calculated, eloquent, and heart-piercing words, delivered his speech—which was the best guide for the material and spiritual life of this group—with complete patience, composure, and compassion. However, let us see how this misguided people responded to him.

They replied with four sentences, all of which reflected their stubbornness, ignorance, and lack of awareness.

First, "They said: O Shu'ayb! We do not understand much of what you say" (قالُوا يا شُعَيْبُ ما نَفْقَهُ كَثِيراً مِمَّا تَقُولُ‌).

Essentially, they were saying: Your words have no beginning or end! There is no valuable content or logic in them for us to even think about! For this reason, there is nothing we wish to use as a basis for action; therefore, do not exhaust yourself further and go to others! Second: "And indeed, we see you as weak among us" (وَ إِنَّا لَنَراكَ فِينا ضَعِيفاً).

Thus, if you think you can establish your illogical words through power and force, that too is a mistake.

Do not imagine that if we do not settle accounts with you, it is out of fear of your power; "If it were not for your family and tribe, and the respect we have for them, we would have killed you in the worst possible way—that is, we would have stoned you!" (وَ لَوْ لا رَهْطُكَ لَرَجَمْناكَ‌).

Interestingly, they referred to Shu'ayb's tribe as "raht," which in the Arabic language refers to a small group of three to seven, or ten, or at most—according to some—forty people. This was an indication that even your tribe has no power in our eyes; rather, it is other considerations that hold us back from this act. This is exactly like when we say to someone: "If it weren't for these four people in your family, we would have taught you a lesson," while in reality, his family is not just four people; rather, the intention is to state that they are of no importance in terms of power.

Finally, they said: "And you are not powerful or invincible against us" (وَ ما أَنْتَ عَلَيْنا بِعَزِيزٍ).

Even if you are considered among the elders of your tribe, because of the path you have chosen, you have no proximity or status in our eyes.

Without losing his composure due to their offensive words and insults, Shu'ayb responded to them with the same eloquent logic and expressive speech:

O my people! Are these few people of my tribe "more dear to you than Allah"? (قالَ يا قَوْمِ أَ رَهْطِي أَعَزُّ عَلَيْكُمْ مِنَ اللَّهِ‌).

You, who do not harm me for the sake of my family—who by your own admission are but a few—why do you not accept my words for the sake of "Allah"? Do a few people count for anything against the majesty of the Lord?

Do you have any respect for Allah? "While you have cast Him and His command behind your backs"? (وَ اتَّخَذْتُمُوهُ وَراءَكُمْ ظِهْرِيًّا [^1]).

And in the end, he says: Do not think that Allah does not see your deeds or hear your words; "Be certain that my Lord encompasses all that you do" (إِنَّ رَبِّي بِما تَعْمَلُونَ مُحِيطٌ).

An eloquent speaker is one who, in the face of all the opposing side's stances, clarifies his own position within his speech. Since the polytheists of Shu'ayb's people, at the end of their speech, implicitly threatened him with stoning and boasted of their power, Shu'ayb defines his position against their threat as follows:

"O my people! Do whatever is in your power and do not hold back, and do not refrain from anything you are capable of doing" (وَ يا قَوْمِ اعْمَلُوا عَلى‌ مَكانَتِكُمْ‌) [^1] "I too am doing my work" (إِنِّي عامِلٌ‌).

"But soon you will know who will be overtaken by a humiliating punishment—me or you—and who is the liar—me or you?" (سَوْفَ تَعْلَمُونَ مَنْ يَأْتِيهِ عَذابٌ يُخْزِيهِ وَ مَنْ هُوَ كاذِبٌ‌).

And since this is the case, you wait, and I too am waiting (وَ ارْتَقِبُوا إِنِّي مَعَكُمْ رَقِيبٌ‌) [^2]. "You wait to see if you can prevail over me with your power, numbers, wealth, and influence; I too am waiting for the painful divine punishment to soon seize you, the misguided multitude, and wipe you from the face of the earth!"

[^1]: Safinat al-Bihar, under the root "Shu'ayb".

[^1]: In the Arabic language, when they want to metaphorically express indifference toward something, they say "I put it under my feet," or "I put it behind my ear," or "I put it behind my back," or "I made it dhihriyya" (I ignored it—I left it behind). Dhihri is derived from the root dhahr (back), and the ya is the ya of attribution (nisba), and the kasra on the dhad is due to changes that sometimes occur in the attributed noun.

[^1]: "Makanah" is a verbal noun or a noun of manner meaning having power over something.

[^2]: "Raqib" means protector, observer, and guardian. It is originally derived from "raqabah" which means neck. This meaning is either because the protector guards the neck of the one under his protection (a metaphor for protecting his life) or because he stretches his neck to carry out the task of guarding and observation.

Ayahs

وَلَمَّا جَآءَ أَمْرُنَا نَجَّيْنَا شُعَيْبًا وَٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ مَعَهُۥ بِرَحْمَةٍ مِّنَّا وَأَخَذَتِ ٱلَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا۟ ٱلصَّيْحَةُ فَأَصْبَحُوا۟ فِى دِيَـٰرِهِمْ جَـٰثِمِينَ (٩٤)
When Our edict came, We delivered Shu‘ayb and the faithful who were with him by a mercy from Us. And the Cry seized those who were wrongdoers, whereat they lay lifeless prostrate in their homes, (94)
كَأَن لَّمْ يَغْنَوْا۟ فِيهَآ ۗ أَلَا بُعْدًا لِّمَدْيَنَ كَمَا بَعِدَتْ ثَمُودُ (٩٥)
as if they had never lived there. Behold, away with Midian!—just as Thamud was done away with! (95)

Commentary

The End of the Life of the Transgressors of Midian

In the history of past nations, we have read many times in the Holy Quran that, in the first stage, the Prophets rose to invite them toward Allah and did not withhold any form of enlightenment, warning, or advice. In the next stage, when the advice proved fruitless for a group, they emphasized the threat of Divine punishment so that the last of those capable of acceptance would submit to the Truth and return to the path of Allah, and so that the argument would be fully established (exhausted). In the third stage, when none of these measures availed, by the decree of Divine Sunnah (law) regarding the purification and cleansing of the earth, the punishment would arrive and remove these thorns from the path.

In the case of the people of Shu’ayb, namely the people of "Midian," the final stage eventually arrived, as the Quran states:

"And when Our command (for the punishment of this misguided, oppressive, and stubborn people) came, We saved Shu’ayb and those who believed with him by a mercy from Us" (وَ لَمَّا جاءَ أَمْرُنا نَجَّيْنا شُعَيْباً وَ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا مَعَهُ بِرَحْمَةٍ مِنَّا).

"Then the heavenly blast and the great death-dealing cry seized the wrongdoers" (وَ أَخَذَتِ الَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا الصَّيْحَةُ).

"As-Sayhah" (the blast), as we have mentioned before, means any great sound. The Quran recounts the destruction of several sinful nations by a heavenly blast; this blast was likely caused by a thunderbolt or the like. As we explained in the story of the people of Thamud, sometimes sound waves can be so powerful that they cause the death of a group.

Following this, it says: "The people of Shu’ayb, due to this heavenly blast, fell prostrate in their homes and died," and their lifeless bodies remained there for a time as a lesson for others (فَأَصْبَحُوا فِي دِيارِهِمْ جاثِمِينَ‌).

The scroll of their lives was folded in such a way that "it was as if they had never dwelt there" (كَأَنْ لَمْ يَغْنَوْا فِيها).

All that wealth for which they committed sin and oppression, and all those palaces, ornaments, glitz, and clamor—all vanished and fell silent.

Finally, just as stated at the end of the accounts of the people of ‘Ad and Thamud, it says: "Away with Midian from the grace and mercy of the Lord, just as Thamud were taken away" (أَلا بُعْداً لِمَدْيَنَ كَما بَعِدَتْ ثَمُودُ).

It is clear that "Midian" here refers to the people of Midian; it was they who were distanced from the mercy of Allah.

Educational Lessons in the Story of Shu’ayb

The memories of the Prophets and the life stories of past nations are always inspiring for subsequent generations because their life trials—trials that sometimes lasted decades or centuries—are placed at everyone's disposal within a few pages of history, and anyone can draw inspiration from them in their own life.

The story of this great Prophet (Shu’ayb) also teaches us many lessons, including:

1- The Importance of Economic Issues In this account, we read that after inviting them to Monotheism (Tawhid), Shu’ayb invited them to truth and justice in financial matters and trade. This itself shows that the economic issues of a society cannot be taken lightly. It also shows that the Prophets were not only commissioned with moral issues; rather, reforming disorganized social and economic conditions also formed an important part of their mission, to the extent that they placed it immediately after the call to Monotheism.

2- Principles Should Not Be Sacrificed for Prejudice In this story, we read that one of the factors in the fall of this misguided nation into the lap of misery was that they forgot the truths due to personal grudges and animosities. However, a wise and realistic person is one who hears and accepts the truth from anyone, even if it be his number one enemy.

3- Prayer Invites to Monotheism and Purity The misguided people of Shu’ayb asked him in astonishment whether his prayer commanded him that they should leave what their fathers worshipped or leave off doing what they willed with their wealth (cheating and fraud).

Perhaps they thought: what effect could these movements and recitations have on such matters? Yet we know that the strongest relationship exists between the two. If prayer is in its true sense—meaning the presence of a human being with his entire existence before Allah—this presence is a ladder of perfection, a means of educating the soul and spirit, and a cleanser of the rust of sin from the heart. This presence strengthens the human will, makes his resolve firm, and distances pride and arrogance from him.

4- Self-Centeredness is the Secret to Stagnation The people of Shu’ayb, as inferred from the above verses, were selfish and self-centered individuals. They considered themselves wise and Shu’ayb ignorant; they mocked him, called his words hollow, and described his person as weak and powerless. This self-centeredness and selfishness eventually darkened the sky of their lives and brought them to ruin!

Not only humans, but even an animal, if it is self-centered, will stop in its tracks. It is said: a horseman reached a stream but noticed with surprise that the horse was unwilling to cross that small, shallow stream. No matter how much he insisted, it was of no use. A wise man arrived and said: "Stir the water of the stream so it becomes muddy; the problem will be solved!" They did this, and the horse crossed calmly. They were surprised and asked him for the secret to solving the problem.

The wise man said: "When the water was clear, the horse saw its own reflection in the water and thought it was itself, and it was not willing to step on itself. As soon as the water became muddy and it forgot itself, it crossed easily!"

5- Faith and Action are Inseparable There are still many who think that by having a simple belief one can be a Muslim, even if no action proceeds from them. There are still many who want a religion that does not create an obstacle in the way of their rebellious desires and leaves them free in every respect.

The story of Shu’ayb shows that this nation also desired such a religion; hence they said to him: we are neither willing to forget the idols of our ancestors nor to lose our freedom of action regarding our property and wealth.

They had forgotten that, fundamentally, the fruit of the tree of faith is action, and the religion of the Prophets was intended to reform human self-indulgence and practical deviations; otherwise, a tree without branches, leaves, and fruit is good for nothing but burning.

Today, this way of thinking has, most unfortunately, gained strength among a group of Muslims who summarize Islam into a set of dry beliefs that stay with them inside the mosque, and as soon as they step out of the mosque door, they say goodbye to it; there is no trace of Islam in their offices, markets, and workplaces.

Traveling through many Islamic countries—even those that were the center of Islam's emergence—reveals this bitter reality: that Islam has been summarized into a handful of beliefs and a few spiritless acts of worship. There is no sign of awareness, social justice, cultural growth, or Islamic insight and ethics in them.

Fortunately, however, in the light of some Islamic revolutions, especially among the youth, a kind of movement toward true Islam and the blending of "faith" and "action" has emerged, and the phrase "What does Islam have to do with our actions?" or "Islam is related to the heart, not life" is heard less often.

Also, the "thesis" that eclectic groups propose—saying we take belief from Islam and economics from Marx—which is similar to the mindset of the misguided people of Shu’ayb, is also condemned. But in any case, this separation and division has existed since old times and exists today, and it must be fought against.

6- Unconditional Ownership is a Source of Corruption The people of Shu’ayb were also caught in the mistake that no one can impose the slightest restriction on the owners' disposal of their property. They even expressed surprise at Shu’ayb, saying: "Is it possible for someone like you, with such intellect and wisdom, to prevent our freedom of action regarding our wealth?" Whether they said this in mockery or as a statement of their belief, it shows they considered restrictions on financial disposal as evidence of a lack of intellect and wisdom! Yet their great mistake was exactly this: if people are free in the disposal of their wealth, corruption and misery will engulf the entire society. Financial matters must always be under correct and calculated regulations presented by the Divine Prophets; otherwise, society will be led to ruin.

7- The Goal of the Prophets was Only Reform The slogan "I only desire reform as far as I am able" (إِنْ أُرِيدُ إِلَّا الْإِصْلاحَ مَا اسْتَطَعْتُ‌) was not only the slogan of Shu’ayb but was among the slogans of all Prophets and all true leaders. Their words and deeds also serve as evidence for this goal. They did not come to entertain people, nor to sell forgiveness for sins, nor to sell Paradise to them, nor to support the powerful and sedate the masses; rather, their goal was reform in the absolute and broad sense of the word: reform in thought and reflection, reform in ethics, reform in the cultural, economic, and political systems of society, and reform in all dimensions of the community.

And in realizing this goal, their only reliance was on Allah, and they feared no conspiracy or threat, just as Shu’ayb said: "And my success is not but through Allah. Upon Him I have relied, and to Him I return" (وَ ما تَوْفِيقِي إِلَّا بِاللَّهِ عَلَيْهِ تَوَكَّلْتُ وَ إِلَيْهِ أُنِيبُ‌).

Ayahs

وَلَقَدْ أَرْسَلْنَا مُوسَىٰ بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَا وَسُلْطَـٰنٍ مُّبِينٍ (٩٦)
Certainly We sent Moses with Our signs and a manifest authority (96)
إِلَىٰ فِرْعَوْنَ وَمَلَإِي۟هِۦ فَٱتَّبَعُوٓا۟ أَمْرَ فِرْعَوْنَ ۖ وَمَآ أَمْرُ فِرْعَوْنَ بِرَشِيدٍ (٩٧)
to Pharaoh and his elite, but they followed Pharaoh’s dictates, and Pharaoh’s dictates were not right-minded. (97)
يَقْدُمُ قَوْمَهُۥ يَوْمَ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ فَأَوْرَدَهُمُ ٱلنَّارَ ۖ وَبِئْسَ ٱلْوِرْدُ ٱلْمَوْرُودُ (٩٨)
On the Day of Resurrection he will lead his people and conduct them into the Fire: an evil goal for the incoming! (98)
وَأُتْبِعُوا۟ فِى هَـٰذِهِۦ لَعْنَةً وَيَوْمَ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ ۚ بِئْسَ ٱلرِّفْدُ ٱلْمَرْفُودُ (٩٩)
They are pursued by a curse in this [world], as well as on the Day of Resurrection; evil is the award conferred [upon them]! (99)

Commentary

The Hero of the Struggle Against Pharaoh

After concluding the story of Shu'ayb and the People of Midian, the Quran refers to a portion of the life of Moses bin Imran (عليه السلام) and his struggles against Pharaoh; this is the seventh story of the prophets mentioned in this Surah.

The life story of Moses (عليه السلام) appears in the Quran more than that of any other prophet, as the events concerning Moses, Pharaoh, and the Children of Israel are referenced more than a hundred times in over thirty Surahs.

The distinguishing feature of Moses' story compared to prophets like Shu'ayb, Salih, Hud, and Lut—whom we read about previously—is that while they rose against misguided peoples, Moses additionally rose against an autocratic government and the tyrannical apparatus of Pharaoh.

Fundamentally, water must always be purified at its source; as long as corrupt governments are in power, no society will see prosperity. Therefore, divine leaders in such societies must, above all else, crush these centers of corruption.

However, it should be noted that in this part of Moses' story, we read only one segment—a segment that, despite its brevity, carries a great message for all humanity.

The first verse says: "And We certainly sent Moses with Our signs and a clear authority" (وَلَقَدْ أَرْسَلْنَا مُوسَىٰ بِآيَاتِنَا وَسُلْطَانٍ مُبِينٍ).

"Sultan," which means dominance or authority, is sometimes used for physical power and sometimes for logical dominance—a dominance that leaves the opponent in a deadlock such that they find no way to escape! In the above verse, it appears that "Sultan" is used in the second sense, and "signs" (Ayat) refers to the clear miracles of Moses (commentators have also offered other possibilities regarding these two words).

In any case, We sent Moses with those crushing miracles and that powerful logic to Pharaoh and his chiefs (إِلَىٰ فِرْعَوْنَ وَمَلَئِهِ).

As we have mentioned many times, "Mala" refers to those whose outward appearance fills the eyes [with awe], even though they are hollow inside. In Quranic logic, it is mostly applied to the elite, the aristocrats, and the fake personalities who surround oppressive powers.

However, Pharaoh's entourage, who saw their illegitimate interests endangered by Moses' uprising, were not willing to submit to him, his miracles, and his logic; "so they followed the command of Pharaoh" (فَاتَّبَعُوا أَمْرَ فِرْعَوْنَ).

"But the command of Pharaoh was never a guarantee of their prosperity or a source of guidance and salvation" (وَمَا أَمْرُ فِرْعَوْنَ بِرَشِيدٍ).

Of course, this success did not come easily for Pharaoh; he used every kind of trickery, conspiracy, and force to advance his goals and incite the people against Moses (عليه السلام), and in this path, he did not overlook even the slightest psychological point.

Sometimes he would say: "Moses wants to take your lands and expel you, who are the original owners," (يُرِيدُ أَنْ يُخْرِجَكُمْ مِنْ أَرْضِكُمْ) (Surah Al-A'raf, Verse 110).

Sometimes he would provoke the religious sentiments of his people, saying: "I fear that he will change your religion," (إِنِّي أَخَافُ أَنْ يُبَدِّلَ دِينَكُمْ) (Surah Ghafir, Verse 26).

"Or that he will cause corruption to appear in the land," (أَوْ أَنْ يُظْهِرَ فِي الْأَرْضِ الْفَسَادَ) (Surah Ghafir, Verse 26).

At times he would slander Moses, at times he would threaten, at other times he would boast of his power and glory to the people of Egypt, and at times he claimed to provide wise leadership that guaranteed their well-being and interests.

Since on the Day of Resurrection every nation, people, and group will enter the gathering place with their leader—and the leaders of this world are the leaders of that world—Pharaoh, who was the leader of the misguided of his era, will enter this scene at the head of his people (يَقْدُمُ قَوْمَهُ يَوْمَ الْقِيَامَةِ).

But instead of this leader taking his followers toward a pleasant spring of pure water in that scorching heat, "he leads them into the Fire" (فَأَوْرَدَهُمُ النَّارَ).

"And how wretched and evil it is that fire should be the watering place for a human to enter!" (وَبِئْسَ الْوِرْدُ الْمَوْرُودُ).

The very thing that, instead of quenching thirst, burns the person's entire being and increases their thirst instead of satiating it.

It should be noted that "Wurud" originally means moving toward water and approaching it, but later the word "Wurud" was applied to any kind of entering into something.

"Wird" (rhyming with 'dhikr') means the water that a person enters upon, and it has also been used to mean the act of entering upon water.

And "Mawrud" means the water that is entered upon (it is a passive participle).

Therefore, the meaning of the phrase (بِئْسَ الْوِرْدُ الْمَوْرُودُ) becomes: "The Fire is an evil watering place to be entered." [1] It also seems necessary to mention that the world after death—as we have previously pointed out—is a realm where our deeds and actions in this world are manifested on a vast scale. The happiness and misery of that world are a reflection of our works in this world. Those who were leaders of the people of Paradise here will also lead groups toward Paradise and prosperity there, and those who were leaders of the oppressors, the misguided, and the people of Hell will also lead their followers toward Hell there, being their vanguard!

Then it says: "They were followed by a curse in this [world] and [will be] on the Day of Resurrection" (وَأُتْبِعُوا فِي هَٰذِهِ لَعْنَةً وَيَوْمَ الْقِيَامَةِ).

Their disgraceful names will always be recorded in the pages of history as a misguided and tyrannical people; thus, they lost out both in this world and in the next.

"And the Fire of Hell is a wretched gift that has been given to them" (بِئْسَ الرِّفْدُ الْمَرْفُودُ).

"Rifd" originally means helping to perform a task; even if something is leaned against another thing, it is referred to as "Rifd." However, gradually this word came to be applied to a gift or bestowal, because it is a help from the giver to the recipient. [1]

[^1]: From a grammatical perspective, this sentence is structured such that "Bi'sa" is a verb of dispraise, its subject is "al-Wird," "al-Mawrud" is an adjective, and the specific object of dispraise is the word "al-Nar" (the Fire), which is omitted. Some grammarians have suggested that "al-Mawrud" is the specific object of dispraise and nothing is omitted in the verse, but the first possibility seems stronger.

[^1]: The grammatical structure of this sentence is the same as what we explained for the phrase "بِئْسَ الْوِرْدُ الْمَوْرُودُ".

Ayahs

ذَٰلِكَ مِنْ أَنۢبَآءِ ٱلْقُرَىٰ نَقُصُّهُۥ عَلَيْكَ ۖ مِنْهَا قَآئِمٌ وَحَصِيدٌ (١٠٠)
These are from the accounts of the townships, which We recount to you. Of them, there are some that still stand, and some that have been mown down. (100)
وَمَا ظَلَمْنَـٰهُمْ وَلَـٰكِن ظَلَمُوٓا۟ أَنفُسَهُمْ ۖ فَمَآ أَغْنَتْ عَنْهُمْ ءَالِهَتُهُمُ ٱلَّتِى يَدْعُونَ مِن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ مِن شَىْءٍ لَّمَّا جَآءَ أَمْرُ رَبِّكَ ۖ وَمَا زَادُوهُمْ غَيْرَ تَتْبِيبٍ (١٠١)
We did not wrong them, but they wronged themselves. When your Lord’s edict came, their gods whom they would invoke besides Allah were of no avail to them in any wise, and they did not increase them in anything but ruin. (101)
وَكَذَٰلِكَ أَخْذُ رَبِّكَ إِذَآ أَخَذَ ٱلْقُرَىٰ وَهِىَ ظَـٰلِمَةٌ ۚ إِنَّ أَخْذَهُۥٓ أَلِيمٌ شَدِيدٌ (١٠٢)
Such is the seizing of your Lord when He seizes the townships that are wrongdoing. Indeed His seizing is painful and severe. (102)
إِنَّ فِى ذَٰلِكَ لَـَٔايَةً لِّمَنْ خَافَ عَذَابَ ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ ۚ ذَٰلِكَ يَوْمٌ مَّجْمُوعٌ لَّهُ ٱلنَّاسُ وَذَٰلِكَ يَوْمٌ مَّشْهُودٌ (١٠٣)
There is indeed a sign in that for those who fear the punishment of the Hereafter. That is a day on which all mankind will be gathered, and it is a day witnessed [by all creatures]. (103)
وَمَا نُؤَخِّرُهُۥٓ إِلَّا لِأَجَلٍ مَّعْدُودٍ (١٠٤)
And We do not defer it but for a determinate term. (104)

Commentary

In the verses of this Surah, the stories of seven ancient nations and glimpses into the history of their Prophets were presented. Each of these accounts illuminated significant aspects of the eventful life of humanity and contained numerous lessons for reflection.

At this point, referring to all those stories, He provides a summary, stating: "These are some of the reports of the cities and settlements which We relate to you" (ذلِكَ مِنْ أَنْباءِ الْقُرى‌ نَقُصُّهُ عليكَ‌).

"Some of those cities and settlements are still standing, while others have been completely destroyed like a mown field" (مِنْها قائِمٌ وَ حَصِيدٌ).

"Standing" (قائم) refers to the cities and settlements of ancient nations that have remained standing, such as the land of Egypt, which was the dwelling place of the Pharaohs. After that oppressive and tyrannical group was drowned, it remained as it was—its gardens, its fields, and many of its stunning buildings.

"Mown" (حصيد) refers to lands like those of the people of Noah and Lot, one of which was submerged under water and the other overturned and pelted with stones.

"But do not think that We wronged them; rather, they wronged themselves" (وَ ما ظَلَمْناهُمْ وَ لكِنْ ظَلَمُوا أَنْفُسَهُمْ‌).

"They sought refuge in their idols and false gods, but their gods, whom they invoked instead of 'Allah,' could not solve any of their problems against the command of your Lord" (فَما أَغْنَتْ عَنْهُمْ آلِهَتُهُمُ الَّتِي يَدْعُونَ مِنْ دُونِ اللَّهِ مِنْ شَيْ‌ءٍ لَمَّا جاءَ أَمْرُ رَبِّكَ‌).

Indeed, "these fake gods added nothing to them except loss, ruin, and misery" (وَ ما زادُوهُمْ غَيْرَ تَتْبِيبٍ‌) [1]. Yes, "such was the punishment of your Lord for the cities and settlements that were oppressive when He consigned them to destruction" (وَ كَذلِكَ أَخْذُ رَبِّكَ إِذا أَخَذَ الْقُرى‌ وَ هِيَ ظالِمَةٌ).

"Indeed, the punishment of your Lord is painful and severe" (إِنَّ أَخْذَهُ أَلِيمٌ شَدِيدٌ).

This is a general Divine law; it is a constant principle and program that whenever any nation or people stains itself with oppression and tyranny, oversteps the boundaries of Divine commands, and pays no heed to the leadership, guidance, and advice of God's Prophets, Allah will eventually seize them firmly and crush them in the grip of torment.

The fact that the aforementioned program is a universal one and a perpetual Sunnah (tradition) is clearly understood from other verses of the Quran as well. In reality, it is a warning to all the people of the world: do not imagine that you are an exception to this law or that this decree was exclusive to ancient nations.

Of course, "oppression" (ظلم) in its broad sense encompasses all sins. Describing the "village" (قرية), city, or settlement as being oppressive (وَ هِيَ ظالِمَةٌ)—even though this attribute belongs to the inhabitants of the city—is perhaps a subtle indication that they were so deeply immersed in oppression and injustice that it was as if the city and settlement had become a single entity of tyranny.

This expression is similar to the idiom we have in Persian when we say: "Oppression and corruption are raining down from the doors and walls of such-and-such a city."

[1]: "Tatbib" (تتبيب) is derived from the root "tabb" (تب), meaning persistence in loss, and it has also been interpreted as destruction and annihilation.

And since this is a general and universal law, He immediately states: "In these exemplary stories and the ominous and painful events that befell those of the past, there is a sign and a mark for finding the path of truth for those who fear the punishment of the Hereafter" (إِنَّ فِي ذلِكَ لَآيَةً لِمَنْ خافَ عَذابَ الْآخِرَةِ).

This is because everything in this world is small and insignificant compared to the next abode—even its punishments and torments—and the other world is broader in every respect. Those who believe in the Resurrection are shaken by seeing any of these examples in this world; they take heed and find their way.

At the end of the verse, He refers to two characteristics of the Day of Resurrection, saying:

"That is a Day for which all people will be gathered" (ذلِكَ يَوْمٌ مَجْمُوعٌ لَهُ النَّاسُ‌).

"And that is a Day that will be witnessed by all" (وَ ذلِكَ يَوْمٌ مَشْهُودٌ).

This indicates that just as Divine laws and traditions are universal in this world, the gathering of people in that Court will also be universal and even simultaneous. It is a day manifest to everyone, such that all human beings will be present and will witness it.

It is interesting that He says: "يَوْمٌ مَجْمُوعٌ لَهُ النَّاسُ‌" (a Day for which people are gathered) and does not say "فيه الناس" (in which people are). This expression indicates that the Resurrection is not merely a container for the gathering of people, but rather a goal and a destination toward which humans are advancing in their evolutionary path.

In Surah At-Taghabun, verse 9, we also read: يَوْمَ يَجْمَعُكُمْ لِيَوْمِ الْجَمْعِ ذلِكَ يَوْمُ التَّغابُنِ‌: "The Day He will assemble you for the Day of Assembly—that is the Day of mutual loss and gain (deprivation)!"

And since some might say that talk of that Day is like a "credit" (deferred) matter and it is not clear when it will arrive, the Quran immediately says: "We do not delay it except for a limited term" (وَ ما نُؤَخِّرُهُ إِلَّا لِأَجَلٍ مَعْدُودٍ).

This delay is for a clear wisdom: so that the people of the world may experience the arenas of trial and cultivation, the final program of the Prophets may be implemented, and the last link in the chain of evolution for which this world has the capacity may appear, after which the end will be announced.

The term "Ma'dud" (معدود - numbered/counted) points to the proximity of the Resurrection, for anything that is countable and falls under a number is limited and near.

In short, the delay of that Day should never make the oppressors arrogant. For "though the Resurrection may come late, it will surely come"—and even describing it as "coming late" is not truly accurate regarding it!

Ayahs

يَوْمَ يَأْتِ لَا تَكَلَّمُ نَفْسٌ إِلَّا بِإِذْنِهِۦ ۚ فَمِنْهُمْ شَقِىٌّ وَسَعِيدٌ (١٠٥)
The day it comes, no one shall speak except by His leave. [On that day,] some of them will be wretched and [some] felicitous. (105)
فَأَمَّا ٱلَّذِينَ شَقُوا۟ فَفِى ٱلنَّارِ لَهُمْ فِيهَا زَفِيرٌ وَشَهِيقٌ (١٠٦)
As for the wretched, they shall be in the Fire: their lot therein will be groaning and wailing. (106)
خَـٰلِدِينَ فِيهَا مَا دَامَتِ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتُ وَٱلْأَرْضُ إِلَّا مَا شَآءَ رَبُّكَ ۚ إِنَّ رَبَّكَ فَعَّالٌ لِّمَا يُرِيدُ (١٠٧)
They shall remain in it for as long as the heavens and the earth endure—except what your Lord may wish; indeed your Lord does whatever He desires. (107)
۞ وَأَمَّا ٱلَّذِينَ سُعِدُوا۟ فَفِى ٱلْجَنَّةِ خَـٰلِدِينَ فِيهَا مَا دَامَتِ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتُ وَٱلْأَرْضُ إِلَّا مَا شَآءَ رَبُّكَ ۖ عَطَآءً غَيْرَ مَجْذُوذٍ (١٠٨)
As for the felicitous, they will be in paradise. They will remain in it for as long as the heavens and the earth endure—except what your Lord may wish—an endless bounty. (108)

Commentary

Prosperity and Misery

In the preceding verses, there was a reference to the issue of the Resurrection and the gathering of all people in that great tribunal.

In the verses under discussion, a portion of the people's destiny on that day is described. First, it says:

"When that day comes, no soul shall speak except by His permission" (يَوْمَ يَأْتِ لا تَكَلَّمُ نَفْسٌ إِلَّا بِإِذْنِهِ).

Sometimes it is imagined that this verse, which serves as evidence that people will speak on that day by the permission of the Lord, contradicts verses that absolutely negate speaking, such as verse 65 of Surah Ya-Sin: الْيَوْمَ نَخْتِمُ عَلى‌ أَفْواهِهِمْ وَ تُكَلِّمُنا أَيْدِيهِمْ وَ تَشْهَدُ أَرْجُلُهُمْ بِما كانُوا يَكْسِبُونَ ("This day, We seal up their mouths, and their hands speak to Us, and their feet bear witness to what they used to earn"). And in verse 35 of Surah Al-Mursalat, we read: هذا يَوْمُ لا يَنْطِقُونَ ("This is a day they shall not speak").

For this reason, some great commentators believe that, fundamentally, speaking has no meaning on that day, because speaking is a tool by which we discover the inner state and heart of individuals. If we possessed a sense through which we could become aware of everyone's thoughts, there would never be a need for speech. Therefore, in the Resurrection, where secrets are uncovered and everything enters a state of "manifestation and appearance," speaking fundamentally has no meaning.

In other words: the abode of the Hereafter is the abode of reward, not the realm of action. For this reason, there is no news there of human free will or speaking by one's own desire and volition. Rather, there is only man and his deeds and what pertains to them. Therefore, if he does speak, it is not like the speech of this world which originates from choice and will to reveal inner secrets; rather, whatever he says is a type of reflection and echo of his deeds—deeds that are manifest and apparent there.

Consequently, speaking on that day is not like speaking in this world, where a person can choose to speak the truth or a lie at will.

In any case, that day is the day of the discovery of the realities of things and the return of the "unseen" (ghayb) to the "witnessed" (shuhud), and it bears no resemblance to this world.

However, this interpretation of the above verse is not very consistent with the apparent meaning of other Quranic verses, as the Quran narrates many dialogues between believers and criminals, leaders and tyrants and their followers, as well as Satan and those he deceived, and the inhabitants of Hell and Paradise, which indicates the existence of speech similar to the speech of this world.

It is even understood from some verses of the Quran that some sinners will even tell lies in response to certain questions. For example, in Surah Al-An'am, verses 22-24, we read: وَ يَوْمَ نَحْشُرُهُمْ جَمِيعاً ثُمَّ نَقُولُ لِلَّذِينَ أَشْرَكُوا أَيْنَ شُرَكاؤُكُمُ الَّذِينَ كُنْتُمْ تَزْعُمُونَ- ثُمَّ لَمْ تَكُنْ فِتْنَتُهُمْ إِلَّا أَنْ قالُوا وَ اللَّهِ رَبِّنا ما كُنَّا مُشْرِكِينَ- انْظُرْ كَيْفَ كَذَبُوا عَلى‌ أَنْفُسِهِمْ وَ ضَلَّ عَنْهُمْ ما كانُوا يَفْتَرُونَ ("And on the Day We gather them all together, We shall say to those who associated others with Allah: 'Where are your partners whom you used to claim?' Then their only plea will be to say: 'By Allah, our Lord, we were not polytheists.' See how they lie against themselves, and that which they used to forge shall fail them.")

Therefore, it is better to say in response to the question regarding the apparent contradiction of the verses related to speaking what many commentators have said: that people on that day pass through various stages, each of which has specific characteristics. In some stages, no questions are asked of them and even their mouths are sealed; only their bodily limbs, which have preserved the effects of their deeds within themselves, speak with a wordless tongue. However, in other stages, the locks are removed from their tongues and by Allah's permission they begin to speak, confessing to their sins, and the wrongdoers blame one another—rather, they try to cast their own sins upon others!

In any case, at the end of the verse, it refers to the division of all people into two groups, saying:

"Of them, some will be wretched and some will be blessed" (فَمِنْهُمْ شَقِيٌّ وَ سَعِيدٌ).

"Sa'id" (blessed/prosperous) is from the root "sa'adat" meaning the availability of the means of blessing, and "shaqi" (wretched/miserable) is from the root "shaqawat" meaning the availability of the means of hardship, punishment, and calamity. Therefore, the blessed ones in that world are the righteous who are situated amidst various blessings, and the wretched ones are the evildoers who are trapped in the heart of Hell amidst various punishments.

In any case, this misery and that prosperity are nothing but the result of the deeds, actions, words, and intentions of man in this world.

And it is strange that some commentators have used this verse as a pretext for their false belief in predestination (jabr), while the verse does not have the slightest indication of this meaning. Rather, it speaks of the prosperous and the wretched of the Resurrection who have all reached this stage because of their own deeds. Perhaps they have confused the meaning of this verse with certain hadiths that speak about prosperity and misery before birth, which is a separate matter.

Then, it describes the states of the wretched and the blessed in concise and expressive terms:

"As for those who are wretched, they shall be in the Fire; for them therein is 'Zafir' and 'Shahiq' (sighing and wailing)" (فَأَمَّا الَّذِينَ شَقُوا فَفِي النَّارِ لَهُمْ فِيها زَفِيرٌ وَ شَهِيقٌ).

And it adds: "Abiding therein as long as the heavens and the earth endure" (خالِدِينَ فِيها ما دامَتِ السَّماواتُ وَ الْأَرْضُ).

"Except as your Lord wills" (إِلَّا ما شاءَ رَبُّكَ).

"Indeed, your Lord is the Doer of what He intends" (إِنَّ رَبَّكَ فَعَّالٌ لِما يُرِيدُ).

"And as for those who are blessed, they shall be in Paradise, abiding therein as long as the heavens and the earth endure" (وَ أَمَّا الَّذِينَ سُعِدُوا فَفِي الْجَنَّةِ خالِدِينَ فِيها ما دامَتِ السَّماواتُ وَ الْأَرْضُ).

"Except as your Lord wills" (إِلَّا ما شاءَ رَبُّكَ).

"A gift that shall never be cut off" (عَطاءً غَيْرَ مَجْذُوذٍ).

Key Points

1- Are Prosperity and Misery Innate?

1- As we mentioned, some have sought to prove the innateness of prosperity and misery from the above verses, while not only do the above verses not indicate this, but they clearly prove that prosperity and misery are acquired. This is because it says:

"فَأَمَّا الَّذِينَ شَقُوا" (As for those who became wretched) and it says: "أَمَّا الَّذِينَ سُعِدُوا" (But as for those who were made blessed). If misery and prosperity were innate, it should have said "As for the wretched (al-ashqiya) and as for the blessed (al-su'ada)" and the like. From here it becomes clear that what appears in the Tafsir of Fakhr al-Razi—that "in these verses Allah has already decreed that a group in the Resurrection are blessed and a group are wretched, and those whom Allah has condemned to such a decree and knows will ultimately be blessed or wretched in the Resurrection, it is impossible for them to change, otherwise it would necessitate that Allah's report be a lie and His knowledge be ignorance"!!—is completely baseless.

This is the same famous objection of "Allah's Knowledge" in the issue of predestination and free will, the answer to which has been given since long ago:

If we do not wish to impose our preconceived thoughts upon the verses, their meanings are clear. These verses say: on that day, a group is blessed in the light of their deeds and a group is wretched because of their deeds, and Allah knows which people will step onto the path of prosperity by their own will, desire, and choice, and which group will step onto the path of misery by their own will. Therefore, contrary to what he said, if people were forced to choose this path, Allah's knowledge would be ignorance, because everyone chooses their path by their own desire and choice.

The evidence for this is that the above verses follow the stories of previous nations, a great number of whom were caught in painful punishments in this world due to oppression, deviation from the path of truth and justice, pollution with severe moral vices, and fighting against divine leaders. The Quran recounts these stories for our education and guidance, to show the path of truth from falsehood, and to separate the path of prosperity from misery.

Fundamentally, if we—as Fakhr al-Razi and those who think like him imagine—were condemned to innate prosperity and misery and were drawn toward evil and good without will, then education and training would be futile and vain. The coming of the Prophets, the revelation of Heavenly Books, advice, exhortation, encouragement, rebuke, blame, accountability, questioning, and finally punishment and reward would all be considered useless or oppressive.

Those who consider people forced in performing good and evil—whether they consider this compulsion to be divine compulsion, natural compulsion, economic compulsion, or environmental compulsion—only advocate this school of thought when speaking or studying books. However, in practice, even they themselves never hold such a belief. For this reason, if their rights are violated, they consider the violator deserving of rebuke, blame, trial, and punishment. They are never willing to overlook him on the grounds that he was forced to do this, nor do they consider his punishment oppressive, nor do they say he could not have avoided this act because Allah willed it or it was the compulsion of environment and nature. This itself is another proof of the primordial (fitri) nature of the principle of free will.

In any case, we find no proponent of predestination who adheres to this belief in his daily practice; rather, his interaction with all humans is an interaction with free, responsible, and autonomous individuals.

All the nations of the world, by virtue of establishing courts and judicial systems to punish offenders, have practically accepted the freedom of will.

All the educational institutions of the world have also implicitly accepted this principle: that man acts by his own desire and will, and through education and training, he can be guided and directed, and kept away from errors, mistakes, and crooked thinking.

2- The Verbs "Shaqu" and "Su'idu"

2- It is interesting that in the above verses, "شَقُوا" (shaqu - they became wretched) is used as an active verb, while "سُعِدُوا" (su'idu - they were made blessed) is used as a passive verb. [^1]

[^1]: "Su'idu" is from the root "sa'ada," which according to a group of linguists is an intransitive verb and does not take an object; therefore, it does not have a passive form. Thus, they were forced to consider it a contraction of "as'idu" (the passive verb from the Af'al form). However, as Alusi narrated in Ruh al-Ma'ani under this verse from some linguists, its trilithic form is also transitive, and "Allah made him happy (sa'adahu Allah)" and "blessed (mas'ud)" are said. Therefore, there is no need for us to consider this passive verb to be from the Af'al form. (Reflect on this).

This difference in expression is perhaps a subtle allusion to the point that man traverses the path of misery with his own feet, but to traverse the path of prosperity, he will not succeed unless there is divine aid and assistance to help him on his path. Undoubtedly, this aid and assistance only include those who have taken the first steps by their own will and choice and have found the merit for such aid. (Reflect on this).

3- The Issue of "Khulud" (Abiding Forever) in the Quran

"Khulud" in its original linguistic sense means both "long-term staying" and "eternity." Therefore, the word khulud alone is not proof of eternity, as it includes any type of long-term staying.

However, in many verses of the Quran, it is mentioned with qualifiers from which the concept of eternity is clearly understood. For example, in verses 100 of At-Tawbah, 11 of At-Talaq, and 9 of At-Taghabun, regarding the inhabitants of Paradise, it uses the expression خالِدِينَ فِيها أَبَداً (abiding therein forever), the concept of which is the eternity of Paradise for these groups. In other verses, such as 169 of An-Nisa and 23 of Al-Jinn, the same expression خالِدِينَ فِيها أَبَداً is seen regarding a group of the inhabitants of Hell, which is evidence for the eternity of their punishment.

Other expressions such as "staying therein forever" (makithina fiha abada) in verse 3 of Al-Kahf, and "they will not desire any change therefrom" (لا يَبْغُونَ عَنْها حِوَلًا) (Al-Kahf: 108), and the like, show that certainly a group of the inhabitants of Paradise and Hell will remain eternally in blessing or in punishment.

Some who have been unable to resolve the difficulties of khulud and the eternity of punishment in their own view have been forced to cling to its linguistic meaning and take it to mean a long duration, while expressions like the verses above do not tolerate such an interpretation.

For further explanation, we direct your attention to the following discussion.

An Important Question

Here, a large question mark is immediately drawn in the mind of every listener: how is this inequality between "sin" and "punishment" possible in Allah's work? How can it be accepted that a person who worked good or evil for his entire life—which was at most 80 or 100 years—should receive reward or punishment for millions upon millions of years and more?!

Of course, this matter is not so significant regarding reward, because however much the gift and reward may be, it is a sign of the grace and generosity of the Rewarder; therefore, there is no room for objection or criticism.

But regarding evil deeds, sin, oppression, and disbelief, the question remains: how is eternal punishment in exchange for a limited sin consistent with the principle of Allah's justice?

Why should someone whose period of rebellion, defiance, and transgression does not exceed a maximum of one hundred years be tortured in fire and punishment forever?!

Does justice not require that a type of balance be established here, and for example, he be punished for 100 years (equivalent to his wrongful deeds)?!

Unsatisfactory Answers

The complexity of the answer to this objection has caused some to think of justifying the verses of khulud and interpreting them in a way that eternal punishment—which in their view is contrary to the principle of justice—is not understood from them.

1- Some say: the intent of "khulud" is its metaphorical or figurative meaning, i.e., a relatively long period. Just as it is said of individuals who must remain in prison until the end of their lives that they are sentenced to "life imprisonment," even though there is certainly no eternity in any prison and it ends with the end of the prisoner's life. Even in the Arabic language, "yakhludu fi al-sijn" which is from the root khulud is used in such cases.

2- Others say: these types of defiant tyrants and rebels whose entire being has been encompassed by sin, and whose existence has taken on the color of disbelief and sin—even if they will be in Hell forever, Hell does not remain in one state forever. A day will come when its fire, like any other fire, finally goes out! And a specific tranquility will envelop the inhabitants of Hell!

3- Others have suggested the possibility that with the passage of time and after enduring many punishments, finally a type of adaptation to the environment will occur in the inhabitants of Hell and they will take on the color of their environment—meaning they will gradually get used to it and become accustomed! And in this state, they will not feel any sense of discomfort or torment!

Of course, as we said, all these justifications are due to the inability and helplessness in the face of solving the problem of khulud and the eternity of punishment; otherwise, the appearance of the verses of khulud regarding the eternity of punishment for a specific group cannot be denied.

Final Resolution of the Objection

To solve this problem, we must return to previous discussions and correct the mistake that occurs in comparing the punishments of the Resurrection with other punishments, so that it becomes clear that the issue of khulud is never contrary to the principle of the Lord's justice.

To explain this discussion, three principles must be noted:

1- Eternal and everlasting punishment, as previously mentioned, is limited to those who have closed all windows of salvation upon themselves and have knowingly and intentionally drowned in corruption, depravity, disbelief, and hypocrisy. The dark shadow of sin has covered their entire heart and soul, and in truth, they have taken on the color of sin and disbelief, just as we read in Surah Al-Baqarah: بَلى‌ مَنْ كَسَبَ سَيِّئَةً وَ أَحاطَتْ بِهِ خَطِيئَتُهُ فَأُولئِكَ أَصْحابُ النَّارِ هُمْ فِيها خالِدُونَ ("Yes, whoever earns an evil deed and his sin encompasses him—those are the companions of the Fire; they will abide therein eternally") (Al-Baqarah, verse 81).

2- It is a mistake for some to imagine that the duration and time of punishment must be equal to the duration and time of the sin, because the relationship between "sin" and "punishment" is not a temporal relationship but a "qualitative" relationship. That is, the amount of time of punishment is proportionate to the quality of the sin, not the amount of its time. For example, someone might commit murder in a single moment and, according to some laws, be sentenced to life imprisonment. Here we see the time of the sin was only a moment, while its punishment will sometimes be 80 years of prison.

Therefore, what is relevant is the "quality," not the "quantity of the sin in terms of time."

3- We said that the punishments and penalties of the Resurrection are mostly of the nature of the natural effect of the deed and the property of the sin. To put it more clearly: the sufferings, pains, and discomforts that sinners endure in the other world are the effect and result of their own deeds that envelop them. We read in the Quran: فَالْيَوْمَ لا تُظْلَمُ نَفْسٌ شَيْئاً وَ لا تُجْزَوْنَ إِلَّا ما كُنْتُمْ تَعملُونَ ("So this Day [the Day of Resurrection] no soul shall be wronged in anything, and you shall not be recompensed except for what you used to do!") (Surah Ya-Sin, verse 54).

And: وَ بَدا لَهُمْ سَيِّئاتُ ما عَمِلُوا وَ حاقَ بِهِمْ ما كانُوا بِهِ يَسْتَهْزِؤُنَ ("And the evil of what they did will appear to them, and they will be enveloped by what they used to mock") (Surah Al-Jathiyah, verse 33). فَلا يُجْزَى الَّذِينَ عَمِلُوا السَّيِّئاتِ إِلَّا ما كانُوا يَعْمَلُونَ ("Those who did evil deeds shall not be recompensed except for what they used to do") (Surah Al-Qasas, verse 84).

Now that these three preliminaries are clear, the final solution is not far from our reach. To reach it, it is sufficient to answer the following few questions:

Suppose someone, due to continuous consumption of alcoholic beverages over a period of one week, becomes afflicted with a severe stomach ulcer, such that he is forced to live with this pain and suffer until the end of his life. Is this equality between the bad deed and its result contrary to justice? Now, if the life of this human were one thousand years or one million years instead of 80 years, and he had to suffer for a million years because of one week of self-indulgence, would this be contrary to the principle of justice? While the existence of this danger in wine-drinking had been announced to him previously, and its consequence had also been explained to him.

And also suppose someone forgets the driving rules and regulations—the implementation of which is certainly for the benefit of the public and causes a reduction in accidents and the discomforts resulting from them—and does not listen to the repeated warnings of wise friends, and an accident comes upon him in a short moment—and all accidents occur in a moment—and he loses his eye or hand or foot in the accident, and following that, he is forced to endure the suffering of blindness or being crippled for long years. Does this phenomenon have any contradiction with the principle of the Lord's justice?!

Here we have another example—and examples bring intellectual realities closer to the mind and prepare it for the final and logical conclusion—suppose we scatter a few grams of camel-thorn seeds on our path, and after a few months or a few years, we find ourselves facing a vast desert of thorns that constantly disturbs us and hurts us...

Or that we—consciously—scatter a few grams of flower seeds, and it is not long before we find ourselves facing a desert of the most beautiful and fragrant flowers that always perfumes the nostrils of our soul and caresses our eyes and hearts.

Do these matters, which are all effects of deeds, have any contradiction with the principle of justice, while equality and parity between the quantity of this deed and its result do not exist? From the sum of what has been said, we conclude:

When reward and punishment are the result and effect of man's own deed, the issue of equality and parity in terms of quantity and quality will not be raised. There may be a seemingly small deed whose effect is a lifetime of deprivation, torture, and discomfort, and there may be a seemingly small deed that will be the source of goodness and blessings for a lifetime. (Do not be mistaken; our meaning of "small" is in terms of the amount of time, otherwise the deeds and sins that cause khulud in punishment will certainly not be small in terms of quality and importance). Therefore, when sin, disbelief, rebellion, and defiance encompass the entire existence of a human, and all the wings of his soul and spirit are burned in the fire of injustice and hypocrisy, what room is there for surprise that in the other abode he should be deprived forever of the blessing of flying in the sky of Paradise and be constantly afflicted with the pain and suffering of this great deprivation?

Did they not announce it to him, and did they not make him aware of this great danger?!

Yes... the Divine Prophets on one hand, and the command of reason on the other, gave him the necessary awareness. Did he commit such an act and find such a destiny without attention and choice? No, it was out of knowledge, intent, and choice.

Did anyone other than himself and the direct result of his deeds provide this destiny for him? No, whatever it was, it was from the effects of his own work.

Therefore, neither does a place for complaint remain, nor an objection or criticism against anyone, nor is it in contradiction with the law of the Lord's justice. [^1]

[^1]: Resurrection and the World After Death, pages 385 to 393.

4- "Khulud" in the Verses Under Discussion

Is "khulud" in the verses under discussion in the sense of eternity, or does it convey the same concept of a long duration which is its linguistic meaning?

Some commentators, in view of the fact that khulud here is qualified by "ما دامَتِ السَّماواتُ وَ الْأَرْضُ" (as long as the heavens and the earth endure), have sought to conclude that khulud in this specific case is not in the sense of eternity, because the heavens and the earths do not have eternity, and according to the explicit text of the Quran, a time will come when the heavens will be rolled up and this earth will be destroyed and changed into another earth. [^1] However, considering that these types of expressions in Arabic literature are usually metaphors for eternity and everlastingness, the verses under discussion also express khulud in the sense of eternity.

For example, the Arab says: "This situation will remain ma laha kawkab (as long as a star shines)" or "ma laha al-jadidan (as long as night and day exist)" or "ma ada'a fajr (as long as dawn shines)" or "ma akhtalafa al-laylu wa al-nahar (as long as night and day follow one another)" and the like, all of which are metaphors for eternity.

In the words of Imam Amir al-Mu'minin Ali عليه السلام in Nahj al-Balagha, we read:

When some uninformed critics objected to the Imam as to why he observed equality in the distribution of the public treasury (Bayt al-Mal) and did not prefer some over others to strengthen the foundations of government, the Imam became upset and said:

"Do you command me to seek victory through injustice toward those over whom I have been given authority? By Allah, I will not go near this as long as people sit for nightly conversation and as long as a star in the sky follows another star." [^1]

In the poems of Di'bil al-Khuza'i, in the famous qasida he recited in the presence of Imam Ali ibn Musa al-Rida عليه السلام, this verse appears:

"I shall weep for them as long as a rising sun sheds light on the horizon, and as long as a caller to goodness calls for prayers." [^2]

Of course, this is not exclusive to Arabic literature; it exists more or less in other languages as well, and in any case, the indication of the verse for eternity should not be a matter of debate.

In this way, there will be no need for the words of those who say the heaven and earth here are the heaven and earth of the Resurrection, which are eternal.

[^1]: Verse 48 of Ibrahim and verse 104 of Al-Anbiya. [^1]: Nahj al-Balagha, Subhi al-Salih, Sermon 126. [^2]: Nur al-Absar, page 140, Al-Ghadir, and other books.

5- What is the Meaning of the Exception in the Verse?

The exceptional phrase "إِلَّا ما شاءَ رَبُّكَ" (Except as your Lord wills), which appears in the above verses regarding both the people of Paradise and the people of Hell, has become a wide field for discussion among commentators. The great commentator Tabarsi, in the interpretation of this exception, has narrated ten views from commentators, many of which, in our opinion, are weak and not at all consistent with the preceding or following verses. Therefore, we refrain from mentioning them and only bring here what seems most correct to us, which are two interpretations:

1- The goal of stating this exception is so that it is not imagined that the khulud and punishment of faithless individuals and the reward of true believers are such that they are without Divine will and volition, or that they limit His power, ability, and will, taking on a form of compulsion and necessity. Rather, despite the eternity of these two, His power and will are dominant over everything, even though by the requirement of His wisdom, He keeps the reward and punishment eternal for these two groups.

The evidence for this is that in the second sentence regarding the blessed ones, after mentioning this exception, He says: "عَطاءً غَيْرَ مَجْذُوذٍ" (A gift that shall never be cut off). This shows that the exceptional phrase was only for the expression of power.

2- Since these verses discuss two groups, the wretched and the blessed, and the wretched are not all necessarily faithless individuals who deserve khulud—rather, it is possible that among them is a group of sinful believers—therefore, the exception pertains to this group.

But this question arises: what meaning will this exception have in the second sentence? (Regarding the blessed ones).

In response, it has been said that it also pertains to the sinful believers who must first go to Hell for a period and be purified, then join the ranks of the people of Paradise. In truth, the exception in the first sentence is with respect to the end of the matter, and in the second sentence, it is with respect to the beginning of the matter. (Reflect on this).

This possibility also exists in response to the above question: that the exception in the first sentence refers to the sinful believers who are released from Hell after a period, and in the second sentence, it refers only to the power and ability of the Lord. The evidence for this is that in the first sentence after the exception, the phrase "إِنَّ رَبَّكَ فَعَّالٌ لِما يُرِيدُ" (Indeed, your Lord is the Doer of what He intends), which is evidence for the execution of this will, is mentioned, and in the second sentence, "عَطاءً غَيْرَ مَجْذُوذٍ," which is evidence for eternity, has come. (Reflect on this).

As for the possibility some have suggested—that this reward and punishment pertain to the Paradise and Hell of the Barzakh (purgatory), the duration of which is limited and comes to an end—it is a very remote possibility, because the preceding verses explicitly discuss the Resurrection, and the link of these verses with them is unbreakable.

Likewise, the possibility that khulud here, like some other verses of the Quran, means a long duration and not eternity, is not consistent with the phrase "عَطاءً غَيْرَ مَجْذُوذٍ" and with the exception itself, which is evidence for the eternity of the sentences preceding it.

6- "Zafir" and "Shahiq" Regarding the Inhabitants of Hell

6- In the above verses regarding the inhabitants of Hell, it says: they have therein "Zafir" and "Shahiq."

Linguists and commentators have given several possibilities for the meaning of these two words. Some have said: "Zafir" means a cry that is accompanied by the exhaling of breath, and "Shahiq" is a moan accompanied by the inhaling of breath.

Others have interpreted "Zafir" as the beginning of a donkey's braying and "Shahiq" as its end, which perhaps does not differ much from the first meaning.

In any case, both of these are the sounds of the cries and moans of those who wail from grief and sorrow—a wailing that fills their entire being and is a sign of the ultimate discomfort and intensity of punishment.

It should be noted that "Zafir" and "Shahiq" are both verbal nouns. Zafir originally means taking a heavy load upon the shoulders, and because such an act is the source of sighs and moans, it is called Zafir. Shahiq originally means being long, just as a high mountain is called "jabal shahiq," and then it was applied to long moans.

The Causes of Prosperity and Misery

Prosperity (sa'adat), which is the lost object of all humans and which everyone seeks in something and looks for somewhere, consists in brief of the availability of the means of perfection for an individual or a society. Its opposite is misery and wretchedness (shaqawat), which everyone detests, and it consists of: the unfavorability of the conditions for victory, progress, and perfection.

Therefore, whoever has more means at his disposal for reaching lofty goals in terms of spiritual, physical, familial, environmental, and cultural conditions is closer to prosperity, or in other words, more prosperous.

And whoever is afflicted with deficiencies and inadequacies in the above aspects will be wretched and deprived of prosperity.

However, it must be noted that the primary foundation of prosperity and misery is the will and desire of man himself. It is he who can provide the necessary means for building himself and even his society, and it is he who can rise to fight against the factors of wretchedness and misery or surrender to them.

In the logic of the Prophets, prosperity and misery are not something within the essence of man; even environmental, familial, and hereditary inadequacies are changeable and transformable in the face of man's own decision and will—unless we deny the principle of man's will and freedom and consider him condemned to deterministic conditions and consider his prosperity and misery innate or the deterministic product of environment and the like, which is a view certainly condemned in the school of the Prophets as well as the school of reason.

It is interesting that in the narrations narrated from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt عليهم السلام, the finger has been placed on various matters as causes of prosperity or causes of misery. Studying them acquaints man with the Islamic way of thinking on this important issue. Instead of following superstitious matters, fantasies, and false traditions that exist in many societies—where baseless matters are imagined to be causes of prosperity and misery—he will follow objective realities and the true causes of prosperity.

A Few Hadiths from the Leaders Regarding Prosperity and Misery

As an example, pay attention to the following few meaningful hadiths:

1- Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام narrates from his grandfather Amir al-Mu'minin Ali عليه السلام:

"The reality of prosperity is that a man's deeds end with prosperity, and the reality of misery is that a man's deeds end with misery." [^1]

This narration explicitly says that the final stage of a man's life and his deeds in this stage are expressive of his prosperity and misery. In this way, it completely negates innate prosperity and misery, places man in the pledge of his deeds, and considers the path of return open for him in all stages until the end of life.

2- In another hadith from Ali عليه السلام, we read:

"The prosperous one is he who takes heed from others, and the wretched one is he who is deceived by his whims and his delusion." [^2]

[^1]: Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, vol. 2, page 398. [^2]: Nahj al-Balagha, Subhi al-Salih, Sermon 86.

This word of Ali عليه السلام is also a renewed emphasis on the voluntary nature of prosperity and misery and explains some of the important causes of these two.

3- The Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله says:

"Four things are from the causes of prosperity and four things are from the causes of misery. As for the four that are from prosperity: a righteous wife, a spacious dwelling, a righteous neighbor, and a comfortable mount. And the four that are from misery: a bad neighbor, a bad wife, a cramped dwelling, and a bad mount." [^1]

Considering that these four subjects play an effective role in the material and spiritual life of everyone and can be factors of victory or failure, the breadth of the concept of prosperity and misery in the logic of Islam becomes clear.

A good wife encourages a person toward all kinds of goodness; a spacious house gives peace to a person's spirit and thought and prepares him for more activity; a bad neighbor creates calamity and a good neighbor is an effective help to comfort and even the progress of a person's goals; a useful mount is an effective factor for reaching social tasks and duties, while a dilapidated and worn-out mount is a factor of backwardness, as it can rarely bring its owner to the destination.

4- And also from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, this hadith is narrated:
