"Among the signs of misery are: dryness of the eyes (inability to weep), hardness of the heart, intense greed in seeking sustenance, and persistence in sin." [^1]

[^1]: Makarim al-Akhlaq, page 65. [^1]: Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, vol. 2, page 398.

These four matters mentioned in the above hadith are voluntary matters that originate from the acquired deeds and morals of man himself. In this way, removing these causes of misery is within the choice of humans themselves.

If we compare the causes mentioned for prosperity and misery in the above hadiths—considering the objectivity of all of them and their effective role in human life—with the superstitious causes and signs that even in our age, the age of the atom and space, a large group adheres to, we reach the reality of how logical and calculated Islamic teachings are.

There are still many who consider a horseshoe a cause of good luck and the number thirteen a cause of misfortune; jumping over fire on certain nights of the year a cause of good luck and the crowing of a hen a cause of misfortune; splashing water behind a traveler a cause of good luck and passing under a ladder a cause of misfortune; and even hanging a blue bead on oneself or on a vehicle a cause of good luck and a sneeze a sign of misfortune in performing the intended task, and similar superstitions that are abundant in the East and West among various nations and peoples.

And how many humans have been held back from activity in life due to being trapped by these superstitions and have been afflicted with many calamities.

Islam has drawn a red line through all these superstitious fantasies and considers the prosperity and misery of man to lie in positive and negative activities, moral strengths and weaknesses, practical programs, and the way of thinking and belief of each person, examples of which have been clearly stated in the four hadiths above.

Ayahs

فَلَا تَكُ فِى مِرْيَةٍ مِّمَّا يَعْبُدُ هَـٰٓؤُلَآءِ ۚ مَا يَعْبُدُونَ إِلَّا كَمَا يَعْبُدُ ءَابَآؤُهُم مِّن قَبْلُ ۚ وَإِنَّا لَمُوَفُّوهُمْ نَصِيبَهُمْ غَيْرَ مَنقُوصٍ (١٠٩)
So do not be in doubt about what these worship: they worship just as their fathers worshiped before, and We shall surely pay them their full share, undiminished. (109)
وَلَقَدْ ءَاتَيْنَا مُوسَى ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ فَٱخْتُلِفَ فِيهِ ۚ وَلَوْلَا كَلِمَةٌ سَبَقَتْ مِن رَّبِّكَ لَقُضِىَ بَيْنَهُمْ ۚ وَإِنَّهُمْ لَفِى شَكٍّ مِّنْهُ مُرِيبٍ (١١٠)
Certainly We gave Moses the Book, but differences arose about it, and were it not for a prior decree of your Lord, a decision would have been made between them; indeed they are in grave doubt concerning it. (110)
وَإِنَّ كُلًّا لَّمَّا لَيُوَفِّيَنَّهُمْ رَبُّكَ أَعْمَـٰلَهُمْ ۚ إِنَّهُۥ بِمَا يَعْمَلُونَ خَبِيرٌ (١١١)
Your Lord will indeed recompense everyone fully for their works. Indeed, He is well aware of what they do. (111)
فَٱسْتَقِمْ كَمَآ أُمِرْتَ وَمَن تَابَ مَعَكَ وَلَا تَطْغَوْا۟ ۚ إِنَّهُۥ بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ بَصِيرٌ (١١٢)
So be steadfast, just as you have been commanded—[you] and whoever has turned [to Allah] with you—and do not overstep the bounds. Indeed, He watches what you do. (112)

Commentary

Steadfastness, Steadfastness!

These verses were revealed as a means of comfort and consolation for the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and to outline his duties and responsibilities. In fact, one of the significant outcomes derived from recounting the stories of past nations is that the Prophet, and subsequently the true believers, should not fear the multitude of enemies. They should harbor no doubt regarding the eventual defeat of the idol-worshipping and oppressive group they face, and they must remain confident in Divine assistance.

Therefore, in the first verse, He says: "Do not entertain any doubt regarding what these people worship; they are merely following the same path that a group of their predecessors took. They worship only as their forefathers worshipped before them; thus, they shall have no better fate than theirs" (فَلا تَكُ فِي مِرْيَةٍ مِمَّا يَعْبُدُ هؤُلاءِ ما يَعْبُدُونَ إِلَّا كَما يَعْبُدُ آبائهم من قبل) [1]. Consequently, He immediately adds: "We shall surely give them their full portion and share of punishment and torment without any reduction," and should they return to the path of truth, their portion of Our reward is preserved (وَ إِنَّا لَمُوَفُّوهُمْ نَصِيبَهُمْ غَيْرَ مَنْقُوصٍ‌).

Although the word "Muwaffuhum" (giving them in full) itself implies the complete fulfillment of a right, the mention of the phrase "Ghayra Manqus" (without reduction) is for further emphasis on this matter.

[1]: "Miryah" (pronounced like Jizyah or Qaryah) means hesitation in decision-making. Some have interpreted it as doubt accompanied by signs of suspicion. The original root of this word refers to squeezing a camel's udder after milking in hopes that the remains within the udder will come out. Since this act is done with doubt and uncertainty, the word has been applied to any kind of doubt or hesitation.

In reality, this verse illustrates the fact that what we read regarding the history of past nations was not a myth or legend, nor was it exclusive to those of the past; it is an eternal and everlasting Sunnah (divine law) concerning all human beings of yesterday, today, and tomorrow.

However, while these punishments manifested as terrifying and massive calamities for many past nations, they took a different form regarding the enemies of the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله. That is, Allah granted such power and strength to His Prophet that he was able, through the group of believers, to crush the stubborn and ruthless enemies who would not follow any straight path.

Again, to console the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, He adds: "If your people dispute and make excuses regarding your Heavenly Book—the Quran—do not be distressed, for We gave Moses the Heavenly Book (the Torah), and they differed over it; some accepted it and some denied it" (وَ لَقَدْ آتَيْنا مُوسَى الْكِتابَ فَاخْتُلِفَ فِيهِ‌). And if you see that We do not hasten in punishing your enemies, it is because the interests of education, training, and the guidance of this people necessitate it. Had it not been for this interest and the plan that your Lord had previously ordained in this regard, which necessitated the delay, judgment would have been passed between them and punishment would have overtaken them (وَ لَوْ لا كَلِمَةٌ سَبَقَتْ مِنْ رَبِّكَ لَقُضِيَ بَيْنَهُمْ‌).

"Even though they still do not believe this reality and remain in doubt and hesitation regarding it—a doubt mixed with suspicion and pessimism" (وَ إِنَّهُمْ لَفِي شَكٍّ مِنْهُ مُرِيبٍ‌) [1].

[1]: There is a discussion among commentators regarding what the pronoun "hum" (they) in this verse and the pronoun "minhu" (of it) refer to. A group believes that "hum" refers to the people of Moses and "minhu" refers to the Book of Moses, meaning: "These people are still in doubt regarding the Book of Moses." However, another group of commentators prefers that the first pronoun refers to the polytheists of Mecca and the second to the Quran (or to their punishment). Given that the preceding and succeeding verses are all focused on comforting and consoling the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, the second interpretation seems stronger, and we have chosen it in the text.

"Murib" is derived from "Rayb," meaning a doubt mixed with pessimism, suspicion, and contrary evidence. Therefore, the concept of this word is that the idolaters not only doubted the truthfulness of the Quran or the descent of punishment upon the wicked, but they also claimed to have evidence to the contrary.

"Raghib" in the book Al-Mufradat defines "Rayb" as a doubt that will later be unveiled and turn into certainty. Thus, the meaning of the verse becomes: soon the veil will be lifted from the truthfulness of your call and the punishment of the wicked, and the reality of the matter will become manifest.

For further emphasis, He adds that "Your Lord will surely pay each of these two groups (believers and disbelievers) the full recompense for their deeds and will deliver their actions back to them without any reduction" (وَ إِنَّ كُلًّا لَمَّا لَيُوَفِّيَنَّهُمْ رَبُّكَ أَعْمالَهُمْ‌). And this task presents no difficulty or hardship for Allah, "for He is aware of everything and is well-acquainted with whatever they do" (إِنَّهُ بِما يَعْمَلُونَ خَبِيرٌ).

It is interesting that He says: "We give them their deeds," which is another allusion to the concept of the "embodiment of deeds" (Tajassum al-A'mal)—that reward and punishment are, in reality, the human's own actions that change form and return to him.

After mentioning the history of the prophets and past nations and the secret of their success and victory, and after comforting and strengthening the Prophet's will through this means, in the next verse, He gives the most important command to the Noble Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, saying: "Stand firm (be steadfast) as you have been commanded" (فَاسْتَقِمْ‌).

Be steadfast in the path of propagation and guidance; be steadfast in the way of struggle and battle; be steadfast in fulfilling Divine duties and implementing the teachings of the Quran.

But this steadfastness should not be for the sake of pleasing this person or that, nor out of ostentation and hypocrisy, nor to gain the title of a hero, nor to acquire status, wealth, success, or power; rather, it must be solely for the sake of Allah's command and "as you have been commanded" (كَما أُمِرْتَ‌).

However, this command is not limited to you alone; both you must be steadfast "and all those who have turned from polytheism toward faith and have accepted the call of Allah" (وَ مَنْ تابَ مَعَكَ‌).

"A steadfastness free from excess and negligence, increase and decrease; a steadfastness in which there is no transgression" (وَ لا تَطْغَوْا).

"For Allah is aware of your deeds," and no movement, stillness, word, or plan remains hidden from Him (إِنَّهُ بِما يَعْمَلُونَ بَصِيرٌ).

A Profound and Exhausting Verse.

In a famous Hadith from Ibn Abbas, we read:

ما نزل على رسول اللَّه (ص) آية كانت اشد عليه و لا اشق من هذه الاية، و لذلك قال لاصحابه حين قالوا له اسرع اليك الشيب يا رسول اللَّه! شيبتنى هود و الواقعه‌

[1]

[1]: Tafsir Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 5, p. 199.

"No verse was revealed to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله that was more severe or difficult for him than this verse. Therefore, when his companions asked him, 'O Messenger of Allah, why has gray hair come upon you so quickly and the signs of old age appeared?' he replied, 'Surah Hud and Al-Waqi'ah have made me old!'"

In another narration, we read: When the above verse was revealed, the Prophet said:

شمروا، شمروا، فما رئى ضاحكا

"Gird your loins, gird your loins (for it is the time for work and effort), and after that, the Prophet was never seen laughing!" [1] The reason for this is clear, as there are four important commands in this verse, each of which places a heavy burden on a person's shoulders.

Most importantly is the command for steadfastness (Istiqaamah). Istiqaamah, which is derived from the root "Qiyam" (standing), is used because a person has more control over their work and effort while standing. Istiqaamah means seeking to stand; that is, creating such a state within yourself that weakness finds no way into you. What a difficult and heavy command!

Achieving victories is always relatively easy, but maintaining them is very difficult—especially in a society so backward and far from reason and knowledge, against a stubborn and headstrong people, amidst a multitude of determined enemies, and on the path to building a healthy, proud, faithful, and pioneering society. Steadfastness on this path was no simple task.

Another command is that this steadfastness must have a purely Divine motive and remain far from any satanic whispers—meaning, achieving the greatest political and social powers, and doing so solely for Allah!

Third is the issue of leading those who have returned to the path of truth and making them also remain steadfast.

[1]: Al-Durr al-Manthur, under the verse.

Fourth is leading the struggle on the path of truth and justice and preventing any transgression or rebellion (Tughyan). For it has often happened that individuals exert the utmost steadfastness in reaching a goal, but maintaining justice is impossible for them, and they often fall into rebellion and exceeding the limits.

Yes, the combination of these factors joined hands and submerged the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله under such a weight of responsibility that it even took away his opportunity to smile and made him old!

In any case, this was not a command only for yesterday; it is for today and all the tomorrows to come.

Today, too, our important responsibility as Muslims, and especially as Islamic leaders, is summarized in these four phrases: Steadfastness—Sincerity—Leading the believers—and Avoiding rebellion and transgression. Without implementing these principles, victory over the enemies who surround us from every side, internally and externally, and who utilize all cultural, political, economic, social, and military means against us, is not possible.

Ayah

وَلَا تَرْكَنُوٓا۟ إِلَى ٱلَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا۟ فَتَمَسَّكُمُ ٱلنَّارُ وَمَا لَكُم مِّن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ مِنْ أَوْلِيَآءَ ثُمَّ لَا تُنصَرُونَ (١١٣)
Do not incline toward the wrongdoers, lest the Fire should touch you, and you will not have any friend besides Allah; then you will not be helped. (113)

Commentary

Relying on Oppressors and Tyrants

This verse outlines one of the most fundamental social, political, military, and ideological programs. Addressing the Muslims in general, it states as a definitive duty: "And do not incline toward those who do wrong" (وَ لا تَرْكَنُوا إِلَى الَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا), meaning you must not rely on or depend upon them in your affairs.

"Lest the Fire should touch you" (فَتَمَسَّكُمُ النَّارُ), "and you would have no protectors or helpers besides Allah" (وَ ما لَكُمْ مِنْ دُونِ اللَّهِ مِنْ أَوْلِياءَ).

In such a state, it is clear that "then you shall not be helped" (ثُمَّ لا تُنْصَرُونَ).

Here are several important points that must be noted

1- The Concept of "Rukun"

  • "Rukun" is derived from the root "rukn," which refers to the pillars and walls that keep a building or other objects standing. Subsequently, it has been used to mean trusting and relying upon something.

Although commentators have provided many meanings for this word in the context of this verse, all or most of them return to a comprehensive and general concept. For instance, some have interpreted it as "inclination," some as "cooperation," some as "expressing satisfaction" or "friendship," and others as "well-wishing and obedience." All of these are gathered within the comprehensive concept of dependence, trust, and reliance.

2- In what matters should one not rely on oppressors?

  • It is obvious that, in the first degree, one must not participate in their injustices and tyrannies or seek their help for such acts. In the second degree, reliance on them in matters that cause the weakness and debility of the Islamic society, the loss of independence and self-sufficiency, and becoming a dependent and powerless member must be eliminated. Such forms of "rukun" result in nothing but the defeat, failure, and weakening of Islamic societies.

However, if, for example, Muslims maintain commercial or scientific relations with non-Muslim societies based on preserving the interests of Muslims and the independence and stability of Islamic societies, this neither falls under the concept of "relying on oppressors" nor is it something prohibited by Islam. Such interactions existed during the era of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and in subsequent eras.

3- The Philosophy of Prohibiting Reliance on Oppressors

Relying on an oppressor creates numerous evils and disorders that are generally hidden from no one. However, the more we investigate this issue, the more new points we discover:

  • Relying on oppressors leads to their empowerment, and their empowerment leads to the expansion of the scope of injustice, corruption, and the ruin of societies. In Islamic teachings, we read that a person should not seek their rights through an unjust and tyrannical judge unless forced (and sometimes even when forced). This is because referring to such a judge or government to claim a right implies a tacit recognition and empowerment of them, and the harm of this action is sometimes greater than the loss incurred by losing the right itself.

  • Relying on oppressors gradually affects the intellectual culture of society, removing the ugliness of injustice and sin, and encouraging people to be unjust and to become oppressors.

  • Fundamentally, reliance and trust in others that manifests as overt dependency results in nothing but misery, let alone when this support is an oppressor and tyrant.

  • A progressive, pioneering, honorable, and strong society is one that stands on its own feet, as Allah says in a beautiful example in Surah Al-Fath, verse 29: فَاسْتَوى‌ عَلى‌ سُوقِهِ‌: "...standing firm upon its stalk," like a green plant that stands on its own and does not need dependency on anything else to survive and flourish.

An independent and free society is one that is self-sufficient in every respect, and its bonds and connections with others are based on mutual interests, not on the reliance of the weak upon the strong. This dependency—whether intellectual, cultural, military, economic, or political—will yield nothing but captivity and exploitation. If this dependency is on oppressors and tyrants, the result will be dependency on their injustice and participation in their agendas.

Of course, the command of the above verse is not limited to the relations of societies; it also includes the bond and relationship between two individuals. Even a free and faithful person should never be dependent on an oppressor, for in addition to losing independence, it leads to being drawn into the circle of their injustice and the strengthening and expansion of corruption and tyranny.

4- Who are "those who do wrong" (الَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا)?

Commentators have mentioned various possibilities in this regard. Some have interpreted them as polytheists (mushrikin), but as another group has stated, there is no reason to limit them to polytheists. If the instances of oppressors at the time of the verse's revelation were polytheists, it does not serve as proof of exclusivity.

Similarly, the interpretation of this word in narrations as polytheists is not proof of exclusivity, as we have repeatedly stated that such narrations often explain the most prominent and obvious examples.

Therefore, all those who have engaged in injustice and corruption among the servants of Allah, made them their servants and slaves, and exploited their powers for their own benefit, fall under the general concept of "those who do wrong" (الَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا) and are among the instances of the verse.

However, it is certain that those who have committed a minor injustice in their lives and have occasionally been instances of this title are not included in its core concept. Otherwise, very few people would be exempt, and reliance on anyone would become impermissible—unless we define "rukun" as reliance and trust specifically in the aspect of their injustice and tyranny, in which case it would include even those who have stained their hands with injustice even once.

5- The predicament of some Sunnis regarding two commands: submission to the Sultan and not relying on oppressors

Some Sunni commentators have raised an issue here that is not easily answered based on their principles. On one hand, their narrations state that one must submit to the Sultan of the time—as the "Ulu al-Amr"—whoever he may be. For example, they have narrated a tradition from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله that it is necessary for you to obey the Sultan:

"Even if he takes your wealth and strikes your back!"

And other narrations that emphasize obedience to the Sultan in the broadest sense of the word.

On the other hand, the above verse says: "Do not rely on or trust those who are oppressors."

Are these two commands reconcilable?

Some have sought to resolve this contradiction with an exception: that obedience to the Sultan is necessary as long as he does not follow the path of disobedience (to God) or step into the path of disbelief.

However, the tone of their narrations regarding obedience to the Sultan is not very consistent with such an exception.

In any case, we believe—as stated in the school of the Ahl al-Bayt عليهم السلام—that obedience is only necessary for a "Wali al-Amr" who is knowledgeable and just, and who can be considered a general successor to the Prophet and the Imam. If the Umayyad and Abbasid caliphs fabricated and polished hadiths in their own favor in this regard, they are in no way consistent with the principles of our school or the teachings we have taken from the Quran. We must qualify such narrations if they are qualifiable, and if not, set them aside entirely, for any narration that contradicts the Book of Allah is rejected. The Quran is explicit that the Imam and leader of the believers must not be an oppressor, and the above verse also explicitly says: do not rely on or trust the oppressors!

Alternatively, such narrations must be considered specific to cases of absolute necessity and compulsion.

Ayahs

وَأَقِمِ ٱلصَّلَوٰةَ طَرَفَىِ ٱلنَّهَارِ وَزُلَفًا مِّنَ ٱلَّيْلِ ۚ إِنَّ ٱلْحَسَنَـٰتِ يُذْهِبْنَ ٱلسَّيِّـَٔاتِ ۚ ذَٰلِكَ ذِكْرَىٰ لِلذَّٰكِرِينَ (١١٤)
Maintain the prayer at the two ends of the day, and during the early hours of the night. Indeed good deeds efface misdeeds. That is an admonition for the mindful. (114)
وَٱصْبِرْ فَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَا يُضِيعُ أَجْرَ ٱلْمُحْسِنِينَ (١١٥)
And be patient; indeed Allah does not waste the reward of the virtuous. (115)

Commentary

"Prayer" and "Patience"

In these verses, the finger is placed upon two of the most important Islamic instructions, which are in fact the spirit of faith and the foundation of Islam:

First, the command to establish prayer is given, saying: "And establish prayer at the two ends of the day and at the approaches of the night" (وَ أَقِمِ الصَّلاةَ طَرَفَيِ النَّهارِ وَ زُلَفاً مِنَ اللَّيْلِ‌).

The apparent meaning of the expression tarafay al-nahar (two ends of the day) is that it describes the Morning (Fajr) and Sunset (Maghrib) prayers, which are located at the two ends of the day. "Zulaf," which is the plural of zulfah meaning "nearness," refers to the early parts of the night that are close to the day; therefore, it applies to the Night ('Isha) prayer.

This same interpretation has also been narrated in the traditions of the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt عليهم السلام, stating that the above verse refers to three prayers (Morning, Sunset, and Night).

Here, the question arises: why, among the five daily prayers, is reference made here only to the three prayers of Morning, Sunset, and Night, while the Noon (Zuhr) and Afternoon ('Asr) prayers are not mentioned?

The complexity of answering this question has led some commentators to interpret tarafay al-nahar so broadly that it includes the Morning, Noon, Afternoon, and Sunset prayers, and with the expression zulafan min al-layl, which refers to the Night prayer, it would encompass all five daily prayers.

However, in fairness, tarafay al-nahar cannot sustain such an interpretation, especially considering that the Muslims of the early era were committed to performing the Noon prayer at the beginning of its time and the Afternoon prayer around mid-time (between noon and sunset).

The only thing that can be said here is that in the verses of the Quran, sometimes all five prayers are mentioned, such as: "Establish prayer at the decline of the sun [from its meridian] until the darkness of the night and [also] the Qur'an of dawn" (Al-Isra: 78). Sometimes three prayers are mentioned, like the verse under discussion, and sometimes only one prayer is mentioned, such as: "Guard strictly your [habit of] prayers, especially the Middle Prayer; and stand before Allah in a devout [frame of mind]" (Al-Baqarah: 238).

Therefore, it is not necessary for all five prayers to be mentioned together in every instance, especially since sometimes circumstances require emphasizing only the Noon prayer (the Middle Prayer) because of its importance, and sometimes the Morning, Sunset, and Night prayers, which might be subject to neglect due to fatigue or sleep.

Then, for the importance of the daily prayers specifically, and all acts of worship, obedience, and good deeds generally, He says: "Indeed, good deeds do away with misdeeds" (إِنَّ الْحَسَناتِ يُذْهِبْنَ السَّيِّئاتِ‌).

"That is a reminder for those who remember" (ذلِكَ ذِكْرى‌ لِلذَّاكِرِينَ‌).

The above verse, like several other verses of the Quran, describes the effect of good deeds in removing the evil traces of bad deeds. In Surah An-Nisa, verse 31, we read: "If you avoid the major sins which you are forbidden, We will remove from you your lesser sins" (إِنْ تَجْتَنِبُوا كَبائِرَ ما تُنْهَوْنَ عَنْهُ نُكَفِّرْ عَنْكُمْ سَيِّئاتِكُمْ‌).

And in verse 7 of Surah Al-Ankabut, we read: "And those who believe and do righteous deeds - We will surely remove from them their misdeeds" (وَ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَ عَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ لَنُكَفِّرَنَّ عَنْهُمْ سَيِّئاتِهِمْ‌).

In this way, the neutralizing effect of obedience and good deeds upon sin is established.

From a psychological perspective, there is no doubt that every sin and ugly act creates a kind of darkness in the human soul and spirit. If this continues, their effects accumulate and transform the person in a terrifying way.

However, a good deed that springs from a Divine motive grants a refinement to the human soul that can wash away the traces of sin and turn those darknesses into light.

But since the above sentence ("Indeed, good deeds do away with misdeeds") is mentioned immediately after the command for prayer, one of its clearest examples is the daily prayers. If we see that in the traditions it is interpreted only as the daily prayers, it is not a proof of exclusivity; rather, as we have said many times, it is the statement of a clear and definitive example.

The Extraordinary Importance of Prayer

In numerous traditions narrated under the above verses from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and the Infallible Imams عليهم السلام, expressions are seen that unveil the extraordinary importance of prayer in the school of Islam.

Abu Uthman says: I was sitting with Salman al-Farsi under a tree. He took a dry branch and shook it until all its leaves fell off. Then he turned to me and said, "You didn't ask why I did this."

I said, "Tell me, what was your intention?"

He said: "This is exactly what the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله did when I was sitting with him under a tree. Then the Prophet asked me this question and said, 'Salman, do you not ask why I did this?' I said, 'Please tell me, why?' He said:

'Indeed, when a Muslim performs ablution and performs it well, then performs the five daily prayers, his sins fall away just as the leaves of this branch fell.' Then he recited this verse: And establish prayer..." [1]

In another Hadith from one of the companions of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله named "Abu Umamah," we read that he said: One day we were sitting with the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله in the mosque when a man came and said, "O Messenger of Allah, I have committed a sin for which a legal punishment (hadd) becomes necessary; execute that punishment upon me." He said, "Did you pray with us?" He replied, "Yes, O Messenger of Allah." He said, "Allah has forgiven your sin—or your punishment." [2]

It is also narrated from Ali عليه السلام that he said: We were in the mosque with the Messenger of Allah waiting for prayer when a man stood up and said, "O Messenger of Allah, I have committed a sin." The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله turned away from him. When the prayer was finished, the same man stood up and repeated his first statement. The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله said, "Did you not perform this prayer with us? and did you not perform ablution well for it?" He said, "Yes." He said, "This is the expiation for your sin!" [1]

Also, it is narrated from Ali عليه السلام from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله that he said: "The status of the five daily prayers for my Ummah is like a running river at the door of one of you. What do you think, if there were filth on his body and then he bathed in that river five times a day, would any filth remain on his body? (Certainly not). Likewise, by Allah, are the five daily prayers for my Ummah." [2]

In any case, there is no room for doubt that whenever prayer is performed with its conditions, it immerses the human being in a world of spirituality and divinity that strengthens his bonds of faith with God such that it washes away the pollutions and traces of sin from his heart and soul.

Prayer insures a person against sin, and prayer also removes the rust of sin from the mirror of the heart.

Prayer grows the buds of high human virtues in the depths of the human soul; prayer strengthens the will, purifies the heart, and cleanses the spirit. In this way, provided it is not a body without a soul, prayer is a supreme school of education.

[1] and [2]: Majma' al-Bayan, under the verse.

The Most Hope-Giving Verse of the Quran

In the commentary of the verse under discussion, an interesting Hadith is narrated from Ali عليه السلام to the effect that one day he turned to the people and said: "In your opinion, which is the most hope-giving verse in the Quran?"

Some said the verse: "Indeed, Allah does not forgive associating others with Him, but He forgives what is less than that for whom He wills" (An-Nisa: 48).

The Imam said: "It is good, but it is not what I want." Some said the verse: "And whoever does a wrong or wrongs himself but then seeks forgiveness of Allah will find Allah Forgiving and Merciful" (An-Nisa: 110). The Imam said: "It is good, but it is not what I want."

Others said the verse: "Say, 'O My servants who have transgressed against themselves [by sinning], do not despair of the mercy of Allah'" (Az-Zumar: 53). He said: "It is good, but it is not what I want!" Others said the verse: "And those who, when they commit an immorality or wrong themselves [by transgression], remember Allah and seek forgiveness for their sins - and who can forgive sins except Allah?" (Al-Imran: 135). Again the Imam said: "It is good, but it is not what I want."

At this moment, the people turned toward the Imam from all sides and murmured. He said: "What is the matter, O Muslims?" They said: "By Allah, we know of no other verse in this regard."

The Imam said: "I heard from my beloved, the Messenger of Allah, who said: 'The most hope-giving verse in the Quran is this verse: And establish prayer at the two ends of the day and at the approaches of the night. Indeed, good deeds do away with misdeeds. That is a reminder for those who remember.'" [1]

[1]: Same source.

Of course, as we mentioned under verse 48 of Surah An-Nisa, it is stated in another Hadith that the most hope-giving verse of the Quran is: "Indeed, Allah does not forgive associating others with Him, but He forgives what is less than that for whom He wills."

However, considering that each of these verses looks at an angle of this discussion and expresses one dimension of its dimensions, there is no contradiction between them:

In fact, the verse under discussion speaks of those who perform their prayers well—a prayer with spirit and presence of heart that washes the traces of other sins from their hearts and souls.

But the other verse speaks of those who do not possess such a prayer and only enter through the door of repentance. Thus, this verse is the most hope-giving for this group, and that verse for that group.

What hope is greater than this: that a person knows whenever his foot slips or passion overcomes him (without him being persistent in sin, his foot is dragged into sin), when the time for prayer arrives, he performs ablution and stands in the presence of the Beloved for intimate supplication, and the feeling of shame—which is a requirement of turning to God—regarding past actions comes over him, his sin is forgiven and its gloom and darkness depart from his heart.

Following the soul-building program of prayer and the explanation of the effect that good deeds have in erasing misdeeds, in the next verse He commands "patience" and says: "And be patient, for indeed, Allah does not allow to be lost the reward of those who do good" (وَ اصْبِرْ فَإِنَّ اللَّهَ لا يُضِيعُ أَجْرَ الْمُحْسِنِينَ‌).

Although some commentators have wanted to limit the meaning of patience here to the case of prayer or the harassment and injuries from enemies against the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله.

But it is clear that we have no reason to limit the meaning of patience in the verse under discussion; rather, it contains a general and comprehensive concept that includes every kind of steadfastness against difficulties, oppositions, harassments, excitements, rebellions, and various calamities. Standing firm against all these events is included in the comprehensive concept of patience.

"Patience" is a general principle and an Islamic foundation that is mentioned alongside prayer in some parts of the Quran. Perhaps this is because prayer creates "movement" in a person, and the command for patience necessitates "resistance." When these two—"movement" and "resistance"—join hands, they become the primary factor for any kind of victory.

Fundamentally, no kind of goodness is possible without steadfastness and patience, because completing good deeds certainly requires perseverance. For this reason, in the above verse, following the command to be patient, He says Allah does not waste the reward of the doers of good; meaning, doing good is not possible without patience and steadfastness.

It is also necessary to mention this point: that people are divided into several groups in the face of unpleasant events:

  1. A group that immediately loses their composure and, as the Quran says, resorts to anxiety and wailing: "When evil touches him, [he is] distressed" (Al-Ma'arij: 20).

  2. Another group that does not lose their composure and stands against the event with endurance and forbearance.

  3. Another group that, in addition to endurance and forbearance, also practices gratitude.

  4. Another group that, in the face of such events, rises with love to strive and effort, planning to neutralize the negative effects of the event, showing tireless struggle and battle, and does not rest until they remove the problem from their path.

Allah has promised victory to such patient ones: "If there are among you twenty [who are] patient, they will overcome two hundred" (Al-Anfal: 65).

And He has counted the blessings of Paradise as the reward of their other abode: "And will reward them for what they patiently endured with a garden [in Paradise] and silk [garments]" (Al-Insan: 12).

Ayahs

فَلَوْلَا كَانَ مِنَ ٱلْقُرُونِ مِن قَبْلِكُمْ أُو۟لُوا۟ بَقِيَّةٍ يَنْهَوْنَ عَنِ ٱلْفَسَادِ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ إِلَّا قَلِيلًا مِّمَّنْ أَنجَيْنَا مِنْهُمْ ۗ وَٱتَّبَعَ ٱلَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا۟ مَآ أُتْرِفُوا۟ فِيهِ وَكَانُوا۟ مُجْرِمِينَ (١١٦)
Why were there not among the generations before you a remnant [of the wise] who might forbid corruption in the earth, except a few of those whom We delivered from among them? Those who were wrongdoers pursued that in which they had been granted affluence, and they were a guilty lot. (116)
وَمَا كَانَ رَبُّكَ لِيُهْلِكَ ٱلْقُرَىٰ بِظُلْمٍ وَأَهْلُهَا مُصْلِحُونَ (١١٧)
Your Lord would never destroy the townships unjustly while their inhabitants were bringing about reform. (117)

Commentary

The Factor Behind the Ruin of Societies

To complete the previous discussions, these two verses present a fundamental social principle that guarantees the salvation of societies from destruction. It is this: in any society, as long as there exists a group of committed and responsible thinkers who do not remain silent in the face of corruption, but rise to struggle and hold the intellectual and ideological leadership of the people, that society will not be led toward ruin and annihilation.

However, when indifference and silence prevail at all levels, and the society remains defenseless against the factors of corruption, then corruption—and subsequently, certain destruction—is inevitable.

In the first verse, while referring to previous nations that were afflicted by various calamities, it says:

"Why were there not among the generations (and nations) before you people of virtue and power of thought who would forbid corruption in the land?" (فَلَوْ لا كانَ مِنَ الْقُرُونِ مِنْ قَبْلِكُمْ أُولُوا بَقِيَّةٍ يَنْهَوْنَ عَنِ الْفَسادِ فِي الْأَرْضِ‌).

Then, as an exception, it says: "Except a few of those whom We saved from among them" (إِلَّا قَلِيلًا مِمَّنْ أَنْجَيْنا مِنْهُمْ‌).

This small group, although they practiced the enjoining of good and the forbidding of evil—such as Lut (peace be upon him) and his small family, Nuh (peace be upon him) and his limited number of believers, and Salih (peace be upon him) and the few who followed him—were so few in number that they did not succeed in the overall reformation of society.

In any case, the transgressors, who formed the majority of these societies, pursued luxury, pleasure, and indulgence. They became so intoxicated with the wine of arrogance, opulence, and pleasures that they committed all kinds of sins (وَ اتَّبَعَ الَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا ما أُتْرِفُوا فِيهِ وَ كانُوا مُجْرِمِينَ‌).

Then, to emphasize this reality, the next verse adds: The reason you see Allah sent these nations to the realm of non-existence was because there were no reformers among them; for "Your Lord would never destroy the towns and nations for their wrongdoing while their people were reformers" (وَ ما كانَ رَبُّكَ لِيُهْلِكَ الْقُرى‌ بِظُلْمٍ وَ أَهْلُها مُصْلِحُونَ‌).

This is because in every society, there is usually some degree of injustice and corruption. However, the important thing is that the people feel there is injustice and corruption and are on the path of reform. With such a feeling and by taking steps toward reform, Allah grants them respite, and the law of creation recognizes their right to life.

But as soon as this feeling vanishes and the society becomes indifferent, and corruption and injustice rapidly engulf everywhere, then according to the Sunnah (tradition) of creation, there is no longer a right to life for them. This reality can be explained with a clear example:

In the human body, there is a well-equipped defensive force called "white blood cells." Whenever any foreign microbe invades the body through air, water, food, or skin abrasions, these self-sacrificing soldiers stand against them and destroy them, or at least prevent their expansion and growth.

Obviously, if one day this great defensive force, consisting of millions of soldiers, were to go on strike and the body remained defenseless, it would become such a battlefield for harmful microbes that various diseases would rapidly attack it.

The whole of human society is in a similar state. If the defensive force, which is the "Ulu Baqiyyah" (people of virtue/remnant), is removed from it, the social disease-causing microbes that exist in the corners of every society will rapidly grow and multiply, making the society sick from head to toe.

The role of the "Ulu Baqiyyah" in the survival of societies is so sensitive that it must be said: without them, the right to life is stripped from the people, and this is exactly what the above verses point toward.

Who are the "Ulu Baqiyyah"?

"Ulu" means "possessors" and "Baqiyyah" means "remnant" or "that which remains." Usually, in the Arabic language, this expression is used to mean "Ulu al-Fadl" (possessors of virtue, character, and the good and pure). This is because humans usually store the better goods and more precious objects, and these remain with them; for this reason, this word carries the concept of goodness and preciousness.

Furthermore, in the field of social struggles, those who are weaker leave the scene sooner or are destroyed, and only those remain who are stronger in terms of thought or physical strength. Therefore, the "remnants" are the powerful ones. For this reason, there is an Arabic proverb that says: "In the corners there are hidden things, and among men there are remnants" (meaning there are still hidden issues to be found and among men, there are still great personalities remaining).

Also for this reason, the word "Baqiyyah," which appears in three places in the Holy Quran, carries this same concept. In the story of Talut and Jalut in the Holy Quran, we read:

"The sign of his kingship is that the Ark will come to you, in which is tranquility from your Lord and a remnant (Baqiyyah) of what the family of Musa and the family of Harun left behind..." [1]. And also in the story of Shu'ayb (peace be upon him) in this same Surah, we read that he said to his people:

"What remains with Allah (Baqiyyatullah) is better for you, if you are believers" (Hud: 86).

[1]: Surah Al-Baqarah, Verse 248.

The fact that in some expressions the name "Baqiyyatullah" is given to the Promised Mahdi (peace be upon him) also refers to this same subject. For He is a being full of grace and a great Divine treasure who has been preserved to roll up the carpet of oppression and injustice in the world and to raise the flag of justice and equity.

From here, it becomes clear what a great right these valuable personalities, the fighters against corruption, and the "Ulu Baqiyyah" have over human societies, for they are the secret to the survival and life of nations and their salvation from destruction and perdition!

Another point that draws attention in the above verse is that the Quran says: Allah does not destroy any city or settlement for its wrongdoing while its people are "reformers" (Muslihun). Paying attention to the difference between "Muslih" (reformer) and "Salih" (righteous) clarifies this point: that "righteousness" alone is not the guarantor of survival. Rather, if a society is not yet fully righteous but is stepping on the path of reform, it still has the right to survival and life. But the day it is neither righteous nor reforming, according to the Sunnah of creation, it has no right to life and will soon perish. In other words, whenever a society is oppressive but comes to its senses and intends to reform, such a society remains; but if it is oppressive and is not on the path of reform and purification, it will not remain.

Another point is that one of the sources of oppression and crime mentioned in the above verses is following whims, pleasure-seeking, and indulgence, which the Quran refers to as "Itraf" (luxury/opulence). This unconditional opulence and pleasure-seeking is the source of various deviations that arise in the affluent classes of societies, because the intoxication of lust prevents them from attending to authentic human values and understanding social realities, drowning them in rebellion and sin.

Ayahs

وَلَوْ شَآءَ رَبُّكَ لَجَعَلَ ٱلنَّاسَ أُمَّةً وَٰحِدَةً ۖ وَلَا يَزَالُونَ مُخْتَلِفِينَ (١١٨)
Had your Lord wished, He would have made mankind one community; but they continue to differ, (118)
إِلَّا مَن رَّحِمَ رَبُّكَ ۚ وَلِذَٰلِكَ خَلَقَهُمْ ۗ وَتَمَّتْ كَلِمَةُ رَبِّكَ لَأَمْلَأَنَّ جَهَنَّمَ مِنَ ٱلْجِنَّةِ وَٱلنَّاسِ أَجْمَعِينَ (١١٩)
except those on whom your Lord has mercy—and that is why He created them—and the word of your Lord has been fulfilled: ‘I will surely fill hell with jinn and humans, all together!’ (119)

Commentary

In the first verse under discussion, reference is made to one of the laws of creation which, in fact, forms the foundation for other matters related to human beings: the issue of diversity and difference in the spiritual and physical constitution, intellect, taste, and passion of humans, as well as the issue of freedom of will and choice.

It states: "And if your Lord had willed, He could have made mankind one single community; but they will not cease to differ" (وَ لَوْ شاءَ رَبُّكَ لَجَعَلَ النَّاسَ أُمَّةً واحِدَةً وَ لا يَزالُونَ مُخْتَلِفِينَ‌).

This is so that no one imagines that the Lord's emphasis and insistence on obeying His command is evidence that He was unable to place them all on a single path and within a specific program.

Indeed, there was no obstacle for Him to create all humans uniformly, as believers in the truth and compelled to accept faith, by way of coercion and necessity.

However, neither would such faith have been beneficial, nor would such unity and harmony. Compulsory faith arising from involuntary motives is neither a proof of personality, nor a means of perfection, nor a cause for reward and recompense. It would be exactly like how Allah created the honeybee such that it follows the nectar of flowers by the necessity of instinct, or how He placed the malaria mosquito such that it nests only in marshes; neither of them has any choice in this path.

Fundamentally, the value and distinction of the human being—and his most important difference from other creatures—is possessing this gift of freedom of will and choice, as well as having various and differing tastes, temperaments, and thoughts, each of which builds a part of society and fulfills one of its dimensions.

On the other hand, when freedom of will exists, difference in choosing beliefs and schools of thought is natural—a difference that causes one group to accept the path of truth and another the path of falsehood. This remains the case unless humans are nurtured and receive correct teachings within the embrace of the Lord's mercy and by utilizing His gifts. At that time, despite all the differences they have and while preserving their freedom and choice, they will step onto the path of truth, although even within this path, they will still have differences.

For this reason, in the following verse, He says: "People differ in accepting the truth, except those on whom your Lord has bestowed mercy" (إِلَّا مَنْ رَحِمَ رَبُّكَ‌).

But this Divine mercy is not restricted to a specific group; everyone can (provided they wish to) benefit from it. In fact, "Allah created people to receive this mercy and gift" (وَ لِذلِكَ خَلَقَهُمْ‌).

For those who wish to place themselves under this umbrella of Divine mercy, the way is open—a mercy that has been bestowed upon all people through the discernment of intellect and the guidance of the Prophets and Heavenly Books.

And whenever they utilize this mercy and gift, the gates of Paradise and eternal felicity will be opened to them.

Otherwise, "the decree of Allah has been issued: I will surely fill Hell with the rebellious and defiant among jinn and men all together" (وَ تَمَّتْ كَلِمَةُ رَبِّكَ لَأَمْلَأَنَّ جَهَنَّمَ مِنَ الْجِنَّةِ وَ النَّاسِ أَجْمَعِينَ‌).

Here, several points must be noted

[Freedom of Will]

1- Freedom of will is the basis of human creation and the call of all Prophets. Fundamentally, without it, man would not advance even a single step on the path of perfection (human and spiritual perfection). For this reason, it is emphasized in numerous verses of the Quran that if Allah had willed, He would have guided everyone by compulsion, but He did not so will.

The work of Allah is only to invite to the path of truth, to show the way, to set signposts, to warn against deviance, and to appoint a guide and a program for traversing the path.

The Quran says: إِنَّ عَلَيْنا لَلْهُدى‌! "Indeed, it is for Us to show the way" (Surah Al-Layl, Verse 12).

And it also says: إِنَّما أَنْتَ مُذَكِّرٌ لَسْتَ عَلَيْهِمْ بِمُصَيْطِرٍ: "You are only a reminder, you are not a dictator over them" (Surah Al-Ghashiyah, Verse 21).

And in Surah Ash-Shams (Verse 8), He says: فَأَلْهَمَها فُجُورَها وَ تَقْواها: "Allah created the soul and inspired it with its wickedness and its righteousness."

And also in Surah Al-Insan (Verse 4), we read: إِنَّا هَدَيْناهُ السَّبِيلَ إِمَّا شاكِراً وَ إِمَّا كَفُوراً: "Indeed, We guided him to the way, be he grateful or ungrateful."

Therefore, the verses under discussion are among the clearest verses emphasizing the freedom of human will and the negation of the school of determinism (Jabr), and they are proof that the final decision rests with the human being himself.

[The Purpose of Creation]

2- Regarding the purpose of creation, various statements have appeared in the verses of the Quran, each of which actually refers to one of the dimensions of this goal. Among them: وَ ما خَلَقْتُ الْجِنَّ وَ الْإِنْسَ إِلَّا لِيَعْبُدُونِ‌: "I did not create the jinn and mankind except to worship Me" (Adh-Dhariyat, 56); meaning that in the school of servitude and worship, they find "perfection" and reach the highest station of humanity in this school.

Elsewhere we read: الَّذِي خَلَقَ الْمَوْتَ وَ الْحَياةَ لِيَبْلُوَكُمْ أَيُّكُمْ أَحْسَنُ عَمَلًا: "He who created death and life to test you [as to] which of you is best in deed" (meaning a test mixed with nurturing and resulting in perfection) — Surah Al-Mulk, Verse 2.

And in the verse under discussion, He says: وَ لِذلِكَ خَلَقَهُمْ‌: "For the acceptance of mercy—a mercy mixed with guidance and the power of decision-making—He created people."

As you observe, all these lines lead to a single point, which is the nurturing, guidance, progress, and perfection of human beings, which is considered the ultimate goal of creation.

A goal whose benefit returns to the human being himself, not to Allah, for He is an Infinite Being in all respects, and such a Being has no deficiency within Him that He would seek to resolve through the creation of creatures.

[Filling Hell with Jinn and Men]

3- At the end of the last verse, we read the emphatic decree of Allah regarding filling Hell with jinn and men. However, it is obvious that this certain decree has only one condition: exiting the circle of Divine mercy and turning one's back on the guidance and direction of His messengers. In this way, this verse is not only not evidence for the school of determinism, but it is a renewed emphasis on free choice.

Ayahs

وَكُلًّا نَّقُصُّ عَلَيْكَ مِنْ أَنۢبَآءِ ٱلرُّسُلِ مَا نُثَبِّتُ بِهِۦ فُؤَادَكَ ۚ وَجَآءَكَ فِى هَـٰذِهِ ٱلْحَقُّ وَمَوْعِظَةٌ وَذِكْرَىٰ لِلْمُؤْمِنِينَ (١٢٠)
Whatever We relate to you of the accounts of the apostles are those by which We strengthen your heart, and there has come to you in this [surah] the truth and an advice and admonition for the faithful. (120)
وَقُل لِّلَّذِينَ لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ ٱعْمَلُوا۟ عَلَىٰ مَكَانَتِكُمْ إِنَّا عَـٰمِلُونَ (١٢١)
Say to those who do not have faith, ‘Act according to your ability; we too are acting. (121)
وَٱنتَظِرُوٓا۟ إِنَّا مُنتَظِرُونَ (١٢٢)
And wait! We too are waiting.’ (122)
وَلِلَّهِ غَيْبُ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ وَإِلَيْهِ يُرْجَعُ ٱلْأَمْرُ كُلُّهُۥ فَٱعْبُدْهُ وَتَوَكَّلْ عَلَيْهِ ۚ وَمَا رَبُّكَ بِغَـٰفِلٍ عَمَّا تَعْمَلُونَ (١٢٣)
To Allah belongs the Unseen of the heavens and the earth, and to Him all matters are returned. So worship Him and trust in Him. Your Lord is not oblivious of what you do. (123)

Commentary

Studying the History of Predecessors Has Four Effects

In this verse, with which the verses of Surah Hud conclude, a general conclusion of the collective discussions of the Surah is presented. Since a major part of this Surah consisted of the cautionary stories of the Prophets and past nations, it summarizes the precious results of mentioning these stories into four subjects:

First, It says: "We relate to you various stories of the messengers to strengthen your heart and make your will firm and steady" (وَ كُلًّا نَقُصُّ عَلَيْكَ مِنْ أَنْباءِ الرُّسُلِ ما نُثَبِّتُ بِهِ فُؤادَكَ‌).

The word "كُلًّا" (all/each) refers to the diversity of these accounts, each pointing to a different type of opposition against the Prophets, a different type of deviation, and a different type of punishment. This diversity casts a bright light on the various dimensions of human life.

The "strengthening of the heart" of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and the fortification of His will, mentioned in this verse, is a completely natural matter. This is because the stubborn opposition of relentless and ruthless enemies would inevitably affect the heart of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, as He too was a human being.

However, so that the slightest dust of despair or hopelessness never settles upon His pure heart, and so His iron will does not weaken due to these oppositions and sabotages, Allah explains the stories of the Prophets, the difficulties of their work, their resistance against stubborn nations, and their victories one after another. This ensures that the spirit and heart of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, as well as the believers who participate alongside Him in this great struggle, become stronger every day than the day before.

Then, it points to the second great result of relating these stories, saying:

"In these accounts of the Prophets, the truths and realities regarding life and existence, victory and defeat, and the factors of success and misery have all come to you" (وَ جاءَكَ‌ فِي هذِهِ الْحَقُ‌) [1]. The third and fourth striking effects of relating these histories are that "they are an exhortation and a reminder for the believers" (وَ مَوْعِظَةٌ وَ ذِكْرى‌ لِلْمُؤْمِنِينَ‌). It is interesting that the author of Al-Manar says regarding this verse: In this verse, the miracle of conciseness and brevity is such that it is as if it has gathered all the miraculous nature of past histories within itself and expressed all their benefits in a few short sentences.

In any case, this verse emphasizes once again that the histories in the Quran should not be taken lightly or used merely as entertainment for listeners, for they are a collection of the best life lessons in all fields and a pathfinder for all humans of "today" and "tomorrow."

Then, He commands the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله to say to the enemies, in the face of their stubbornness and persistence, exactly what some of the previous Prophets said to such individuals: "Say to those who do not believe: Do whatever is in your power and spare no effort; we too shall do what we can" (وَ قُلْ لِلَّذِينَ لا يُؤْمِنُونَ اعْمَلُوا عَلى‌ مَكانَتِكُمْ إِنَّا عامِلُونَ‌).

[1]: From what was said above, it becomes clear that the referent of the pronoun "هذه" (this) is "أَنْباءِ الرُّسُلِ" (the news of the messengers). Given the proximity of this referent and its mention in the text, and its suitability with the discussions in this verse, the return of the pronoun to such a referent is quite clear. However, other possibilities—such as the referent being "the world" or specifically "the preceding verses"—seem unlikely. The view of many commentators that the referent is the Surah itself is compatible with what we have said, as the major part of the preceding Surah was an account of the stories of previous Prophets.

"Wait, and we too are waiting to see who will be victorious and who will be defeated" (وَ انْتَظِرُوا إِنَّا مُنْتَظِرُونَ‌).

You wait in the vain hope of our defeat, while we truly wait for the divine punishment for you, which you will suffer either at our hands or directly from Allah! Such threats mentioned in the form of a "command" (imperative) are seen in other parts of the Quran as well, such as: "Do what you will; indeed, He is Seeing of what you do" (Surah Fussilat, Verse 40), and regarding Satan we read: "And incite whomever you can among them with your voice and assault them with your cavalry and infantry" (Surah Al-Isra, Verse 64).

It is obvious that none of these imperative forms are meant to compel action; rather, they all carry the aspect of a threat.

The final verse of this Surah addresses Monotheism (Tawhid of Knowledge, Tawhid of Actions, and Tawhid of Worship), just as the opening verses of this Surah spoke of the knowledge of Monotheism. In reality, this verse places its finger on three branches of Monotheism: First, the Lord's Monotheism of Knowledge; the unseen of the heavens and the earth belongs specifically to Allah, and it is He who is aware of all secrets, manifest and hidden (وَ لِلَّهِ غَيْبُ السَّماواتِ وَ الْأَرْضِ‌). Other than Him, knowledge is limited, and even within that limitation, it is derived from divine teaching. Therefore, unlimited knowledge—and that too, essential knowledge—regarding everything within the expanse of the earth and sky belongs exclusively to the Pure Essence of the Lord.

On the other hand, the reins of all affairs are in the hand of His power; everything returns to Him (وَ إِلَيْهِ يُرْجَعُ الْأَمْرُ كُلُّهُ‌).

And this is the stage of Monotheism of Actions (Tawhid al-Af'ali).

Then it concludes: Now that unlimited awareness and infinite power belong exclusively to His Pure Essence, and the return of everything is toward Him, "Therefore, worship Him alone" (فَاعْبُدْهُ).

And rely only upon Him (وَ تَوَكَّلْ عَلَيْهِ).

And this is the stage of Monotheism of Worship (Tawhid al-Ibadah).

And refrain from whatever constitutes disobedience, rebellion, transgression, and sin, for "Allah is not heedless of what you do" (وَ ما رَبُّكَ بِغافِلٍ عَمَّا تَعْمَلُونَ‌).

Here, several points must be noted

1- The Knowledge of the Unseen Belongs Exclusively to Allah.

As we explained in detail in Volume 7 under Verse 188 of Al-A'raf and in Volume 5 under Verse 50 of Surah Al-An'am, there is no doubt that awareness of hidden secrets and the secrets of the past and future belongs exclusively to Allah. Various verses of the Quran testify to this reality; He is unique in this attribute, and no one is like or similar to Him.

If we see in some verses of the Quran that a portion of the knowledge of the unseen is attributed to the Prophets, or if we read in numerous verses and traditions about the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, Ali عليه السلام, and the Ma'sum Imams عليهم السلام that they sometimes gave news of future events and hidden secrets, it must be understood that this too is through divine teaching.

It is He who, whenever He deems appropriate, teaches a portion of the secrets of the unseen to His special servants. However, this knowledge is neither essential nor unlimited; rather, it is through divine instruction and limited to the amount He has willed.

With this explanation, the answer to all the detractors who have criticized the Shia belief regarding the knowledge of the unseen—claiming that they consider the Prophets and Imams to be "Knowers of the Unseen" (Alim al-Ghayb)—becomes clear.

Not only does Allah teach the secrets of the unseen to the Prophets and Imams when necessary, but He sometimes grants such teaching to those other than a Prophet or Imam, as we read in the story of the mother of Moses in the Quran, where Allah said to her: "Do not fear and do not grieve; indeed, We will return him to you and will make him one of the messengers" (Surah Al-Qasas, Verse 7).

Even birds and other living creatures sometimes, due to the necessities of life, gain awareness of hidden secrets and even the relatively distant future, the conception of which is difficult and complex for us. In this way, some matters that are considered "unseen" for us are not unseen for them.

2- Worship is Exclusive to Him.

In the above verse, a subtle reason for the exclusivity of worship to Allah is stated: if worship is due to greatness and the attributes of beauty and majesty, these attributes are found most of all in Allah, and others are insignificant before Him. The greatest sign of greatness is unlimited knowledge and infinite power, which the above verse says both belong exclusively to Him. And if worship is for the sake of seeking refuge in a deity to solve problems, such an act is worthy only of one who is aware of all the needs and requirements of the servants and their hidden secrets, and has the ability to answer the call and fulfill the requests. For this reason, Monotheism of Attributes leads to Monotheism of Worship (reflect on this).

3- Man's Journey in "فَاعْبُدْهُ وَ تَوَكَّلْ عَلَيْهِ‌"

3- Some commentators have said that the entire journey of man on the path of servitude to the Lord is summarized in two phrases in the above verse: "فَاعْبُدْهُ وَ تَوَكَّلْ عَلَيْهِ‌" (Worship Him and rely upon Him). This is because worship—whether it be physical worship like ordinary acts of devotion, or spiritual worship like reflecting on the world of creation and the system of the secrets of existence—is the beginning of this journey.

And reliance (Tawakkul) means absolute surrender to Allah and entrusting everything to His hand, which is considered a type of "annihilation in Allah" (Fana fi-Allah), and is the final point of this journey.

Throughout this path, from beginning to end, attention to the reality of the Monotheism of Attributes aids the travelers of this way and compels them toward effort and striving mixed with love.

O Lord! Grant that we may know You through Your attributes of majesty and beauty. And grant that we may move toward You with awareness.

O Lord! Give us the success to "worship" You sincerely and to "rely" upon You lovingly.

O Lord! In this period of time, where following our glorious Islamic Revolution, increasing problems have surrounded us from every side, and enemies strive to extinguish the light of this revolution, You are our only hope, and Your Pure Essence is the support for solving these problems.

O Lord! It was not we who traversed the path this far; rather, it was Your manifest and hidden support that empowered us everywhere to reach this stage. In the remainder of the path, do not deprive us of this great blessing and do not withhold Your special grace from us. Also, grant us the success to be able to complete this Tafsir, which opens a new window into Your great Heavenly Book.

(End of Surah Hud)

Surah Yusuf (Containing 111 verses, all revealed in Mecca)

At the beginning of Surah Yusuf, before entering the commentary of the verses of this Surah, several matters must be mentioned:

1- The Meccan Origin of this Surah

1- There is no dispute or objection among commentators that this Surah was revealed in Mecca. It is only narrated from Ibn Abbas that four of its verses (the first three verses and the seventh verse) were revealed in Medina.

However, careful examination of the connection between these verses and the other verses of this Surah shows that they cannot be separated from the rest. Therefore, the possibility of these four verses being revealed in Medina is very weak.

2- The Content of this Surah

2- All the verses of this Surah, except for a few verses at the end, relate the interesting, sweet, and cautionary story of the Prophet of God, Yusuf عليه السلام. For this reason, this Surah is named Yusuf. Also, for this reason, out of the total 27 times the name Yusuf is mentioned in the Quran, 25 times are in this Surah, and only two instances are in other Surahs (Surah Ghafir, Verse 34 and Al-An'am, Verse 84). The content of this Surah, unlike other Surahs of the Quran, is entirely interconnected and relates different episodes of a single story, presented in more than ten sections with exceptionally eloquent, attractive, condensed, deep, and exciting expression.

Although aimless storytellers, or those with lowly and tainted goals, have tried to turn this instructive account into a provocative love story for the hedonistic and to distort the true face of Yusuf and his history—even bringing it to the cinema screen in the form of a romance film—the Quran, in which everything is a model and "example," has reflected within this story the highest lessons of chastity, self-control, piety, faith, and mastery over the self. This is such that any human—even if they have read it many times—is involuntarily affected by its powerful attractions upon reading it again.

For this reason, the Quran has given it the beautiful name "Ahsan al-Qasas" (the best of stories), and in it, has expressed lessons for "those of understanding" (ulu al-albab).

3- The Quran is a Miracle in All Its Dimensions

3- Reflection on the verses of this Surah makes this reality clearer to man: that the Quran is a miracle in all its dimensions. This is because the heroes it introduces in its stories—real heroes and not imaginary ones—are each unique in their own kind. Abraham, the idol-breaking hero with that lofty and uncompromising spirit against tyrants.

Noah, that hero of patience, perseverance, steadfastness, and compassion throughout that long and blessed life.

Moses, that hero of educating a stubborn population against a rebellious tyrant.

Yusuf, that hero of purity, virtue, and piety, in the face of a beautiful, hedonistic, and cunning woman.

Beyond this, the power of the expression of Quranic revelation has manifested in this story in such a way that it leaves man in wonder. For this story, as we know, leads in some instances to very delicate matters of love, and the Quran, without omitting them or bypassing them, describes all these scenes with their intricacies in such a way that the slightest negative or undesirable feeling is not created in the listener. It enters the heart of all events, yet everywhere, a powerful ray of piety and purity surrounds the discussions.

4- The Story of Yusuf Before and After Islam

Undoubtedly, before Islam, the story of Yusuf was famous and well-known among the people, as this story is mentioned in detail in fourteen chapters of the Book of Genesis (from Chapter 37 to 50) in the Torah.

Of course, a careful study of these fourteen chapters shows that what is in the Torah has many differences from the Holy Quran. A comparison of these differences shows to what extent what has come in the Quran is refined, pure, and free from any superstition. The fact that the Quran says to the Prophet: "Before this, you were among the unaware" (assuming "Ahsan al-Qasas" refers to the story of Yusuf) refers to the Prophet's lack of awareness of the pure reality of this cautionary account.

From the current Torah, it appears that when Jacob saw Yusuf's blood-stained shirt, he said: "This is my son's tunic; a wild beast has eaten him. Surely Yusuf has been torn to pieces." Then Jacob tore his clothes, put sackcloth around his waist, and mourned for his son for many days. All his sons and daughters rose to comfort him, but he refused to be comforted and said, "I will go down to the grave mourning for my son."

While the Quran says: Jacob, with his intelligence and insight, became aware of his children's lie and did not wail or show restlessness in this calamity. Rather, as is the tradition of the Prophets, he dealt with that calamity patiently, even though his heart burned and his tears flowed. Naturally, he lost his sight due to excessive weeping, but in the words of the Quran, with "beautiful patience" and self-control (kazim), he tried to refrain from acts such as tearing clothes, wailing, and tying sackcloth around the waist, which were specific signs of mourning.

In any case, after Islam, this story has also appeared in the writings of Eastern and Western historians, sometimes with additional embellishments. In Persian poetry, the first story of Yusuf and Zulaikha is attributed to Ferdowsi, followed by the Yusuf and Zulaikha of Shahabuddin Am'aq and Mas'udi Qomi, and after them, the Yusuf and Zulaikha of Abdul Rahman Jami, the famous poet of the ninth century [1].

5- Why is the Story of Yusuf Told All at Once, Unlike the Accounts of Other Prophets?

  • One of the characteristics of the story of Yusuf is that it is all told in one place, unlike the accounts of other Prophets which are spread out in separate parts across various Surahs of the Quran.

This characteristic is because separating the episodes of this story, given its specific nature, would break its essential connection. For a complete conclusion, all must be mentioned together. For example, the dream of Yusuf and the interpretation his father provided for it, which appears at the beginning of this Surah, has no meaning without mentioning the end of the story.

Therefore, we read toward the end of this Surah that when Jacob and Yusuf's brothers came to Egypt and humbled themselves before his magnificent position, Yusuf turned to his father and said: "O my father! This is the interpretation of my dream of before; my Lord has made it a reality" (Verse 100).

[1]: Kashf al-Zunun, Hajji Khalifa, Vol. 2, p. 661.

This example clarifies the unbreakable bond between the beginning and the end of this story, whereas the stories of other Prophets are not like this, and each of their episodes is independently understandable and yields a conclusion.

Another characteristic of this Surah is that the stories of other Prophets mentioned in the Quran usually describe their struggles with rebellious and tyrannical nations, where eventually a group would believe and another group would continue their opposition until their destruction by divine punishment.

However, in the story of Yusuf, there is no mention of this subject; rather, it mostly portrays the life of Yusuf himself and his passage through the harsh trials of life, which eventually transforms into a powerful government that was exemplary in its kind.

6- The Virtue of Surah Yusuf.

In Islamic traditions, various virtues are mentioned for reciting this Surah. Among them, we read in a Hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام:

"Whoever recites Surah Yusuf every day or every night, Allah will raise him on the Day of Resurrection while his beauty is like the beauty of Yusuf, no distress of the Day of Resurrection shall strike him, and he will be among the best of Allah's righteous servants." [1]

We have said many times that the traditions mentioned regarding the virtue of the Surahs of the Quran do not mean a superficial reading without reflection and action. Rather, it is a recitation that is a prelude to reflection, and reflection that is the beginning of action. Given the content of this Surah, it is clear that if someone takes their life program from it and practices self-control in the face of the intense storms of lust, wealth, status, and position—to the point of preferring the dungeon of a prison coupled with chastity over the tainted palace of kings—such a person's beauty of soul and spirit is like the beauty of Yusuf. In the Resurrection, when everything within is revealed, they will possess a dazzling beauty and will be in the ranks of the righteous servants of Allah.

It is necessary to mention that in a few traditions, teaching this Surah to women has been discouraged. Perhaps this is because the verses related to the "wife of the Aziz of Egypt" and the hedonistic Egyptian women, despite all the chastity of expression observed in them, might be provocative for some women. Conversely, it has been emphasized that Surah An-Nur (which contains the verses of Hijab) should be taught to them.

However, the chains of these traditions are generally not very reliable. Furthermore, in some traditions, the opposite is seen, where teaching this Surah to families is encouraged. Beyond this, reflection on the verses of this Surah shows that not only is there no negative point for women in it, but the story of the tainted life of the wife of the Aziz of Egypt is a lesson for all those who fall into satanic temptations.

[1]: Majma' al-Bayan, under the verse.
